characteristic_fr:
SituĂŠe sur la cĂ´te victorienne, Ă  l'ouest du promontoire de Wilsons, la baie, de forme semi-circulaire, a ĂŠtĂŠ dĂŠcrite par $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ comme ÂŤ trĂ¨s grande et trĂ¨s profonde Âť. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ nota ĂŠgalement que ÂŤ plusieurs pitons trĂ¨s ĂŠlevĂŠs se distinguaient sur la cĂ´te orientale decette baie, et que l'on apercoit dans le lointain une chaĂŽne de hautes montagnes Âť. Il faisait probablement rĂŠfĂŠrence aux montagnes et collines granitiques du promontoire Wilsons.
 Le fait dâavoir donnĂŠ Ă  cette baie le nom du colonel William $Paterson$William_Paterson_(botaniste)$ (1755-1810), alors lieutenant-gouverneur de la colonie, constituait un honneur rare, non dĂŠnuĂŠ de naĂŻvetĂŠ, accordĂŠ par $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ qui voyait en lui "un respectable savant et voyageur ... l'un des plus intimes amis de M $Bass$George_Bass$".
characteristic:
Situated on the Victorian coast, west of Wilsons Promontory, the bay is roughly semi-circular in shape and was described by $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ as âa very large and very deep bay.â $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ also noted that âseveral very high peaks could be seen on the eastern coast of the bay, and a chain of high mountains could be glimpsed in the distance.â He was likely referring to the granitic mountains and hills of Wilsons Promontory.
 The fact that this bay was given the name of Colonel William $Paterson$William_Paterson_(explorer)$ (1755â1810), then lieutenant-governor of the colony, represented a rare honour â not without a touch of naĂŻvetĂŠ â bestowed by $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$, who saw in him âa respectable scholar and traveller âŚ one of Mr. $Bass$George_Bass$âs closest friends.â
history_fr: $Paterson$William_Paterson_(botaniste)$ avait escortĂŠ les savants de lâexpĂŠdition autour de Port Jackson lors de leur escale (de la fin juin au 18 novembre 1802) et leur avait fourni des informations sur lâhistoire naturelle de la rĂŠgion. Il avait ĂŠgalement aidĂŠ $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ Ă  ĂŠtablir des listes de mots de la langue aborigĂ¨ne et fourni des spĂŠcimens aux minĂŠralogistes en visite. En retour, LeschĂŠnault, le botaniste de lâexpĂŠdition, lui avait offert des ĂŠchantillons de plantes quâil avait collectĂŠs, que $Paterson$William_Paterson_(botaniste)$ envoya Ă  $Joseph Banks$Joseph_Banks$ en Angleterre pour identification. Cependant, $Paterson$William_Paterson_(botaniste)$, en bon lieutenant anglais soucieux de dĂŠfendre les intĂŠrĂŞts de sa patrie, recueillit ĂŠgalement quelques renseignements sur les objectifs du voyage franĂ§ais et sur les intentions des savants en Australie, quâil transmit au gouverneur King. Ce qui inquiĂŠta le plus ce dernier fut lâinformation (attribuĂŠe Ă  $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$) selon laquelle les FranĂ§ais envisageaient dâĂŠtablir une colonie en Tasmanie.
history:
$Paterson$William_Paterson_(explorer)$ had escorted the expeditionâs scientists around Port Jackson during the expedition stopover (late June-18 November 1802) and provided them with information about the areaâs natural history. He also assisted $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ in compiling lists of words in the Aboriginal language and supplied specimens to the visiting mineralogists. In return, LeschĂŠnault, the expeditionâs botanist, gave him plant specimens he had collected which $Paterson$William_Paterson_(explorer)$ sent to $Joseph Banks$Joseph_Banks$ in England for identification. 
 However, when in the company of the French scientists, $Paterson$William_Paterson_(explorer)$ also gathered some intelligence about the aims of the French voyage and their intentions in Australia, which he communicated to Governor King. Most disturbing to the governor was information (sourced to $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$) that the French intended to form an establishment in Tasmania.

characteristic_fr: Cette grande baie ouverte est similaire Ă  la baie de Waratah. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ note que ÂŤ Les terres de cette seconde baie sont assez hautes vers ses deux pointes ; mais toutes celles qui en forment le contour sont beaucoup plus basses.Âť. Les deux extrĂŠmitĂŠs auxquelles $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ fait rĂŠfĂŠrence sont probablement les falaises de Bunurong (avec leurs parois abruptes et leurs promontoires rocheux) Ă  l'ouest et la falaise rocheuse du cap Liptrap Ă  l'est, un site gĂŠologique important dans l'Ătat de Victoria, prĂŠsentant des sĂŠquences gĂŠologiques allant des roches plissĂŠes du Cambrien Ă  des formations sableuses rĂŠcentes. Dans son rĂŠcit du voyage, $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ explique : ÂŤ Ă l'aube du 29 mars, nous avons commencĂŠ Ă  longer une deuxiĂ¨me baie qui, comme la premiĂ¨re [$Baie Paterson$William_Paterson_(botaniste)$/Waratah Bay], se trouve au nord-ouest du promontoire [Wilsons]. Elle a ĂŠtĂŠ baptisĂŠe Venus Bay en l'honneur d'un navire commandĂŠ par M. $Bass$George_Bass$... Âť. Dans son rĂŠcit du voyage, $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ explique : ÂŤ Le 29 mars, Ă  la pointe du jour, nous commenĂ§Ă˘mes Ă  pro longer une seconde baie, qui se trouve, comme la prĂŠcĂŠdente, au N. O. du promontoire; elle fut nommĂŠe Baie de la VĂŠnus, d'un navire commandĂŠ par M. $Bass$George_Bass$ [$George Bass$George_Bass$] Âť.
characteristic:
This large open bay is similar to Waratah Bay. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ notes that âthe land in this second bay [next to Waratah Bay] is quite high around its two points, but all the land around it is much lower.â The two extremities $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ refers to are probably the Bunurong Cliffs (with their steep sea cliffs and rocky headlands) to the west and the rocky cliff of Cape Liptrap to the east, a significant geological teaching site in Victoria, featuring geological sequences ranging from folded Cambrian rocks to recent sands.
 In his narrative of the voyage, $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ explained: âAt daybreak on the 29th of March we began coasting a second bay, which, like the first, [$Baie Paterson$William_Paterson_(explorer)$/Waratah Bay] lies north-west of the [Wilsons] promontory. It was named Venus Bay after a vessel commanded by Mr $Bass$George_Bass$âŚâ.
history_fr:
Le 14 novembre 1802, deux jours avant le dĂŠpart de lâexpĂŠdition franĂ§aise de Port Jackson, $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ aperĂ§ut la VĂŠnus, un brick appartenant conjointement Ă  $George Bass$George_Bass$ et Ă  un groupe dâamis. Le navire, destinĂŠ au transport de marchandises Ă  des fins commerciales, rentrait alors au port avec $Bass$George_Bass$ Ă  son bord, de retour dâun voyage entrepris pour acheter du porc salĂŠ dans les ĂŽles des Amis, les ĂŽles de la SociĂŠtĂŠ et les ĂŽles Sandwich. Il est trĂ¨s probable que $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ revit $Bass$George_Bass$ le 15 novembre, lorsque ce dernier fut invitĂŠ Ă  bord du Naturaliste en compagnie du gouverneur King. On peut supposer que, pour lâoccasion, $Bass$George_Bass$ offrit Ă  lâexpĂŠdition franĂ§aise quelques spĂŠcimens dâoiseaux tropicaux du Pacifique, ainsi que des objets ethnographiques rapportĂŠs par la VĂŠnus.
 
 $Bass$George_Bass$ ne naviguait pas sur la VĂŠnus lorsquâil explora les cĂ´tes australiennes, mais sur une simple chaloupe. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ rapporte dâailleurs :
 
 ÂŤ La dĂŠcouverte du dĂŠtroit qui sĂŠpare la Nouvelle-Hollande de la terre de DiĂŠmen avoit ĂŠtĂŠ faite sur une simple chaloupe baleiniĂ¨re, commandĂŠe par M. $Bass$George_Bass$, chirurgien du vaisseau la Reliance [le navire qui avait amenĂŠ $Bass$George_Bass$ Ă  Port Jackson en 1795]. ConsacrĂŠe, pour ainsi dire, par cette grande dĂŠcouverte, par cette audacieuse navigation, la chaloupe de M. $Bass$George_Bass$ est conservĂŠe dans le port [Port Jackson] avec une sorte de respect religieux : quelques tabatiĂ¨res faites avec le bois de sa quille sont des reliques dont les possesseurs se montrent aussi fiers que jaloux ; et M. le Gouverneur lui-mĂŞme ne crut pas pouvoir faire un prĂŠsent plus honorable Ă  notre Chef que celui dâun morceau du bois de cette chaloupe, enchĂ˘ssĂŠ dans un large ĂŠtui dâargent, autour duquel ĂŠtoient gravĂŠs les principaux dĂŠtails de la dĂŠcouverte du dĂŠtroit de $Bass$George_Bass$. Âť
history:
$PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ saw the Venus, as, on 14 November 1802, two days before the French expedition left Port Jackson, $Bass$George_Bass$ was just coming back on board this copper-sheathed cargo from his trade voyage to purchase salt pork from Ăle des Amis, Society Islands and Sandwich Islands. He saw probably $Bass$George_Bass$ on the 15, when the later was on board le Naturaliste with Governor King and possibly offered to the French expedition some specimens of pacific tropical birds and ethnographic artifacts brought back on the Venus.
 $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$âs voyage narrative emphasized that Venus Bay honored one of âthe most interesting men to have appeared thus far in these southern, English colonies, âŚMr $Bass$George_Bass$, the surgeon aboard the Reliance [the vessel that that brought $Bass$George_Bass$ to Port Jackson in 1795], and the very same who daringly ventured out into unknown waters in a small whaleboat and was the first to discover that famous strait, upon which public gratitude has bestowed his name [$Bass$George_Bass$ Strait]â.

characteristic_fr: French island se trouve dans la baie communĂŠment appelĂŠe Western Port Bay. Le capitaine Hamelin, qui visita les cĂ´tes du dĂŠtroit de $Bass$George_Bass$ Ă  bord du Naturaliste en avril 1802, soupĂ§onnait que la copie du croquis rĂŠalisĂŠ par $Bass$George_Bass$ en 1797â1798, quâil avait Ă  bord, ĂŠtait en partie ÂŤ inexacte Âť. Il envoya donc son gĂŠographe Faure, accompagnĂŠ de ses officiers $Milius$Pierre_Bernard_Milius$ et BrĂŠvedent, Ă  bord de son grand et petit canots, afin dâeffectuer un relevĂŠ du port et son bassin. Il leur donna pour instruction de faire les observations nĂŠcessaires pour permettre au Naturaliste dâentrer et de mouiller dans le port, et dâen dresser une carte. Ils dĂŠcouvrirent qu' ÂŤ Ă  lâest, au fond du bassin, une ĂŽle ĂŠtait reliĂŠe au continent par un banc de limon Âť. Sur lâautre embarcation, BrĂŠvedent fut ÂŤ le premier Âť [EuropĂŠen] Ă  en faire le tour. Elle fut baptisĂŠe Ăle des FranĂ§ois [FranĂ§ais]. Il est Ă  noter que cette mission dâexploration et de reconnaissance se dĂŠroula simultanĂŠment Ă  la rencontre dĂŠsormais cĂŠlĂ¨bre entre Baudin et Flinders, tandis que Le GĂŠographe et Le Naturaliste naviguaient alors indĂŠpendamment lâun de lâautre.LâĂŽle est aujourdâhui un parc national.
characteristic: French Island lie within Western Port, commonly known as Western Port Bay. Captain Hamelin, who visited the coasts of $Bass$George_Bass$ Straight aboard the Naturaliste in April 1802, suspected that the copy of the sketch made by $Bass$George_Bass$ in 1797-98 he had on board was partly âinaccurate.â He therefore sent his geographer Faure, accompanied by his officers $Milius$FRPierre_Bernard_Milius$ and BrĂŠvedent, in both his large and small boats to carry out a survey of Western Port, instructing them to make the necessary observations to enable the Naturaliste to enter and anchor in the port, and to produce a chart of it. They discovered that âthere to the east, at the back of the basin, an island was joined to the mainland by a silt bankâ. On the other boat, BrĂŠvedent was âthe firstâ to circumnavigate it. It was named Ăle des FranĂ§ois [FranĂ§ais]. Notably, thisexploratory and reconnaissance mission occurred simultaneously with the celebrated encounter between Baudin and Flinders, while Le GĂŠographe and Le Naturaliste were navigating independently. The island is now a national park.
history_fr: Ă bord d'une des chaloupes, $Milius$Pierre_Bernard_Milius$ et Faure dĂŠcouvrirent quâÂŤ Ă  lâest, au fond du bassin [Western Port/Bay], une ĂŽle se trouvait rattachĂŠe au continent par un banc de vase Âť. Depuis lâautre embarcation, BrĂŠvedent en fit la circumnavigation, et lâĂle fut baptisĂŠe Ăle des FranĂ§ois [FranĂ§ais]. Dans son journal, le botaniste Leschenault qui accompagnait $Milius$Pierre_Bernard_Milius$ note a propos de l'Ăle: ÂŤ Le 20. germinal an 10. [10 avril 1802] nous dĂŠscendimes le soir sur le cĂ´tĂŠ occidentale de la 2.eme passe le capit.ne de frĂŠgate Miliu [$Milius$Pierre_Bernard_Milius$] a reconnu que cette portion de terre indiquĂŠe sur la carte anglaise comme faisant partie du continent est une ile ; il paroit quâelle nâest habitĂŠe que trĂ¨s passagerement, car nous ne trouvames que quelques traces de feu, fort anciennes, et dĂŠja la vĂŠgĂŠtation avoit rĂŠparĂŠ les ravages occasionnĂŠs par les flammes, avec toute la vigueur que peut favoriser un sol excĂŠllent dont lâhumiditĂŠ est, sans cesse, entretenue par un hombrage habituel ; aussi ce ne fut quâavec difficultĂŠ que nous trouvames un petit emplacement moins embarassĂŠ dâarbustes et de lianes pour passer la nuit, elle fut assez dĂŠsagreable Ă  cause du grand nombre de mousquites qui nous assaillirent, cependant un grand feu, autour duquel nous nous couchames nous en dĂŠlivra en partie. Âť
history:
On board their small boat, $Milius$FRPierre_Bernard_Milius$ and Faure discovered that âto the east, at the bottom of the basin [Western Port], an island was connected to the mainland by a mud bank.â From the other boat accompanying the surveying mission, BrĂŠvedent circumnavigated it, and the island was named Ăle des FranĂ§ois [French Island]. In his journal, the botanist Leschenault, who was accompanying $Milius$FRPierre_Bernard_Milius$, noted the following about the island: "On the 20th of Germinal, Year X [10 April 1802], we landed in the evening on the
western side of the second channel. Lieutenant-commander $Milius$FRPierre_Bernard_Milius$ perceived that this portion
of land, which is marked on the English chart as forming part of the mainland, is in fact an
island. It appears to be inhabited only occasionally, as we came across just a few, very old,
traces of fire, and the vegetation had already recovered from the ravages of the flames â with
all the vigour that occurs where an excellent soil is kept damp by a perpetual cover of shade.
When we looked for a place to spend the night, it was only with difficulty that we found a
small area that was not entirely clogged with bushes and creepers. It was a rather unpleasant
night, as we were attacked by swarms of mosquitoes. We slept around a large fire, which kept
them at bay to some extent."

characteristic_fr: Phillip Island, situĂŠe Ă  lâentrĂŠe de la baie de Western Port, fut dâabord explorĂŠe par $George Bass$George_Bass$ lors de son voyage en baleiniĂ¨re de 1797â1798. Un relevĂŠ plus systĂŠmatique fut ensuite entrepris par le lieutenant $James Grant$James_Grant$ en 1801. Ignorant les travaux antĂŠrieurs de $Grant$James_Grant$, lâexpĂŠdition franĂ§aise cartographia plus tard lâĂŽle dans le but dâamĂŠliorer les observations prĂŠliminaires de $Bass$George_Bass$ ainsi que la carte sommaire quâil en avait dressĂŠe. Voir French Island.
characteristic: Phillip Island, located at the entrance to Western Port Bay, was first examined by $George Bass$George_Bass$ during his whaleboat voyage of 1797â1798. A more systematic survey was subsequently undertaken by Lieutenant $James Grant$James_Grant_(navigator)$ in 1801. Unaware of $Grant$James_Grant_(navigator)$âs prior work, the French expedition later charted the island with the intention of improving upon $Bass$George_Bass$âs preliminary observations, and the map he had roughly drawn up. See French Island
history_fr: Le botaniste $Leschenault$Jean-Baptiste_Leschenault_de_La_Tour$ notes: "Cette ile a environ 4 lieues dâetendue, de lâest Ă  lâouest et une lieue et demi du nord au sud. Lâendroit ou nous dĂŠbarquames est une plage sabloneuse et autant que jai pĂť mâavancer dans lâintĂŠrieur je nâai trouvĂŠ que du sable, cependant les plantes y sont trĂ¨s prĂŠssĂŠes et vĂŠgĂŠtant avec beaucoup de vigueur, ce que jâattribue Ă  lâhumiditĂŠ et au sol limoneux sur lequel je pense que ce sable repose. âŚ nous y demeurames toute la journĂŠe du 26. [germinal â 16 avril 1802] que les cit. ns $Milius$Pierre_Bernard_Milius$ et Faure employant Ă  faire les relevements des dangers de la grande entrĂŠe du port."
history: The botanist $Leschenault$Jean-Baptiste_Leschenault_de_La_Tour$ notes that : âThis island is around four leagues long, from east to west, and one and a half leagues from north to south. We landed on a sandy beach, and as far as I was able to venture inland I found only sand. However, the plants grew densely and vigorously, which I attribute to the dampness of the ground and to the alluvial soil which, I believe, must lie beneath the sand. That evening we returned to the headland where I had stayed alone, and we passed the night there. We remained there for the whole of the 26th [of Germinal â 16 April 1802] while Citizens $Milius$FRPierre_Bernard_Milius$ and Faure plotted all the hazards that lie in the main entrance to the harbour.â

characteristic_fr: Ce chenal relie le dĂŠtroit de $Bass$George_Bass$ Ă  la partie ouest de la baie, donnant accĂ¨s aux installations portuaires de Hastings. $Lâofficier Milius$Pierre_Bernard_Milius$, qui en fit lâhydrographie, la jugea bien mieux adaptĂŠe Ă  la navigation que le chenal opposĂŠ de lâest.
characteristic: This channel leads from $Bass$George_Bass$ Strait into the western section of the bay, giving access to the harbor facilities of Hastings. Lieutenant Milius, who carried out its hydrographic survey, considered it far better suited for navigation than the opposite eastern channel.
history_fr: Dans son rapport de mission, $Milius$Pierre_Bernard_Milius$ explique: "LâextrĂŠmitĂŠ occidentale de cette ile [Phillip Island] forme la pointe sud de lâentrĂŠe du canal qui conduit au Port Western ; il faut en venant du promontoire Wilson, arrondir cette pointe a cause de lâIlot dont je viens de parler [Seal Rock] âŚ on prend ensuite le milieu du canal pour ĂŠviter des bancs de sable qui sâĂŠloignent de terre tribord et babord, Ă  la distance dâun mille ; ces bancs ne sont nullement dangereux parce que la mer y brise en tous tems ; on peut encore en venant du Sud ĂŠviter dâarrondir lâIlot en question en passant entre lui et un autre ilot en forme de pĂ˘tĂŠ [Round island] âŚ la passe est belle, et lâon peut sans inquiĂŠtude se rendre au mouillage du grand bassin âŚ"
history: âThe western end of this island forms the southern point of the entrance to the channel that leads to Port Western. When coming from Wilsonâs Promontory, one must round this point because of the islet I have just mentioned [Seal Rock] âŚ Then one should keep to the middle of the channel to avoid the sandbanks that extend from the shore on both starboard and port sides for about a mile; these banks are in no way dangerous because the sea always breaks over them. When coming from the south, one can also avoid rounding the said islet by passing between it and another islet shaped like a mound [Round Island] âŚ The passage is fine, and one may proceed without concern to the anchorage in the large basin âŚâ

characteristic_fr: Ce chenal est dĂŠcrit par les FranĂ§ais comme ÂŤ ĂŠtroit Âť. Aujourdâhui, un pont en bĂŠton de 640 m (2 100 pieds) â Ă  lâorigine un pont en bois â relie la ville continentale de San Remo Ă  la ville insulaire de Newhaven.
characteristic: This corridor is described by the French as ânarrowâ. Today, a 640 m (2,100 ft) concrete bridge (originally a wooden bridge) connects the mainland town San Remo with the island town Newhaven.

characteristic_fr:
SituĂŠ Ă  une courte distance Ă  l'ouest de l'entrĂŠe de Western Port Bay, dans la pĂŠninsule de Mornington, le cap a ĂŠtĂŠ faĂ§onnĂŠ par l'influence destructrice de l'eau salĂŠe sur les roches basaltiques, avec des formations gĂŠologiques qui se sont formĂŠes au cours de millions d'annĂŠes dans les roches volcaniques (Pulpit Rock).
Il a ĂŠtĂŠ nommĂŠ en l'honneur d'$Armand Jean du Plessis$Armand_Jean_du_Plessis_de_Richelieu$, cardinal de Richelieu (1585-1642), premier ministre sous le rĂ¨gne de $Louis XIII$Louis_XIII$.
characteristic:
.Located a short distance west of the entrance to Western Port Bay, on the Mornington Peninsula, the cape was shaped by the erosive action of salt water on basalt rocks, with geological formations that developed over millions of years in the volcanic strata (Pulpit Rock).
It was named in honor of $Armand Jean du Plessis$Cardinal_Richelieu$, Cardinal Richelieu (1585â1642), who served as chief minister under the reign of $Louis XIII$Louis_XIII$.
history_fr:
Ă lâĂŠpoque napolĂŠonienne, $Richelieu$Armand_Jean_du_Plessis_de_Richelieu$ ĂŠtait perĂ§u comme la figure ayant restaurĂŠ lâautoritĂŠ royale aprĂ¨s les guerres de Religion et lâassassinat du roi $Henri IV$Henri_IV_(roi_de_France)$, et dont la politique visait Ă  faire de la France une puissance dominante en Europe. Richelieu occupait ĂŠgalement une place importante pour la Marine, puisquâil fut le fondateur de la Marine royale franĂ§aise et des compagnies de commerce franĂ§aises, crĂŠĂŠes sur le modĂ¨le de la Compagnie nĂŠerlandaise des Indes orientales (VOC), afin de permettre Ă  la France de contrĂ´ler et de dĂŠvelopper son expansion commerciale.
 $Richelieu$Armand_Jean_du_Plessis_de_Richelieu$ fonda aussi, en 1635, lâAcadĂŠmie franĂ§aise, chargĂŠe de veiller sur la langue franĂ§aise et de la perfectionner comme instrument de pensĂŠe.
 De maniĂ¨re plus gĂŠnĂŠrale, on reconnaissait Ă  $Richelieu$Armand_Jean_du_Plessis_de_Richelieu$ le mĂŠrite dâavoir ĂŠtabli des structures administratives centralisĂŠes qui renforcĂ¨rent le pouvoir de lâĂtat. Ses principes en matiĂ¨re de rĂŠforme organisationnelle ĂŠtaient en accord avec ceux que NapolĂŠon adopta plus tard.
history:
In Napoleonic time, $Richelieu$Cardinal_Richelieu$ was seen as the personality who restore the royal authority after the Wars of Religion and the assassination of the king $Henri IV$Henry_IV_of_France$, and whose policy aimed to make France a European leader. Richelieu was an important person to the Navy as he established the French Royal Navy and founded the French trading companies based on the model of the Dutch East India Company (VOC) to allow France to control and develop its trading expansion. $Richelieu$Cardinal_Richelieu$ created the French Academy (lâAcadĂŠmie franĂ§aise) in 1635, that controls French language to make it a better instrument of thought.
 More generally, $Richelieu$Cardinal_Richelieu$ was acknowledged for establishing centralized administrative structures that strengthened the Stateâs power. His principles in implementing organizational reform were in phase with those later adopted by NapolĂŠon.

characteristic_fr:
La large ĂŠchancrure situĂŠe Ă  lâextĂŠrieur de lâentrĂŠe de la baie de Port Phillip, sur la cĂ´te ouest du Victoria donnant sur le dĂŠtroit de $Bass$George_Bass$, fut ĂŠgalement dĂŠfinie comme une baie par les FranĂ§ais et nommĂŠe dâaprĂ¨s $Charles-Maurice de Talleyrand-PĂŠrigord$Charles-Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PĂŠrigord$ (1754-1838).
 La prĂŠsence du nom de $Talleyrand$Charles-Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PĂŠrigord$ dans la toponymie franĂ§aise ĂŠtait presque incontournable, en reconnaissance de son statut ĂŠminent dâhomme dâĂtat expĂŠrimentĂŠ et de ses talents de nĂŠgociateur en tant que diplomate.
characteristic:
The broad indentation outside the entrance to Port Philipp Bay, on the western Victorian coast of $Bass$George_Bass$ Strait, was also defined as a bay by the French, and named after $Charles-Maurice de Talleyrand-PĂŠrigord$Charles_Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PĂŠrigord$ (1754-1838). 
 The presence of the name $Talleyrand$Charles_Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PĂŠrigord$ in the French nomenclature was almost obligatory in recognition of his eminent status as an experienced statesman and his negotiating skills as a diplomat.
history_fr:
$Talleyrand$Charles-Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PĂŠrigord$ fut contraint de passer plusieurs annĂŠes en exil, d'abord en Angleterre, puis aux Ătats-Unis. AprĂ¨s son rappel, il fut nommĂŠ ministre des Relations extĂŠrieures sous le Directoire en 1797. Environ deux ans plus tard, le 19 novembre 1799, il fut l'un des instigateurs du coup d'Ătat qui vit l'accession au pouvoir de $NapolĂŠon Bonaparte$NapolĂŠon_Ier$, qui nomma $Talleyrand$Charles-Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PĂŠrigord$ au poste de ministre des Affaires ĂŠtrangĂ¨res. Il devint grand chambellan en 1804, puis fut fait prince de BĂŠnĂŠvent (une petite principautĂŠ prise au pape) en 1806.
 Ce que $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ et Freycinet ne soupĂ§onnaient pas lorsqu'ils choisirent son nom pour leur nomenclature, c'est que $Talleyrand$Charles-Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PĂŠrigord$ se rĂŠvĂŠla ĂŞtre un partisan de la paix et de l'ĂŠquilibre en Europe. Il ĂŠtait donc favorable Ă  la fin des conquĂŞtes ambitieuses de NapolĂŠon et Ă  un accord avec l'Angleterre. Lors d'une rencontre entre NapolĂŠon et le tsar Alexandre $Ier$NapolĂŠon_Ier$, au congrĂ¨s d'Erfurt en septembre 1808, $Talleyrand$Charles-Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PĂŠrigord$ joua double jeu et prit secrĂ¨tement contact avec le tsar pour lui conseiller de refuser les propositions d'alliance de NapolĂŠon. De retour en France, il commenĂ§a ĂŠgalement Ă  discuter secrĂ¨tement de la succession de $NapolĂŠon Ier$NapolĂŠon_Ier$, car l'empereur n'avait pas d'hĂŠritier lĂŠgitime et $Talleyrand$Charles-Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PĂŠrigord$ voulait prĂŠparer une transition pacifique.
history:
$Talleyrand$Charles_Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PĂŠrigord$ was forced to spend some years in exĂle, first in England and then in the United States. After his recall, he was appointed Minister of External Relations during le Directoire* in 1797. Some two years later, 19 November 1799, he was one of the instigators of the coup d'ĂŠtat which saw the accession to power of $NapolĂŠon Bonaparte$Napoleon$, who appointed $Talleyrand$Charles_Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PĂŠrigord$ to the position of Minister of External Affairs. He became Grand Chamberlain in 1804, then was made Prince of Benevento (a small principality taken from the Pope) in 1806.
 What $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ and Freycinet did not suspect when they chose his name for their nomenclature is that $Talleyrand$Charles_Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PĂŠrigord$ turned out to be a partisan of peace and equilibrium in Europe. and therefore, was in favour of an end to Napoleonâs ambitious conquests and of an arrangement with England. During a meeting between NapolĂŠon and Tsar Alexander I, at the Congress of Erfurt in September 1808, $Talleyrand$Charles_Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PĂŠrigord$ played a double game and made secret contact with the Tsar to advise him to refuse NapolĂŠon's proposals for an alliance. Also, back in France, he started to secretly discuss the succession of NapolĂŠon I, as the emperor had no legitimate heirs and $Talleyrand$Charles_Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PĂŠrigord$ wanted to make preparation for a peaceful transition.

characteristic_fr:
Cap Suffren, maintenant Point Grey,  se situe Ă  lâentrĂŠe mĂŠridionale de Lorne, le long de la Great Ocean Road. 
Pierre AndrĂŠ de Suffren de Saint-Tropez, bailli de Suffren (1729-1788), fut un amiral franĂ§ais cĂŠlĂ¨bre pour ses campagnes menĂŠes contre les Britanniques dans lâocĂŠan Indien, oĂš son audace et son ĂŠnergie lui valurent de la part de ses ennemis le surnom dâÂŤ amiral Satan Âť.
characteristic:
Cap Suffren, now Point Grey lies at the southern entrance to Lorne, along the Great Ocean Road. 
Pierre AndrĂŠ de Suffren de Saint-Tropez, bailli de Suffren (1729â1788), was a French admiral renowned for his campaigns against the British in the Indian Ocean, where his audacity and energy earned him the nickname âAdmiral Satanâ among his enemies.
history_fr:
PĂŠron dĂŠcrit ce promontoire comme ÂŤ un gros cap Âť, aux formes arrondies, accompagnĂŠ de hauteurs voisines, Ă  lâouest de la baie Talleyrand. Freycinet note de mĂŞme que ÂŤ dans lâintĂŠrieur, on aperĂ§oit de hautes montagnes qui viennent se prolonger vers le rivage Ă  la hauteur du cap Suffren Âť, remarque qui renvoie vraisemblablement Ă  la chaĂŽne des Otway. Le choix de ce nom tenait peut-ĂŞtre aussi, non sans humour, Ă  une allusion Ă  la corpulence bien connue de Suffren.
Baudin avait dâabord donnĂŠ Ă  ce lieu le nom de cap des ReprĂŠsentations. Il expliquait dans son journal : ÂŤ Je nommai le cap qui me parut ĂŞtre le plus au Sud depuis le promontoire de Wilson, le cap des ReprĂŠsentations, sur les diffĂŠrends qui me furent faits par lâĂŠtat-major dans ce mĂŞme moment Âť, faisant ainsi rĂŠfĂŠrence aux dĂŠsaccords survenus au sein de lâexpĂŠdition au sujet de la reprĂŠsentation et de lâinterprĂŠtation du littoral.
history:
PĂŠron describes this headland as âun gros capââa broad, rounded capeâwith high country rising nearby, to the west of Talleyrand Bay. Freycinet likewise noted that âtowards the interior, high mountains are visible, extending towards the shoreline at the height of Cape Suffren,â probably a reference to the Otway Range. The name may also have been chosen in allusion to Suffrenâs famously corpulent figure.
Baudin had originally named this point Cap des ReprĂŠsentations. As he explained in his journal, âI named this cape, which seemed to me the southernmost since Wilsonâs Promontory, Cape of Representations, following the disputes brought before me by the staff at that very moment,â a remark referring to disagreements within the expedition over the representation and interpretation of the coastline.

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Marengo est situĂŠ sur la Great Ocean Road, au sud dâApollo Bay. Freycinet notait que la cĂ´te, depuis le cap Suffren jusquâau cap Marengo, ĂŠtait ĂŠlevĂŠe et prĂŠsentait un aspect Ă  la fois agrĂŠable et fertile.
Son nom dĂŠrive de la bataille de Marengo, livrĂŠe dans la plaine de la Bormida, en Italie, prĂ¨s dâAlexandrie dans le PiĂŠmont, le 14 juin 1800. Elle opposa lâarmĂŠe franĂ§aise de RĂŠserve, commandĂŠe par le Premier Consul Bonaparte, aux forces autrichiennes de la DeuxiĂ¨me Coalition.
characteristic:
Cape Marengo is situated on the Great Ocean Road, south of Apollo Bay. Freycinet noted that the coast from Cape Suffren to Cape Marengo was high and presented an appearance at once pleasant and fertile.
Its name derives from the Battle of Marengo, fought on the plain of the Bormida in Italy, near Alessandria in Piedmont, on 14 June 1800. The battle opposed the French Army of the Reserve, led by the First Consul Bonaparte, to the Austrian forces of the Second Coalition.
history_fr: Selon une tradition bien ĂŠtablie, aprĂ¨s la bataille de Marengo, Bonaparte, accablĂŠ par la fatigue et la faim, demanda Ă  son cuisinier, FranĂ§ois Claude Guignet, dit Dunand, de lui prĂŠparer un repas. Les cuisines de campagne ayant ĂŠtĂŠ endommagĂŠes, celui-ci dut improviser avec les ressources du moment : du poulet, de lâhuile dâolive, des tomates, de lâail, des Ĺufs, des ĂŠcrevisses et des croĂťtons. Le plat aurait tant plu Ă  Bonaparte quâil aurait ensuite demandĂŠ quâon le lui resserve sous le nom de poulet Marengo. Dans des versions ultĂŠrieures de la recette, le poulet fut parfois remplacĂŠ par du veau, jugĂŠ plus raffinĂŠ.
history: According to a well-known legend, after the Battle of Marengo, Bonaparteâfamished after the fightingâasked his cook, FranĂ§ois Claude Guignet, known as Dunand, to prepare him a meal. As the field kitchens had been damaged, the cook had to improvise with whatever could be found: chicken, olive oil, tomatoes, garlic, eggs, crayfish, and croutons. The dish was said to have pleased Bonaparte so much that he later asked for it again under the name chicken Marengo. In subsequent versions, chicken was sometimes replaced by veal, considered a more refined meat.

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Desaix (aujourdâhui Cape Otway) marque lâentrĂŠe occidentale du dĂŠtroit de Bass. Il se trouve dĂŠsormais dans le Great Otway National Park, espace qui abrite une grande diversitĂŠ dâhabitats et de nombreuses espĂ¨ces endĂŠmiques ; le cap Marengo se situe Ă  la limite de ce mĂŞme secteur littoral.
Le cap reĂ§ut le nom du gĂŠnĂŠral Louis Charles Antoine Desaix (1768â1800). Au moment oĂš les FranĂ§ais ĂŠtaient sur le point dâĂŞtre vaincus Ă  Marengo (voir cap Marengo), lâarrivĂŠe de Desaix avec des renforts et une contre-attaque dĂŠcisive contribua Ă  renverser le cours de la bataille en faveur de la France. Son intervention assura lâune des victoires les plus cĂŠlĂŠbrĂŠes du Consulat, bien quâil y trouvĂ˘t lui-mĂŞme la mort.
characteristic:
Cape Desaix (now Cape Otway) marks the western entrance to Bass Strait. It now lies within the Great Otway National Park, an area supporting a wide range of habitats and numerous endemic species; Cape Marengo stands at the edge of the same coastal sector.
The cape was named after General Louis Charles Antoine Desaix (1768â1800). At the moment when the French were on the verge of defeat at Marengo (see Cape Marengo), Desaixâs arrival with reinforcements and a decisive counter-attack helped reverse the course of the battle in favour of France. His intervention secured one of the most celebrated victories of the Consulate, though he himself was killed in action.
history_fr:
Freycinet note: Au cap Desaix, les terres changent toutâĂ˘âcoup dâaspect et de direction; elles prennent leur cours Ă  lâO. N. O., et souvent sâabaissent Ă  tel point, que la mer y forme des marais ou des lagons ĂŠtendus (carte 72.â 13), quâil nous a ĂŠtĂŠ facile dâapercevoir du haut des barres de perroquet. Le caractĂ¨re dâune stĂŠrilitĂŠ profonde est empreint sur toute cette cĂ´te, dâailleurs partout ĂŠcore et taillĂŠe Ă  pic. Quelques grandes taches blanches que lâon distingue en diffĂŠrens endroits, pourroient servir de points de reconnoissance. De hautes terres se voient dans lâintĂŠrieur."
La stĂŠrilitĂŠ relevĂŠe par Freycinet reflĂŠtait probablement la saison Ă  laquelle le levĂŠ fut effectuĂŠ â sous voile, Ă  la fin du mois de mars 1802 â, lorsque la vĂŠgĂŠtation avait ĂŠtĂŠ dessĂŠchĂŠe par lâabsence de pluie Ă  la suite de la sĂŠcheresse estivale.
history: Freycinet notes: âAt Cape Desaix, the land suddenly changes in aspect and direction; it takes a WNW course, and often becomes so low that the sea forms marshes or extensive lagoons, which we could easily see from the tops of the foremast yards. A deep impression of sterility is stamped upon the whole of this coast, which is moreover steep and rugged throughout. Several large white patches visible in different places could serve as landmarks. High land can be seen inland.â The barrenness noted by Freycinet probably reflected the season of the survey â conducted under sail at the end of March 1802 â when the vegetation had been dried by the lack of rain following the summer drought.

origin_fr: Louis Charles Antoine Desaix de Veygoux, nĂŠ leÂ 17Â aoĂťtÂ 1768Â au chĂ˘teau d'Ayat, Ă  Saint-Hilaire d'Ayat, (actuellementÂ Ayat-sur-Sioule), mort leÂ 14Â juinÂ 1800Â Ă Â MarengoÂ (PiĂŠmont), est un gĂŠnĂŠral franĂ§ais qui s'est illustrĂŠ lors desÂ guerres rĂŠvolutionnairesÂ et sous les ordres deÂ Bonaparte, notamment enÂ ĂgypteÂ et enÂ Italie. Il a combattu Ă  la bataille des Pyramides (1798), a conquis la Haute-Ăgypte (1798-99) et a ĂŠtĂŠ tuĂŠ Ă  la bataille de Marengo (1800). Son corps fut transportĂŠ au couvent du Grand-Saint-Bernard oĂš un monument fut ĂŠrigĂŠ en son honneur.

characteristic_fr:
Le cap $Volney$Volney$, situĂŠ Ă  une vingtaine de kilomĂ¨tres Ă  lâouest du cap Otway, forme un promontoire remarquable sur la cĂ´te victorienne. Ses falaises abruptes sâĂŠlĂ¨vent jusquâĂ  190 m. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ note : ÂŤ Le cap $Volney$Volney$, Ă  la hauteur duquel nous ĂŠtions Ă  midi, est remarquable par une chaĂŽne de roches qui se porte trĂ¨s-avant au large. Âť (tome 1, p. 317). Il fait probablement rĂŠfĂŠrence aux ĂŠtroites saillies de falaises qui avancent vers la mer en formant des promontoires et se terminent par des blocs isolĂŠs.
 $Constantin-FranĂ§ois Chasseb$Volney$Ĺuf de $La Giraudais$Volney$ (1757-1820) se fit appeler $Volney$Volney$, en rĂŠfĂŠrence Ă  VOLtaire-FerNEY, pour revendiquer la philosophie de Voltaire, Ferney ĂŠtant le chĂ˘teau oĂš Voltaire rĂŠdigea ses Ĺuvres. Historien, linguiste, spĂŠcialiste des langues orientales et membre de lâInstitut, il est gĂŠnĂŠralement considĂŠrĂŠ comme lâun des principaux pionniers de lâethnologie, de lâanthropologie et de la sociologie du XXe siĂ¨cle.
characteristic: Cape $Volney$Constantin_FranĂ§ois_de_ChassebĹuf,_comte_de_Volney$, located some 20 km to the west of Cape Otway, forms a prominent headland on the Victorian coast. Its steep cliffs rise to a height of 190m. Peron notes: âCape $Volney$Constantin_FranĂ§ois_de_ChassebĹuf,_comte_de_Volney$, which we were level with at midday, is remarkable for a chain of rocks that extends far out to seaâ (tome 1, p.317). He probably refers to the narrow cliffed spurs projecting seaward as headlands and ending as isolated blocks. $Constantin-FranĂ§ois Chasseb$Constantin_FranĂ§ois_de_ChassebĹuf,_comte_de_Volney$Ĺuf de $La Giraudais$Constantin_FranĂ§ois_de_ChassebĹuf,_comte_de_Volney$ (1757 -1820) named himself $Volney$Constantin_FranĂ§ois_de_ChassebĹuf,_comte_de_Volney$ for VOLtaire-FerNEY, to claim Voltaire*'s philosophy, Ferney being Voltaireâs castle where he wrote his works. He was an historian, a linguist, an expert in Oriental languages, and a member of the Institut*. He is generally considered as one of the foremost pioneers of 20th century ethnology, anthropology and sociology.
history_fr: $Volney$Volney$ devint membre de la SociĂŠtĂŠ des IdĂŠologues, dont lâidĂŠal politique aspirait Ă  des rĂŠformes laĂŻques et anti-autoritaristes, guidĂŠes par la raison et la science. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$, pour sa part, en devint membre correspondant. Lorsque la monarchie rĂŠapparut sous la forme de $NapolĂŠon Bonaparte$NapolĂŠon_Ier$, cette vision rĂŠpublicaine de la France se heurta Ă  lâexercice dâun pouvoir concentrĂŠ entre les mains dâun seul homme. NapolĂŠon qualifia les idĂŠologues de ÂŤ rĂŞveurs Âť, de ÂŤ beaux parleurs Âť, ÂŤ bons Ă  jeter Ă  lâeau Âť, et la sociĂŠtĂŠ fut dissoute. NĂŠanmoins, cela ne dissuada pas $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ dâinclure le nom de $Volney$Volney$ dans la nomenclature, probablement parce que celui-ci poursuivait alors dâimportantes recherches sur les langues, affirmant ainsi la valeur intellectuelle et universelle de son hĂŠritage.
history: $Volney$Constantin_FranĂ§ois_de_ChassebĹuf,_comte_de_Volney$ became a member of the SociĂŠtĂŠ des IdĂŠologues, whose political ideal aspired to secular and anti-authoritarian reforms, guided by reason and science. Peron, for his part, became a corresponding member. When the monarchy reappeared in the form of Napoleon Bonaparte, this republican vision of France clashed with the exercise of power concentrated in the hands of a single man. Napoleon described the ideologues as âdreamersâ, âsmooth talkersâ and âgood for nothingâ, and the society was dissolved. Nevertheless, this did not deter Peron from including $Volney$Constantin_FranĂ§ois_de_ChassebĹuf,_comte_de_Volney$'s name in the nomenclature, probably because he was then pursuing important research on languages, thus affirming the intellectual and universal value of his legacy.
origin_fr: Constantin-FranĂ§ois ChassebĹuf de La Giraudais, comte Volney, ditÂ Volney, nĂŠ leÂ 3Â fĂŠvrierÂ 17571Â Ă Â CraonÂ enÂ AnjouÂ et mort le25Â avrilÂ 1820Â Ă Â Paris, est unÂ philosopheÂ etÂ orientalisteÂ franĂ§ais.Â Il est considĂŠrĂŠ comme le prĂŠcurseur des ethnologues, anthropologues et sociologues duÂ xxeÂ siĂ¨cle

characteristic_fr:
Baudin avait initialement nommĂŠ cette petite avancĂŠe le long de la cĂ´te ouest du Victoria Cap des Falaises, oĂš, selon ses mots, ÂŤToute la cĂ´te que nous prolongeĂ˘mes mâa paru inabordable et ne pas mĂŞme offrir dâabri pour une embarcation" avec la mer qui "brise tout le long du rivage", Journal de mer de Baudin, Neuf au Dix Germinal an 10 (30 - 31 mars 1802). Il dĂŠcrivait une partie des falaises calcaires que lâĂŠrosion cĂ´tiĂ¨re a faĂ§onnĂŠes en un groupe de pinacles dĂŠsormais connu sous le nom des Douze ApĂ´tres (bien qu'il ne reste que huit pinacles Ă  ce jour), situĂŠs un peu plus Ă  lâouest.
 $Jean-Charles de Folard$Jean-Charles_de_Folard$, connu sous le nom de Chevalier $de Folard$Jean-Charles_de_Folard$ (1669-1752), auquel ce site fut plus tard dĂŠdiĂŠ, ĂŠtait un stratĂ¨ge militaire et ingĂŠnieur. Il commenta, annota et traduisit Polybe du grec en franĂ§ais, et les ĂŠcrits faisaient autoritĂŠ dans lâĂŠtude de lâart de la guerre. Folard advocated infantry attacks in columns rather than in lines, and his theory was widely discussed in military circles. NapolĂŠon, read his books methodically, especially âNouvelles dĂŠcouvertes sur la guerreâ (new innovations in relation to warfare) and âtraitĂŠ de la colonneâ (treatise on the subject of military column).
characteristic:
Baudin had originally named this small projection along the western Victorian coast Cap des falaises (Cape of Cliffs) as "all the coast that we saw looked to me to be unapproachable and not even to offer shelter for a vessel..." (Baudin's journal, 30 - 31 March 1802). He was describing part of the limestone cliffs from which coastal erosion has fashioned the group of pinnacles now known as the Twelve Apostles (although only eight pinnacles remain today), a short distance further to the west.
 $Jean-Charles de Folard$Chevalier_de_Folard$, known as the Chevalier $de Folard$Chevalier_de_Folard$, (1669-1752), after whom the landmark was later named, was a war strategist and engineer who commented, annotated, and translated Polybius from Greek into French, and whose works included major references used to master the art of war. Folard advocated infantry attacks in columns rather than in lines, and his theory was widely discussed in military circles. NapolĂŠon, read his books methodically, especially âNouvelles dĂŠcouvertes sur la guerreâ (new innovations in relation to warfare) and âtraitĂŠ de la colonneâ (treatise on the subject of military column).
history_fr:
$PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ ĂŠtend la notion dâinhospitalitĂŠ de la cĂ´te jusquâĂ  y inclure la vie animale. Il ĂŠcrit : ÂŤ Ă lâexception de quelques poissons volants, toutes les crĂŠatures marines semblaient avoir fui cette cĂ´te, qui ne leur offrait aucun abri et que des vagues agitĂŠes frappaient sans relĂ˘che. Âť NĂŠanmoins, il dĂŠcouvre ÂŤ quelques espĂ¨ces de Fucus singuliĂ¨res et nouvelles" dont "L'une d'elles, que je dĂŠcrivis sous le nom de Fucus phyllophorus, me parut sur-tout remarquable par la disposition de ses feuillesÂť. Il observe que ÂŤ dans certains cas, un troisiĂ¨me ordre de feuilles peut mĂŞme ĂŞtre vu ĂŠmerger de ces feuilles secondaires [des feuilles du fucus], sans qu'il m'ait paru possible de fixer le terme prĂŠcis de cette sorte de gĂŠnĂŠration, d'autant plus singuliĂ¨re, que les vĂŠritables organes de la reproduction de la plante, les globules, communs Ă  la plupart des Fucus, sont trĂ¨s -dĂŠveloppĂŠs dans cette espĂ¨ce... Âť
Ici, $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ soulĂ¨ve le problĂ¨me auquel sont confrontĂŠs les naturalistes dans leur travail de dĂŠcouverte : celui de ÂŤ fixer le terme prĂŠcis Âť pour dĂŠcrire et classer lâinconnu, ce qui nâa jamais encore ĂŠtĂŠ dĂŠcrit. Aujourdâhui, cependant, nous savons que cette cĂ´te abrite une grande diversitĂŠ dâalgues, dont Ecklonia radiata est lâespĂ¨ce dominante.
history:
$PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ extends the notion of the coastâs inhospitality to include its animal life. He writes, âexcept for some flying fish, all marine creature appeared to have fled this coast, which had no shelter for them and was constantly pounded by rough waves". Nevertheless, he discovers âa few new and curious kinds of fucus [seaweeds]â whose nature, unfamiliar to Europeans, makes them difficult to classify. He observes that âin some instances, a third order of leaves can even be seen emerging from these secondary leaves [the leaves of the fucus], although I have not been able to determine the precise term for this type of generation, which is all the more unusual given that the plant's true reproductive organs, the globules, common to most Fucus species, are highly developed in this species... â
 Here, $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ raises the problem faced by naturalists in their work of discovery: that of âdetermining the precise term" to describe and classify the unknown, that which has never been described before. Today, however, we know that this coast is home to a wide variety of algae, with Ecklonia radiata being the dominant species.

Ăle Latreille (aujourdâhui Mutton Bird Island) est constituĂŠe de dĂŠpĂ´ts cĂ´tiers lithifiĂŠs, notamment de sables calcaires cimentĂŠs en calcarenite (calcaire dâorigine littorale), plutĂ´t que dâun massif calcaire homogĂ¨ne. PĂŠron la dĂŠcrivit comme ÂŤ une portion de terre qui semblait dĂŠtachĂŠe de la grande terre Âť et souligna le caractĂ¨re ÂŤ stĂŠrile Âť de cette portion du littoral.

characteristic:
Ăle Latreille (now Mutton Bird Island) is composed of lithified coastal sediments, including calcareous sands that have been cemented into calcarenite (coastal limestone), rather than forming part of a massive limestone bedrock. PĂŠron described it as âa portion of land that seemed detached from the mainlandâ and emphasized the âsterileâ character of this stretch of coast.
Pierre AndrĂŠ Latreille (1762â1833) was a naturalist and one of the most eminent entomologists of his time.
history_fr:
Sur la carte dressĂŠe sous son autoritĂŠ Ă  bord du *GĂŠographe*, dĂŠjĂ  mise au net et prĂŞte Ă  ĂŞtre validĂŠe ou reproduite, Nicolas Baudin avait dâabord donnĂŠ au cap Latreille le nom de ÂŤ pointe Sans-Espoir Âť. Il notait en effet : ÂŤ Le douze [germinal an X, 2 avril 1802], au matin, nous nous trouvĂ˘mes dans une position fort avantageuse pour commencer, dĂ¨s le lever du soleil, nos relĂ¨vements. La jolie brise quâil faisait alors, du Nord-Est au Nord-Nord-Est, nous donnait mĂŞme lâespoir de les pousser assez avant dans la journĂŠe. Mais nous fĂťmes trompĂŠs dans notre attente par le calme et de petites brises du Nord au Nord-Ouest qui ne nous permirent pas de nous avancer bien avant et qui mĂŞme nous ĂŠloignaient de la terre, de laquelle nous nâĂŠtions, tout au plus, quâĂ  une lieue lorsquâelles survinrent. Âť
Les toponymes de cap Latreille et dâĂŽlots Latreille, ajoutĂŠs au crayon sur les documents cartographiques ĂŠlaborĂŠs Ă  bord du *GĂŠographe* â notamment le profil cĂ´tier de 1802 et la minute cartographique mise au net (voir ces documents dans la fiche ÂŤ En savoir plus Âť) â ne furent toutefois pas retenus par Louis de Freycinet dans les cartes de lâatlas officiel de 1815. Seule fut conservĂŠe lâĂle Latreille, que PĂŠron et Freycinet nommĂ¨rent ÂŤ du nom du naturaliste aussi savant que modeste de ce nom Âť (*Voyage*, t. I, p. 318). Aujourdâhui, Mutton Bird Island est ĂŠgalement connue localement sous le nom de âSowâ (âla truieâ), tandis que les petits rochers voisins â qui correspondent aux ĂŽlots Latreille du profil de 1802 â sont appelĂŠs ses âPigletsâ (âporceletsâ).
history:
On the chart prepared under his authority aboard the *GĂŠographe*, already drawn up fair and ready to be validated or reproduced, Nicolas Baudin had initially given Cape Latreille the name âPointe Sans-Espoirâ (âPoint Without Hopeâ). He recorded: âOn the twelfth [Germinal, Year X, 2 April 1802], in the morning, we found ourselves in a most favourable position to begin our bearings at sunrise. The pleasant breeze then blowing from north-east to north-north-east even gave us hope of carrying them a fair way through the day. But we were disappointed in this expectation by a calm and light breezes from north to north-west, which prevented us from advancing very far and even drove us away from the land, from which we were, at most, only a league distant when they arose.â
The place names Cape Latreille and Latreille Islets, added in pencil to the cartographic documents prepared aboard the *GĂŠographe*ânotably the 1802 coastal profile and the fair copy of the chart (see these documents in the âLearn moreâ section)âwere not retained by Louis de Freycinet in the maps of the official 1815 atlas. Only Ăle Latreille was preserved, which PĂŠron and Freycinet named âafter the naturalist of that name, as learned as he was modestâ (*Voyage*, vol. I, p. 318). Today, Mutton Bird Island is also locally known as the âSow,â while the neighbouring small rocksâcorresponding to the Latreille Islets in the 1802 profileâare called her âPiglets.â

characteristic_fr:
Câest lâun des rares noms de la nomenclature du voyage ayant une signification gĂŠographique utile Ă  la navigation : il dĂŠcrit une formation terrestre ĂŠlevĂŠe, facilement repĂŠrable depuis la mer. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ note : ÂŤ En continuant notre route le long de la cĂ´te, nous arrivĂ˘mes par le travers d'un piton de forme conique : il reĂ§ut la dĂŠnomination de Piton de Reconnoissance, et le cap le plus voisin celle de Cap du Mont-Tabor."
 Le pic conique observĂŠ Ă  la fois par Baudin et $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ fait partie dâun volcan aujourdâhui ĂŠteint, qui entra en ĂŠruption Ă  une ĂŠpoque gĂŠologique relativement rĂŠcente, probablement il y a moins de deux millions dâannĂŠes. characteristic:
This is one of the few names from the voyage's nomenclature with a geographical meaning useful for navigation: It describes a landform with an elevation that is easy to notice from the sea. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ notes âwe came abeam of a conical peak: it was named Piton de la Reconnaissance / Reconnaissance Peak, and the nearest headland Cap du Mont Taborâ. 
 The conical peak seen by both Baudin and $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ is part of an inactive volcano that erupted in relatively recent geological times, probably less than 2 million years ago. The cone emerges from a large crater lake formed during an earlier eruption.
history_fr: Cette hauteur, dĂŠsignĂŠe sur les cartes dressĂŠes Ă  bord du GĂŠographe comme la ÂŤ Montagne de la Reconnaissance Âť et dĂŠcrite dans le journal de Baudin comme un ÂŤ piton ĂŠlevĂŠ restant au nord dans lâintĂŠrieur des terres Âť, constituait un point de repĂ¨re aisĂŠment identifiable, susceptible dâaider les navigateurs Ă  situer leur position le long dâun littoral dĂŠpourvu de reliefs topographiques marquants, dont la linĂŠaritĂŠ et le caractĂ¨re indiffĂŠrenciĂŠ ÂŤ dĂŠsolait Âť parce quâelle compliquait les levĂŠs.
history: This elevation, identified on the charts drawn aboard the GĂŠographe as the âMount of Recognitionâ and described in Baudinâs journal as a âhigh peak remaining to the north inland,â served as an easily recognizable landmark that could help navigators determine their position along a coastline lacking distinctive topographical features, whose linearity and undifferentiated character were âdishearteningâ because they made surveying difficult.

characteristic_fr:
Promontoire proĂŠminent situĂŠ sur la cĂ´te sud-ouest de l'Ătat de Victoria, face Ă  l'ocĂŠan Austral. La ville de Warrnambool se trouve Ă  une courte distance Ă  l'intĂŠrieur des terres.
 Au cours de la campagne d'Ăgypte (voir cette entrĂŠe dans le glossaire) et de la bataille du mont Thabor (Tabor) le 16 avril 1799 (en GalilĂŠe, aujourd'hui Afula, en IsraĂŤl), les FranĂ§ais, sous le commandement de $Bonaparte$NapolĂŠon_Ier$, combattirent les Turcs du pacha de Damas qui tentaient de secourir la population de Saint-Jean-d'Acre (aujourd'hui en IsraĂŤl) assiĂŠgĂŠe par les FranĂ§ais. Physiquement, le mont Thabor ressemble Ă  une colline isolĂŠe ou Ă  une petite montagne hĂŠmisphĂŠrique. $Selon PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$, cette forme se manifeste aussi dans le promontoire proĂŠminent oĂš se situe Pickering Point/Cap du Mont Thabor, lui donnant lâoccasion de rappeler la campagne de $Bonaparte$NapolĂŠon_Ier$ en Ăgypte, qui, si elle ne fut pas victorieuse sur le plan militaire, reprĂŠsenta certainement une entreprise marquante sur le plan culturel pour la France.
characteristic:
This 60 m high headland marks a change in the orientation of the coastline from east-west to southwest-northeast. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ compares it to âthe heel of the bootâ of the Peninsula. Today, it is part of the Newland Head Conservation Park.
$Jean-Pierre Bachasson$Jean-Pierre,_Count_of_Montalivet$, comte de Montalivet (1766-1823) enlisted in the Army of Italy during the French Revolution when his life came under treat during the Terror. After $Bonaparte$Napoleon$âs assumption to power, he was prĂŠfet (prefect) of the department of the Manche (1801) and later Seine-et-Oise (1803) that consisted then of Paris and Ile de France. During the First Empire, Napoleon, aware of Montalivet's skills, appointed him member of the French council of State, and director of the LĂŠgion dâHonneur (see this entry in the glossary).
history_fr: Si la $bataille du Mont Tabor$Bataille_du_Mont-Thabor$ fut une victoire, le siĂ¨ge d'Acre en 1799 fut la premiĂ¨re dĂŠfaite stratĂŠgique de $Bonaparte$NapolĂŠon_ier$, aggravĂŠe par le siĂ¨ge de Jaffa (voir l'entrĂŠe Cap de Jaffa). La peste se dĂŠclara dans le camp, causant de nombreux morts.
history: if the $battle of Mont Tabor$Battle_of_Mount_Tabor_(1799)$ was a victory, the Siege of Acre of 1799 was the first strategic defeat of $Bonaparte$Napoleon$, which was compounded when, after the Siege of Jaffa (see the entry Cap de Jaffa). The plague broke out in the camp, causing many deaths.

characteristic_fr:
Le cap RĂŠaumur marque l'extrĂŠmitĂŠ orientale de la baie de Portland, sur la cĂ´te ouest de l'Ătat de Victoria. Des pointes basaltiques basses et des rĂŠcifs offshore dominent le littoral. Le nom alternatif parfois utilisĂŠ pour dĂŠsigner le cap, Boulder Point [rocher], tĂŠmoigne de l'aspect physique accidentĂŠ du promontoire. Le toponyme RĂŠaumur a ĂŠtĂŠ conservĂŠ sur les cartes actuelles, avec toutefois une orthographe un peu alteree.
 $RenĂŠ Antoine Ferchault$RenĂŠ-Antoine_Ferchault_de_RĂŠaumur$ de RĂŠaumur (1683-1757) ĂŠtait un savant franĂ§ais du XVIIIe siĂ¨cle qui a introduit l'ĂŠchelle de tempĂŠrature RĂŠaumur et qui a contribuĂŠ Ă  de nombreux domaines scientifiques, en particulier ceux qui intĂŠressaient $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$, tels que l'ĂŠtude des insectes, des mollusques, des crustacĂŠs et des coraux. Un exemplaire de son ouvrage sur l'histoire des insectes se trouvait Ă  bord du navire de Baudin.
characteristic:
Cape Reaumur marks the eastern end of Portland Bay on the western Victorian coast. Low basalt points and offshore reefs dominate the shore. The occasionally used alternative name for the cape, that of Boulder Point, attests to the rugged physical appearance of the headland. The toponym RĂŠaumur has been retained on present-day maps, albeit with some spelling changes.
 $RenĂŠ Antoine Ferchault$RenĂŠ_Antoine_Ferchault_de_RĂŠaumur$ de RĂŠaumur (1683 -1757) was an eighteenths century French savant (scientist) who introduced the Reaumur temperature scale and contributed to many different scientific fields, especially to those in which $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ was interested, such as the study of insects, molluscs, shellfish and corals. A copy of his work on the history of insects was in Baudinâs ship.
history_fr:
RĂŠaumur est aujourdâhui surtout connu pour avoir crĂŠĂŠ lâĂŠchelle de tempĂŠrature qui porte son nom, oĂš zĂŠro marque le point de congĂŠlation de lâeau et quatre-vingts degrĂŠs son point dâĂŠbullition. Les thermomĂ¨tres utilisĂŠs par lâexpĂŠdition â appelĂŠs thermomĂ¨tres de RĂŠaumur â ĂŠtaient calibrĂŠs selon ce systĂ¨me novateur, tĂŠmoignant de son influence durable sur la science de la mesure.
 
 Le 1áľĘł avril 1802, $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$, utilisant lâĂŠchelle de RĂŠaumur, releva la tempĂŠrature des eaux cĂ´tiĂ¨res entre lâĂle $Fourcroy$Antoine-FranĂ§ois_Fourcroy$*, le Cap RĂŠaumur et la Baie Tourville*, puis compara ses rĂŠsultats Ă  la tempĂŠrature moyenne observĂŠe en France au mois dâavril. Il ĂŠcrit : ÂŤ Le baromĂ¨tre, pendant ce jour, se soutint de. Ă  28Ë6ââ, et le thermomĂ¨tre me donna pour terme moyen 15Ër de chaleur [18,75 Â°C], tempĂŠrature analogue Ă  celle du printemps dans notre patrie. Âť Ces observations avaient pour but de montrer que les tempĂŠratures cĂ´tiĂ¨res automnales (en avril) de cette rĂŠgion dâAustralie, equivalentes Ă  celles du printemps franĂ§ais, ĂŠtaient de faite plus ĂŠlevĂŠes que celles de lâautomne en France, en raison de la latitude plus basse.
history: RĂŠaumur is best remembered today for creating the RĂŠaumur temperature scale, where zero marks the freezing point of water and eighty degrees its boiling point. The expeditionâs thermometersâknown as thermomĂ¨tres de RĂŠaumurâwere calibrated according to this innovative system, reflecting his enduring influence on the science of measurement. On April 1, 1802, $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$, using the Reaumur scale, noted the temperature off the coasts between Ăle $Fourcroy$Antoine-FranĂ§ois_de_Fourcroy$*, Cap RĂŠaumur and Baie Tourville*, and compared the results with the average temperature observed in April in France. He writes: âThroughout this day the barometer stood at 28Ë6ââ, and the thermometer readings gave me an average of 15Ër [18.75Â°C], a temperature like that of spring in our countryâ. Beyond, this indicated that autumnal (in April) coastal temperatures in this part of Australia were higher than those experienced in Autumn in France, because of the lower latitude.

characteristic_fr:
Câest une petite ĂŽle plate (2 km de long et 1 km de large) situĂŠe Ă  courte distance au large de la cĂ´te ouest du Victoria. Elle sâest formĂŠe Ă  la suite dâĂŠruptions volcaniques sous-marines survenues il y a environ 6 Ă  8 millions dâannĂŠes. Le plateau de surface est bordĂŠ de falaises sur tous les cĂ´tĂŠs. Des plates-formes rocheuses et des rĂŠcifs empĂŞchent les dĂŠbarquements de navires, Ă  lâexception dâune crique abritĂŠe sur le cĂ´tĂŠ nord. Plusieurs espĂ¨ces dâoiseaux marins ainsi que lâotarie Ă  fourrure australienne utilisent lâĂŽle pour la reproduction.
$Antoine-FranĂ§ois Fourcroy$Antoine-FranĂ§ois_Fourcroy$ (1755-1809) prĂŠsenta une thĂ¨se sur lâusage de la quinine dans le traitement des fiĂ¨vres intermittentes pour lâobtention de son diplĂ´me de docteur en mĂŠdecine, mais il ne pratiqua pas la mĂŠdecine. Sa principale contribution scientifique rĂŠside dans sa collaboration avec le chimiste $Antoine Lavoisier$Antoine_Lavoisier$, en 1782, sur une nouvelle nomenclature chimique normalisĂŠe. Il mena ĂŠgalement dâimportantes recherches sur lâanalyse des minĂŠraux et dĂŠmontra que lâaragonite et la calcite ĂŠtaient deux formes de carbonate de calcium. Il fut Ă  lâorigine de la mĂŠthode dâanalyse proximale [mĂŠthode chimique utilisĂŠe pour dĂŠterminer la composition gĂŠnĂŠrale des matiĂ¨res organiques] et rĂŠdigea ou co-rĂŠdigea de nombreuses publications.
characteristic: This is a small flat island (2 km long and 1 km wide) located a short distance off the western Victorian coast. It was formed by sub-marine volcanic eruptions between about 6 and 8 million years ago and is Australiaâs only offshore volcano. The plateau surface is bounded by cliffs on all sides. Shore platforms and reefs prevent ship landings apart from a sheltered cove on the northern side. Several seabird species and Australian fur seal use the island for breeding. $Antoine-FranĂ§ois Fourcroy$Antoine-FranĂ§ois_de_Fourcroy$ (1755-1809) submitted a thesis on the use of quinine for the treatment of intermittent fevers for his diploma of Doctor of Medicine but did not practice medicine. His best claim to scientific achievement is his collaborative work with the acclaimed chemist $Antoine Lavoisier$Antoine_Lavoisier$*, in 1782, on a new standardized chemical nomenclature. He also carried out extensive research on the analysis of minerals and demonstrated that aragonite and calcite were two forms of calcium carbonate. He initiated the method of proximate analysis [to determine the general composition of organic substances]. He was the author or co-author of many publications.
history_fr: Peron rapporte: "A huit heures du matin, le 1 .er avril [1802], nous dĂŠcouvrĂŽmes une petite ĂŽle, que nous nommĂ˘mes Ile $Fourcroy$Antoine-FranĂ§ois_Fourcroy$ : d'une forme presque quadrangulaire, lĂŠgĂ¨rement ĂŠchancrĂŠe sur ses bords, elle est basse, uniformĂŠment aplatie, d'une couleur triste et grisĂ˘tre, stĂŠrile comme le rivage du continent, taillĂŠe Ă  pic comme lui ..."
history: Peron reports: âAt eight oâclock in the morning on April 1st [1802], we sighted a small island, which we named $Fourcroy$Antoine-FranĂ§ois_de_Fourcroy$ Island: almost quadrangular in shape, slightly notched along its edges, it is low, uniformly flat, of a dull grayish color, barren like the mainland shore, and rising steeply like it âŚâ
origin_fr: Antoine FranĂ§ois FourcroyÂ ouÂ de Fourcroy, nĂŠ leÂ 15Â juinÂ 1755Â Ă Â ParisÂ et mort leÂ 16Â dĂŠcembreÂ 1809Â Ă Â Paris, est unchimisteÂ franĂ§aisÂ et un dĂŠputĂŠ Ă  laÂ Convention nationale.

characteristic_fr:
La baie de Portland est situĂŠe sur une pĂŠninsule basaltique dans lâouest du Victoria, formĂŠe par des roches volcaniques et recouverte plus tard par des dunes de sable. La ville de Portland â la plus ancienne colonie europĂŠenne du Victoria â se trouve sur ses rives, dans les terres traditionnelles du peuple Gunditjmara.
La baie a ĂŠtĂŠ nommĂŠe par les franĂ§ais en lâhonneur dâ$Anne-Hilarion de Costentin$Anne_Hilarion_de_Costentin_de_Tourville$, comte de Tourville (1642â1701), officier de la marine franĂ§aise au XVIIáľ siĂ¨cle, devenu marĂŠchal de France et ayant jouĂŠ un rĂ´le clĂŠ dans le renouveau de la marine franĂ§aise, tant par la construction navale que par la formation des officiers.
characteristic:
Portland Bay is located on a basalt peninsula in western Victoria, formed by volcanic rock and later overlain by sand dunes. The city of Portlandâthe oldest European settlement in Victoriaâis situated on its shores, within the traditional lands of the Gunditjmara people.
The bay was named by the French in honor of $Anne-Hilarion de Costentin$Anne_Hilarion_de_Tourville$, Comte de Tourville (1642â1701), a seventeenth-century French naval officer who became Marshal of France and played a key role in renewing the French Navy through shipbuilding and officer training..
history_fr: $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ la prĂŠsente comme ÂŤ une trĂ¨s grande baie Âť. Baudin lâavait initialement nommĂŠe Baie de Cancale, dâaprĂ¨s le nom dâun petit port du nord de la Bretagne, situĂŠ Ă  lâouest de la trĂ¨s grande baie du Mont-Saint-Michel.
history: $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ described it as âa very large bay.â Baudin had initially named it Baie de Cancale, after a small port in northern Brittany, located to the west of the very large bay of Mont-Saint-Michel.
origin_fr: Anne Hilarion de Costentin, comte de Tourville (1642-1701), ĂŠtait un commandant naval franĂ§ais et l'un des plus grands techniciens navals de son temps. Il fut nommĂŠ vice-amiral du Levant en 1689. Pendant la guerre de la Ligue d'Augsbourg, il se distingua Ă  nouveau au cap BĂŠveziers (1690), Ă  la bataille de la Hougue (1692) et l'annĂŠe suivante lors de la prise du convoi de Smyrne. Fait marĂŠchal de France, il se retira Ă  la fin de la guerre et mourut Ă  Paris en 1701.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂle du Dragon fait partie des Lawrence Rocks, deux ĂŽlots rocheux formĂŠs Ă  partir des vestiges dâun volcan ĂŠteint. Ils furent ÂŤ dĂŠcouverts Âť en 1800 par $James Grant$James_Grant$, qui les nomma en lâhonneur du capitaine Effingham Lawrence, un marchand anglais.
Câest lâun des rares noms dâorigine approuvĂŠs par Baudin qui ait ĂŠtĂŠ aussi conservĂŠ dans la nomenclature de $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ et Freycinet. Baudin ĂŠcrivit dans son journal : ÂŤ Cette ĂŽle fut nommĂŠe lâĂŽle du Dragon, par la parfaite ressemblance Ă  lâEst comme Ă  lâOuest quâelle a avec lâanimal fabuleux auquel on a donnĂŠ ce nom Âť.
Aujourdâhui, ces rochers sont classĂŠs en rĂŠserve naturelle et abritent une importante colonie de fous de Bassan. BirdLife International les a reconnus comme une *zone importante pour la conservation des oiseaux* (ZICO), car ils accueillent plus de 10 % de la population mondiale de fous de Bassan. Ces oiseaux, par milliers, ainsi que dâautres espĂ¨ces, y viennent chaque annĂŠe pour nicher et se reproduire.
characteristic:
The Ăle du Dragon is part of the Lawrence Rocks, two rocky islands formed from the remnants of an extinct volcano. They were âfirstâ sighted in 1800 by $James Grant$James_Grant_(navigator)$ who named them for Captain Effingham Lawrence, an English merchant.
This is one of the few original names sanctioned by Baudin which has been kept in $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ and Freycinet nomenclature. Baudin wrote in his journal: âWe named this island Ăle du Dragon on account of its perfect resemblance, from both East and West, to the fabulous beast of that nameâ.
history_fr: $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ dĂŠcrit la couleur ÂŤ noirĂ˘tre Âť de lâile et note : ÂŤ Nous la nommĂ˘mes Ile du Dragon, Ă  cause de sa forme bizarre ; cette ĂŽle, en effet, vers l'une de ses pointes, se dessine comme le museau entrouvert d'un ĂŠnorme reptile. Les flots qui la baignent, les ĂŽlots qui s'y rattachent, ĂŠtoient alors couverts d'innombrables lĂŠgions d'oiseaux pĂŠlagiens, trop jeunes encore pour pouvoir s'envoler âŚ leur nombre prodigieux, leurs cris assourdissans et confus, la tendre sollicitude des pĂ¨res et mĂ¨res qui voltigeoient par milliers au-dessus de leurs petits âŚ tout cet ensemble avoit quelque chose d'intĂŠressant et de pittoresque.Âť
history: $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ describes its âblackishâ color, and its form analogous to the body of a dragon, with âone of the points on this island stands out like a the grapping muzzle of an enormous reptileâ. He also notes that the islets attached to Dragon Island were 'covered with innumerable legions of pelagic birds, still too young to be able to fly away' and that '... the tender solicitude of the thousands of fathers and mothers who fluttered around the little ones, screaming as the young ones did with fright, the strange shape of the islets... the whole thing was something interesting and picturesque'.

characteristic_fr:
Le cap, qui fait partie dâune pĂŠninsule volcanique (avec les baies Tourville et $Descartes$RenĂŠ_Descartes$, ainsi que le cap $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$), se situe Ă  lâintĂŠrieur des limites du Cape Nelson State Park, dans lâĂtat de Victoria.
Les voyageurs franĂ§ais le nommĂ¨rent ainsi en hommage Ă  $Michel de Montaigne$Michel_de_Montaigne$, alors largement lu â et qui est encore ĂŠtudiĂŠ aujourdâhui â, notamment par $Bonaparte$NapolĂŠon_Ier$, qui avait traitĂŠ les Essais $de Montaigne$Michel_de_Montaigne$ durant ses annĂŠes de formation Ă  lâĂŠcole militaire.
Voyageurs et penseurs sâintĂŠressaient ĂŠgalement Ă  Montaigne pour lâimportance quâil accordait aux voyages dans lâĂŠducation. Son Ĺuvre Les Essais faisait partie de la bibliothĂ¨que personnelle de Baudin Ă  bord du GĂŠographe.
characteristic: The cape, part of a volcanic peninsula (with Baies Tourville and $Descartes$RenĂŠ_Descartes$ and Cap $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$), lies within the boundaries of Victoriaâs Cape Nelson State Park. The French voyagers named it after $Michel de Montaigne$Michel_de_Montaigne$ was (and still is) very much read, including by $Bonaparte$Napoleon$ who studied Montaigneâs Essais during his earlier years, at his military college. Travellers and theorists also studied Montaigne for the educational importance he assigned to voyages. Montaigne's work, Les Essais, were part of Baudin's personal Library on board the Geographe.
history_fr:
Montaigne faisait preuve dâune ouverture dâesprit remarquable envers les autres cultures. Ainsi, malgrĂŠ les prĂŠjugĂŠs de son ĂŠpoque Ă  lâĂŠgard des peuples autochtones, il se rendit Ă  Rouen pour rencontrer trois hommes venus du BrĂŠsil, rĂŠcemment arrivĂŠs en France et rĂŠputĂŠs ĂŞtre des ÂŤ cannibales Âť. Dans ses Essais, Montaigne affirme que les EuropĂŠens ÂŤ les surpassent [les Indiens] en toutes sortes de barbaries Âť.
Bien que NapolĂŠon ne fĂťt pas un humaniste, sa personnalitĂŠ nâĂŠtait pas dĂŠnuĂŠe de paradoxes, et les ĂŠcrits $de Montaigne$Michel_de_Montaigne$ lâaccompagnĂ¨rent tout au long de sa vie.

$PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$, lui aussi, eut ses propres contradictions. Celles-ci ne lâempĂŞchĂ¨rent pas de choisir le nom Montaigne pour la nomenclature du voyage, alors mĂŞme quâil sâĂŠloignait des idĂŠes de lâĂŠcrivain. Au dĂŠbut de lâexpĂŠdition, il percevait les peuples autochtones Ă  la maniĂ¨re $de Montaigne$Michel_de_Montaigne$ â comme des ĂŞtres fondamentalement humains et capables dâamitiĂŠ â, mais Ă  son retour, sa vision avait changĂŠ. Il ne considĂŠrait plus ceux quâil appelait autrefois ses ÂŤ amis Âť comme de ÂŤ bons sauvages Âť, mais comme des ÂŤ peuples sauvages Âť inefficaces, sur lesquels il mena des expĂŠriences au dynamomĂ¨tre afin dâĂŠvaluer leur force physique.
history:
Montaigne displayed a remarkable openness toward other cultures. For example, despite the cultural prejudices of his time against Indigenous peoples, he went to Rouen to meet three men from Brazil who had just arrived in France and were reputed to be âcannibals.â In his Essais, Montaigne argues that Europeans âsurpass them [the Indians] in all kinds of barbarism.â Although Napoleon was not a humanist, his character was not without paradoxes, and Montaigneâs writings accompanied him throughout his life.
$PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$, too, had his own contradictions. These did not prevent him from choosing the name Montaigne for the voyageâs nomenclature, even as he moved away from Montaigneâs ideas. At the beginning of the expedition, he viewed Indigenous peoples much as Montaigne hadâas fundamentally human and capable of friendshipâbut upon his return, his outlook had changed. He no longer described those he had once called his âfriendsâ as âgood savages,â but rather as ineffective âpeoplesâ on whom he conducted dynamometer experiments to measure their physical strength.

characteristic_fr:
Le site se trouve Ă  l'extrĂŠmitĂŠ sud-ouest du cap Bridgewater, sur une pĂŠninsule basaltique dans l'ouest de l'Ătat de Victoria. Les fouilles menĂŠes au cap $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ ont mis au jour un paysage cĂ´tier aborigĂ¨ne datant du dĂŠbut de l'HolocĂ¨ne, avec une sĂŠrie de dĂŠpĂ´ts coquilliers aborigĂ¨nes Ă  ciel ouvert situĂŠs au sommet d'une falaise de 50 mĂ¨tres de haut, dont la base est actuellement battue par les vagues. L'expĂŠdition franĂ§aise tĂŠmoigne de cette prĂŠsence aborigĂ¨ne et de leur utilisation du feu, qui a faĂ§onnĂŠ les paysages cĂ´tiers, qui figurent sur les profils cĂ´tiers faits Ă  bord du GĂŠographe. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$, dont lâĂŠcriture se distingue par une rhĂŠtorique Ă  la fois emphatique, sensible et parfois excessive, observe ĂŠgalement que ÂŤ le terrain prĂŠsente Ă§Ă  et lĂ  quelques arbrisseaux tristes et languissants Âť, introduisant une nuance Ă  peine perceptible dans le ÂŤ tableau de dĂŠpression et de stĂŠrilitĂŠ Âť quâil attribue au reste du littoral.
Le cap $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ a ĂŠtĂŠ nommĂŠ en l'honneur d'$Abraham Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ (dĂŠcĂŠdĂŠ en 1688), un officier vĂŠnĂŠrĂŠ de la marine franĂ§aise du XVIIe siĂ¨cle.
characteristic:
The site lies at the southwestern tip of Cape Bridgewater, on a basalt peninsula in western Victoria. Investigations at the Cape $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ have revealed an early-Holocene Aboriginal coastal landscape, with a series of open-air Aboriginal shell midden deposits situated at the top of a 50 m high cliff, with the ocean currently washing against its base. The French expedition bears witness to this Aboriginal presence and their use of fire, which shaped the coastal landscapes, as indicated by the coastal profiles made aboard the GĂŠographe. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$, whose writing is characterized by a rhetoric that is at once emphatic, sensitive, and at times excessive, also notes that âthe terrain presents here and there a few sad and languishing shrubs,â introducing a barely perceptible nuance into the âscene of desolation and sterilityâ he ascribes to the rest of the coastline.
Cape $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ was named in honour of $Abraham Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ (died in 1688), a distinguished seventeenth-century officer of the French navy.
history_fr: $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$, en collaboration avec $Jean-Baptiste Colbert$Jean-Baptiste_Colbert$, a contribuĂŠ Ă  la crĂŠation et Ă  l'institutionnalisation des grades navals dans la Marine royale. Bien que rĂŠputĂŠ pour ĂŞtre un individu peu sympathique, il a ĂŠtĂŠ cĂŠlĂŠbrĂŠ pour sa victoire dĂŠcisive sur l'amiral nĂŠerlandais Ruyter en MĂŠditerranĂŠe, un triomphe qui lui a valu d'ĂŞtre reconnu comme le plus grand capitaine franĂ§ais de son ĂŠpoque, Ă  ĂŠgalitĂŠ avec Ruyter et les plus grands commandants anglais et nĂŠerlandais.Le nom de $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ dans la nomenclature tĂŠmoigne de son utilisation comme figure exemplaire Ă  lâĂŠpoque napolĂŠonienne, incarnant le modĂ¨le du grand marin que NapolĂŠon souhaitait ĂŠriger pour soutenir sa politique de reconstruction navale aprĂ¨s le dĂŠsastre de Trafalgar. Dans cette perspective de restauration Ă  long terme de la marine franĂ§aise, lâun des deux navires-ĂŠcoles crĂŠĂŠs en 1810 pour assurer la formation annuelle de 300 ĂŠlĂ¨ves-officiers de marine reĂ§ut le nom $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ (celui basĂŠ Ă  Toulon).
history: $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$, together with $Jean-Baptiste Colbert$Jean-Baptiste_Colbert$, contributed to the creation and institutionalization of naval ranks in the Royal Navy. Though reputed to be an unsympathetic individual, he was celebrated for his decisive victory over the Dutch Admiral Ruyter in the Mediterranean Seaâa triumph that earned him recognition as the greatest French sea captain of his time, on par with Ruyter and the foremost English and Dutch commanders. The name $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ in the nomenclature reflects his use as an exemplary figure during the Napoleonic era, embodying the model of the great naval commander that Napoleon sought to promote in support of his long-term policy to rebuild the French Navy after the disaster of Trafalgar. In this context of naval restoration, one of the two training ships established in 1810 to educate 300 naval cadets each year was named $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ (the one based in Toulon).
origin_fr: Abraham Duquesne, l'un des grands officiers de la marine de guerre franĂ§aise du XVIIe siĂ¨cle.

characteristic_fr:
La baie se situe au sein du bassin dâOtway, lâun des nombreux bassins longeant la marge continentale sud de lâAustralie, formĂŠs lors de la sĂŠparation de lâAntarctique et de lâAustralie.
DĂŠcrite par $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ comme ÂŤ vaste Âť, elle abrite une faune marine diversifiĂŠe et fait partie du parc marin national de Discovery Bay. Freycinet dĂŠcrivit les rivages de la baie comme ÂŤ bas, sablonneux et stĂŠriles Âť ; toutefois, au sud de lâĂŠtendue sablonneuse de la baie $Descartes$RenĂŠ_Descartes$ sâĂŠtend une ĂŠtroite bande de rĂŠcif basaltique remarquable, caractĂŠrisĂŠe par ses parois et falaises verticales. 
La baie fut nommĂŠe en hommage Ă  $RenĂŠ Descartes$RenĂŠ_Descartes$, connu pour avoir ĂŠlaborĂŠ le projet dâune science universelle fondĂŠe sur la raison, capable dâunifier lâĂŠtude des rĂŠalitĂŠs physiques et mĂŠtaphysiques â telles que Dieu et lâĂ˘me â selon les principes de certitude quâil avait ĂŠtablis dans les mathĂŠmatiques.
characteristic:
The bay is situated within the Otway Basinâone of several basins along the southern Australian continental margin formed during the separation of Antarctica and Australia. 
Described by $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ as âlarge,â it supports diverse marine life and forms part of the Discovery Bay Marine National Park. Freycinet portrayed the bayâs shores as âlow, sandy, and sterileâ; however, south of the sandy expanse at $Descartes$RenĂŠ_Descartes$ Bay lies a narrow band of prominent basalt reef, distinguished by vertical walls and cliffs.
The bay was named in honor of $RenĂŠ Descartes$RenĂŠ_Descartes$, known for developing the concept of a universal science based on reason, capable of unifying the study of both physical and metaphysical realities â including God and the soul â according to the principles of certainty he had established in mathematics.
history_fr: Bien que $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ nourrĂŽt le rĂŞve dâune nature prodigue et accueillante, son observation de la baie $Descartes$RenĂŠ_Descartes$ le conduit Ă  un constat plus dĂŠsenchantĂŠ. Il ĂŠcrit : ÂŤ Vers le fond de la baie $Descartes$RenĂŠ_Descartes$, quelques fumĂŠes sâĂŠlevoient au-delĂ  des dunes ; mais, en gĂŠnĂŠral, la nature stĂŠrile et sauvage de toute cette cĂ´te doit en repousser lâespĂ¨ce humaine : on peut croire au moins quâelle ne sauroit fournir quâĂ  lâexistence dâun petit nombre dâhommes. Âť
history: Although $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ cherished the ideal of a bountiful and welcoming nature, his observation of $Descartes$RenĂŠ_Descartes$ Bay led him to a more disenchanted conclusion. He wrote: âAt the back of $Descartes$RenĂŠ_Descartes$ Bay, a few columns of smoke rose beyond the dunes; but, in general, the sterile and wild nature of this entire coast must repel the human species: one may at least believe that it could provide for the existence of only a small number of men.â
