characteristic_fr:
Ce promontoire de 60 mĂ¨tres de hauteur marque une inflexion dans lâorientation du littoral, qui passe dâun axe est-ouest Ă  un alignement sud-ouest/nord-est. PĂŠron le comparait au ÂŤ talon de la botte Âť de la pĂŠninsule. Il fait aujourdâhui partie du Newland Head Conservation Park.
Jean-Pierre Bachasson, comte de Montalivet (1766-1823), entra dans lâarmĂŠe dâItalie pendant la RĂŠvolution franĂ§aise, alors que sa vie ĂŠtait menacĂŠe sous la Terreur. AprĂ¨s lâarrivĂŠe de Bonaparte au pouvoir, il fut nommĂŠ prĂŠfet de la Manche en 1801, puis de Seine-et-Oise en 1803, dĂŠpartement qui englobait alors Paris et lâĂle-de-France. Sous le Premier Empire, NapolĂŠon, conscient des qualitĂŠs administratives de Montalivet, le nomma successivement conseiller dâĂtat, directeur de la LĂŠgion dâhonneur (voir glossaire), puis ministre de lâIntĂŠrieur.
characteristic:
This 60-metre headland marks a change in the orientation of the coastline, shifting from an eastâwest axis to a south-west/north-east alignment. PĂŠron compared it to âthe heel of the bootâ of the peninsula. It now forms part of Newland Head Conservation Park.
Jean-Pierre Bachasson, comte de Montalivet (1766â1823), joined the Army of Italy during the French Revolution, when his life was threatened during the Terror. After Bonaparteâs rise to power, he was appointed prefect of the Manche in 1801 and later of Seine-et-Oise in 1803, then a department encompassing Paris and the Ăle-de-France. During the First Empire, NapolĂŠon, recognising Montalivetâs administrative abilities, successively appointed him councillor of state, director of the LĂŠgion dâhonneur (see glossary), and finally Minister of the Interior.
history_fr: Sur la carte de lâICSM, le cap Montalivet est situĂŠ sur lâĂŽle Kangourou, Ă  lâest de la baie Nepean. Dans lâatlas de Freycinet, en revanche, il figure sur la cĂ´te sud-est de la pĂŠninsule de Fleurieu, correspondant Ă  lâactuel Newland Head. NapolĂŠon voyait en Montalivet ÂŤ un honnĂŞte homme qui mâest demeurĂŠ, je crois, toujours tendrement attachĂŠ Âť (p. 68). Voir aussi les ĂŽles Montalivet.
history: On the ICSM map, Cape Montalivet is located on Kangaroo Island, to the east of Nepean Bay. In Freycinetâs atlas, however, it appears on the south-eastern coast of the Fleurieu Peninsula, corresponding to present-day Newland Head. NapolĂŠon regarded Montalivet as âan honest man who has, I believe, always remained tenderly attached to meâ (p. 68). See also West and East Montalivet Islands.

origin_fr: Jean-Pierre Bachasson, comte de Montalivet, nĂŠ leÂ 5 juillet 1766Â Ă Â SarregueminesÂ et mort leÂ 22 janvier 1823Â Ă Â Saint-BouizeÂ (Cher) auÂ chĂ˘teau de Lagrange-Montalivet[2], est un officier, magistrat et homme d'Ătat franĂ§ais. characteristic_fr:
Le cap $Dupleix$Joseph_FranĂ§ois_Dupleix$ est situĂŠ dans la partie mĂŠridionale de la pĂŠninsule de Fleurieu, au sein de "Deep Creek Conservation Park". De courts cours dâeau et des cascades dĂŠvalent les falaises cĂ´tiĂ¨res avant de rejoindre le niveau de la mer.
$Joseph-FranĂ§ois Dupleix$Joseph_FranĂ§ois_Dupleix$ (1697â1763), au service de la Compagnie franĂ§aise des Indes orientales, a contribuĂŠ au dĂŠveloppement de Chandernagor, dans le Bengale, et entretenu des relations cordiales avec les nĂŠgociants britanniques.
characteristic:
Cap $Dupleix$Joseph_FranĂ§ois_Dupleix$ is located in the southern part of the Fleurieu Peninsula, within Deep Creek Conservation Park. Short streams and waterfalls cascade over the coastal cliffs, reaching sea level.
$Joseph-FranĂ§ois Dupleix$Joseph_FranĂ§ois_Dupleix$ (1697â1763) worked for the French East India Compagnie. He contributed to the development of Chandernagore in Bengal and established good relations with British traders.
history_fr:
$Joseph-FranĂ§ois Dupleix$Joseph_FranĂ§ois_Dupleix$ fut nommĂŠ gouverneur de PondichĂŠry en 1742. SecondĂŠ par son ĂŠpouse, polyglotte et imprĂŠgnĂŠe de multiples cultures, il parvint Ă  renforcer la position commerciale franĂ§aise dans le commerce des Indes orientales. Lorsque la guerre de Succession dâAutriche, opposant la France Ă  la Grande-Bretagne, sâĂŠtendit jusquâĂ  lâocĂŠan Indien, $Dupleix$Joseph_FranĂ§ois_Dupleix$ sâappuya sur les officiers militaires franĂ§ais placĂŠs sous lâautoritĂŠ de $Bertrand FranĂ§ois MahĂŠ de La Bourdonnais$Bertrand-FranĂ§ois_MahĂŠ_de_La_Bourdonnais$ pour dĂŠfendre les intĂŠrĂŞts franĂ§ais en Inde. Leurs divergences, nombreuses et profondes, dĂŠgĂŠnĂŠrĂ¨rent en une relation conflictuelle, et $Dupleix$Joseph_FranĂ§ois_Dupleix$ mourut ruinĂŠ.
Pourtant, $Dupleix$Joseph_FranĂ§ois_Dupleix$ devint progressivement une figure emblĂŠmatique du rĂŞve oriental de NapolĂŠon, incarnant lâhomme qui avait ĂŠtabli des comptoirs commerciaux et ouvert lâInde Ă  lâempire colonial franĂ§ais. Son hĂŠritage servit ainsi Ă  promouvoir lâexpansion franĂ§aise, symbolisant les ambitions impĂŠriales de la France en Orient.
history:
$Joseph-FranĂ§ois Dupleix$Joseph_FranĂ§ois_Dupleix$ assumed the governorship of Pondicherry in 1742. Supported by his multilingual wife, whose upbringing spanned diverse cultures, he significantly consolidated Franceâs commercial influence within the East India trade. As the War of the Austrian Successionâpitting France against Britainâextended into the Indian Ocean, $Dupleix$Joseph_FranĂ§ois_Dupleix$ enlisted the support of French military officers, notably under the command of $Bertrand$FRClaude_Bertrand_(astronome)$ FranĂ§ois MahĂŠ de La Bourdonnais, to safeguard French interests in India. Their collaboration, however, was marked by persistent disagreements, ultimately souring into a contentious relationship that contributed to $Dupleix$Joseph_FranĂ§ois_Dupleix$âs eventual financial ruin.

Nonetheless, $Dupleix$Joseph_FranĂ§ois_Dupleix$âs legacy underwent a reevaluation during the Napoleonic era, as he came to symbolize the ambitions of Franceâs colonial expansion in the East. His establishment of trading outposts and the facilitation of French access to the Indian subcontinent positioned him as a pivotal figure in NapolĂŠonâs vision of oriental imperialism, embodying the potential for French territorial and economic growth.
origin_fr: Joseph-FranĂ§ois Dupleix (1er janvier 1697 - 10 novembre 1763) fut gouverneur gĂŠnĂŠral des ĂŠtablissements franĂ§ais de l'Inde et un grand rival de Robert Clive.

characteristic_fr:
Le dĂŠtroit de Colbert correspond Ă  une ancienne vallĂŠe glaciaire envahie par la mer, qui sĂŠpare lâĂŽle Kangourou de la pĂŠninsule de Fleurieu. Freycinet le dĂŠfinit comme lâintervalle le plus resserrĂŠ entre lâĂŽle DecrĂ¨s (Kangaroo Island) et lâentrĂŠe du golfe JosĂŠphine (Gulf St Vincent).
Le nom de dĂŠtroit de Colbert rend hommage Ă  une illustre lignĂŠe nobiliaire, dont lâancĂŞtre le plus cĂŠlĂ¨bre, Jean-Baptiste Colbert (1619-1683), fut le principal ministre de Louis XIV.
characteristic:
Colbert Strait corresponds to an ancient glacial valley later flooded by the sea, separating Kangaroo Island from Fleurieu Peninsula. Freycinet defined it as the narrowest interval between Ăle DecrĂ¨s (Kangaroo Island) and the entrance to Golfe JosĂŠphine (Gulf St Vincent).
The name Colbert Strait pays tribute to an illustrious noble lineage, whose most famous ancestor, Jean-Baptiste Colbert (1619â1683), was the principal minister of Louis XIV.
history_fr: Sous lâEmpire, plusieurs de ses descendants se distinguĂ¨rent Ă  leur tour dans les guerres napolĂŠoniennes, notamment Auguste-Marie-FranĂ§ois, baron Colbert de Chabanais (1777-1809), cĂŠlĂŠbrĂŠ comme un modĂ¨le de bravoure. La prĂŠsence de ce nom dans la nomenclature associe ainsi la gĂŠographie du dĂŠtroit Ă  la mĂŠmoire dâune famille ĂŠtroitement liĂŠe, Ă  des titres divers, Ă  lâĂtat, au service du souverain et Ă  lâhonneur militaire.
history: Under the Empire, several of his descendants in turn distinguished themselves in the Napoleonic wars, notably Auguste-Marie-FranĂ§ois, Baron Colbert de Chabanais (1777â1809), who was celebrated as a model of bravery. The presence of this name in the nomenclature thus links the geography of the strait with the memory of a family closely associated, in different ways, with the state, service to the sovereign, and military honour.
origin_fr: Jean-Baptiste Colbert (29 aoĂťt 1619 - 6 septembre 1683) ĂŠtait un homme politique franĂ§ais qui a servi comme ContrĂ´leur gĂŠnĂŠral des finances de 1665 Ă  1683 sous le rĂ¨gne du roi Louis XIV.

characteristic_fr:
Les Pages sont un groupe de petites ĂŽles situĂŠes dans le dĂŠtroit de $Colbert$Jean-Baptiste_Colbert$/Blackstair Passage. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ les prĂŠsente comme ÂŤ trois petites ĂŽles entourĂŠes de rĂŠcifs Âť qui se trouvent Ă  peu prĂ¨s Ă  ĂŠgale distance entre le continent australien et lâĂŽle DecrĂ¨s (aujourdâhui connue sous le nom de Kangaroo Island).
Les FranĂ§ais nommĂ¨rent ces ĂŽles Ăles Bourdet en lâhonneur de $Jean-Pierre BourdĂŠ$Jacques-Pierre_BourdĂŠ_de_La_Villehuet$ de $La Villehuet$Jacques-Pierre_BourdĂŠ_de_La_Villehuet$ (ĂŠgalement orthographiĂŠ $BourdĂŠ$Jacques-Pierre_BourdĂŠ_de_La_Villehuet$, 1732â1789), un officier de marine franĂ§ais renommĂŠ pour ses compĂŠtences en sciences nautiques.
characteristic:
The Pages are a group of small islands situated within the passage, characterized by $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ as âthree little islands surrounded by reefs.â They are located roughly equidistant between the Australian mainland and Ăle DecrĂ¨s (now known as Kangaroo Island).
The French designated these islands as Ăles Bourdet, honoring $Jean-Pierre BourdĂŠ$FRJacques-Pierre_BourdĂŠ_de_La_Villehuet$ de $La Villehuet$FRJacques-Pierre_BourdĂŠ_de_La_Villehuet$ (also spelt $BourdĂŠ$FRJacques-Pierre_BourdĂŠ_de_La_Villehuet$, 1732â1789), a distinguished officer in the French Navy renowned for his expertise in nautical science.
history_fr:
$Jean-Pierre BourdĂŠ$Jacques-Pierre_BourdĂŠ_de_La_Villehuet$ de $La Villehuet$Jacques-Pierre_BourdĂŠ_de_La_Villehuet$ (1732â1789) fut saluĂŠ pour son traitĂŠ de rĂŠfĂŠrence sur la manĹuvre navale, "Le manoeuvrier, ou Essai sur la thĂŠorie et la pratique des mouvements du navire et des ĂŠvolutions navales". Ses contributions savantes sâĂŠtendirent ĂŠgalement Ă  des ouvrages pratiques, tels que le Manuel des marins, ou Dictionnaire des termes de la Marine, un guide exhaustif destinĂŠ aux marins.
Son fils, Guillaume-FranĂ§ois-Joseph $BourdĂŠ$Jacques-Pierre_BourdĂŠ_de_La_Villehuet$ de $La Villehuet$Jacques-Pierre_BourdĂŠ_de_La_Villehuet$ (1763â1851), embrassa Ă  son tour une carriĂ¨re navale distinguĂŠe. Promu capitaine en mai 1798, il prit part Ă  lâexpĂŠdition dâĂgypte, consolidant ainsi lâhĂŠritage maritime de sa famille.
history:
$Jean-Pierre BourdĂŠ$FRJacques-Pierre_BourdĂŠ_de_La_Villehuet$ de $La Villehuet$FRJacques-Pierre_BourdĂŠ_de_La_Villehuet$ (1732â1789) earned acclaim for his authoritative treatise on naval maneuvering, which was subsequently rendered into English under the title The Manoeuverer, or Skilful Seaman: An Essay on the Theory and Practice of the Various Movements of a Ship at Sea, as well as of Naval Evolutions in General. His scholarly contributions extended to practical works, including the Manuel des marins, ou Dictionnaire des termes de la Marine, a comprehensive guide for mariners.
His son, Guillaume-FranĂ§ois-Joseph $BourdĂŠ$FRJacques-Pierre_BourdĂŠ_de_La_Villehuet$ de $La Villehuet$FRJacques-Pierre_BourdĂŠ_de_La_Villehuet$ (1763â1851), likewise pursued a distinguished naval career. Attaining the rank of captain in May 1798, he participated in the historic Egyptian expedition, further solidifying the familyâs legacy in maritime service.
origin_fr: Jacques BourdĂŠ de la Villehuet (nĂŠ vers 1730 Ă  Saint-Coulomb) est un officier de la Compagnie franĂ§aise des Indes orientales et auteur de plusieurs ouvrages sur la marine qui connurent un grand succĂ¨s au XVIIIe siĂ¨cle.

characteristic_fr:
Le golfe JosĂŠphine (golfe Saint-Vincent) constitue lâun des deux plus vastes bras de mer de la cĂ´te mĂŠridionale de lâAustralie-MĂŠridionale, en position orientale. La capitale, AdĂŠlaĂŻde, sâĂŠtend sur sa rive orientale.
$PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ souligne que ce golfe est ÂŤ le plus proche de lâĂŽle DecrĂ¨s [aujourdâhui lâĂŽle Kangourou], le plus oriental et le plus ĂŠtroit Âť*, et quâil lâa dĂŠdiĂŠ ÂŤ Ă  notre auguste impĂŠratrice Âť, $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$, ĂŠpouse de Bonaparte, devenu lâempereur NapolĂŠon IáľĘł.
Le golfe $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$, associĂŠ Ă  son homologue, le golfe $Bonaparte$NapolĂŠon_Ier$*, ĂŠtait perĂ§u comme une rĂŠgion clĂŠ de la Terre NapolĂŠon. Ainsi, Ă  lâapogĂŠe de lâEmpire napolĂŠonien, il ĂŠtait naturel pour PĂŠron de dĂŠdier lâun des golfes Ă  JosĂŠphine, figure ĂŠminente de lâEmpire en tant que premiĂ¨re dame, et dâattribuer dâautres lieux de cette rĂŠgion Ă  dix-huit femmes marquantes : certaines liĂŠes Ă  elle-mĂŞme ou Ă  son ĂŠpoux ($Hortense$Hortense_de_Beauharnais$, Laetitia, $Pauline$Pauline_Bonaparte$, Caroline, Julie, AmĂŠlie, AdĂ¨le, Eliza), dâautres associĂŠes Ă  des ĂŠvĂŠnements militaires (Jeanne dâArc, $Jeanne Hachette$Jeanne_Hachette$) ou au domaine culturel (huit noms).
characteristic:
Golfe JosĂŠphine (Gulf St Vincent, ) forms the eastern of the two largest inlets of the sea on the southern coast of South Australia. The capital city of Adelaide lies on the gulfâs eastern shore.
$PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ emphasizes that this gulf is âthe closest to Ăle DecrĂ¨s [now Kangaroo Island], the easternmost and the narrowestâ*, and that he dedicated it âto our august Empress,â JosĂŠphine de Beauharnais, wife of Bonaparte, who had become Emperor Napoleon I.
Golfe $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$ was seen, together with its counterpart Golfe $Bonaparte$Napoleon$*, as the key region of Terre NapolĂŠon*. Thus, during the apogee of the Napoleonic empire, it was natural to PĂŠron to dedicate one of the gulfs to JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais who was the very prominent first lady of the Empire, and other places in this gulf exclusively to 18 ladies who were special either to her and her husband ($Hortense$Hortense_de_Beauharnais$, $Laetitia$Letizia_Bonaparte$, $Pauline$Pauline_Bonaparte$, Caroline, Julie, Amelie, Adele, Eliza) or were fundamentally linked to military events (Jeanne dâArc, $Jeanne Hachette$Jeanne_Hachette$) or to the cultural sphere (8 names).
history_fr: JosĂŠphine portant un vif intĂŠrĂŞt Ă  lâexpĂŠdition Baudin et Ă  ses collections dâhistoire naturelle, NapolĂŠon Bonaparte, alors Premier Consul (de 1799-1804), passa outre Ă  lâinterdiction des collections privĂŠes pour une expĂŠdition financĂŠe par lâĂtat. Il demanda Ă  son ministre de la Marine de lâĂŠpoque, Alexandre Forfait, dâordonner Ă  Baudin de constituer ÂŤâŻune collection particuliĂ¨re destinĂŠe Ă  madame BonaparteâŻÂť. Celle-ci devait comprendre ÂŤâŻdes animaux de toute espĂ¨ce, vivants ou naturalisĂŠs, et principalement des oiseaux aux plus beaux plumagesâŚ, des quadrupĂ¨desâŚ, des fleurs, des arbustes, des graines, des pierres prĂŠcieuses, des bois propres Ă  la marqueterie, des insectes, des papillonsâŚâŻÂť. Ainsi, malgrĂŠ les frustrations exprimĂŠes par les savants du MusĂŠum, une partie des collections rapportĂŠes par lâexpĂŠdition Baudin fut livrĂŠe Ă  La Malmaison, rĂŠsidence de JosĂŠphine.
history: As JosĂŠphine took a great deal of interest in the Baudin expedition and its natural history collections, Napoleon Bonaparte, at the time the First Consul, ignored the direction disallowing private collections on a voyage sponsored by the State, by asking his then Navy Minister, Alexandre Forfait, to order Baudin to make âa specific collection intended for Madame BonaparteâŚâ. It was to include âanimals of all kinds, alive or preserved, and mainly birds with beautiful feathers âŚ quadrupeds âŚ flowers, bushes, seeds, precious stones, woods suitable for carved furniture, insects, butterflies âŚâ. Hence, despite some frustrations expressed by the savants of the Museum, part of the collections brought back by the Baudin expedition were delivered to La Malmaison, Josephine's residence.

characteristic_fr:
Le cap dâAlembert, connu aujourdâhui sous le nom de Cape Jervis, constitue lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ de la pĂŠninsule de Fleurieu ainsi que la limite orientale du golfe Saint-Vincent. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$, poursuivant sa comparaison de la pĂŠninsule avec la forme de ÂŤ botte Âť de la pĂŠninsule italienne, relĂ¨ve que ÂŤ le Grand Cap dâAlembert termine la pĂŠninsule de Fleurieu Ă  lâest, formant ainsi lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ de la botte Âť.
$Jean Le Rond$Jean_Le_Rond_d%27Alembert$ dâAlembert (1717-1783), mathĂŠmaticien du XVIIIe siĂ¨cle, est lâauteur de mĂŠmoires et de traitĂŠs sur la mĂŠcanique des fluides, le calcul intĂŠgral et la dynamique, dans lesquels il analyse le mouvement des corps interconnectĂŠs, quâil dĂŠcompose en quantitĂŠs de mouvements effectifs et virtuels.
characteristic:
Cap dâAlembert, known today as cape Jervis, forms the tip of Fleurieu Peninsula* as well as the eastern boundary of Gulf St Vincent*. Peron, continuing his comparison of the peninsula with the âbootâ shape of the Italian peninsula, notes that âthe Grand Cap d'Alembert ends the Fleurieu peninsula to the east, forming the toe of the bootâ.
$Jean Le Rond$FRJean_Le_Rond_d%27Alembert$ d'Alembert (1717-1783) was an Eighteen century mathematician who wrote memoirs or treatises on the mechanics of fluids, on integral calculus, on dynamics analyzing the motion of interconnected bodies, which he decomposed into quantities of effective and virtual movements.
history_fr: Dans une lettre adressĂŠe Ă  lâancien ministre de lâIntĂŠrieur, M. de Champagny, en date du 18 mars 1807, lâEmpereur exprimait son admiration pour dâAlembert, quâil considĂŠrait comme le mathĂŠmaticien franĂ§ais du siĂ¨cle prĂŠcĂŠdent ayant le plus contribuĂŠ au progrĂ¨s des mathĂŠmatiques, ÂŤ la premiĂ¨re des sciences Âť. Il y annonĂ§ait par ailleurs son projet de transfĂŠrer Ă  lâInstitut de France (voir glossaire) une statue remarquable de dâAlembert, initialement commandĂŠe par un financier, artiste et homme de lettres franĂ§ais dĂŠcĂŠdĂŠ avant son achĂ¨vement, afin de lâinstaller dans la salle des sĂŠances de la classe des sciences mathĂŠmatiques, la plus prestigieuse de ses divisions.
history: In a letter addressed to the former Minister of the Interior, M. de Champagny, on March 18, 1807, Emperor Napoleon expressed his admiration for dâAlembert, whom he regarded as the French mathematician of the previous century who had most significantly advanced the progress of mathematicsâ"the foremost of sciences." The Emperor further announced his intention to relocate a remarkable statue of dâAlembert, originally commissioned by a French financier, artist, and man of letters who had passed away before its completion. The statue was to be transferred to the Institut de France (see glossary) and installed in the meeting hall of the mathematics division, the most prestigious of its academic sections.

characteristic_fr: âŻLa pointe de Rapid Head, situĂŠe sur la cĂ´te orientale de la pĂŠninsule de Fleurieu, marque lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ septentrionale dâune longue succession de falaises et dâescarpements abrupts, pouvant atteindre 150 mĂ¨tres de hauteur, qui sâĂŠtendent vers le sud jusquâau cap Jervis. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ y fait rĂŠfĂŠrence comme au ÂŤâŻpoint de dĂŠpart exact du golfe $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$âŻÂť (t. 1, p. 87).âŻâŻSon nom franĂ§ais, cap SĂŠvignĂŠ, rend hommage Ă  $Marie de Rabutin-Chantal$Madame_de_SĂŠvignĂŠ$, connue sous le nom de marquise $de SĂŠvignĂŠ$Madame_de_SĂŠvignĂŠ$, ou encore $Madame de SĂŠvignĂŠ$Madame_de_SĂŠvignĂŠ$ (1626-1696). Celle-ci fut cĂŠlĂŠbrĂŠe pour les lettres quâelle adressait depuis Paris Ă  sa fille, alors ĂŠloignĂŠe dâelle Ă  Grignan, dans le sud-est de la France.âŻ
characteristic: Rapid Head, on the eastern coast of Fleurieu Peninsula, marks the northernmost extent of a long line of steep cliffs and bluffs, up to 150 m high, that extends south to Cape Jervis, which $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ referred to as the âexact start of golfe $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$â (t.1, p.87). Its French name, Cap SĂŠvignĂŠ, refers to $Marie de Rabutin-Chantal$Marie_de_Rabutin-Chantal,_marquise_de_SĂŠvignĂŠ$, known as the marquise $de SĂŠvignĂŠ$Marie_de_Rabutin-Chantal,_marquise_de_SĂŠvignĂŠ$, or $Madame de SĂŠvignĂŠ$Marie_de_Rabutin-Chantal,_marquise_de_SĂŠvignĂŠ$ (1626-1696). She was celebrated for the letters she wrote in Paris to her daughter who was living far from her, in Grignan, in the southeast of France.
history_fr: âŻLa fraĂŽcheur naturelle, la vivacitĂŠ et la fluiditĂŠ du style de $Madame de SĂŠvignĂŠ$Madame_de_SĂŠvignĂŠ$ devinrent une rĂŠfĂŠrence littĂŠraire, sâĂŠcartant des formes plus ĂŠrudites alors en usage dans les cercles lettrĂŠs du XVIIeâŻsiĂ¨cle. Les femmes de la cour napolĂŠonienne adoptĂ¨rent Ă  leur tour ce modĂ¨le ĂŠpistolaire pour rendre compte de leurs voyages, avec des rĂŠsultats divers. $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$, quant Ă  elle, envoya Ă  sa fille des lettres marquĂŠes par la tendresse, mais oĂš la description de sa vie et de ses sentiments occupait une place moins centrale que dans la correspondance de MmeâŻ$de SĂŠvignĂŠ$Madame_de_SĂŠvignĂŠ$.
history:
The natural freshness, vividness and lightness of the style of $Madame de SĂŠvignĂŠ$Marie_de_Rabutin-Chantal,_marquise_de_SĂŠvignĂŠ$ became a literary reference that broke with the more scholarly form of writing that had been practised in literary circles in the 17th century.
Women at the Napoleonic court mirrored the epistolary style of Mme $de SĂŠvignĂŠ$Marie_de_Rabutin-Chantal,_marquise_de_SĂŠvignĂŠ$ to report on their travel, albeit with varying levels of success. $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$ also sent letters to her daughter filled with tenderness, although these were not as descriptive of her own life and sentiments as those written by Mme $de SĂŠvignĂŠ$Marie_de_Rabutin-Chantal,_marquise_de_SĂŠvignĂŠ$.

characteristic_fr:
La baie de Yankalilla est une vaste ĂŠtendue cĂ´tiĂ¨re, longue et large, bordĂŠe de falaises atteignant jusquâĂ  120 mĂ¨tres de hauteur, sur la cĂ´te occidentale de la pĂŠninsule de Fleurieu. Freycinet, qui explora cette rĂŠgion durant les journĂŠes torrides de lâĂŠtĂŠ, y releva lâariditĂŠ des sols et lâabsence apparente dâeau douce, ignorant alors que trois cours dâeau aux noms aborigĂ¨nes â les riviĂ¨res Bungala et Yankalilla, ainsi que le ruisseau de Carrickalinga â sây dĂŠversaient.
Les premiers visiteurs franĂ§ais nommĂ¨rent cette baie en hommage Ă  $Hortense de Beauharnais$Hortense_de_Beauharnais$ (1783â1837), fille de lâimpĂŠratrice $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$ et dâ$Alexandre de Beauharnais$Alexandre_de_Beauharnais$, guillotinĂŠ sous la Terreur.
characteristic: Yankalilla Bay is a long, wide bay bordered by cliffs up to 120 m high, on the western coast of Fleurieu Peninsula. Freycinet, who surveyed this coast during the very hot days of summer, noted the aridity of the soil and the lack of fresh water, although three waterways carrying Aboriginal names, the Bungala and Yankalilla Rivers and the Carrickalinga Creek all flow into bay. The bay was first named by the French visitors after $Hortense de Beauharnais$Hortense_de_Beauharnais$ (1783-1837), the daughter of the empress $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$ and her first husband, $Alexandre de Beauharnais$Alexandre_de_Beauharnais$, guillotined during the Terror.
history_fr:
$Hortense de Beauharnais$Hortense_de_Beauharnais$ occupait une place centrale dans lâactualitĂŠ politique et mondaine au dĂŠbut du rĂ¨gne de $NapolĂŠon Bonaparte$NapolĂŠon_Ier$. Son mariage avec Louis $Bonaparte$Caroline_Bonaparte$, frĂ¨re de lâEmpereur, en 1802, puis leur accession au trĂ´ne de Hollande en 1806, furent orchestrĂŠs pour des raisons politiques et afin de consolider les liens familiaux.
Faute dâhĂŠritier direct avec $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$, NapolĂŠon veilla Ă  ce que la Constitution de lâEmpire franĂ§ais, adoptĂŠe en 1804, prĂŠvĂŽt que la succession pĂťt revenir au fils ou petit-fils de lâun de ses frĂ¨res. Ainsi, NapolĂŠon-Charles, premier fils dâHortense et de Louis, fut un temps considĂŠrĂŠ comme lâhĂŠritier prĂŠsomptif de NapolĂŠon, avant de mourir prĂŠmaturĂŠment en 1807, emportĂŠ par le croup, une infection virale.
history:
$Hortense de Beauharnais$Hortense_de_Beauharnais$ held a central place in the political and social news of the early years of Napoleon $Bonaparte$Napoleon$âs reign. Her marriage to Louis $Bonaparte$Napoleon$, the Emperorâs brother, in 1802, followed by their ascension to the throne of Holland in 1806, was arranged for political reasons and to strengthen familial ties.
Lacking a direct heir with $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$, Napoleon ensured that the Constitution of the French Empire, adopted in 1804, stipulated that succession could pass to the son or grandson of one of his brothers. Thus, NapolĂŠon-Charles, the first son of Hortense and Louis, was for a time regarded as Napoleonâs presumptive heir, before dying prematurely in 1807 from croup, a viral infection.
origin_fr: Hortense de Beauharnais (10 avril 1783 - 5 octobre 1837) ĂŠtait reine consort de Hollande par son mariage avec Louis Bonaparte, frĂ¨re de NapolĂŠon. Elle ĂŠtait la fille de JosĂŠphine de Beauharnais.

characteristic_fr:
Le promontoire, formĂŠ dâune alternance de grĂ¨s et de schistes, marque lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ septentrionale dâune baie sableuse le long de laquelle sâĂŠtend le modeste bourg de Carrickalinga. Son nom aborigĂ¨ne signifie ÂŤ lieu oĂš lâon rĂŠcolte le bois de chauffage de gommier rouge Âť.
Laetitia (1750â1836), nĂŠe $Letizia Ramolino$Letizia_Bonaparte$ â les $Ramolino$Letizia_Bonaparte$ ĂŠtant une famille noble originaire de Toscane, ĂŠtablie en Corse â, fut la mĂ¨re de NapolĂŠon.
characteristic:
The headland, composed of interbedded sandstone and shales, marks the northern end of a sandy bay along which the small township of Carrickalinga is located. Its Aboriginal name means 'place for redgum firewood'.
Laetitia (1750-1836), born as $Letizia Ramolino$Letizia_Bonaparte$ (the $Ramolino$Letizia_Bonaparte$ were a noble family originally from Tuscany, who had settled in Corsica) was NapolĂŠonâs mother.
history_fr:
Ă la suite de lâascension de $NapolĂŠon Bonaparte$NapolĂŠon_Ier$ au pouvoir, Laetitia $Bonaparte$Caroline_Bonaparte$ manifesta son dĂŠsaccord quant au mariage de son fils avec $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$, quâelle qualifiait, selon les sources de lâĂŠpoque, de ÂŤ vieille femme Âť. Elle alla jusquâĂ  refuser dâassister Ă  la cĂŠrĂŠmonie du sacre de NapolĂŠon et $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$, qui se dĂŠroula le 2 dĂŠcembre 1804 en la cathĂŠdrale Notre-Dame de Paris, quelques mois aprĂ¨s le retour du GĂŠographe. MalgrĂŠ cette absence, NapolĂŠon ordonna Ă  Jacques-Louis David, peintre officiel de lâĂŠvĂŠnement, dâintĂŠgrer sa mĂ¨re dans la reprĂŠsentation picturale solennelle du couronnement. Cette inclusion rĂŠpondait Ă  une stratĂŠgie dĂŠlibĂŠrĂŠe : il sâagissait dâaffirmer la fondation dâune nouvelle dynastie, symboliquement incarnĂŠe par la figure maternelle et par lâĂŠpouse impĂŠriale, dont on attendait quâelle donnĂ˘t un hĂŠritier susceptible de perpĂŠtuer la lignĂŠe impĂŠriale.
Par dĂŠcret, NapolĂŠon confĂŠra Ă  sa mĂ¨re le titre dâAltesse ImpĂŠriale Madame, MĂ¨re de lâEmpereur, assorti de revenus, dâune suite, dâun chĂ˘teau ainsi que des responsabilitĂŠs au sein dâinstitutions charitables. NĂŠanmoins, Laetitia $Bonaparte$Caroline_Bonaparte$ continua de mener une existence modeste.
history:
Following $NapolĂŠon Bonaparte$Napoleon$âs rise to power, $Laetitia Bonaparte$Letizia_Bonaparte$ expressed disapproval of her sonâs marriage to $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$, whom she reportedly referred to as âthe old woman.â She further demonstrated her opposition by declining to attend the coronation ceremony of NapolĂŠon and $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$, which was held on 2 December 1804 at Notre-Dame Cathedral in Paris, shortly after the return of Le GĂŠographe. Despite her absence, NapolĂŠon instructed the official artist of the event, Jacques-Louis David, to include his mother in the formal coronation painting. This inclusion served NapolĂŠonâs broader objective of legitimizing a new dynasty, symbolically anchored by both his mother and his wife, the latter of whom was expected to produce an heir to secure the imperial succession.
NapolĂŠon subsequently conferred upon $Laetitia Bonaparte$Letizia_Bonaparte$ the title of Imperial Highness Madame, Mother of the Emperor by decree, along with financial allowances, a personal retinue, a residence, and philanthropic duties. Nevertheless, she maintained a modest lifestyle, seemingly unaffected by her elevated status.
origin_fr: Laetitia Ramolino, mĂ¨re de NapolĂŠon Bonaparte (24 aoĂťt 1750 - 2 fĂŠvrier 1836).

characteristic_fr:
La baie VendĂ´me (ou baie dâAldinga), situĂŠe Ă  courte distance au sud dâAdĂŠlaĂŻde, est bordĂŠe de plages sableuses et dâescarpements abrupts qui sâouvrent, vers lâintĂŠrieur des terres, sur les riches plaines fertiles du bassin de Willunga. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ la dĂŠcrivit comme une baie ÂŤ de faible profondeur Âť.
Le toponyme VendĂ´me renvoie Ă  la lignĂŠe des ducs de VendĂ´me, eux-mĂŞmes descendants dâ$Henri de Bourbon$Henri_IV_(roi_de_France)$, le roi $Henri IV$Henri_IV_(roi_de_France)$ (1553â1610), et de sa maĂŽtresse, Gabrielle dâEstrĂŠes.
characteristic:
Baie VendĂ´me or Aldinga Bay, a short distance to the south of Adelaide, is fringed by sandy beaches and steep escarpments which open inland to the fertile plains of the Willunga Basin. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ described the bay as âshallowâ.
VendĂ´me was the family name of the Dukes of VendĂ´me, who were descendants of $Henri de Bourbon$Henry_IV_of_France$, King $Henri IV$Henry_IV_of_France$ (1553â1610), and his mistress, Gabrielle d'EstrĂŠes.
history_fr: La place VendĂ´me, ĂŠdifiĂŠe Ă  Paris sous le rĂ¨gne de $Louis XIV$Louis_XIV$, fut nommĂŠe en lâhonneur de cette lignĂŠe. Ă lâĂŠpoque napolĂŠonienne, elle abritait lâatelier de Marie-Ătienne Nitot (aujourdâhui la maison Chaumet), qui devint le joaillier officiel de lâempereur. Nitot conĂ§ut et rĂŠalisa les bijoux de mariage de $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$, ainsi que la couronne du sacre de NapolĂŠon, des joyaux et des diadĂ¨mes pour les trois sĹurs de lâempereur, sa belle-fille Hortense et sa mĂ¨re Laetitia (principalement des colliers). Peut-ĂŞtre est-ce la raison pour laquelle le nom de VendĂ´me figure dans le golfe dĂŠdiĂŠ Ă  $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$, Ă  proximitĂŠ de la baie Hortense et du cap Laetitia. NapolĂŠon fit ĂŠlever au centre de la place une haute colonne surmontĂŠe de sa statue.
history: The Place VendĂ´me, laid out in Paris during the reign of $Louis XIV$Louis_XIV$, was named in honor of this lineage. During the Napoleonic era, it housed the workshop of Marie-Ătienne Nitot (now the House of Chaumet), who became the emperorâs official jeweler. Nitot designed and crafted $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$âs wedding jewelry, as well as Napoleonâs coronation crown, jewels, and tiaras for the emperorâs three sisters, his stepdaughter Hortense, and his mother Laetitia (primarily necklaces). Maybe this is why the name VendĂ´me appears in the gulf dedicated to $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$ and next to Baie Hortense and Cap Laetitia. Napoleon had a tall column erected at the center of the square, topped with his statue.
origin_fr: Colonne VendĂ´me. ĂrigĂŠe sur ordre de NapolĂŠon Ier entre 1806 et 1810 pour commĂŠmorer la bataille d'Austerlitz. Le joaillier Nitot, fondateur de Chaumet et joaillier de l'impĂŠratrice JosĂŠphine, ĂŠtait installĂŠ place VendĂ´me.

characteristic_fr:
La presquâĂŽle Fleurieu se situe au sud dâAdĂŠlaĂŻde, capitale de lâĂtat dâAustralie-MĂŠridionale. Ses cĂ´tes orientales dĂŠlimitent la baie Rencontre (Encounter Bay*), tandis que ses rivages mĂŠridionaux sâavancent vers lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ orientale de lâĂŽle Kangourou (Ăle DecrĂ¨s*), et ses cĂ´tes occidentales bordent le golfe Saint-Vincent (Golfe $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$*).
Cette presquâĂŽle, que $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ comparait Ă  la botte de lâItalie, fut dĂŠdiĂŠe Ă  $Charles-Pierre Claret$Charles_Pierre_Claret_de_Fleurieu$ de Fleurieu (1738-1810), officier de marine expĂŠrimentĂŠ, ĂŠminent spĂŠcialiste des sciences nautiques, de lâhistoire maritime, de la cartographie et de la gĂŠographie des ocĂŠans. Il commanda la frĂŠgate Isis lors dâune expĂŠdition dâune annĂŠe destinĂŠe Ă  tester un nouveau chronomĂ¨tre marin, conĂ§u par $Ferdinand Berthoud$Ferdinand_Berthoud$*, pour le calcul de la longitude en mer. Il rĂŠdigea ĂŠgalement les instructions fournies Ă  $La PĂŠrouse$Jean-FranĂ§ois_de_Galaup$, dâEntrecasteaux et Baudin pour leurs expĂŠditions respectives.
characteristic:
The Fleurieu Peninsula is located to the south of Adelaide, the state capital of South Australia. Its east coasts form the limits of Encounter Bay*, its south coasts projects towards the eastern end of Kangaroo Island (Ăle DecrĂ¨s*), and its west coasts borders the Gulf St Vincent (Golfe $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$*).
This peninsula, compared by $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ to the boot of Italy, was dedicated to $Charles-Pierre Claret$Charles_Pierre_Claret_de_Fleurieu$ de Fleurieu (1738-1810), who was an experienced Navy officer expert in the nautical sciences, maritime history, cartography, and the geography of the oceans. He commanded the Frigate Isis on a year-long expedition to test a new marine chronometer developed by $Ferdinand Berthoud$Ferdinand_Berthoud$* to calculate longitude at sea. He wrote the instructions provided to $La PĂŠrouse$FRJean-FranĂ§ois_de_Galaup$, dâEntrecasteaux and Baudin for their respective expeditions
history_fr:
Contrairement aux missions prĂŠcĂŠdentes, et afin de rendre les travaux de lâexpĂŠdition Baudin plus efficaces et fructueux, Fleurieu en circonscrivit lâitinĂŠraire Ă  la cartographie des portions encore inconnues des cĂ´tes de ce qui est aujourdâhui lâAustralie (voir lâintroduction).
Ă la suite du retour des savants et officiers des expĂŠditions dâEntrecasteaux et Baudin, Fleurieu continua dâappuyer les rĂŠsultats de ces voyages. En 1803, DecrĂ¨s* lui confia la supervision de lâĂŠdition du rĂŠcit de lâexpĂŠdition dâEntrecasteaux. Puis, en 1806, Fleurieu siĂŠgea, aux cĂ´tĂŠs de Laplace*, $Bougainville$Louis-Antoine_de_Bougainville$*, LacĂŠpĂ¨de* et Cuvier*, au sein de la commission de lâInstitut*, chargĂŠe de rĂŠdiger un rapport en faveur de la publication du rĂŠcit des dĂŠcouvertes de lâexpĂŠdition Baudin. Remis au gouvernement, ce rapport concluait avec enthousiasme ÂŤ que les ouvrages que prĂŠpare [$PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$] doivent contribuer de maniĂ¨re ĂŠclatante aux progrĂ¨s des sciences naturelles Âť. Ce jugement tranchait avec les avis plutĂ´t dĂŠfavorables ĂŠmis par certains scientifiques embarquĂŠs au Havre, qui, confrontĂŠs aux conditions difficiles de la traversĂŠe, avaient abandonnĂŠ lâexpĂŠdition Ă  lâĂŽle de France avant mĂŞme dâen commencer la mission.
$PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ salue pour sa part la ÂŤ rigueur Âť de la pensĂŠe de Fleurieu, tandis que Freycinet rend hommage Ă  son esprit novateur, notamment pour lâimpression de cartes maritimes prĂŠcises Ă  lâaide de planches de cuivre.
history:
In comparison to previous missions, and to make the work of the Baudin expedition more efficient and productive, he limited the itinerary to charting the yet unknown parts of the coasts of what is now known as Australia (see the introduction).
After the return of the savants and officers of the dâEntrecasteaux and the Baudin expeditions, Fleurieu continued to support the findings of these voyages. In 1803, DecrĂ¨s* entrusted him with the task of supervising the edition of the narrative of the dâEntrecasteaux voyage. Then, in 1806, Fleurieu was, together with Laplace*, $Bougainville$Louis_Antoine_de_Bougainville$*, LacĂŠpĂ¨de* and Cuvier*, part of the commission of the Institut* who wrote a report in favour of the publication of the narrative detailing the findings from Baudinâs voyage. Delivered to the government, this report concluded encouragingly âthat the works which he [$PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$] is preparing must contribute splendidly to the progress of the natural sciencesâ.This judgment contrasted with the rather negative opinions expressed by some scientists who had embarked in Le Havre but, faced with the difficult conditions of the voyage, abandoned the expedition in Isle de France before their mission even began.. 
$PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ acknowledges the ârigorousnessâ of Fleurieuâs thoughts, and Freycinet salutes Fleurieuâs innovative thinking to print with copper plates fair charts.

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Snapper marque lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ septentrionale de la baie dâAldinga, au sud dâAdĂŠlaĂŻde. Dans les eaux peu profondes qui entourent le promontoire, un rĂŠcif composĂŠ de formations vĂŠgĂŠtales et animales â dĂŠsignĂŠ sous le nom de Aldinga Reef Aquatic Reserve â constitue le fond marin. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ observa que le cap dĂŠlimitait le cĂ´tĂŠ nord de la baie VendĂ´me, aujourdâhui connue sous le nom dâAldinga Bay.*
$PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ et Freycinet donnĂ¨rent au cap le nom dâune hĂŠroĂŻne franĂ§aise qui, en 1472, aurait empĂŞchĂŠ la prise de Beauvais, sa ville natale, demeurĂŠe fidĂ¨le au roi Louis XI, par les troupes du duc de Bourgogne.
characteristic: Snapper Point marks the northern extent of Aldinga Bay, to the south of Adelaide. In the shallow waters around the headland a reef of aquatic plants and animals, designated the Aldinga Reef Aquatic Reserve, forms the sea floor. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ noted that the cape encloses the northern side of baie VendĂ´me, now Aldinga Bay*. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ and Freycinet named the cape after a French heroine who, in 1472, is said to have prevented the capture of Beauvais, her hometown, loyal to King Louis XI, by the troops of the Duke of Burgundy.
history_fr: ArmĂŠe pour tout viatique dâune simple hachette â dâoĂš son surnom de $Jeanne Hachette$Jeanne_Hachette$ â elle se prĂŠcipita contre le premier des assaillants parvenus au sommet du rempart et le prĂŠcipita, avec lâĂŠtendard quâil venait dây planter, dans les douves. Son geste ranima le courage dâune garnison affaiblie et permit de sauver la ville. En reconnaissance de cet acte hĂŠroĂŻque, le roi institua la tenue dâune procession annuelle. En 1806, NapolĂŠon rĂŠtablit lâantique cĂŠlĂŠbration de la levĂŠe du siĂ¨ge de Beauvais, qui nâavait plus ĂŠtĂŠ observĂŠe depuis prĂ¨s de deux siĂ¨cles, et au cours de laquelle le nom de $Jeanne Hachette$Jeanne_Hachette$ servait dâexemple emblĂŠmatique de la grande mobilisation des femmes franĂ§aises face aux forces ennemies (au premier rang desquelles les Britanniques !).
history: Armed only with a hatchet, hence her nickname, $Jeanne Hachette$Jeanne_Hachette$, she attacked the first of the enemy soldiers to reach the top of the battlement and flung him, and the flag he had planted there, into the moat. Her action revived the courage of the undermanned garrison and saved the city. In recognition of her heroic deed, the king ordered the enactment of an annual procession. In 1806, NapolĂŠon re-instituted the ancient celebration of the lifting of the siege of Beauvais, that had not been observed for some two centuries, in which the name $Jeanne Hachette$Jeanne_Hachette$ served as an example that symbolised the great mobilisation of French women against enemy forces (especially the British!).
origin_fr: Jeanne Hachette, la guerriĂ¨re A-t-elle vraiment existĂŠ ? Ou n'est-elle qu'une invention symbolique pour saluer l'action des femmes dans l'Histoire ? Dans tous les cas, Ă  Beauvais, le 22 juillet 1472, une certaine Jeanne Hachette met en dĂŠroute l'armĂŠe de Charles le TĂŠmĂŠraire.

characteristic_fr:
Le cap StĂŠphanie constitue un promontoire ĂŠlevĂŠ situĂŠ Ă  proximitĂŠ des faubourgs maritimes dâAdĂŠlaĂŻde, Christies Beach et Port Noarlunga. Il forme un escarpement bordĂŠ de falaises attenantes, soumises Ă  une ĂŠrosion active et qui ont rĂŠcemment nĂŠcessitĂŠ des mesures de protection (notamment la mise en place dâimportants enrochements). Freycinet dĂŠcrivait cette portion du littoral comme ĂŠtant ÂŤcoupĂŠe Ă  pic Âť.
$StĂŠphanie Louise Adrienne$StĂŠphanie_de_Beauharnais$ $de Beauharnais$Alexandre_de_Beauharnais$ ĂŠtait la niĂ¨ce de $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$ par le premier mariage de celle-ci avec le comte $Alexandre de Beauharnais$Alexandre_de_Beauharnais$.
characteristic:
Cap StĂŠphanie forms an elevated headland situated near the Adelaide seaside suburbs of Christies Beach and Port Noarlunga. It forms a bluff with adjacent cliffs that have been subject to active erosion and have recently require protective measures (installation of extensive
rock protection). Freycinet described the coast as âcut off sharplyâ.
$StĂŠphanie Louise Adrienne$StĂŠphanie_de_Beauharnais$ $de Beauharnais$Alexandre_de_Beauharnais$ was $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$âs niece by $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$âs first marriage with the count $Alexandre de Beauharnais$Alexandre_de_Beauharnais$.
history_fr: $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$ envoie StĂŠphanie au pensionnat de Mme Campan (voir le glossaire) pour y poursuivre son ĂŠducation, oĂš ĂŠtudiaient ĂŠgalement les autres jeunes filles des familles de $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$ et de $Bonaparte$Caroline_Bonaparte$. ConformĂŠment Ă  lâambition de NapolĂŠon dâĂŠlever sa famille au rang dâune dynastie rĂŠgnante, lâempereur adopte StĂŠphanie en 1806 et la marie au futur grand-duc de Bade. Hortense ĂŠcrit dans ses MĂŠmoires : ÂŤ Caroline [soeur de l'empereur] fut si contrariĂŠe par cette ĂŠlĂŠvation que, dans les grands cercles oĂš le nouveau rang de ma cousine [StĂŠphanie] la plaĂ§ait Ă  ses cĂ´tĂŠs, elle lui tournait le dos et affectait de ne pas lui adresser la parole. Âť
history: $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$ sent StĂŠphanie to Mme Campanâs boarding school (see the glossary) for further education, which the other young ladies of $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$ and Bonaparteâs families also attended. In line with NapolĂŠonâs ambition of raising his family to the rank of a reigning dynasty, the emperor adopted StĂŠphanie in 1806 and married her to the future grand duke of Bade. Hortense wrote in her memoires: âCaroline [one of the emperor's sisters] was so upset at this elevation that in the big circles in which my cousinâs [Stephanie] new ranking has placed her side by side with her [Caroline], she was turning her back on her affecting not to speak to herâ.
origin_fr: StĂŠphanie Louise Adrienne de Beauharnais, princesse franĂ§aise et par mariage, grande-duchesse de Bade, est nĂŠe Ă  Versailles, en France, le 28 aoĂťt 1789 et morte Ă  Nice, dans le royaume de Sardaigne, le 29 janvier 1860. Fille du comte Claude de Beauharnais, parent par alliance de l'impĂŠratrice JosĂŠphine, elle est adoptĂŠe, en 1806, par NapolĂŠon Ier, qui en fait ainsi une princesse impĂŠriale franĂ§aise. MariĂŠe au futur grand-duc Charles II de Bade, elle est grande-duchesse de Bade de 1811 Ă  1818.

characteristic_fr:
Le site prĂŠsente des promontoires rocheux dominant une plage plate et pierreuse, situĂŠe Ă  environ treize kilomĂ¨tres au sud dâAdĂŠlaĂŻde. Le cap porte aujourdâhui le nom de Marino Rocks. Ă proximitĂŠ des rochers de Marino, le Marino Conservation Park protĂ¨ge dĂŠsormais les derniers reliquats de la vĂŠgĂŠtation de lande cĂ´tiĂ¨re.
Le nom de Cap Nemours pourrait renvoyer Ă  Marie dâOrlĂŠans, duchesse de Nemours (1625-1707), femme de lettres du XVIIáľ siĂ¨cle, reconnue pour son esprit, sa vivacitĂŠ dâanalyse et son regard critique sur la noblesse franĂ§aise Ă  laquelle elle appartenait par naissance.
Une autre hypothĂ¨se â dâautant plus plausible que le cap voisin porte le nom de Cap Lafayette â est que cette dĂŠnomination fasse rĂŠfĂŠrence au principal protagoniste du cĂŠlĂ¨bre roman de $Madame de La Fayette$Madame_de_La_Fayette$, La Princesse de ClĂ¨ves : le duc de Nemours, ĂŠpris avec passion de la princesse dĂŠjĂ  mariĂŠe.
characteristic:
The place presents cliff tops that dominate a flat rocky beach at about 13km south of Adelaide. The cap is now named Marino rocks. Next to the Marino rocks, the Marino Conservation Park now protects the remaining stands of coastal heath vegetation.
The name Cap Nemours may refer to Marie d'OrlĂŠans, duchess of Nemours (1625-1707), who was a seventeenth century woman of letter with a sense of wit and critical spirit regarding French nobility to which she belonged by birth.
Alternatively, and considering that the next cap is named Cap Lafayette, it may refer to the main character of $Madame de La Fayette$Madame_de_La_Fayette$*âs most notorious novel, la princesse de ClĂ¨ve, the duc de Nemours, who falls passionately in love with the already married princess.
history_fr: Dans ses mĂŠmoires, Marie d'OrlĂŠans dĂŠcrit la Fronde et ses figures clĂŠs avec un rĂŠalisme sans concession et une grande perspicacitĂŠ. Cette pĂŠriode charniĂ¨re de dissidence et de guerre civile en France, qui dura de 1648 Ă  1653, remit en question l'avenir de la monarchie et menaĂ§a l'unitĂŠ nationale. Dans son texte, elle souligne le caractĂ¨re parfois ridicule de certaines figures de la Fronde, notamment le comte de Beaufort, dĂŠsireux dâĂŠvincer un autre ÂŤ Frondeur Âť, Monsieur dâElbeuf. Elle rapporte ainsi lâĂŠpisode suivant : "M. de Beaufort crut avoir trouvĂŠ un moyen infaillible quâil proposa Ă  M. de BelliĂ¨vre en lui demandant par maniĂ¨re dâavis si, en donnant un soufflet Ă  M. dâElbeuf, il ne changerait point la face des affaires ; Ă  quoi M. de BelliĂ¨vre rĂŠpondit, dâun sang-froid plus digne de sa gravitĂŠ que de la question, quâil ne croyait pas que cela pĂťt changer autre chose que la face de M. dâElbeuf".
history:
In her memoirs, Marie d'OrlĂŠans describes $The Fronde$The_Fronde$ and its key figures with uncompromising realism and keen insight. This pivotal period of dissent and civil war in France, lasting from 1648 to 1653, cast doubt on the future of the monarchy and threatened national unity. 
In her text, she underscores the occasionally ludicrous behaviour of certain figures of the Fronde, notably the Duke of Beaufort, who sought to remove another âFrondeur,â Monsieur dâElbeuf. She recounts the following episode: âM. de Beaufort believed he had found an infallible solution, which he submitted to M. de BelliĂ¨vre, asking him, as a simple matter of advice, whether slapping M. dâElbeuf would not alter the course of affairs. Prototype du roman psychologique, La Princesse de ClĂ¨ves est ĂŠgalement un roman historique. history:
A prototype of the psychological analysis novel, La Princesse de ClĂ¨ves is also a historical novel. Nourished by the rituals of the Court of both Henry II and $Louis XIV$Louis_XIV$, tinged with Jansenism (movement that attempt to reconcile the theological concepts of free will and God) and a literary style free of exaggeration or incivility, the novel is still part of the French school curriculum.
Madame de La Fayette knew La Rochefoucauld, Racine and Boileau, was very close to $Madame de SĂŠvignĂŠ$Marie_de_Rabutin-Chantal,_marquise_de_SĂŠvignĂŠ$, and frequented the salons of $Madame de ScudĂŠry$FRMarie-Madeleine_de_ScudĂŠry$.

Elle ĂŠcrit : ÂŤ M. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$, dĂ¨s ce jour, gagna ma plus sincĂ¨re estime ; dĂ¨s ce premier moment, il sâattacha Ă  moi. Âť
Ă propos de la femme aimĂŠe de $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$, elle note : ÂŤ Elle venait dâĂŞtre mariĂŠe lorsque son amant [$PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$] revint pour lui vouer sa vie. history:
$Victorine de Chastenay$Victorine_Chastenay$ met $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$, together with Lesueur and Bonpland, at a dinner hosted by M. de Courbeton, the brother of one of her friends, who regularly welcomed artists and travellers into his private salon. In her MĂŠmoires, Victorine recounts this encounter, during which $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ confided to her the story of his thwarted love and assisted her in understanding the nature of her fatherâs illness: âFrom that day, M. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ won my most sincere esteem; from that first day he became attached to me.â She relates how he spoke to her of the woman he had loved: âShe had just been married when her lover [$PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$] returned to devote his life to her. She died shortly afterward in childbirth. Science and fame undoubtedly consoled $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$, but he never ceased to cherish his tender memories: he never saw me more than three times without feeling the need to confide in me, and he often returned to this subject in our conversations.â
Victorine adds: âLearned in theory, $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ was of great help in reasoning with my father about his ailments, possible treatments, and the difficult decisions of his slow convalescence...!â

history_fr:
$Madeleine de ScudĂŠry$Marie-Madeleine_de_ScudĂŠry$, passionnĂŠe par les jardins ornĂŠs dâarbres et de fleurs exotiques, sâinscrivait dans une tradition horticole que les jardiniers sâefforĂ§aient dâacclimater dans les parcs des chĂ˘teaux, anticipant ainsi, prĂ¨s dâun siĂ¨cle plus tard, les initiatives de lâimpĂŠratrice $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$ Ă  Malmaison.

Dans ClĂŠlie, histoire romaine (1654â1660), ScudĂŠry dĂŠveloppe une idĂŠe profondĂŠment subversive pour le XVIIe siĂ¨cle, ĂŠpoque oĂš les femmes ĂŠtaient tenues au silence et Ă  la rĂŠserve quant Ă  lâexpression de leurs sentiments. Cette Ĺuvre, comme dâautres, tĂŠmoigne de son engagement en faveur dâune ĂŠmancipation fĂŠminine par lâĂŠcriture.
LâexpĂŠdition dâĂgypte menĂŠe par $Bonaparte$Caroline_Bonaparte$ (1798â1801) aurait pu raviver lâintĂŠrĂŞt pour un autre de ses romans, ArtamĂ¨ne ou le Grand Cyrus (1649â1653), dont lâintrigue se dĂŠroule dans lâAssyrie antique. Ce monument littĂŠraire, le plus long roman jamais ĂŠcrit en langue franĂ§aise â en dix tomes â, illustre lâambition encyclopĂŠdique $de ScudĂŠry$Marie-Madeleine_de_ScudĂŠry$, mĂŞlant histoire, gĂŠographie et fiction pour captiver un public avide de dĂŠpaysement et dâĂŠrudition.
history: $Madeleine de ScudĂŠry$FRMarie-Madeleine_de_ScudĂŠry$ was fond of gardens planted with exotic trees and flowers, which gardeners tried to acclimatise in the parks of castles as did the empress $JosĂŠphine$JosĂŠphine_de_Beauharnais$ a century later. The heroines of ScudĂŠry's novels challenge the amorous casuistry: In her novel âClĂŠlie, histoire romaineâ, ScudĂŠry presented a remarkably subversive idea for her time, when women were expected to remain silent and refrain from speaking about their feelings. $Pointe Pauline$Pauline_Bonaparte$ is a headland located on the eastern coast of Gulf St Vincent, approximately 70â80 km north of Adelaide. This promontory extends from a low-lying coastal plain characterized by sandy beaches, fronted by intertidal mudflats roughly 2 km wide.

history_fr:
NapolĂŠon organisa le premier mariage de $Pauline$Pauline_Bonaparte$, qui ĂŠpousa le gĂŠnĂŠral Victor-Emmanuel $Leclerc$Georges-Louis_Leclerc_de_Buffon$ en 1797 ; en 1801, il nomma $Leclerc$Georges-Louis_Leclerc_de_Buffon$ commandant de lâexpĂŠdition de Saint-Domingue. Cette campagne militaire avait pour objectif de rĂŠprimer lâinsurrection sur lâĂŽle, visant en rĂŠalitĂŠ Ă  rĂŠtablir lâesclavage en ĂŠcrasant le soulĂ¨vement autonome des Noirs menĂŠ par Toussaint Louverture. $Leclerc$Georges-Louis_Leclerc_de_Buffon$ succomba peu aprĂ¨s Ă  la fiĂ¨vre jaune sur place.
NapolĂŠon exploita publiquement le deuil de $Pauline$Pauline_Bonaparte$ânotamment son geste symbolique de couper ses cheveux pour les dĂŠposer dans le cercueil de son ĂŠpouxâĂ  des fins politiques. En imposant un deuil officiel dans toutes les cours europĂŠennes, il affirma sa suprĂŠmatie sur les monarchies du continent et renforĂ§a la prĂŠĂŠminence de la France parmi les Ătats europĂŠens.
history:
Napoleon arranged the first marriage of $Pauline$Pauline_Bonaparte$, who married General Victor-Emmanuel $Leclerc$Georges-Louis_Leclerc,_Comte_de_Buffon$ in 1797; in 1801 he appointed $Leclerc$Georges-Louis_Leclerc,_Comte_de_Buffon$ commander of the Saint-Domingue expedition. This military campaign aimed to suppress the insurrection on the islandâeffectively seeking to restore slavery by crushing the autonomous Black uprising led by Toussaint Louverture. $Leclerc$Georges-Louis_Leclerc,_Comte_de_Buffon$ died of yellow fever on the island shortly thereafter.
Napoleon exploited $Pauline$Pauline_Bonaparte$âs public display of griefâmost notably her cutting of her hair to place it in her husbandâs coffinâas a political instrument. He ordered all European courts to observe mourning, thereby asserting his dominance over the continentâs monarchies and reinforcing Franceâs preeminence among European states.

history_fr:
Dans lâĂŠvocation dĂŠlicate du monde pastoral, les idylles de DeshouliĂ¨res consacrĂŠes aux oiseaux, aux moutons ou Ă  lâhiver se rĂŠvĂ¨lent autant de prĂŠtextes pour critiquer la mentalitĂŠ humaine. Elles dĂŠmontrent avec finesse que lâĂŠgoĂŻsme des ĂŠmotions et des impulsions humaines, quâil sâexerce de maniĂ¨re directe ou insidieuse, gouverne trop souvent lâintelligence de lâhomme et rend son existence misĂŠrable, Ă  lâinverse de celle des animaux. Ainsi, son poĂ¨me dĂŠdiĂŠ aux moutons, qui sâouvre sur ce vers mĂŠlancolique : ÂŤ HĂŠlas, petit mouton, que tu es heureuxâŚ Âť et sâachĂ¨ve par ÂŤ Tu es plus heureux et plus sage que nous Âť, a su conquĂŠrir une place privilĂŠgiĂŠe dans le cĹur des femmes lettrĂŠes.
En 1798, son nom fit son entrĂŠe dans le Dictionnaire de lâAcadĂŠmie franĂ§aise, dont la cinquiĂ¨me ĂŠdition illustra lâusage des termes poĂ¨te et poĂŠtesse en citant, aux cĂ´tĂŠs de Sappho, lâexemple mĂŞme dâAntoinette DeshouliĂ¨res, consĂŠcration dâune plume aussi subtile quâaudacieuse.
history:
In the context of the sweet evocation of the pastoral world, DeshouliĂ¨reâs idylls on birds, sheep, or winter, are all means of criticising human mentality and demonstrates that the egotism of human emotions and impulses, is directly or indirectly ruling human intelligence and can very often make human life miserable, unlike that of animals. Hence, her poem on sheep, which starts with the line âalas, little sheep, how happy are you âŚâ and finishes with âyou are happier and cleverer than usâ, has remained prized among literate women.
In 1798, she made her way into the dictionary of the AcadĂŠmie FranĂ§aise, whose fifth edition clarified the usage of poĂ¨te and poĂŠtesse by referring to Sappho and DeshouliĂ¨res.

characteristic:
Pointe Dorothee refers to a small headland to the north of Port Clinton on the low-lying western coast of the Gulf of St Vincent. Freycinet describes pointe DorothĂŠe as low and protruding (p.128). 

The identity of the person after whom Pointe DorothĂŠe was named is uncertain. It may refer to the name given to $Emilie du ChĂ˘telet$Ămilie_du_ChĂ˘telet$ (1706-1749, also spelt Chastellet), who was one of the most remarkable women of the Enlightenment,.a philosopher with a highly critical mind and a great mathematician, by her childhood friend with $FranĂ§oise de Graffigny$FranĂ§oise_de_Graffigny$ (1695-1758), herself a woman of letters (see Pointe Graffigny). Graffigny was accused, perhaps incorrectly, of sending to $Devaux$FranĂ§ois-Antoine_Devaux$ some unpublished writings from Voltaire and get into trouble for including in her letters many biting mockeries about Emilie $du ChĂ˘telet$Ămilie_du_ChĂ˘telet$ and Voltaire. [Voltaire] and DorotĂŠe for her shepherdess [Mme Du ChĂ˘telet]â (letter to $Devaux$FranĂ§ois-Antoine_Devaux$, 15 December 1738, âafter supperâ. DorotĂŠe is spelt without a h in the letter).

history:
As Josephine, AdĂŠlaĂŻde de La Rochefoucauld was born in the Antilles (West Indies). She was related to Josephine first husband, and cousin of Josephineâs children. Through her marriage with Alexandre FranĂ§ois de La Rochefoucauld, she was part of the oldest and most established French noble families. It is on these dynastic and aristocratic bases that Napoleon named AdĂŠlaĂŻde de La Rochefoucauld Josephineâs first lady in waiting, although Josephine would have preferred her friend who share the same cell during the French Revolution, the duchess of Aiguillon (Mme de Girardin). Napoleonâs choice was framed by his desire to graft the Bonaparte imperial court on to the most prestigious and ancient noble dynasties.
AdĂŠlaĂŻde de La Rochefoucauld was described as little and a bit humpbacked, but harsh, and it is said that Napoleon âexperienced some embarrassment with her, as she answered sharply to himâ.

characteristic_fr:
Aujourdâhui, Two Hummock Point jouxte la rĂŠserve aquatique de Blanche Harbor-Douglas Bank, qui protĂ¨ge les mangroves et les herbiers marins locaux, ainsi que les zones de nurserie pour les poissons qui leur sont associĂŠes. Les FranĂ§ais le dĂŠfinirent comme un cap, et PĂŠron le nota avec prĂŠcision : ÂŤ Ă midi, le 22 [janvier 1803], ils [Freycinet et lâĂŠquipage du Casuarina] atteignirent le flanc dâun grand cap, situĂŠ par 32Â° 43' de latitude, auquel on donna le nom de cap Racine Âť (tome 2, p. 92).
Jean-Baptiste Racine (1639-1699) est lâun des trois grands dramaturges franĂ§ais du XVIIe siĂ¨cle, aux cĂ´tĂŠs de Corneille* et de MoliĂ¨re. Il fut admirĂŠ sous NapolĂŠon comme lâun des sommets de la littĂŠrature franĂ§aise et comme un modĂ¨le dâĂŠlĂŠgance classique.
characteristic:
Today, Two Hummock Point adjoins the Blanche Harbor-Douglas Bank Aquatic Reserve, which protects the local mangrove and seagrass communities, as well as the associated fish nursery areas. The French described it as a cape, and PĂŠron recorded it precisely: âAt noon on the 22nd [January 1803], they [Freycinet and the crew of the Casuarina] came alongside a large cape, situated at latitude 32Â° 43', which was named Cape Racineâ (volume 2, p. 92).

Jean-Baptiste Racine (1639â1699) was one of the three major French playwrights of the seventeenth century, alongside Corneille* and MoliĂ¨re. Under Napoleon, he was admired as one of the summits of French literature and as a model of classical elegance.
history_fr:
Le nom de Racine sâest imposĂŠ dans le golfe Bonaparte au mĂŞme titre que celui de Corneille, dans la mesure oĂš, Ă  lâĂŠpoque du Consulat puis de lâEmpire, Racine demeurait un pilier du rĂŠpertoire classique franĂ§ais. Ses grandes tragĂŠdies continuaient dâĂŞtre jouĂŠes Ă  la ComĂŠdie-FranĂ§aise et dans les thĂŠĂ˘tres impĂŠriaux.
NapolĂŠon Bonaparte respectait Racine parce quâil incarnait la perfection littĂŠraire franĂ§aise : la puretĂŠ de la langue, lâĂŠlĂŠgance du vers, la grandeur du XVIIe siĂ¨cle et le prestige du classicisme. Une ĂŠdition de luxe de ses Ĺuvres fut publiĂŠe chez Pierre Didot Ă  partir de 1801 ; cette entreprise sâinscrivait dans une logique de prestige culturel et national, en prĂŠsentant Racine comme lâun des joyaux de la tradition franĂ§aise.
history:
The name Racine established itself in Bonaparte Gulf in the same way as that of Corneille, insofar as, during the Consulate and then the Empire, Racine remained a pillar of the French classical repertoire. His great tragedies continued to be performed at the ComĂŠdie-FranĂ§aise and in the imperial theatres.
Napoleon Bonaparte respected Racine because he embodied French literary perfection: the purity of the language, the elegance of the verse, the grandeur of the seventeenth century, and the prestige of classicism. A luxury edition of his works was published by Pierre Didot from 1801 onwards; this undertaking formed part of a broader logic of cultural and national prestige, presenting Racine as one of the jewels of the French tradition.

characteristic_fr:
False Bay est une baie orientĂŠe vers le sud-sud-est, large dâenviron 11 kilomĂ¨tres, situĂŠe dans la partie septentrionale du golfe Spencer, en Australie-MĂŠridionale. Elle fut nommĂŠe par les FranĂ§ais en lâhonneur de Pierre Corneille, dramaturge franĂ§ais du XVIIe siĂ¨cle, qui renouvela la comĂŠdie et transforma profondĂŠment lâart de la tragĂŠdie. Voir aussi Ile Corneille.
characteristic:
False Bay is a south-south-east-facing bay, about 11 kilometres (6.8 miles) wide, in the upper Spencer Gulf region of South Australia. It is characterised by extensive tidal sand and salt flats of very gentle gradient, reaching up to 7 kilometres (4.3 miles) in width.
It was named by the French in honour of Pierre Corneille, the seventeenth-century French playwright who renewed comedy and profoundly transformed the art of tragedy. See also Ile Corneille
history_fr: Ă lâĂŠpoque napolĂŠonienne, et dĂ¨s le Consulat, Pierre Corneille incarnait une image particuliĂ¨rement forte de la grandeur franĂ§aise : celle dâun thĂŠĂ˘tre classique, hĂŠroĂŻque et volontiers romain, fondĂŠ sur lâhonneur, le devoir, la raison dâĂtat et la maĂŽtrise de soi. Si Racine ĂŠtait admirĂŠ comme lâun des sommets de la littĂŠrature franĂ§aise, Corneille ĂŠtait, pour NapolĂŠon Bonaparte, le dramaturge par excellence du pouvoir.
history: In the Napoleonic period, and already under the Consulate, Pierre Corneille embodied a particularly powerful image of French greatness: that of a classical, heroic, and often Roman theatre, founded on honour, duty, reason of state, and self-mastery. While Racine was admired as one of the high points of French literature, Corneille was more particularly favoured by NapolĂŠon Bonaparte as a dramatist of power.

Condillac's conception of a âscience of ideasâ became one of their principal sources of inspiration.

characteristic_fr:
Le Knob, nommĂŠ cap Mably par les FranĂ§ais, se situe Ă  lâentrĂŠe de Franklin Harbor, vaste baie abritĂŠe. Autrefois plus largement ouverte sur la mer, celle-ci vit peu Ă  peu son ouverture se resserrer Ă  mesure que le sable et les galets sâaccumulaient le long du rivage. Le promontoire conserve une sĂŠrie dâanciens cordons de plage, formĂŠs par lâaction des tempĂŞtes, qui montrent comment le littoral a lentement avancĂŠ et changĂŠ de forme au fil du temps. Ces traits en font un tĂŠmoin important de lâhistoire cĂ´tiĂ¨re du havre.
Les FranĂ§ais donnĂ¨rent Ă  ce lieu le nom de cap Mably, en lâhonneur de Gabriel Bonnot de Mably (1709-1785), ĂŠcrivain et philosophe des LumiĂ¨res, frĂ¨re de Condillac (voir cap Condillac).
characteristic:
The Knob, named  Cap Mably by the French, stands at the edge of Franklin Harbor, a broad sheltered inlet. In earlier times, this was a more open bay, but over a long period sand and shingle gradually built out from the shore, narrowing its entrance. The headland preserves a series of old beach ridges, formed by storm waves, which show how the coastline slowly advanced and changed shape through time. These features make the site an important witness to the coastal history of the harbour. 
It was named by the French Cap Mably, after Gabriel Bonnot de Mably (1709  -1785),  a writer and philosopher of the Enlightenment, brother of Condillac. (see Cap Condillac).
history_fr: Dans ses ĂŠcrits, Mably dĂŠnonĂ§ait ce quâil appelait le ÂŤ despotisme lĂŠgal Âť et portait un regard critique sur le systĂ¨me politique anglais. Ses rĂŠflexions sur Sparte influencĂ¨rent le jeune Bonaparte, qui associait la GrĂ¨ce antique Ă  sa Corse natale et cherchait dans les citĂŠs grecques des modĂ¨les de gouvernement. Toutefois, Ă  mesure quâil sâĂŠleva vers le pouvoir, NapolĂŠon Bonaparte sâĂŠloigna en grande partie de ces premiĂ¨res influences intellectuelles, ou les laissa tomber dans lâoubli.
history: In his writings, Mably denounced what he called âlegal despotismâ and offered a critical reading of the English political system. His reflections on Sparta are known to have influenced the young Bonaparte, who associated ancient Greece with his native Corsica and looked to the Greek city-states for models of government. As he advanced towards power, however, NapolĂŠon Bonaparte largely set aside, or forgot, these early intellectual influences.

characteristic_fr:

characteristic:
Laplace Bay appears on maps to the north of Port Neill, on the eastern coast of Eyre Peninsula. On present-day mapping, it would fall within part of Dutton Bay; its position in Freycinetâs atlas, however, suggests that Laplace Bay corresponds more closely to modern Tumby Bay. Freycinet in fact placed it farther south, along a sector of coast that is deeply indented and strongly controlled by bedrock outcrops.

history_fr:
PĂŠron note, dâaprĂ¨s les informations recueillies auprĂ¨s de Freycinet, quâÂŤ Ă  mesure quâon se rapproche de lâentrĂŠe du golfe [Spencer Gulf], sa largeur augmente rapidement ; elle est dĂŠjĂ  de 60 milles Ă  la hauteur de la baie Laplace, dont le milieu se trouve placĂŠ par 34Â° 15' de latitude, et par 134Â° de longitude orientale [du mĂŠridien de Paris]. Cette baie a prĂ¨s de trois lieues dâouverture, et se termine au Nord par le cap MĂŠchain, au Sud par la pointe Lacaille, vis-Ă -vis de laquelle est la petite ĂŽle dâAlembert Âť.
Bonaparte admirait Laplace comme savant. Soucieux dâassocier le prestige de la science au gouvernement du pays, il le nomma ministre de lâIntĂŠrieur en 1799. Laplace ne demeura toutefois en fonctions que six semaines, Bonaparte le jugeant trop portĂŠ vers les abstractions thĂŠoriques. Si NapolĂŠon dira plus tard, non sans mordant, que Laplace ÂŤ portait lâesprit de lâinfiniment petit dans lâadministration Âť, il continua nĂŠanmoins Ă  lâhonorer et Ă  le soutenir. La prĂŠsence de son nom dans le golfe Bonaparte tĂŠmoigne ainsi de lâestime durable accordĂŠe par le rĂŠgime Ă  lâune des plus grandes figures savantes de son temps.
history:
PĂŠron notes, on the basis of information supplied by Freycinet, that âas one approaches the entrance to the gulf [Spencer Gulf], its width increases rapidly; it is already 60 miles across at the latitude of Laplace Bay, the centre of which lies at 34Â° 15' latitude and 134Â° east longitude [from the Paris meridian]. This bay is nearly three leagues wide at its entrance, and is bounded to the north by Cape MĂŠchain and to the south by Point Lacaille, opposite which lies the small Ăle dâAlembert.â
Bonaparte admired Laplace as a man of science. Wishing to associate scientific prestige with the governance of the country, he appointed him Minister of the Interior in 1799. Laplace, however, remained in office for only six weeks, Bonaparte judging him too much inclined towards theoretical abstractions. Although NapolĂŠon later remarked, with some sharpness, that Laplace âcarried the spirit of the infinitely small into administration,â he nonetheless continued to honour and support him. The presence of his name in Bonaparte Gulf thus reflects the lasting esteem accorded by the regime to one of the greatest scientific figures of the age.

characteristic_fr:
La baie Jussieu sâinscrit sur un secteur littoral de haute ĂŠnergie, faĂ§onnĂŠ par la houle ocĂŠanique, les vagues poussĂŠes par le vent et la persistance de vents violents. Ces dynamiques ont engendrĂŠ dâimportants champs transgressifs de dunes paraboliques Ă  lâintĂŠrieur des terres. Les dunes les plus rĂŠcentes ont progressĂŠ dâenviron 2 km vers lâintĂŠrieur, tandis que les systĂ¨mes plus anciens, aujourdâhui stabilisĂŠs par la calcrĂ¨te ou par la vĂŠgĂŠtation, sâĂŠtendent Ă  plus de 5 km du rivage actuel.
La dynastie des Jussieu occupa une place de premier plan parmi les botanistes du Jardin du Roi Ă  Paris, devenu en 1793 le Jardin des Plantes, sur le site duquel fut instituĂŠ, pendant la RĂŠvolution franĂ§aise, le MusĂŠum national dâhistoire naturelle (voir le glossaire). Elle joua un rĂ´le pionnier dans lâĂŠlaboration de nouveaux systĂ¨mes de classification des vĂŠgĂŠtaux, dâabord avec Bernard de Jussieu (1699-1777) au XVIIie siĂ¨cle, puis avec son neveu Antoine-Laurent de Jussieu (1748-1836), qui donna Ă  cette nouvelle classification sa formulation la plus influente Ă  la fin du mĂŞme siĂ¨cle.
characteristic:
Jussieu Bay lies along a high-energy stretch of coast shaped by ocean swell, wind-driven waves, and persistent strong winds. These forces have produced extensive transgressive fields of parabolic dunes inland. The modern dunes have advanced some 2 km inland, whereas older systems, now stabilised by calcrete or vegetation, reach more than 5 km from the present shoreline.
The Jussieu dynasty occupied a leading place among the botanists of the Jardin du Roi in Paris, which became the Jardin des Plantes in 1793 and on whose grounds the National Museum of Natural History (see glossary) was established during the French Revolution. It played a pioneering role in the development of new systems of plant classification, first with Bernard de Jussieu (1699â1777) in the eighteenth century, and then with his nephew Antoine-Laurent de Jussieu (1748â1836), who gave this new classification its most influential formulation at the close of the same century.
history_fr: En donnant ce nom Ă  la baie, PĂŠron place les Jussieu au nombre de ces familles savantes dont les travaux participent tout ensemble au progrĂ¨s des sciences et Ă  la grandeur nationale. Il note ainsi : ÂŤ Au-delĂ  de ce dernier cap [cap GrĂŠcourt], une nouvelle baie se prĂŠsente : elle n'a pas moins de 5 Ă  6 milles d'ouverture, et s'enfonce de prĂ¨s d'une lieue dans les terres ; elle fut nommĂŠe baie Jussieu, en l'honneur de cette famille qui en fait tant elle-mĂŞme aux sciences et Ă  la patrie Âť (Voyage de dĂŠcouvertes aux Terres australes, t. II, p. 98).
history: By giving the bay this name, PĂŠron places the Jussieus among those learned families whose work, in his view, contributed both to the advancement of science and to national greatness. He writes: âBeyond this last cape [Cape GrĂŠcourt], a new bay opens out; it is no less than 5 to 6 miles wide, and extends nearly a league inland; it was named Jussieu Bay, in honour of that family, which itself has done so much for science and for the nationâ (Voyage de dĂŠcouvertes aux Terres australes, vol. II, p. 98).

characteristic_fr:
Le long de la baie Lavoisier, aujourdâhui dĂŠsignĂŠe sous le nom de Sleaford Bay, le littoral exposĂŠ au sud-ouest est soumis Ă  une houle de forte ĂŠnergie ainsi quâĂ  des vagues gĂŠnĂŠrĂŠes par des vents puissants. Cet environnement hydrodynamique a favorisĂŠ la mise en place, en arriĂ¨re de la baie, dâun champ transgressif de dunes paraboliques couvrant une superficie dâenviron 25 kmÂ˛. Les dunes actuelles ont progressĂŠ dâenviron 2 km vers lâintĂŠrieur des terres, tandis que les formations dunaires plus anciennes, dĂŠsormais stabilisĂŠes par la calcrĂ¨te ou par la vĂŠgĂŠtation, sâĂŠtendent sur plus de 5 km Ă  lâintĂŠrieur du continent. Ă Wanna, sur la marge orientale de Sleaford Bay, lâaction persistante de lâĂŠrosion marine a faĂ§onnĂŠ des falaises littorales atteignant jusquâĂ  50 m de hauteur.
Le toponyme Lavoisier rend hommage Ă  Antoine-Laurent Lavoisier (1743-1794), chimiste pionnier qui joua un rĂ´le majeur dans la refondation de la nomenclature chimique et qui mourut guillotinĂŠ pendant la Terreur.
characteristic:
Along Lavoisier Bay, now known as Sleaford Bay, the southwest-facing coastline is exposed to high-energy swell and to waves generated by strong winds. This hydrodynamic setting has promoted the development, landward of the bay, of a transgressive field of parabolic dunes covering approximately 25 kmÂ˛. The present-day dunes have advanced about 2 km inland, whereas the older dune formations, now stabilized by calcrete or vegetation, extend for more than 5 km inland. At Wanna, on the eastern margin of Sleaford Bay, the persistent action of marine erosion has carved sea cliffs reaching heights of up to 50 m.
The place name Lavoisier commemorates Antoine-Laurent Lavoisier (1743-1794), a pioneering chemist who played a major role in the re-foundation of chemical nomenclature and who was guillotined during the Reign of Terror.
history_fr:

Cape Tournefort forms part of a south-west-facing coastline exposed to strong swell, wind-driven waves, and persistent winds. history_fr: PĂŠron observe depuis le GĂŠographe : ÂŤ Ă ce point, les terres du continent se dessinent sur deux plans trĂ¨s distincts. Celui qui constitue le rivage est abaissĂŠ, d'une coupe gĂŠnĂŠralement abrupte, et paroĂŽt ĂŞtre plus particuliĂ¨rement formĂŠ de dunes de sable qui reposent sur un sol grĂŠseux et jaunĂ˘tre. Quelques nuances d'un vert foncĂŠ semblent indiquer Ă§Ă  et lĂ  une vĂŠgĂŠtation languissante : nous n'avons pu y distinguer aucun arbre. Au second plan appartiennent des terres assez hautes, surmontĂŠes encore, de distance en distance, par des pitons peu saillans, et qui, du point oĂš nous ĂŠtions, affectoient Ă  nos yeux cette nuance aĂŠrienne et bleuĂ˘tre qui caractĂŠrise les terrains ĂŠlevĂŠs vus dans l'ĂŠloignement. history: PĂŠron observes from aboard the GĂŠographe: âAt this point, the lands of the continent are outlined in two very distinct planes. The one forming the shore is low-lying, generally abrupt in profile, and appears to consist chiefly of sand dunes resting on a yellowish, sandy sandstone ground. A few dark green tones here and there seem to indicate sparse and struggling vegetation; we were unable to distinguish a single tree. The second plane consists of rather elevated land, still surmounted at intervals by low prominences, which, from where we stood, took on that airy bluish hue that characterises high ground seen in the distance.â

history_fr:

In the context of a voyage of discovery and hydrographic surveys of coasts then unknown to Europeans, and at a time when the Casuarina was short of fresh water and separated from the GĂŠographe, Freycinet likely interpreted the aridity of these shores as an indication of limited resources, particularly for a crew putting in ashore.

history:
At the time of the Baudin expedition , HallĂŠ was a member of la SociĂŠtĂŠ des Observateurs de lâHomme*, the institute that provided anthropological instructions for the voyage. He was a professors at the school of medicine, where PĂŠron attended his course on âphysique mĂŠdicale et hygiĂ¨neâ (see the first page of the course outline). HallĂŠâs theory was that health has as much to do with the man-made physical environment as with diet and exercise (see the reference to the structure of the course that HallĂŠ gave in La mĂŠdecine ĂŠclairĂŠe par les sciences physiques).
HallĂŠ was working closely with Fourcroy* and Jussieu* through the Revolution to develop a âuseful medicineâ by organising health services and advocating for the improvement of living conditions and hygiene of French people. He wrote pioneer articles in the field of public health theory in the EncyclopĂŠdie mĂŠthodique, and in the new journal mentioned above called âla mĂŠdecine ĂŠclairĂŠe par les sciences physiquesâ, in which he put forward his conception that âphysics applied to the knowledge of the human body, contributes to its preservation and to the cure of its illnessesâ, or, in todayâs words, the notion that the concepts and methods of physics must be applied to the prevention, diagnosis and treatment of human diseases to improve human health and well-being. For example, in 1795, HallĂŠ used the electrotherapy method to treat a facial palsy.

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Lavoisier, aujourdâhui Point Brown, constitue lâun des promontoires dâĂŠolianite qui encadrent la baie de Streaky. characteristic:
Cap Lavoisier, now Point Brown, forms one of the aeolianite headlands that frame Streaky Bay. Rising to about 30 metres, it is bordered by bedrock reefs of etch origin, which contribute to the distinctive geomorphology of this sector of the coast. 
The French gave it the name Cap Lavoisier, in honour of Antoine-Laurent Lavoisier (1743â1794), the pioneering chemist who helped reform the system of chemical nomenclature and who was guillotined during the Terror.
history_fr: En quĂŞte dâaiguades indispensables au ravitaillement en eau douce du Casuarina, Freycinet considĂŠrait le cap Lavoisier comme la limite orientale dâun littoral marquĂŠ par une ÂŤ stĂŠrilitĂŠ affligeante Âť qui, ĂŠcrivait-il, ÂŤ se reproduit sur tous les points de la cĂ´te entre le cap Lavoisier et le cap des Adieux Âť. Il ajoutait : ÂŤ Rarement aperĂ§oit-on Ă§Ă  et lĂ  de foibles arbrisseaux et quelques plantes languissantes ; les terres, gĂŠnĂŠralement basses, ont partout une teinte jaunĂ˘tre et rembrunie qui inspire la tristesse. Âť Plus gĂŠnĂŠralement, il soulignait combien le ÂŤ dĂŠfaut absolu dâeau douce Âť constituait une difficultĂŠ ÂŤ fort grave pour les navigateurs Âť. Dans cette perspective, il ĂŠvoquait un mĂŠmoire dont Lavoisier ĂŠtait co-auteur, Nouvelle construction dâalambic pour faire toute sorte de distillation : en grand, etc., en deux parties (Paris, Imprimerie royale, 1781), que Freycinet jugeait susceptible dâapporter des amĂŠliorations utiles Ă  lâapprovisionnement en eau douce Ă  bord des navires.
history: In search of watering places essential to the replenishment of fresh water for the Casuarina, Freycinet regarded Cape Lavoisier as the eastern limit of a coastline marked by what he called an âafflicting sterility,â which, he wrote, âreappears at every point along the coast between Cape Lavoisier and Cape des Adieux.â He added: âOnly rarely does one perceive here and there a few feeble shrubs and some languishing plants; the land, generally low-lying, everywhere bears a yellowish and darkened hue that inspires sadness.â More broadly, he emphasised how the âabsolute lack of fresh waterâ constituted a difficulty âof the gravest kind for navigators.â In this connection, he referred to a treatise of which Lavoisier was co-author, Nouvelle construction dâalambic pour faire toute sorte de distillation : en grand, etc., en deux parties (Paris, Imprimerie royale, 1781), which he believed might offer useful improvements for the supply of fresh water aboard ships.

Jean Denis Barbie du Bocage (1760 - 1825)

and his son Jean-Guillaume Barbie du Bocage (1795 - 1848) were French cartographers and cosmographers active in Paris during late 18th and early 19th centuries. The elder Barbie du Bocage, Jean Denis, was trained as a cartographer and engraver in the workshops of mapmaking legend J. B. B. d'Anville. At some point Jean Denis held the post of Royal Librarian of France and it was through is associations with d'Anville that the d'Anville collection of nearly 9000 maps was acquired by French Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Jean-Denis BarbiĂŠ, dit BarbiĂŠ du Bocage1, nĂŠ le 28 avril 1760 Ă  Paris et mort dans cette ville le 28 dĂŠcembre 1825, est un gĂŠographe et cartographe franĂ§ais, doyen de la FacultĂŠ de lettres de Paris, membre de l'Institut.
