Il bĂŠnĂŠficie dâune pension Ă  vie par dĂŠcret impĂŠrial du 13 mars 1804 signĂŠ par NapolĂŠon (Hamy, 1984) et dâun poste de surintendant qui lui est accordĂŠ par lâImpĂŠratrice JosĂŠphine (Foucault, 1990).
Il reste 5 ans au service de JosĂŠphine en tant que Surintendant des jardins de la Malmaison et du domaine de Navarre. Il y expĂŠrimente et crĂŠe lâun des plus beaux jardins dâacclimatation (privĂŠs) dâEurope. Tout au long de cette pĂŠriode, AimĂŠ Bonpland accueille chaleureusement les patriotes sud-amĂŠricains qui sĂŠjournent en Europe. Sa relation avec Humboldt est moins ĂŠtroite Ă  ce moment-lĂ . Ils publient, nĂŠanmoins ensemble, la relation de leur expĂŠdition, une Ĺuvre sans ĂŠgale qui est le rĂŠsultat de leurs expĂŠriences de voyageurs et de leurs recherches scientifiques, intitulĂŠe ÂŤ Voyage aux rĂŠgions ĂŠquinoxiales du Nouveau Continent Âť, et qui comporte 30 volumes in-folio et in-quatro publiĂŠs de 1807-1834*. Dans la prĂŠface Ă  la GĂŠographie des plantes (Paris, Schoell 1807), Humboldt rend hommage Ă  Bonpland : ÂŤ Quoique les Plantes ĂŠquinoxiales, comme tous les travaux de notre expĂŠdition, portent le nom de Bonpland et le mien, il sâen faut de beaucoup que les deux signataires aient eu part ĂŠgale Ă  cet ouvrage. M. Bonpland ne lâa pas seulement rĂŠdigĂŠ lui seul dâaprĂ¨s nos manuscrits, mais câest Ă  lui quâest due la plus grande partie de ce travail. Âť

history: Georges Cuvier (1769-1832) gave precise instructions to $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ to collect human anatomical specimens, particularly skulls, to advance work on the physical differences between peoples. $PĂŠron$FranĂ§ois_PĂŠron$ attempted to fulfil this macabre mission by excavating an Aboriginal ossuary on Maria Island, a site known today as Cape des Tombeaux (Cape of the Tombs).

File Baie Cuvier in Tasmania: PĂŠron also had precise instructions from Georges Cuvier to collect human anatomical specimens and portraits to advance the work of the German comparative anatomist J. F. Blumenbach, who had distinguished physical differences between Caucasians, Mongols and Ethiopians and sought further to isolate differences or potential affinities among the inhabitants of Lapland, the Americas, the South Seas and New Holland. For Cuvier, the âprincipal and the most necessaryâ human specimens to obtain were skulls, which he wanted boiled in a solution of sodium or potassium hydroxide. Aware that this might appear barbarous to sailors, he asserted that the leaders of an expedition aimed at the advancement of science had to be guided by reason. Each head, once removed of its flesh, was to be enveloped in cloth (for fear that loose bones might become separated) and labelled. 93 PĂŠron attempted to fulfil his macabre orders by excavating an ossuary at an Aboriginal cremation site he discovered on Maria Island. In his book he tells his readers that he shuddered with horror on drawing forth âthe jaw-bone of a man, to which there yet adhered some remains of fleshâ. 94 Lesueur even captured the subsequent moment in a pencil sketch: PĂŠron kneeling with a broad-brimmed hat on his head and (it would seem) a femur in his left hand. 95 To this day the site is known as Cape des Tombeaux,

characteristic_fr: Au sein de l'archipel Fleurieu, le canal Hope sĂŠpare l'ĂŽle Hunter de l'ĂŽle Three Hummock, au large de la cĂ´te nord-ouest de la Tasmanie. Freycinet l'a nommĂŠ en l'honneur de FranĂ§ois PĂŠron (1775-1810) et note que "A lâĂŠgard du canal PĂŠron, nous [Freycinet aux commandes du Casuarina et son ĂŠquipage] lâavons traversĂŠ deux fois, en nous tenant toujours, il est vrai, vers sa partie Orientale; mais nous nâavons dĂŠcouvert nulle part lâapparence dâun danger. La sonde nous y a donnĂŠ de 13 Ă  33 brasses. "
characteristic:
Within the Fleurieu group of islands, Hope Channel separates Hunter Island from Three Hummock Island, off the northwest coast of Tasmania. Freycinet named it after Francois PĂŠron (1775-1810), and notes that the Casuarina crossed the canal (now Hope Channel) twice on its eastern side and did not find any danger, p.82.
,
history_fr: Parmi les membres de lâexpĂŠdition, FranĂ§ois PĂŠron se distingue par le nombre de toponymes qui lui furent dĂŠdiĂŠs, par Freycinet en l'occurence: six au total, un record dans la nomenclature. Lâappellation Canal PĂŠron ne renvoie d'ailleurs pas Ă  des dĂŠcouvertes directes de PĂŠron dans cette zone, celui-ci nâayant pas participĂŠ a la mission de Freycinet aux Iles Hunter. Dans son journal nautique datĂŠ des 6-7 dĂŠcembre 1802, Freycinet consigne les circonstances de son ancrage dans le dĂŠtroit sĂŠparant les deux principales ĂŽles de lâarchipel : "Voyant que je ne pouvais pas gagner au vent avec la voilure que le vent me forĂ§ait de garder, je me suis dĂŠcidĂŠ Ă  venir mouiller dans le dĂŠtroit formĂŠ par les deux plus grandes des Iles Hunters. Ă 3h.soir laissĂŠ tomber l'ancre dans le NE de l'ile longue par 20 brasses fond de sable. Mr Boullanger & moi, nous sommes rĂŠsolus Ă  ne pas quiter notre actuel mouillage, sans en avoir fixĂŠ prĂŠalablement sa position, pour des observations de latitude & de longitude." Sur la carte de lâatlas rĂŠsultant de lâexpĂŠdition, Freycinet localise avec prĂŠcision ce point dâancrage, dâoĂš lui et Boullanger firent les relevĂŠs des ĂŽles Hunter. Ces mesures servirent de base Ă  la triangulation de lâarchipel, contribuant ainsi Ă  sa cartographie scientifique.
history:
Among the members of the expedition, FranĂ§ois PĂŠron stands out for the number of place names dedicated to him by Freycinet: six in total, a record in the nomenclature. However, the name Canal PĂŠron does not refer to any direct discoveries made by PĂŠron in this area, as he did not take part in Freycinet's mission to the Hunter Islands.
In his logbook dated 6-7 December 1802, Freycinet recorded the circumstances of his anchoring in this strait/channel separating the two main islands of the archipelago: "Seeing that I could not gain windward with the sails that the wind forced me to keep, I decided to anchor in the strait formed by the two largest of the Hunter Islands. At 3 o'clock in the evening, I dropped anchor in the northeast of the long island in 20 fathoms of sandy bottom. Mr Boullanger and I decided not to leave our current anchorage without first determining its position for latitude and longitude observations." On the map in the atlas produced as a result of the expedition, Freycinet precisely locates this anchorage, from where he and Boullanger took bearings of the Hunter Islands. These measurements served as the basis for the triangulation of the archipelago, thus contributing to its scientific cartography.

"

characteristic_fr:
La baie de Two People's Bay est situĂŠe entre les massifs granitiques du mont Gardner et du mont Manypeaks, Ă  environ vingt-cinq kilomĂ¨tres au nord-est dâAlbany. Cette baie constitue un habitat dâimportance internationale pour plusieurs espĂ¨ces menacĂŠes, notamment le noisy scrub-bird (Atrichornis clamosus).
La toponymie fait rĂŠfĂŠrence Ă  la rencontre, le 20 fĂŠvrier 1803, dans ÂŤ une jolie baie Âť, entre le lieutenant Jacques Ransonnet et son ĂŠquipe, et le capitaine amĂŠricain Isaac Pendleton, commandant le brick Union, mouillĂŠ dans la baie depuis deux jours. Baudin avait dĂŠpĂŞchĂŠ Ransonnet en mission ÂŤ pour examiner la portion de cĂ´te comprise entre le mont Gardner et Bald IslandâŚ Âť.
characteristic:
Two People's Bay is located between the granite massifs of Mt Gardner and Mt Manypeaks, approximately 25 kilometres north-east of Albany. The bay is an internationally significant habitat for several endangered species, such as the noisy scrub-bird.
The name refers to the encounter, on 20th February 1803, in âa Pretty Bayâ between Lieutenant Jacques Ransonnet* and his party with the American Captain Isaac Pendleton, in command of the brig Union, who had already been anchored in the bay for two days. Baudin had sent Ransonnet on a mission âto visit the section of the coast lying between Mt Gardner and Bald IslandâŚâ.
history_fr: Les FranĂ§ais invitĂ¨rent Pendleton Ă  dĂŽner Ă  bord du GĂŠographe. Il informa Baudin quâil avait quittĂŠ New York en septembre 1802 afin de se procurer des peaux de phoques destinĂŠes Ă  ĂŞtre vendues en Chine et que, ÂŤ jusquâĂ  prĂŠsent, il nâavait pas eu de chanceâŚ il nâavait obtenu que trois ou quatre cents peaux Âť et quâÂŤ il lui en fallait vingt mille avant dâenvisager de mettre le cap sur la Chine Âť. Baudin nota dans son journal : ÂŤ Avant que de nous mettre Ă  table, il me pria de lui donner, sâil ĂŠtait possible, une carte de la cĂ´te de la Nouvelle Hollande, nâayant aucun renseignement qui put le guider dans les routes quâil allait faire pour la recherche des lieux que frĂŠquentent les loups marins ni pour la direction de la cĂ´te ou des dangers qui sây trouvent. Je lui donnai deux des cartes du citoyen Beautemps BeauprĂŠ et une des dĂŠtroits de Bass et de Banks par M. Flinders, de mĂŞme que des observations particuliĂ¨res sur lâentrĂŠe des ports Philips et de Bass ainsi que de la position de lâĂŽle KingÂť, datĂŠe du 4 ventĂ´se an XI de la RĂŠpublique franĂ§aise (23 fĂŠvrier 1803).
history: The French invited Pendleton to dinner on board le GĂŠographe. He told Baudin that he had left New York in September 1802 to procure seal skins intended for sale in China and that, âso far, he had not been lucky, âŚhe had obtained only three or four hundred skinsâ and âhad to have twenty thousand before thinking [of] heading for Chinaâ. Baudin noted in his journal: âBefore we sat down, he asked me to give him, if possible, a map of the coast of New Holland, for he had no informationâŚ. I gave him two of Citizen Beautemps BeauprĂŠâs charts and one of Bass and Banks Straits drawn up by FlindersâŚâ dated 4 Ventose, year 11 of the French Republic (February, 23, 1803).

characteristic_fr:
Le fleuve Kalgan se jette dans le port dâOyster Harbour, situĂŠ dans la rĂŠgion du Grand Sud de lâAustralie-Occidentale, une zone explorĂŠe par George Vancouver en 1791. Le terme ÂŤ Kalgan Âť, issu de la langue noongar, signifie soit ÂŤ lieu aux nombreuses eaux Âť soit ÂŤ lieu des poissons Âť, reflĂŠtant ainsi les variations saisonniĂ¨res de son dĂŠbit ainsi que son importance historique en tant que site de pĂŞche et de collecte pour les familles noongar.
Les 22 et 23 fĂŠvrier 1803, Faure et Bailly, accompagnĂŠs de leur commandant, remontĂ¨rent le cours du fleuve Kalgan afin dâen ĂŠvaluer la navigabilitĂŠ, mais furent arrĂŞtĂŠs par une sĂŠrie de digues construites par les AborigĂ¨nes. MalgrĂŠ les preuves dâune occupation autochtone le long de ses rives, PĂŠron baptisa nĂŠanmoins le cours dâeau ÂŤ RiviĂ¨re des FranĂ§ais Âť.
characteristic:
The Kalgan River drains into Oyster Harbour in the Great Southern region of Western Australia, an area visited by George Vancouver in 1791. 'Kalgan' is a Noongar word meaning either 'place of many waters' or 'place of fish'âa reflection of the riverâs seasonal flows and its long-standing significance as a fishing and gathering site for Noongar families.
On 22-23 February 1803, Faure*and Bailly*, in the company of their commander, followed the Kalgan River to establish if it was navigable but were stopped by a series of dykes built by the Aborigines. Regardless of the evidence of native habitation along its banks, PĂŠron named the river RiviĂ¨re des FranĂ§ois [FranĂ§ais].
history_fr: Baudin dĂŠcrit la riviere comme "un bras de mer qui sâavance profondĂŠment dans le pays en faisant une infinitĂŠ de sinuositĂŠs dans des directions diffĂŠrentes." Il note aussi qu'aux environs ..." nous trouvĂ˘mes deux monuments assez particuliers et intĂŠressants, ĂŠlevĂŠs par les naturels du pays, si on (nâ)en pouvait connaĂŽtre le motif. Le premier ĂŠtait placĂŠ Ă  sept ou huit pieds du ruisseau dans un lieu dĂŠpourvu dâherbes ayant trois pieds de circonfĂŠrence et entourĂŠ de sagaies bien effilĂŠes et peintes en rouge du cĂ´tĂŠ de la pointe. Il y en avait, en tout, onze. ParallĂ¨lement Ă  ce trophĂŠe, de lâautre cĂ´tĂŠ du ruisseau, sâen trouvait un lot semblable pour la forme et le nombre de sagaies ... Plusieurs personnes ayant pensĂŠ que ces sagaies avaient ĂŠtĂŠ peintes en rouge avec du sang, nous en prĂŽmes une pour la mieux examiner et nous reconnĂťmes que cette couleur leur avait ĂŠtĂŠ donnĂŠe avec de la rĂŠsine de lâeucalyptus."
history: Baudin describes the river as "an inlet that extends deep into the country, winding endlessly in different directions." He also notes that in the vicinity... "we found two rather unusual and interesting monuments, erected by the natives of the country, although we could not ascertain the reason for this. The first was located seven or eight feet from the stream in a place devoid of grass, three feet in circumference and surrounded by well-sharpened spears painted red on the pointed side. There were eleven in all. Parallel to this trophy, on the other side of the stream, there was a similar set in terms of shape and number of spears ... Several people thought that these spears had been painted red with blood, so we took one to examine it more closely and realised that the colour had been applied using eucalyptus resin."

characteristic_fr:
LâĂle de lâObservatoire, aujourdâhui appelĂŠe Mistaken Island, est situĂŠe Ă  environ cinq kilomĂ¨tres au sud-est dâAlbany, sur la cĂ´te sud de l'Australie-Occidentale, dans la baie King George Sound, dĂŠnommĂŠ ainsi par Vancouver en 1791.
Sa dĂŠnomination franĂ§aise renvoie Ă  lâemplacement oĂš lâastronome de lâexpĂŠdition, Pierre-FranĂ§ois Bernier, installa son observatoire en vue dâobserver une ĂŠclipse de soleil.*
characteristic:
Ăle de lâObservatoire, now named Mistaken Island, is located approximately 5 kilometres south-east of Albany, on the south coast of Western Australia, in King George Sound, named by Vancouver in 1791.
Its French name refers to the location where the expeditionâs astronomer Pierre-FranĂ§ois Bernier* set up his Observatory to observe an eclipse of the sun.
history_fr:
Lorsqu'un incendie s'est dĂŠclarĂŠ sur l'ĂŽle, Bernier a dĂť se dĂŠpĂŞcher de dĂŠplacer la tente contenant ses instruments. Le ciel couvert l'a empĂŞchĂŠ d'observer l'ĂŠclipse. Cependant, Bernier a calculĂŠ avec prĂŠcision la latitude et la longitude de l'ĂŽle Ă  l'aide d'un tĂŠlescope, d'une boussole magnĂŠtique et d'une montre. Cela a permis Ă  Freycinet, Ransonnet et Faure d'amĂŠliorer encore le plan dĂŠjĂ  trĂ¨s bien cartographiĂŠ de Vancouver. Le choix de PĂŠron d'appeler l'ĂŽle ÂŤ ĂŽle de l'Observatoire Âť aurait pu prĂŞter Ă  confusion, car d'Entrecasteaux avait dĂŠjĂ  donnĂŠ ce nom, pour des raisons similaires, Ă  une ĂŽle situĂŠe un peu plus Ă  l'est sur la mĂŞme cĂ´te sud de la Nouvelle-Hollande. Freycinet dĂŠcrit l'ĂŽle comme ÂŤ agrĂŠable, bien boisĂŠe et liĂŠe
Ă  la grande terre par une traĂŽnĂŠe de rĂŠcifs qui sâavance, au Nord et dans lâEst de lâĂŽle, Ă  une ou depx encĂ˘blures de distance."
history: When a fire started on the island, Bernier had to make haste to move the tent containing his instruments. Overcast skies prevented him from observing the eclipse. However, Bernier calculated with accuracy the latitude and the longitude of the island, making use of the telescope, magnetic compass, and watch. It enabled Freycinet*, Ransonnet* and Faure* to further improve Vancouverâs well charted plan. PĂŠron's choice of the name ile de l'Observatoire could have led to confusion, as d'Entrecasteaux had for similar reasons already given that name to an island a little further to the east on the same south coast of New Holland. Freycinet describes the island as âpleasant, well wooded and connected to the mainland by a chain of reefsâ.

characteristic_fr: L'Anse de l'Aiguade, aujourd'hui appelĂŠe Frenchman Bay, est situĂŠe sur la rive sud de la baie de King George Sound. Elle doit son nom Ă  la prĂŠsence d'une source d'eau douce (ÂŤ aiguade Âť), mentionnĂŠe pour la premiĂ¨re fois dans le journal de Vancouver. Baudin envoya son canot pour reconnaĂŽtre le point d'eau le 18 fĂŠvrier 1803. Son ĂŠquipage rapporta que l'eau ĂŠtait ÂŤ trĂ¨s bonne et facile d'accĂ¨s Âť.
characteristic: The Anse de lâAiguade, now named Frenchman Bay, is located on the southern side of the King George Sound. It was named for the presence of a fresh water source ("aiguade"), first mentioned in the journal of Vancouver. Baudin sent his long boat to reconnoitre the watering-place on the 18th of February 1803. His crew reported the water to be âvery good and easy to accessâ.
history_fr:
Dans l'Anse de l'Aiguade, PĂŠron rapporte "j'ai tuĂŠ moi-mĂŞme un reptile de 182 centimĂ¨tres [ 6 pieds ] de longueur, et qui, dans la mĂŠthode de M. DE LacĂŠpĂ¨de, devroit servir de type Ă  un genre nouveau, voisin de celui des Boa: ce terrible reptile est armĂŠ de crochets venimeux".
Il notait encore: "en un mot, sous le rapport de la pĂŞche et des ressources qu'elle peut offrir, le port du Roi-George paroissoit ĂŞtre, Ă  l'ĂŠpoque oĂš nous nous y trouvions, un des endroits les plus prĂŠcieux que les navigateurs puissent frĂŠquenter dans ces parages; on pourroit, au besoin, y faire d'abondantes salaisons. D'autres animaux moins utiles mĂŠritent pourtant d'ĂŞtre indiquĂŠs
ici. De l'ordre des Batraciens, j'ai dĂŠcouvert une charmante et nouvelle espĂ¨ce du genre Hyla [Grenouille]..."
history:
In the Anse de lâAiguade, PĂŠron killed a âterrible reptile armed with venomous fangsâ, which was â182 cm long and which, in Mr de LacĂŠpĂ¨de âs system, should serve as the type specimen of a new genus, close to that of the boasâ.
More generally, PĂŠron considered that âKing George Sound appeared, at the time we were there, to be one of the most valuable places that navigators can frequent in these regionsâ and, by the diversity of its endemic species, bears witness that âthere isn't a single well-known species of animal that âŚ is indiscriminately specific to all parts of the globeâŚâ. With this statement, Peron develops the notion of species adapting to their natural environment.

characteristic_fr: Wilson Head, nommĂŠ cap PingrĂŠ par les FranĂ§ais en lâhonneur de lâastronome Alexandre Guy PingrĂŠ (voir aussi la notice Ăle PingrĂŠ, en SA), dĂŠlimite la limite orientale de la baie de Ratcliffe, sâavanĂ§ant vers les ĂŠtendues mĂŠridionales de lâocĂŠan Austral. Ce secteur cĂ´tier escarpĂŠ, exposĂŠ aux houles puissantes en provenance des latitudes australes, bĂŠnĂŠficie de la prĂŠsence de blocs granitiques ĂŠrodĂŠs le long du littoral, qui agissent comme une barriĂ¨re naturelle. Ces formations protĂ¨gent des plages comme Ocean Beach, devenue un site rĂŠputĂŠ pour la pratique du surf.
characteristic: Wilson Head, named Cap PingrĂŠ by the French in honor of the astronomer Alexandre Guy PingrĂŠ (see the entry Ăle PingrĂŠ, in SA), delineates the eastern boundary of Ratcliffe Bay, projecting into the southern reaches of the Southern Ocean.. This rugged coastal segment is frequently subjected to the relentless force of substantial swells originating from the southern latitudes. However, the presence of weathered granite boulders along the shoreline serves as a natural barrier, sheltering beaches such as Ocean Beach, which has gained prominence as a favored destination for surfers.
history_fr: Aux commandes du Casusarina, Freycinet effectua des relevĂŠs topographiques de cette partie de la cĂ´te Ă  partir du cap PingrĂŠ, le 6 mars 1803. Il la dĂŠcrivit comme ĂŠtant basse, mais avec "des terres plus hautes en second plan", et suggĂŠra la prĂŠsence de "lagons ou ĂŠtangs salĂŠs communiquant avec la mer" derriĂ¨re les dunes de sable.
history: When in command of le Casusarina, Freycinet took bearings of this part of the coast starting from Cap PingrĂŠ, on the 6th of March 1803. He described it as low-lying, but with higher land in the background, and suggested the presence of lagoons or salt lakes behind the sandhills.

Ce cap fait dĂŠsormais partie de la rĂŠserve naturelle de Quarram, situĂŠe dans lâĂŠcorĂŠgion de Warren, et abrite une grande diversitĂŠ de vĂŠgĂŠtation indigĂ¨ne rĂŠsiduelle.
characteristic: In southwest of Western Australia on the Southern Ocean, Cap Faujas is named in honor of an eminent geologist of the time (see the entry Piton Faujas*, in Tasmania). The cap is now part of the Quarram Nature Reserve in the Warren ecoregion, with a rich diversity of native remnant vegetation.
history_fr:
Freycinet note: "Le cap Faujas, sans ĂŞtre trĂ¨s-ĂŠlevĂŠ, lâest cependant plus que ceux qui en sont
voisins; il est taillĂŠ Ă  pic."

characteristic_fr: La cĂ´te du cap, caractĂŠrisĂŠe par de petites anses et des rochers arrondis, fut nommĂŠe en lâhonneur de Sylvestre-FranĂ§ois Lacroix (1765â1843), mathĂŠmaticien renommĂŠ et auteur dâun traitĂŠ influent sur le calcul diffĂŠrentiel et intĂŠgral. Son Ĺuvre, qui marqua profondĂŠment les sciences exactes, trouve ici un ĂŠcho gĂŠographique, soulignant lâintersection entre les avancĂŠes thĂŠoriques et leur application dans la cartographie des formes cĂ´tiĂ¨res.
characteristic: The coast of the cap, formed of small embayments and rounded boulders, was named for Sylvestre-FranĂ§ois Lacroix or De la Croix (1765 â 1843) who was a mathematician who wrote an influential treatise on differential and integral calculus
history_fr: En 1799, Lacroix fut nommĂŠ professeur Ă  lâĂcole polytechnique et ĂŠtait par ailleurs membre de lâInstitut. Les intĂŠgrales servaient alors au calcul des distances, des aires ou des volumes, outils particuliĂ¨rement utiles aux gĂŠographes, aux officiers et aux ingĂŠnieurs. Freycinet le qualifiait dâailleurs de "l'un de nos plus savans gĂŠomĂ¨tres".
history: In 1799, Lacroix was appointed professor at the Ăcole Polytechnique. He was also a member of the Institut. Integrals were used to calculate distances, areas or volumes. They were very useful to geographers, officers and engineers. Freycinet referred to Lacroix as âone of our most learned surveyorsâ.

characteristic_fr:
SituĂŠes dans la partie de lâAustralie-Occidentale connue sous le nom de Great Southern Region, ces ĂŽles se trouvent Ă  environ 1 kilomĂ¨tre au large. BaignĂŠes par les eaux de lâocĂŠan Austral, elles offrent des habitats de reproduction pour des oiseaux marins tels que les puffins Ă  pieds pĂ˘les et les puffins mineurs.
Freycinet aperĂ§ut ces ĂŽles â Saddle, Goose et un troisiĂ¨me ĂŽlot rocheux au large du parc national de Walpole-Nornalup â alors quâil naviguait Ă  lâouest du cap Lacroix. Il les nomma en lâhonneur de son navire, la goĂŠlette le Casuarina, dont il avait pris le commandement en novembre 1802 (voir lâentrĂŠe Piton du Casuarina en Australie-MĂŠridionale).
characteristic: Located in the Great Southern region of Western Australia, these islands are situated approximately 1 km off shore. In the Southern Ocean, they provide breeding habitats for seabirds such as fleshy-footed shearwaters and little shearwaters. Freycinet spotted to these islands (Saddle and Goose islands and a third rocky islet off Walpole-Nornalup national park) when sailing west of Cap Lacroix and named them after his ship, the goelette le Casuarina, of which he had been in command since November 1802 (see the entry Piton du Casuarina* in SA)
history_fr: Freycinet note : ÂŤ A quatre heures [6 mars 1803], je dĂŠcouvris les ĂŽles du Casuarina, et m'avanĂ§ai jusqu'Ă  la hauteur du cap Mably. BientĂ´t aprĂ¨s j'atteignis la pointe de Nuyts, qui m'avoit ĂŠtĂŠ fixĂŠe pour terme de cette reconnoissance, et de suite je fis porter au large pour rejoindre le GĂŠographe. Toute la portion de terre que je venois de dĂŠcouvrir est aride et dĂŠpourvue de vĂŠgĂŠtation : les ĂŽles du CasuĂ˘rina sont rocailleuses et d'un aspect fort triste; elles sont d'ailleurs environnĂŠes de rĂŠcifs, et, sous ce rapport, on ne doit s'en approcher qu'avec beaucoup de rĂŠserve. Âť
history:
At four oâclock [6 March 1803], I sighted the Casuarina Isles and pressed onward as far as the latitude of Cape Mably. Shortly thereafter, I reached Nuyts Point, which had been set as the limit of this reconnaissance. Without delay, I bore away offshore to rejoin the GĂŠographe.
The entire stretch of land I had just discovered was barren and devoid of vegetation: the Casuarina Isles are rugged and bleak in appearance, and are, moreover, ringed by treacherous reefs. In this regard, one must approach them with the utmost caution.

characteristic_fr:
Au cap Mably, aujourd'hui Point Nuyts, la cĂ´te s'ĂŠtend vers l'ouest-nord-ouest en direction du point D'Entrecasteaux.
Le cap fut nommĂŠ en l'honneur du philosophe Gabriel Bonnot de Mably (voir l'entrĂŠe Cap Mably dans SA).
characteristic:
At Cap Mably, now Point Nuyts, the coast trends west-northwest towards Point DâEntrecasteaux.
The cap was named in honour of the philosopher Gabriel Bonnot de Mably (see the entry Cap Mably in SA).
history_fr:
Freycinet soulignait ÂŤ la plus affligeante ariditĂŠ Âť des rĂŠgions cĂ´tiĂ¨res, associant lâaspect apparemment dĂŠsertique du paysage aux feux et aux fumĂŠes liĂŠs aux pratiques des peuples autochtones. Avec PĂŠron, il percevait ces grands feux comme une tradition qui, Ă  travers le prisme europĂŠen, semblait Ă  la fois ancestrale et contre-productive, semblant appauvrir la vĂŠgĂŠtation, ĂŠpuiser la fertilitĂŠ des sols et, selon leur interprĂŠtation, compromettre mĂŞme la gĂŠnĂŠrositĂŠ naturelle de la terre.
Cependant, cette analyse reflĂŠtait une comprĂŠhension limitĂŠe de la dimension ĂŠcologique et culturelle de ces pratiques. Sans saisir les logiques sous-jacentes ou les bĂŠnĂŠfices Ă  long terme des brĂťlages contrĂ´lĂŠs â quâil sâagisse de gestion des terres, de renouvellement des ressources ou de traditions culturelles â les explorateurs franĂ§ais interprĂŠtaient ces phĂŠnomĂ¨nes Ă  travers le filtre de leurs propres cadres agricoles et environnementaux. Ce dĂŠcalage de perspective a contribuĂŠ Ă  une vision plus large, et souvent rĂŠductrice, des peuples des PremiĂ¨res Nations dâAustralie, illustrant les dĂŠfis des rencontres interculturelles et la complexitĂŠ dâĂŠvaluer des pratiques inconnues Ă  travers un regard extĂŠrieur.
history: Freycinet noted the âthe distressing aridityâ of the coastal region, attributing the apparent desolation of the landscape to the fires and smoke associated with the practices of the Indigenous peoples. Alongside PĂŠron, he perceived these large-scale fires as a practice that seemed, from their European perspective, both ancient and counterproductiveâappearing to diminish vegetation, deplete soil fertility, and even, in their view, undermine the natural abundance of the land. However, their assessment reflected a limited understanding of the ecological and cultural significance of these practices. Without recognizing the potential rationale or long-term benefits of controlled burningâsuch as land management, resource renewal, or cultural traditionsâthe French explorersâ interpretations were shaped by their own agricultural and environmental frameworks. This gap in understanding contributed to a broader, and often uncharitable, perception of Australiaâs First Nations peoples, highlighting the challenges of cross-cultural encounter and the complexities of evaluating unfamiliar practices through an external lens.

characteristic_fr: LâexpĂŠdition ne sâest pas approchĂŠe de la cĂ´te comprise entre le cap Leeuwin et le cap Gosselin, ce dernier ĂŠtant difficile Ă  localiser sur les cartes actuelles. Freycinet le qualifiait de ÂŤ cĂ´te inaccessible Âť. Il signalait que lâĂŽlot Saint-Allouarn se trouvait au sud du cap Gosselin et quâun rĂŠcif sâĂŠtendait Ă  lâouest de celui-ci (voir la planche 21 de l'atlas). Sur les cartes contemporaines, le cap correspondant Ă  cette description est le cap Leeuwin, bien que Freycinet le situe plus au sud-est sur ses propres relevĂŠs. Pascal-FranĂ§ois-Joseph Gosselin (ou Gossellin) (1751â1830) ĂŠtait un gĂŠographe et occupait la fonction de bibliothĂŠcaire en chef de lâinstitution correspondant aujourdâhui Ă  la BibliothĂ¨que nationale de France.
characteristic: The expedition did not approach the coast between Cape Leeuwin and Cap Gosselin, and the latter cape is difficult to locate on current maps. Freycinet referred to it as an âinaccessible coastlineâ. He noted that the Ilot St Allouarn, was situated to the south of Cape Gosselin, and that a reef lay to its west (see the plate 21 of the atlas). On current maps, the heading that fits this description is Cape Leeuwin, although Freycinet charts it further to the south-east. Pascal-FranĂ§ois-Joseph Gosselin (or Gossellin) (1751â1830) was a geographer and served as the chief librarian of the institution that corresponds today to the BibliothĂ¨que nationale de France.
history_fr:
Dans sa fonction de bibliothĂŠcaire en chef de la bibliothĂ¨que impĂŠriale, Gosselin a pu assister PĂŠron ou Freycinet dans leur travail dâĂŠdition du rĂŠcit du voyage.
Ce nom pourrait ĂŠgalement dĂŠsigner Nicolas Gossellin, gabier sur Le GĂŠographe. Celui-ci quitta le navire Ă  lâĂŽle de France (Maurice) lors du voyage de retour. Toutefois, aucune raison connue ne permet dâexpliquer pourquoi son nom aurait ĂŠtĂŠ retenu dans la nomenclature.
history: In this capacity, Gosselin may have helped PĂŠron or Freycinet in their work of editing the narrative of the voyage. The name may also apply to Nicolas Gossellin, a leading seaman on Le GĂŠographe. He left the ship at the Isle de France (Mauritius) on its return voyage. Though, there is no known reason to explain why his name should have been chosen in the nomenclature.

characteristic_fr: Cape Hamelin prĂŠsente une sĂŠrie de crĂŞtes granitiques et dâamas de blocs orientĂŠs nordâsud, atteignant environ 20 m de hauteur. Plus Ă  lâintĂŠrieur, ces formations sâattĂŠnuent sous une couverture de dunes cĂ´tiĂ¨res. Le cap doit son nom Ă  Jacques FĂŠlix Emmanuel Hamelin (1768â1839), commandant de la corvette Le Naturaliste, la corvette qui accompagnait Le GĂŠographe.
characteristic: Cape Hamelin features a series of northâsouth-oriented granite ridges and boulder fields that reach heights of roughly 20 m. Inland, these formations taper beneath a blanket of coastal dunes. The cape bears the name of Jacques FĂŠlix Emmanuel Hamelin (1768â1839), commander of the corvette Le Naturaliste, the sister ship to Le GĂŠographe.Several other nearby locationsâsuch as a bay and an islandâwere also later named after Hamelin.
history_fr: Hamelin fut choisi par le ministre de la Marine â alors, et jusquâen 1801, Pierre-Alexandre-Laurent Forfait â en reconnaissance des services quâil avait rendus dans la Marine durant la RĂŠvolution, aprĂ¨s que Jacques RĂŠmy Maingon* eut dĂŠclinĂŠ lâoffre pour raisons de santĂŠ. Comme Louis Freycinet accordait une plus grande confiance aux compĂŠtences nautiques de Hamelin quâĂ  celles de Baudin, il rendit hommage au commandant du Naturaliste en donnant son nom au cap Hamelin (et plus au nord au Havre Hamelin*), tandis quâil nâattribua aucun toponyme au commandant de lâexpĂŠdition.
history: Hamelin was chosen by the navy minister (at the time, and until 1801, Pierre-Alexandre-Laurent Forfait,) in recognition of his services in the Navy during the Revolution, and after Jacques RĂŠmy Maingon* had declined the offer for health reasons. Because Louis Freycinet had greater confidence in Hamelin's navigational skills than in Baudin's, he honored the captain of le Naturaliste by naming cape Hamelin (and further north Havre Hamelin*) while he did not assign any place names to the commander of the voyage.

characteristic_fr:
Cap Freycinet est situĂŠ sur le littoral entre le cap Hamelin et le cap Naturaliste, sur la cĂ´te sud-ouest de lâAustralie-Occidentale. Il marque la limite continentale du plateau sous-marin du Naturaliste, une bande continentale qui sâest dĂŠtachĂŠe de la marge sud-ouest de lâAustralie lors de la fragmentation de lâest du Gondwana au CrĂŠtacĂŠ infĂŠrieur. Le long de ses crĂŞtes sâalignent les caps et pointes de Hamelin, Freycinet, Mentelle et Clairault, aujourdâhui intĂŠgrĂŠs au parc national de Leeuwin-Naturaliste.
Ce toponyme rend hommage Ă  Louis Freycinet ainsi quâĂ  son frĂ¨re, Henri. En 1801, tous deux furent chargĂŠs de missions dâexploration et de levĂŠs cartographiques le long des cĂ´tes occidentales de la Nouvelle-Hollande (Voir ĂŠgalement lâentrĂŠe PresquâĂŽle Freycinet en Tasmanie, qui met en lumiĂ¨re le rĂ´le des deux frĂ¨res).
characteristic: Cape Freycinet is on the coastline between Cape Hamelin and Cape Naturaliste, on the southwestern coast of Western Australia, that marks the landward extent of the submarine Naturaliste Plateauâa continental ribbon that rifted from the southwest Australian margin during the Early Cretaceous breakup of East Gondwana. Along its ridges lie the headlands of Cape Hamelin, Point Freycinet, Cape Mentelle, and Cape Clairault, now part of the Leeuwin-Naturaliste National Park. Pointe Freycinet honors Louis Freycinet as well as his brother, Henri. In 1801, both were entrusted with various surveying missions along the western coasts of New Holland. (See the entry PresquâĂŽle Freycinet in Tasmania, which features both brothers.)
history_fr: Nicolas Baudin entreprit un second relevĂŠ de cette partie des cĂ´tes occidentales de la Nouvelle-Hollande, jugeant insuffisante lâexpĂŠdition initiale menĂŠe en 1801. Ce voyage se heurta cependant Ă  des difficultĂŠs logistiques et opĂŠrationnelles persistantes, marquĂŠes notamment par la sĂŠparation frĂŠquente des corvettes puis du Geographe et du Casuarina. Ces incidents retardĂ¨rent la progression du voyage et aggravĂ¨rent les tensions. Dans son journal de bord, le 9 mars 1803, Baudin exprime son irritation et attribue en partie ces inefficacitĂŠs Ă  lâinexpĂŠrience de son ĂŠquipe scientifique. Il constate que leur manque de savoir-faire pratique â en particulier dans lâĂŠvaluation des distances cĂ´tiĂ¨res et des profondeurs â limitait leur contribution, malgrĂŠ leurs connaissances thĂŠoriques. Tout en reconnaissant leur potentiel dâamĂŠlioration avec lâexpĂŠrience, Baudin souligne lâimportance cruciale dâune formation sur le terrain.
history: Nicolas Baudin undertook a second survey of this part of western New Holland, deeming the initial 1801 survey inadequate. The voyage, however, faced persistent logistical and operational difficulties, notably the frequent separation of the corvettes and then the Geographe and the Casuarina.which delayed progress and exacerbated tensions. Baudinâs journal reflects his frustration, attributing the inefficiencies in part to the inexperience of his scientific team. In his sea journal, on 9 March 1803, il observed that their lack of practical expertiseâparticularly in assessing coastal distances and depthsâhindered their contributions, despite their theoretical knowledge. While acknowledging their potential for improvement with experience, Baudin emphasized the critical role of hands-on training in fieldwork.

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Mentelle est un promontoire calcaire le calcaire de Tamala) situĂŠ sur la cĂ´te de l'ocĂŠan Indien, dans la rĂŠgion des caps, au sud-ouest de l'Australie occidentale. Il se trouve dans le parc national Leeuwin-Naturaliste.
Son nom rend hommage au gĂŠographe Edme Mentelle (1730-1816), dont les travaux ont constituĂŠ des rĂŠfĂŠrences importantes aux officiers et gĂŠographes du voyage.
characteristic:
Cape Mentelle is a limestone promontory (Tamala limestone) located on the Indian Ocean coast, in the Cape region, also known as Cape to Cape, south-west of Western Australia. It is located in Leeuwin-Naturaliste National Park.
Its name pays tribute to the geographer Edme Mentelle (1730-1816), whose work served as an important reference for the officers and geographers on the voyage.
history_fr:
Mentelle enseignait la gĂŠographie Ă  l'Ăcole militaire et, aprĂ¨s la RĂŠvolution, dans les nouvelles ĂŠcoles ĂŠlitistes de Paris auxquelles les ĂŠlĂ¨ves ĂŠtaient admis par concours. Il fut ĂŠlu membre de l'Institut. Mentelle promouvait une nouvelle gĂŠographie qui superposait diffĂŠrentes strates d'informations sur des globes et des cartes.
Il expliqua sa mĂŠthode dans le pĂŠriodique "le magasin encyclopĂŠdique" et dans un rapport publiĂŠ en 1796, intitulĂŠ ÂŤ La GĂŠographie enseignĂŠe par une mĂŠthode nouvelle, ou application de la SynthĂ¨se Ă  l'ĂŠtude de la gĂŠographie, ouvrage destinĂŠ aux Ăcoles primaires, avec neuf cartes enluminĂŠes Âť.
history:
Mentelle taught geography at the Ecole militaire and after the Revolution, in the new elitist schools in Paris to which students were admitted by competitive examinations. He was elected to membership of the Institut*. Mentelle promoted a new geography that superposed different layers of information onto globes and maps.
He explained his method in le magasin encyclopĂŠdique and in a report published in 1796, entitled âLa GĂŠographie enseignĂŠe par une mĂŠthode nouvelle, ou application de la SynthĂ¨se Ă  lâĂŠtude de la gĂŠographie, ouvrage destinĂŠ aux Ăcoles primaires, avec neuf cartes enluminĂŠesâ. (Geography taught by a new method, or the application of synthesis to the study of geography, intended for teaching in elementary schools, with nine illuminated maps.)

characteristic_fr:
Cap Clairault se situe au sud de Yallingup, dans la rĂŠgion cĂ´tiĂ¨re comprise entre le cap Naturaliste et le cap Leeuwin, au sein de la rĂŠgion des Caps.
Alexis-Claude Clairaut (1713â1765), plus communĂŠment dĂŠsignĂŠ sous le nom de Clairault, fut un ĂŠminent mathĂŠmaticien et astronome franĂ§ais dont les travaux marquĂ¨rent profondĂŠment les dĂŠbats scientifiques du XVIIIe siĂ¨cle.
characteristic:
Cape Clairault is located south of Yallingup in the coastal region between Cape Naturaliste and Cape Leeuwin, in the Capes Region.
Alexis Claude Clairaut (1713â1765), often referred to as Clairault, was a distinguished French mathematician and astronomer whose contributions profoundly influenced the scientific discourse of the 18th century.
history_fr: Figure centrale de la cĂŠlĂ¨bre expĂŠdition en Laponie â Pour approfondir ce sujet, on se reportera utilement Ă  lâentrĂŠe ĂŽle Maupertuis, qui dĂŠtaille cet ĂŠpisode marquant â Clairaut y effectua des observations rigoureuses qui jouĂ¨rent un rĂ´le dĂŠterminant dans la confirmation de lâhypothĂ¨se newtonienne et huygĂŠnienne relative Ă  lâaplatissement polaire de la Terre. Ses recherches ne se limitĂ¨rent pas Ă  cette entreprise : elles aboutirent Ă  lâĂŠnonciation du thĂŠorĂ¨me de Clairaut, rĂŠsultat fondamental exposĂŠ dans son ouvrage majeur, ThĂŠorie de la figure de la Terre, tirĂŠe des principes de lâhydrostatique (1743), oĂš il analyse lâĂŠquilibre des fluides et la forme de notre planĂ¨te.
history: A pivotal figure in the celebrated expedition to Laplandâdetailed further in the entry Ăle MaupertuisâClairautâs meticulous observations played a crucial role in corroborating the Newton-Huygens hypothesis regarding the Earthâs polar flattening. His scholarly pursuits extended beyond this expedition, culminating in the formulation of Clairautâs theorem, a foundational result presented in his seminal work, ThĂŠorie de la figure de la Terre tirĂŠe des principes de l'hydrostatique (1743), which examined the equilibrium of fluids and the shape of the Earth.

characteristic_fr: Le cap Naturaliste constitue lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ la plus septentrionale de la crĂŞte Leeuwin-Naturaliste, sĂŠparant les eaux relativement abritĂŠes de la baie GĂŠographe de lâĂŠtendue ouverte de lâocĂŠan Indien austral. Le cap tire son nom de la seconde corvette de lâexpĂŠdition Baudin, commandĂŠe par Jacques FĂŠlix Emmanuel Hamelin. Construite Ă  lâorigine comme gabarre sous le nom de La MenaĂ§ante, elle fut acquise pour le voyage, rebaptisĂŠe Le Naturaliste en 1800, puis refondue et reclassĂŠe en corvette.
characteristic: Cape Naturaliste marks the northernmost extremity of the Leeuwin-Naturaliste Ridge, delineating the relatively sheltered waters of Geographe Bay from the open expanse of the southern Indian Ocean. The cape derives its name from the second corvette of the Baudin expedition, commanded by Jacques FĂŠlix Emmanuel Hamelin. Originally constructed as a storeship under the name La MenaĂ§ante, she was purchased for the voyage, rechristened Le Naturaliste in 1800, and subsequently refitted and reclassified as a corvette.
history_fr: LâamĂŠnagement de la gabarre ĂŠtait identique Ă  celui du GĂŠographe, Ă  quelques modifications prĂ¨s, dĂŠcrites par le sous-ingĂŠnieur Lefevre : Une cale Ă  vin entiĂ¨rement Ă  lâavant, la grande cabine ĂŠquipĂŠe dâarmoires de type bibliothĂ¨que sur les deux cĂ´tĂŠs, le bĂ˘timent est gouvernĂŠ Ă  la barre franche... .LâĂŠquipage du Naturaliste comptait initialement environ 88 Ă  90 hommes au dĂŠpart du Havre.
history: The fitting-out of the storeship was the same as that of the GĂŠographe, with a few modifications described by the assistant engineer Lefevre: âA wine hold entirely forwardâŚ the great cabin fitted with bookcase-type lockers on both sidesâŚ the vessel is steered with a tiller.â The crew of the Naturaliste initially consisted of approximately 88 to 90 men when they left France. As with the GĂŠographe, the number of crew members varied throughout the voyage due to illness, death, and transfers between the two ships.

characteristic_fr: Anse Depuch [Eagle Bay], une petite crique au nord de Pointe Picquet, a ĂŠtĂŠ nommĂŠe en l'honneur du minĂŠralogiste le plus confirmĂŠ de l'expĂŠdition, Louis Depuch (1774-1803).
characteristic: Anse Depuch [Eagle Bay], a small cove to the north of Pointe Picquet, was named in honour to the expeditionâs senior mineralogist Louis Depuch (1774-1803).
history_fr: Depuch avait ĂŠtĂŠ ĂŠtudiant Ă  lâĂcole des mines de Paris, premier ĂŠtablissement en France Ă  proposer des formations diplĂ´mantes en gĂŠologie. Le 30 mai 1801, Depuch, accompagnĂŠ du jardinier Anselm RiedlĂŠ, fit partie dâune petite ĂŠquipe de reconnaissance, sĂŠlectionnĂŠe par Baudin et placĂŠe sous le commandement dâHenry Freycinet. La mission de Depuch consistait Ă  ÂŤÂ reconnaĂŽtre la nature des solsÂ Âť, tandis que RiedlĂŠ ĂŠtait chargĂŠ dâĂŠtudier la vĂŠgĂŠtation des terres. Lâespace Ă  bord de la petite embarcation ĂŠtait limitĂŠ, et PĂŠron, profondĂŠment dĂŠĂ§u, ne put participer Ă  cette expĂŠdition. Toutefois, il reconnut le travail de son collĂ¨gue et souligna que ÂŤÂ M. Depuch dĂŠcouvrit un granite dâune grande beautĂŠ, formant de nombreuses couches rĂŠguliĂ¨resÂ Âť. Baudin portait une haute estime Ă  Depuch. Ă lâinstar de son commandant, ce dernier trouva la mort lors du voyage de retour, sur lâĂŽle de France.
history: He had been a student at the Ecole des mines in Paris, the first institution in France to offer degree courses in geology. On the 30 May 1801, Depuch, together with the gardener Anselm Riedle*, was one of a small reconnaissance party, selected by Baudin, under the command of Henry Freycinet. Depuch was given the task âto reconnoitre the type of soilâ, while Riedle was to examine the landâs vegetation. There was not enough space on board the little boat, and PĂŠron was bitterly disappointed not to have been part of the mission. However, he acknowledges his colleagueâs work and notes that, âM. Depuch found a very beautiful type of granite, forming very many regular layersâ. Baudin held Depuch in high esteem. Like his commander, Depuch died on the return voyage, on the Isle de France.

characteristic_fr: Pointe Picquet est un site situĂŠ Ă  proximitĂŠ dâEagle Bay, oĂš la cĂ´te, caractĂŠrisĂŠe par des roches ocre rouge, sâavance dans les eaux plus profondes de la baie Geographe. Ce lieu est particuliĂ¨rement connu des observateurs de cĂŠtacĂŠs. Il doit son nom Ă  lâenseigne de vaisseau Antoine Furcy Picquet (1777â1831), qui fut chargĂŠ de commander la chaloupe du Naturaliste avec pour mission de ÂŤ dĂŠterminer la position exacte du cap Naturaliste Âť, qui marque lâentrĂŠe mĂŠridionale de la baie Geographe.
characteristic: Point Picquet is a location near Eagle Bay where the coastline with Red ochre rocks protrudes into the deeper water of Geographe Bay. It is well known by whale watchers. It was named after Midshipman Antoine Furcy Piquet (1777-1831) who was placed in command of the shipâs long boat with orders âto fix the exact position of Cape Naturalisteâ, at the southern entrance to Geographe Bay.
history_fr:
Picquet ĂŠtait accompagnĂŠ de lâastronome Pierre-FranĂ§ois Bernier et du gĂŠographe Charles-Pierre Boullanger. Ils constatĂ¨rent que " cette pointe [ĂŠtait] dĂŠfendue de toutes parts par de grosses roches, sur lesquelles la mer brisoit avec fureur". Ils furent contraints " Ă  passer le reste du jour, toute la nuit et une partie du lendemain, sans pouvoir regagner le navire, dont le vent nous ĂŠcartoit sans cesse, en nous entraĂŽnant au large". Ils notaient encore que "la cĂ´te par-tout parut inabordable" et Piquet signala que " un peu dans lâE. de ces rochers une barre qui dĂŠferle avec force, sur un fond de 7_Ă  8 brasses, au moment OĂš lâon sây attend le moins il importe de sâen dĂŠfier. ".
En 1801, Ă  la demande de Baudin, Picquet dĂŠbarqua Ă  Timor en raison de son insubordination et de lâopinion dĂŠfavorable que le commandant avait de ses compĂŠtences maritimes. Pourtant, Freycinet lui rendit hommage en nommant la pointe Picquet dans lâatlas. De retour en France, lâavancement de Picquet dans la Marine fut lent.
history: Picquet was accompanied by the astronomer Pierre-FranĂ§ois Bernier* and the geographer Charles-Pierre Boullanger*. Theyâfound the point completely surrounded by great rocksâ, and the shore âunapproachable everywhereâ. For some time, he was âunable to regain the ship, from which the wind was constantly carrying us away, taking us to sea.â In his report, Piquet explained that âthe coast offered no place for landing, being bordered by reefs and sandy beaches upon which the sea breaks violentlyâ, and that âthe cloudy sky prevented any observations from being takenâ. Picquet disembarked at Timor in 1801, at Baudinâs request, because of his insubordination and as the commander did not think much of Picquetâs seafaring skills. Yet, Freycinet honoured him in the atlas with Pointe Picquet. Back in France, Picquetâs promotion in the Navy progressed slowly.

characteristic_fr:
La baie GĂŠographe est une vaste ĂŠchancrure situĂŠe sur la cĂ´te sud-ouest de lâAustralie occidentale. Elle doit son nom au navire proncipal de lâexpĂŠdition, le GĂŠographe, une appellation choisie par Baudin et, fait notable, validĂŠe dans la nomenclature adoptĂŠe par PĂŠron comme par Freycinet. 
Ce choix fait  ĂŠcho Ă  la nomenclature de lâexpĂŠdition dâEntrecasteaux, dont la Baie de la Recherche commĂŠmorait elle aussi le bĂ˘timent principal. Cette continuitĂŠ souligne lâimportance de la baie GĂŠographe en tant que premiĂ¨re dĂŠcouverte de lâexpĂŠdition et premier lieu dâinvestigation â son ÂŤ premier thĂŠĂ˘tre dâopĂŠrations Âť et, selon Freycinet, ÂŤ le plus remarquable Âť site de la Terre de Leuwin.
characteristic:
Geographe Bay is a broad indentation on the south-west coast of Western Australia. It takes its name from the expeditionâs principal vessel, the GĂŠographe, a designation chosen by Baudin and, significantly, one that was retained in the nomenclature adopted by both PĂŠron and Freycinet.
The choice echoes the naming practice of the dâEntrecasteaux expedition, whose Baie de la Recherche also commemorated its own lead vessel. This continuity underscores the significance of GĂŠographe Bay as the expeditionâs earliest discovery and primary site of investigationâits âfirst theatre of operationâ and, in Freycinetâs words, âthe most remarkableâ locality along the Land of Leeuwin.
history_fr:
Le GĂŠographe ĂŠtait une corvette de la classe Serpente, armĂŠe de vingt canons. LancĂŠ sous le nom dâUranie en 1797, puis rebaptisĂŠ GalatĂŠe en 1799, le navire ne fut cependant pas immĂŠdiatement mis Ă  lâeau : son constructeur, qui nâavait pas ĂŠtĂŠ payĂŠ, sây refusa jusquâĂ  ce quâil fĂťt acquis pour les besoins de lâexpĂŠdition. Il reĂ§ut alors le nom de GĂŠographe. AprĂ¨s son retour en France en 1804, le bĂ˘timent fut dĂŠsarmĂŠ en dĂŠcembre 1807, tout en continuant Ă  servir comme navire de service.
Câest dans cette baie que, entre les 30 et 31 mai, puis le 10 juin 1801, les premiers dĂŠbarquements de lâexpĂŠdition eurent lieu, donnant lieu aux premiĂ¨res reconnaissances Ă  terre en Terres australes ainsi quâaux premiĂ¨res rencontres consignĂŠes avec les habitants aborigĂ¨nes.
history:
The GĂŠographe was a twenty-gun corvette of the Serpente class. Launched under the name Uranie in 1797 and renamed GalatĂŠe in 1799, the vessel was not, however, immediately put afloat: her builder, having not been paid, refused to launch her until she was purchased for the purposes of the expedition. It was then that she received the name GĂŠographe. After returning to France in 1804, the ship was decommissioned in December 1807, though it continued to serve thereafter as a service vessel.
It was in this bay, on 30 and 31 May and again on 10 June 1801, that the expedition made its first landings, marking both its earliest onshore reconnaissance in the southern lands and its first recorded encounters with Aboriginal inhabitants.

characteristic_fr:
Lâestuaire de la Vasse, zone humide lagunaire situĂŠe dans le sud-ouest de lâAustralie-Occidentale, constitue un habitat essentiel pour les oiseaux aquatiques, abritant notamment la plus importante colonie de reproduction rĂŠguliĂ¨re du Cygne noir (Cygnus atratus) de lâĂtat. Son importance ĂŠcologique sâĂŠtend au soutien dâune flore et dâune faune endĂŠmiques diversifiĂŠes, faĂ§onnĂŠes par les processus hydrologiques et gĂŠologiques uniques de lâHolocĂ¨ne (hausse et stabilisation du niveau marin, dĂŠpĂ´ts sĂŠdimentaires ayant engendrĂŠ des eaux peu profondes, riches en nutriments, et une mosaĂŻque dâhabitats variĂŠs).
Câest dans la baie GĂŠographe que se dĂŠroula le premier ĂŠvĂŠnement tragique de lâexpĂŠdition. Le marin Thomas TimothĂŠe Vasse, tentant de regagner son navire en embarquant dans une chaloupe, perdit prise sur la corde et fut emportĂŠ par les vagues. La riviĂ¨re Vasse perpĂŠtue depuis sa mĂŠmoire par son nom.
characteristic:
The Vasse Estuary, a lagoonal wetland in southwestern Western Australia, is a vital habitat for waterbirds, including the largest regular breeding colony of the Black Swan (Cygnus atratus) in the state. Its ecological significance extends to supporting diverse endemic flora and fauna, shaped by the estuaryâs unique hydrological and Holocene geological processes (Sea-level rise and stabilization, sediment deposition that created the shallow, nutrient-rich waters and diverse habitats). 
It was in Geographe bay where the voyageâs first tragic events occurred. The unfortunate sailor, Thomas TimothĂŠe Vasse, in his attempt to board the long boat to return to his ship, lost his grip on the rope and was washed away by the waves. The Vasse River has been named in his memory.
history_fr: Lâun des aspirants du GĂŠographe, un certain Breton, a suggĂŠrĂŠ que Vasse, ĂŠtant fortement enivrĂŠ, nâaurait pas rĂŠussi Ă  se hisser Ă  bord de lâembarcation.
history: One of Le GĂŠographeâs midshipmen, Breton*, has suggested that, being quite drunk, Vasse could not pull himself aboard the boat.

characteristic_fr: Les rĂŠcifs du Naturaliste appartiennent Ă  un ensemble de rĂŠcifs linĂŠaires interprĂŠtĂŠs comme les vestiges dâun ancien littoral. Ils tirent leur nom du second navire de lâexpĂŠdition, le Naturaliste, corvette consĹur du GĂŠographe.

history_fr: PĂŠron rapporte que le 10 juin 1801, en pleine tempĂŞte, " nous courions sur ce redoutable rĂŠcif du Naturaliste, que nous avions reconnu dans la journĂŠe du 30 mai. Nous eĂťmes beaucoup de peine Ă  lâĂŠviter ; et pour y parvenir, il nous fallut, malgrĂŠ les rafales, charger le navire de voiles, au risque de perdre notre mĂ˘ture."t1, p.101
history: Peron reports that on 10 June 1801, in the midst of a storm, "we were sailing towards the formidable Naturalist Reef, which we had recognised on 30 May. We had great difficulty avoiding it; and to do so, despite the gusts, we had to set the ship's sails, at the risk of losing our masts.

characteristic_fr: La pointe du Casuarina est situĂŠ Ă  Bunbury et marque la limite nord de la baie de Geographe. Il tire son nom du troisiĂ¨me navire de l'expĂŠdition Baudin, la goĂŠlette Le Casuarina (voir l'entrĂŠe Casuarina Islets*, en Australie-MĂŠridionale).
characteristic: Casuarina Point is located in Bunbury and marks the northern boundary of Geographe Bay. It is named after the third ship of the Baudin expedition, the schooner Le Casuarina (see the entry Ilots du Casuarina*, in South Australia).
history_fr: PĂŠron note: "Ce fut vers le milieu de la journĂŠe du 9 [mars 1803] que M. Freycinet atteignit la pointe Nord de la baie du GĂŠographe, qu'il dĂŠsigna sous le nom de Pointe du Casuarina. DerriĂ¨re se montroit un petit port bien abritĂŠ; mais jugeant avec raison qu'il ne contenoit pas assez d'eau, mĂŞme pour son foible navire, notre compagnon poursuivit sa route au Nord."
history: PĂŠron notes: "It was around midday on the 9th [9 March 1803] that Mr Freycinet reached the northern tip of Geographer Bay, which he named Casuarina Point. Behind it lay a small, well-sheltered harbour; but as he correctly judged that it did not contain enough water even for his small vessel, our companion continued northwards."

characteristic_fr: Au nord de Pointe Casuarina, un lagon/marais protĂŠgĂŠ de la mer (en rĂŠalitĂŠ un estuaire) a ĂŠtĂŠ baptisĂŠ Port Leschenault, [estuaire Leschenault], en l'honneur du botaniste Jean Baptiste Leschenault de la Tour (1773-1826).
characteristic: To the north of Pointe Casuarina, a lagoon/marsh protected from the sea (in fact, an estuary) was named Port Leschenault, [Leschenault Estuary] after the botanist Jean Baptiste Leschenault de la Tour (1773-1826).
history_fr:
Le 4 juin 1801, Leschenault, accompagnĂŠ de PĂŠron et d'autres membres de l'ĂŠquipage, remonta la riviĂ¨re Vasse et dĂŠcouvrit, au nord de celle-ci, son estuaire. Ils aperĂ§urent quelques indigĂ¨nes, qui gardĂ¨rent les FranĂ§ais Ă  distance.
Dans son journal, Leschenault suggĂŠra que dans la baie GĂŠographe, ÂŤ La culture en serait dâautant plus facile quâon ne rencontre ni pierres, ni pentes, rapides, et quâil ne faudrait retourner la terre que peu profondĂŠment ; les paturages surtout y rĂŠussiraient. Il eft dommage que ce pays paraisse manquer dâeau ; cette raison empĂŞchera les EuropĂŠens dây porter leur industrie ".
history: On the 4th of June 1801, Leschenault, with PĂŠron and other crew members, followed the river Vasse and, to the north of it, discovered its estuary. They saw some natives, who kept the French at a distance. In his journal, Leschenault suggested that in GĂŠographe Bay, âcultivation would be all the easier since there are no stones or steep slopes, and the land would only have to be turned over a little. It is a pity that this country seems to lack water; this reason will prevent the Europeans from bringing their industry thereâ (See the journal de Leschenault, chapter3).

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Bouvard est un cap calcaire bordĂŠ de plages de sable et adossĂŠ Ă  un lac allongĂŠ (le lac Clifton).
Alexis Bouvard (1747-1843) ĂŠtait un astronome franĂ§ais qui aida Freycinet en lui fournissant les observations astronomiques que lui et MĂŠchain (voir l'entrĂŠe Cap MĂŠchain en Afrique du Sud) avaient effectuĂŠes aux observatoires de Seeberg et de Paris.
characteristic:
Cape Bouvard is a limestone cape fringed by sandy beaches, and backed by an elongate lake (Lake Clifton).
Alexis Bouvard (1747-1843) was a French astronomer who helped Freycinet by providing him with astronomical observations he and MĂŠchain (see the entree Cap MĂŠchain in SA) had made at the observatories of Seeberg and Paris.
history_fr: Ces donnĂŠes et observations ont permis Ă  Freycinet et Boullanger (voir l'entrĂŠe Baie Boullanger en Tasmanie) de recalculer ou de corriger certaines latitudes et longitudes du voyage. Bouvard ĂŠtait issu d'un milieu modeste, mais en 1785, lorsqu'il quitta les Alpes pour s'installer Ă  Paris, il suivit les cours de mathĂŠmatiques gratuits dispensĂŠs par le CollĂ¨ge de France et dĂŠveloppa des compĂŠtences exceptionnelles en calcul qui lui permirent d'entrer Ă  l'Observatoire de Paris comme ĂŠtudiant astronome, puis comme astronome en 1795. Conscient du potentiel de Bouvard, Laplace lui obtint un poste au Bureau des longitudes en 1794. Il dĂŠcouvrit huit comĂ¨tes et prĂŠdit l'existence d'une planĂ¨te au-delĂ  de l'orbite d'Uranus, qui fut plus tard baptisĂŠe Neptune.
history: These data and observations allowed Freycinet and Boullanger (see entree Baie Boullanger in Tasm) to recalculate or correct some of the voyageâs latitudes and longitudes. Bouvard came from a poor background, but, in 1785, when he left the Alps to live in Paris, he attended the free mathematical courses offered by the CollĂ¨ge de France and developed great, even exceptional, computing skills that allowed him to enter the Paris Observatory as a student astronomer and then as astronomer, in 1795. Realising Bouvard's potential, Laplace arranged for him a position in the Bureau des longitudes in 1794. He discovered eight comets and predicted the existence of a planet beyond the orbit of Uranus, later named Neptune.

characteristic_fr: Le cap PĂŠron est un cap formĂŠ de falaises calcaires escarpĂŠes. C'est Freycinet qui a donnĂŠ au cap PĂŠron le nom de son collĂ¨gue, avec lequel il partageait la tĂ˘che et la responsabilitĂŠ de publier le rĂŠcit du voyage (voir l'entrĂŠe Canal PĂŠron/Hope Channel en Tasmanie).
characteristic: Cape PĂŠron is a cape formed by rugged limestone cliffs. It was Freycinet who named Cape PĂŠron after his colleague, with whom he shared the task and responsibility for publishing the account of the voyage (see the entry Canal PĂŠron/Hope Channel in Tasmania).
history_fr: Freycinet notes: "Au Nord du cap Bouvard, la cĂ´te se renfonce un peu vers l'Est jusqu'au cap PĂŠron, qui est bas et saillant. Quelques rĂŠcifs se montrent dans le voisinage, et dĂŠfendent tout passage entre ce dernier cap et lâĂŽlot qui gĂŽt au Sud de lâĂŽle Buache. Le Casuarina a tentĂŠ cette route, et l'a poursuivie jusque par 2 brasses 1/2 dâeau; les hauts fonds lâempĂŞchĂ¨rent de la continuer, et il fut obligĂŠ de chercher son salut en sâĂŠchappant au travers des brisans." De son cĂ´tĂŠ, Baudin observait que les relĂ¨vements effectuĂŠs par l'expĂŠdition en 1801 devaient ĂŞtre rectifiĂŠs et avait baptisĂŠ le cap PĂŠron ÂŤ cap de la Surprise Âť.
history: Freycinet notes: "North of Cape Bouvard, the coast curves slightly eastwards to Cape PĂŠron, which is low and protruding. A few reefs can be seen in the vicinity, blocking any passage between this cape and the islet lying south of Buache Island. The Casuarina attempted this route and continued along it until reaching a depth of two and a half fathoms; the shallows prevented it from continuing, and it was forced to seek safety by escaping through the breakers." For his part, Baudin noted that the bearings the expedition took in 1801 needed to be rectified and named cap PĂŠron as cap de la Surprise.

ausEName: Garden Island Baudin's name: le Grand Chariot (Ursa Major)
characteristic_fr:
L'ĂŽle en question se caractĂŠrise par une formation calcaire proĂŠminente, recouverte d'une fine couche de sable ĂŠolien dĂŠposĂŠe pendant une pĂŠriode de baisse significative du niveau de la mer, correspondant probablement au dernier maximum glaciaire (il y a environ 26 500 Ă  19 000 ans). 
Les traditions orales des peuples Noongar, gardiens autochtones de la rĂŠgion, comprennent des rĂŠcits de traversĂŠes vers ces ĂŽles pendant le Temps du RĂŞve, une ĂŠpoque sacrĂŠe qui englobe Ă  la fois les paysages spirituels et physiques de leurs ancĂŞtres. 
Les FranĂ§ais ont donnĂŠ Ă  l'ĂŽle le nom du gĂŠographe Jean-Nicolas Buache ou peut-ĂŞtre de son oncle, Philippe Buache (voir l'entrĂŠe Cap Buache en Tasmanie).
characteristic:
The island in question is characterized by a prominent limestone formation, overlaid by a thin veneer of aeolian sand deposited during a period of significantly lowered sea levels, likely corresponding to the Last Glacial Maximum (c. 26,500â19,000 years ago). 
Oral traditions of the Noongar peoples, the Indigenous custodians of the region, include narratives of traversing to these islands during the Dreamtimeâa sacred era that encapsulates both the spiritual and physical landscapes of their ancestors. 
The French named the island after either the geographer Jean-Nicolas Buache or possibly his uncle, Philippe Buache (see the entry for Cap Buache in Tasmania).
history_fr:
Les notes descriptives fournies par Jean-Nicolas Buache Ă  Baudin donnaient une vision limitĂŠe et plutĂ´t partiale des peuples autochtones de l'Australie occidentale. Cette impression n'a pas ĂŠtĂŠ significativement modifiĂŠe par l'expĂŠdition Baudin, qui s'est de plus en plus alignĂŠe sur le point de vue de Buache.
Bailly, le minĂŠralogiste de l'expĂŠdition, a identifiĂŠ les formations calcaires de l'ĂŽle.
history:
Jean-Nicolas Buacheâs descriptive notes provided to Baudin conveyed a limited and rather prejudiced view of the First Nations inhabitants of Western Australia. This impression was not significantly altered by the Baudin expedition, which increasingly aligned with Buache's perspective.
Bailly, the mineralogist of the expedition, identified the island's limestone formations.

ausEName: Carnac Island Baudin's name: Les Espions (the Spies)
characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Carnac est, Ă  lâinstar de Garden Island, une ĂŽle calcaire protĂŠgĂŠe en tant que rĂŠserve naturelle depuis 1963. Elle constitue un habitat majeur pour lâotarie dâAustralie, une espĂ¨ce rare. PĂŠron attribua Ă  cette petite ĂŽle le nom du chimiste Claude-Louis Berthollet (1748-1822), lequel comptait alors parmi les figures centrales du monde savant.
Peron a donnĂŠ Ă  cette petite ĂŽle le nom du chimiste Claude-Louis Berthollet (1748-1822).
characteristic:
Carnac Island is, like Garden Island, a limestone island that has been protected as a nature reserve since 1963. It is an important habitat for the rare Australian sea lion.
Peron named the small island after the chemist Claude-Louis Berthollet (1748-1822), who was at the time one of the central figures in the academic world.
history_fr:
Berthollet ĂŠtait renommĂŠ pour son utilisation du chlore et du potassium comme agents de blanchiment, ainsi que pour son soutien aux idĂŠes de $Lavoisier$Antoine_Lavoisier$. Avec son ami $Monge$Gaspard_Monge$, il joua un rĂ´le actif dans la crĂŠation de lâĂcole polytechnique. Plus tard, tous deux rencontrĂ¨rent Bonaparte en Italie, qui leur demanda dâorganiser lâexpĂŠdition dâĂgypte, au sein de laquelle un corps expĂŠditionnaire fut accompagnĂŠ par 167 savants et artistes. En 1802, Bonaparte le nomma prĂŠsident de la commission chargĂŠe de rĂŠdiger le rĂŠcit encyclopĂŠdique du voyage, "La Description de lâĂgypte" (en 11 volumes), Ĺuvre qui entrait peut-ĂŞtre en concurrence avec le rĂŠcit du voyage de Baudin par PĂŠron et Freycinet.

Au moment du retour du GĂŠographe, Berthollet publia un ouvrage introduisant la nouvelle notion dâÂŤ affinitĂŠs chimiques Âť, dans lequel il exposait ses idĂŠes rĂŠvolutionnaires : les affinitĂŠs chimiques ne sont pas fixes, mais dĂŠpendent de conditions physiques telles que la concentration, la tempĂŠrature et les proportions relatives des substances engagĂŠes dans une rĂŠaction.
history:
Berthollet was known for his use of chlorine and potassium as bleaching agents and for his support of $Lavoisier$Antoine_Lavoisier$âs ideas. With his friend $Monge$Gaspard Monge$, he took an active part in the establishment of the Ecole Polytechnique. Later they both met Bonaparte in Italy, who asked them to organize his Egyptian Expedition, on which an expeditionary force was accompanied by 167 scientists and artists. In 1802, Bonaparte appointed him President of the Commission responsible for drafting the encyclopaedic narrative of the voyage âThe Description of Egyptâ (11 volumes), which was possibly competing against PĂŠron and Freycinetâs narrative of the Baudin voyage.
At the time of the return of le GĂŠographe, Berthollet published a work that introduces the new notion of chemical affinities, where he set out his revolutionary ideas about how chemical affinities are not fixed but depend on physical conditions such as concentration, temperature, and the relative quantities of substances involved in a reaction.

characteristic_fr:
Le rĂŠcif Giraud "se distingue par la forme dâune des roches qui le composent, et qui ressemble assez bien Ă  un soulier. Cette roche sert  plus particuliĂ¨rement de refuge Ă  un grand nombre dâoiseaux de mer"  selon la description qu'en fit le mineralogiste de l'expedition, Bailly. 
Le rocher porte le nom d'Ătienne Stanislas Giraud (1778-1804, parfois orthographiĂŠ Gireau), aspirant de marine Ă  bord du Naturaliste.
characteristic:
Rocher Giraud is part of a reef and was characterised by the mineralogist of the expedition, Bailly, by âits shape that looks rather like a shoeâ.Bailly adds that âthis rock serves more specifically as a refuge for a large number of seabirds.â 
The rock bears the name of Etienne Stanislas Giraud (1778-1804, sometimes spelt Gireau), midshipman on le Naturaliste.
history_fr:
Le 17 juin 1801, $Hamelin$Jacques_FĂŠlix_Emmanuel_Hamelin$ dĂŠtacha Giraud sous les ordres de son second, le lieutenant $Pierre Bernard Milius$Pierre_Bernard_Milius% afin de reconnaĂŽtre lâĂle Buache ainsi que les ĂŽlots et rĂŠcifs environnants.
Au cours de cette opĂŠration, la chaloupe de la mission sâĂŠchoua et son mĂ˘t se brisa. Giraud regagna alors Le Naturaliste Ă  bord de lâembarcation de secours dĂŠpĂŞchĂŠe par Hamelin pour aller chercher le charpentier et rapporter Ă  Milius lâoutillage nĂŠcessaire (notamment palans, caillornes, toiles goudronnĂŠes, ancres, etc.).

Giraud joua un rĂ´le dĂŠterminant dans la remise Ă  flot de la chaloupe, et ses compĂŠtences nautiques furent hautement apprĂŠciĂŠes par Milius comme par Hamelin.
RentrĂŠ en France en 1803 Ă  bord du Naturaliste, sous le commandement de Hamelin, il mourut toutefois prĂŠmaturĂŠment quelques mois aprĂ¨s son retour.
history: On 17 June 1801, $Hamelin$Jacques_FĂŠlix_Emmanuel_Hamelin$ sent him under his second in command, lieutenant Pierre Bernard Milius, to reconnoitre the Ăle Buache and the surrounding islands and reefs. When the mission's longboat ran aground and its mast broke, Giraud returned to Le Naturaliste aboard the lifeboat sent by Hamelin to fetch the carpenter and ferry tools (such as a block and tackle, caillorne, tarpaulins, anchors, etc.) back to Milius. He played a key role in the successful re-floating of the longboat. Both Milius and Hamelin appreciated Giraudâs nautical skills. He returned to France aboard Le Naturaliste with his captain in 1803 but died prematurely just a few months after his return.

characteristic_fr:
Aujourdâhui, Applecross est une banlieue riveraine situĂŠe juste au sud du centre-ville de Perth.
Ă lâĂŠpoque de lâexpĂŠdition, le lieu ĂŠtait dĂŠcrit comme une ouverture sur lâune des rives de la Swan River, laissant supposer la confluence dâun bras oriental (le Canning River).
Le site fut nommĂŠ en lâhonneur de l'aspirant de marine Charles Moreau (1781-1808).
characteristic:
Today, Applecross is a riverside suburb located just to the south of Perth's CBD. At the time of the expedition, it was described as an opening on one of the banks of the Swan River which suggested the confluence of an eastern arm [the Canning River]. 
It was named in honour of the midshipman Charles Moreau (1781-1808).
history_fr:
Le 16 juin 1801, le capitaine Hamelin dĂŠtacha lâenseigne $Heirisson$FranĂ§ois_Heirisson$ Ă  bord de la grande chaloupe du navire pour explorer le cours de la riviĂ¨re et en ĂŠvaluer le potentiel comme source dâeau douce. $Bailly$Joseph_Charles_Bailly$ et $Moreau$Charles Moreau (marin)$ furent adjoints Ă  cette mission et y jouĂ¨rent tous deux un rĂ´le actif.
AprĂ¨s avoir rencontrĂŠ de grandes difficultĂŠs pour franchir la barre Ă  lâembouchure de la riviĂ¨re Swan, puis plus en amont, le groupe progressa probablement jusquâĂ  lâemplacement actuel du pont Whiteman, Ă  la limite orientale de Perth.
Moreau reĂ§ut le commandement de la chaloupe lorsquâHeirisson partit reconnaĂŽtre les rives.
history: On the 16 June 1801, its captain, Hamelin, dispatched ensign FranĂ§ois_Heirisson in the shipâs longboat to explore the river and assess it as a source of fresh water. Bailly, the mineralogist, and the midshipman Charles Moreau (1781-1808) were assigned to the party, and both played an active role during the mission. After encountering great difficulties in crossing the bar at the mouth of the river and further upstream, they advanced probably as far as Whiteman Bridge at the eastern edge of Perth. Baillyâs hope of reaching the hills they could see in the distance â the Darling Ranges â was not fulfilled, as the shortage of supplies made a speedy return necessary. Bailly was able to survey and describe the geology along the banks of the river and record observations of the fauna and flora. Moreau was given command of the long boat when Heirisson went exploring the shores.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Heirisson est une ĂŽle fluviale et une rĂŠserve naturelle protĂŠgĂŠe situĂŠe dans le fleuve Swan, en Australie-Occidentale. ImplantĂŠe entre les quartiers dâEast Perth et de Victoria Park, elle se distingue par sa population de kangourous gris dâAustralie-Occidentale (Macropus fuliginosus) ĂŠvoluant en libertĂŠ. Ce site revĂŞt  une importance culturelle majeure pour le peuple Noongar, illustrant les liens ancestraux des communautĂŠs autochtones avec le paysage fluvial.
LâĂŽle doit son nom Ă  lâenseigne de vaisseau FranĂ§ois Antoine Heirisson (1776â1834), qui dirigeait lâĂŠquipe chargĂŠe des relevĂŠs hydrographiques du fleuve Swan en juin 1801, dans le cadre des missions de reconnaissance menĂŠes par les FranĂ§ais sur la cĂ´te ouest de la Nouvelle-Hollande (actuelle Australie).
characteristic:
Heirisson Island is a riverine island and protected natural reserve situated within the Swan River in Western Australia. Located between the suburbs of East Perth and Victoria Park, the island is notable for its population of free-ranging western grey kangaroos. It constitutes a site of cultural significance for the Noongar people, reflecting the long-standing Indigenous relationships with the riverine landscape. 
The island is named after FranĂ§ois Antoine Heirisson (1776â1834), who was given command of the reconnaissance mission to survey the Swan River in June 1801, as part of the French exploratory efforts along the west coast of New Holland (present-day Australia).
history_fr: Dans son rapport de mission sur lâexploration du fleuve Swan (voir EntrĂŠe Moreau), Heirisson dĂŠcrit les difficultĂŠs rencontrĂŠes par lui et son ĂŠquipage, notamment lâincident survenu le 21 juin 1801, lorsque leur embarcation sâĂŠchoua en descendant le fleuve. LâĂŠquipage dut alors traĂŽner la chaloupe dans la vase pendant quatre heures. Pour permettre au bateau de flotter Ă  nouveau, Heirisson ordonna de dĂŠposer son grĂŠement lourd sur une ĂŽle voisine, qui fut par la suite baptisĂŠe ĂŽle Heirisson en mĂŠmoire de cet ĂŠvĂŠnement.
history: In his mission report to explore the river Swan (see EntrĂŠe Moreau), Heirisson details the problems he and his crew encountered, such as, on the 21 June 1801, when the boat became grounded while descending the river, which led the crew to drag the long boat on the silt for 4 hours. To allow the boat to float again, Heirisson decided to put down its heavy riggings on a nearby Island, that was given the name of Heirisson Island.

characteristic_fr: Garden Island, les ĂŽles Carnac et les rĂŠcifs adjacents, ainsi que Rottnest Island, ont ĂŠtĂŠ collectivement baptisĂŠs Louis-NapolĂŠon par les FranĂ§ais, d'aprĂ¨s le nom du frĂ¨re de NapolĂŠon. L'empereur a nommĂŠ son frĂ¨re Louis roi de Hollande en 1806 (voir l'entrĂŠe Baie Louis, en SA).
characteristic: Garden Island and Carnac Islands and adjacent reefs, together with Rottnest Island, were collectively given the name of Louis-NapolĂŠon by the French, from the name of Napoleonâs sibling. The emperor appointed his brother Louis as king of Holland, in 1806 (see entry Baie Louis, in SA).
history_fr:
LâarrivĂŠe de Louis-NapolĂŠon Ă  Amsterdam fut marquĂŠe par un accueil triomphal, la ville devenant alors le cĹur politique de son nouveau royaume. Soucieux de sâintĂŠgrer Ă  sa nation dâadoption, il sâefforĂ§a de maĂŽtriser la langue locale, mais une malencontreuse prononciation de ÂŤ roi de Hollande Âť, perĂ§ue comme ÂŤ lapin de Hollande Âť, donna naissance Ă  une expression restĂŠe dans les mĂŠmoires.
Pourtant, au-delĂ  de cet ĂŠpisode anecdotique, Louis-NapolĂŠon cultiva une profonde affection pour le peuple nĂŠerlandais, sâattachant Ă  comprendre ses aspirations, ses usages et ses traditions. Cette proximitĂŠ, bien que sincĂ¨re, le conduisit progressivement Ă  sâĂŠmanciper de lâautoritĂŠ impĂŠriale, un positionnement qui scella finalement sa destitution par NapolĂŠon en 1810, peu aprĂ¨s lâachĂ¨vement de la nomenclature ĂŠtablie par PĂŠron.
history: Louis-NapolĂŠon made a glorious entry in Amsterdam, which became the capital of his kingdom. Attempting to master his subjectsâ language, he mispronounced âKing of Hollandâ to sound like âRabbit of Hollandâ, a slip of the tongue that became a famous expression. However, king Louis-NapolĂŠon developed a great sympathy for the Dutch people, their needs, practices, and traditions, to a degree that led him to defy NapolĂŠonâs authority, which resulted in his removal from office by the emperor in 1810, a few years after PĂŠron had completed his nomenclature.

characteristic_fr:
Depuis Phillip Point, Ă  l'extrĂŠmitĂŠ sud-est de l'ĂŽle Rottnest, une langue de rĂŠcif calcaire Tamala s'ĂŠtend dans les eaux environnantes, formant ce que l'on appelle localement la jetĂŠe naturelle.
Ce cap fut nommĂŠ par les FranĂ§ais en l'honneur de Pierre Ange FranĂ§ois Xavier Faure (1777-1855), gĂŠographe Ă  bord du Naturaliste.
characteristic:
From Phillip Point at the south-eastern extremity of Rottnest Island, a tongue of Tamala Limestone reef extends into the surrounding waters, forming what is locally known as the Natural Jetty.
The point was named after Pierre Ange FranĂ§ois Xavier Faure (1777-1855), the geographer aboard Le Naturaliste.
history_fr: Le 17 juin 1801, Hamelin dĂŠpĂŞcha Freycinet, accompagnĂŠ du gĂŠographe Pierre Faure, Ă  bord dâune petite embarcation afin de procĂŠder au levĂŠ de lâĂŽle de Rottnest ÂŤ pour fixer avec exactitude la partie de cĂ´te que nous avons en vueÂť. Cette opĂŠration ne put toutefois ĂŞtre menĂŠe Ă  terme en raison de conditions mĂŠtĂŠorologiques dĂŠfavorables, notamment de fortes pluies et des vents soutenus, ainsi que dâautres contraintes matĂŠrielles. Parmi celles-ci figuraient lâĂŠchouage de lâembarcation sur un long banc de rochers escarpĂŠs et la rudesse du couvert boisĂŠ de lâĂŽle, un terrain qui mit particuliĂ¨rement Ă  lâĂŠpreuve les gĂŠographes, alors encore peu expĂŠrimentĂŠs dans le travail de terrain Ă  ce stade du voyage. Hamelin note dans son journal de mer: "Ă 19 heures Âž, expĂŠdiĂŠ le 2áľ pousse-pied, notre unique et derniĂ¨re ressource, pour porter six jours de vivres et un calfat pour le petit canot sur lâĂŽle Rotenest. Je lui ai fait donner quelques pelles et palans, et effet nĂŠcessaires pour un ĂŠchouage, et ai mandĂŠ au citoyen Freycinet de me renvoyer le plus tĂ´t possible le citoyen Faure, ingĂŠnieur-gĂŠographe, peu au fait de passer de si mauvaises nuits Ă  la belle ĂŠtoile dans un temps de pluie."[19-20 juin 1801]
history: On 17 June 1801, Hamelin dispatched Freycinet, accompanied by the geographer Pierre Faure (see also the entry Cap Faure in Tasmania), in a small boat to survey Rottnest Island âin order to work on the map of the island and the harbourâ. Their survey, however, was not completed due to adverse weather conditions, including heavy rain and strong winds, as well as other practical difficulties. These included the vessel running aground on a long bank of steep rocks and the islandâs rough woodland terrain, which proved particularly challenging for the geographers, who at this stage of the voyage had limited experience of fieldwork.prevented them from completing their work. can we start with In his sea journal, Hamelin reports: âAt 7Âž p.m., the second push-boat was dispatched, our sole and final resource, to carry six daysâ provisions and a caulker for the small boat to Rottnest Island. I had it supplied with several shovels and tackle and equipment necessary in the event of a grounding, and requested that Citizen Freycinet send back to me as soon as possible Citizen Faure, engineer-geographer, little accustomed to spending such miserable nights in the open air in rainy weather.â [19-20 June 1801]

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Vlamingh est la pointe la plus occidentale et la zone la plus exposĂŠe de l'ĂŽle Rottnest. 
Il a ĂŠtĂŠ nommĂŠ par les FranĂ§ais en l'honneur de Charles Pierre Boullanger (1772-1813, orthographiĂŠ Boulanger dans le journal de Baudin), qui ĂŠtait l'ingĂŠnieur gĂŠographe en chef de l'expĂŠdition Ă  bord du GĂŠographe.
characteristic:
Cape Vlamingh is the westernmost point and most exposed area of Rottnest Island. 
It was named by the French after Charles Pierre Boullanger (1772-1813, spelt Boulanger in Baudinâs journal), who was the expedition chief engineer geographer on board le GĂŠographe.
history_fr: Afin de complĂŠter les rĂŠsultats fragmentaires obtenus en 1801, Boullanger et son collĂ¨gue Faure, Ă  bord du GĂŠographe, procĂŠdĂ¨rent en mars 1803 Ă  une sĂŠrie de relĂ¨vements cĂ´tiers le long des rivages de la Nouvelle-Hollande, entre le cap Leeuwin et lâĂŽle Rottnest. Ces observations visaient Ă  achever les travaux de levĂŠ entrepris en 1801. Il est probable que lâattribution de leurs noms Ă  deux caps distincts de lâĂŽle Rottnest reconnaisse le caractĂ¨re conjoint de leurs travaux de gĂŠographes.
history: To supplement the fragmentary results obtained in 1801, Boullanger and his colleague Faure, aboard Le GĂŠographe, conducted a series of coastal bearings in March 1803 along the shores of New Holland between Cape Leeuwin and Rottnest Island. These observations were intended to complete the survey work undertaken in 1801. It is likely in recognition of the two geographersâ collaborative efforts that their names were later assigned to two separate capes on Rottnest Island.
