history: see details

characteristic_fr:
Le lac Montbazin (ĂŠgalement connu sous le nom de Little Lagoon), situĂŠ sur la pĂŠninsule PĂŠron, est associĂŠ aux birridas, formations lacustres salĂŠes riches en gypse rĂŠsultant de lâassĂ¨chement de lacs salins enclavĂŠs Ă  la suite de la baisse du niveau marin. Ces dĂŠpĂ´ts se caractĂŠrisent par des croĂťtes blanches pulvĂŠrulentes et sont typiques des paysages du parc national FranĂ§ois-PĂŠron, au sein duquel se trouve le lac.
Le lac tient son nom de Louis Charles Gaspard Bonnefoi de Montbazin (1778â1857), aspirant de premiĂ¨re classe Ă  bord du GĂŠographe (voir ĂŠgalement le port Montbazin, en Tasmanie).
characteristic:
Lake Montbazin (also known as Little Lagoon), on the Peron Peninsula, is associated with birridas: gypsum-rich salt lake beds formed from dried, landlocked saline lakes following periods of lowered sea levels. These features produce powdery white crusts and are characteristic of the landscape within FranĂ§ois Peron National Park, where the lake is located.
The lake was named after Louis Charles Gaspard Bonnefoi de Montbazin (1778â1857), a first-class midshipman aboard the GĂŠographe (see also Port Montbazin, Tasmania).
history_fr:
Le 19 mars 1803, Baudin envoya une ĂŠquipe en chaloupe sous le commandement de Montbazin. Outre la tentative dâentrer en contact avec les AborigĂ¨nes que la mission conduite par l'officier Ronsard nâavait pas rĂŠussi Ă  rencontrer, Montbazin reĂ§ut pour instruction ÂŤ dâemployer lâĂŠquipage Ă  se procurer du sel Âť, nĂŠcessaire Ă  la conservation du poisson pĂŞchĂŠ en grande abondance par lâĂŠquipage.
Contraint dâattendre le retour de PĂŠron, Guichenault et Petit â qui avaient rejoint la mission mais sâĂŠtaient engagĂŠs dans une excursion sans en avertir le commandement â Montbazin vit son retour Ă  bord du GĂŠographe retardĂŠ. Le commandant le sanctionna en consĂŠquence pour avoir diffĂŠrĂŠ la marche du voyage.
history:
On 19 March 1803, Baudin dispatched a party in the longboat under the command of Montbazin. In addition to attempting contact with the Aboriginal people whom the party led by the officer Ronsard had failed to encounter, Montbazin was instructed âto employ the crew in procuring salt for usâ, which was required to preserve the fish caught in abundance by the crew.
Owing to the delay caused by having to wait for PĂŠron, Guichenault, and Petitâwho had joined the party but then set off on an excursion without noticeâMontbazinâs return to Le GĂŠographe was postponed. As a result, the commander penalised him for delaying the progress of the voyage..

characteristic_fr:
Herald Bight est une baie situĂŠe Ă  lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ nord-est de la pĂŠninsule PĂŠron. Son large arc de cercle offre une protection naturelle contre les vents dominants de sud-ouest.
La baie fut dĂŠsignĂŠe par les FranĂ§ais sous le nom de baie de lâAttaque, Ă  la suite dâune rencontre avortĂŠe avec les AborigĂ¨nes Malgana.
characteristic:
Herald Bight is a bay located at the north-eastern extremity of the Peron Peninsula. Its broad, sweeping arc affords natural protection from the prevailing south-westerly winds.
The bay was named Baie de lâAttaque by the French, following a failed encounter with the Malgana Aboriginal people.
history_fr:
Emmanuel Hamelin envoya Ă  terre lâenseigne de vaisseau Jacques de Saint-Cricq et le minĂŠralogiste Bailly Ă  bord du petit canot, afin dâexaminer lâorigine de ces grandes et impressionnantes colonnes. Saint-Cricq y dĂŠcouvrit un foyer oĂš des AborigĂ¨nes du groupe Malgana faisaient cuire du poisson (des raies), activitĂŠ brusquement interrompue par lâarrivĂŠe soudaine des FranĂ§ais.
Les FranĂ§ais donnĂ¨rent par la suite Ă  ce lieu le nom de baie de lâAttaque, bien que les AborigĂ¨nes aient probablement agi dans une attitude dĂŠfensive plutĂ´t quâoffensive et pris la fuite, en particulier aprĂ¨s que Saint-Cricq eut tirĂŠ un coup de fusil au-dessus de leurs tĂŞtes. Voir ĂŠgalement, dans les ressources, les Rapports illustrĂŠs de missions, pĂŠninsule PĂŠron (baie des Chiens-Marins), ainsi que lâentrĂŠe cap SaintâCricq.
history:
Emmanuel Hamelin sent Sub-Lieutenant Jacques de Saint-Cricq and the mineralogist Bailly ashore in the small boat to investigate the origin of these large and striking columns. Saint-Cricq discovered a hearth where Aboriginal people of the Malgana group had been cooking fish (rays), but the activity was interrupted by the sudden arrival of the French party.
The French subsequently named the site Baie de lâAttaque (âBay of the Attackâ), although the Aboriginal people were probably acting defensively rather than aggressively and fled the scene, particularly after Saint-Cricq fired a rifle shot over their heads. See also the resources Illustrated Mission Reports, PĂŠron Peninsula (Shark Bay) and the entry Cap Saint-Cricq.

characteristic_fr: La baie situĂŠe Ă  lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ nord-ouest de la pĂŠninsule PĂŠron fut nommĂŠe en lâhonneur de William Dampier, premier Anglais Ă  avoir explorĂŠ la baie des Chiens-Marins, laissant ainsi apparaĂŽtre la reconnaissance, par lâexpĂŠdition franĂ§aise, des dĂŠcouvertes dâun prĂŠdĂŠcesseur europĂŠen.
characteristic: The bay at the north-western extremity of the PĂŠron Peninsula was named after William Dampier, the first Englishman to explore Shark Bay, thereby acknowledging, on the part of the French expedition, the discoveries of a European predecessor.
history_fr: Le 17 mars 1801, Le GĂŠographe jeta lâancre dans la baie de Dampier, oĂš Le Naturaliste avait lui-mĂŞme mouillĂŠ en juin 1801. Depuis la baie de Dampier, Baudin envoya des dĂŠtachements en canots et en chaloupe afin de complĂŠter les travaux de reconnaissance et de levĂŠ prĂŠcĂŠdemment effectuĂŠs par Hamelin et lâĂŠquipage du Naturaliste.
history: On 17 March 1801, Le GĂŠographe anchored in Dampier Bay, where Le Naturaliste itself had previously anchored in June 1801. From Dampier Bay, Baudin dispatched boat and longboat parties to complete the survey work previously carried out by Hamelin and the crew of Le Naturaliste.

characteristic_fr:
IntĂŠgrĂŠe au parc national FranĂ§ois-PĂŠron, au sein de la baie des Chiens-Marins (site naturel inscrit au patrimoine mondial de lâUNESCO), la pointe Guichenault dĂŠlimite au sud-est Herald Bight. Elle est largement recouverte de mangroves.
La pointe porte le nom dâAntoine Guichenault (1783â1867), assistant jardinier Ă  bord du GĂŠographe, fonction que lâon peut rapprocher aujourdâhui de celle dâun technicien supĂŠrieur en agronomie chargĂŠ de collections botaniques.
characteristic:
Situated within the FranĂ§ois Peron National Park, in Shark Bay (a UNESCO Natural World Heritage site), Point Guichenault forms the south-eastern boundary of Herald Bight. The point is largely covered by mangroves.
It was named after Antoine Guichenault (1783â1867), assistant gardener aboard the GĂŠographe, a role broadly comparable to that of a modern senior agronomy technician responsible for botanical collections.
history_fr: Le 19 mars 1803, PĂŠron persuada le jardinier Guichenault de lâaccompagner dans lâexploration de la cĂ´te orientale de la presquâĂŽle. MenĂŠe sans organisation prĂŠalable ni provisions suffisantes, lâexcursion se rĂŠvĂŠla ĂŠprouvante et, au retour vers la chaloupe, Guichenault, affaibli par la chaleur, la soif et lâĂŠpuisement, sâeffondra et fut momentanĂŠment incapable de poursuivre sa marche. PĂŠron proposa alors quâils sâimmergent dans lâeau de mer jusquâĂ  la poitrine et y demeurent quelques instants. Il rapporta par la suite quâÂŤune douce fraĂŽcheur sembloit pĂŠnĂŠtrer par tous les pores ... nous sentĂŽmes renaĂŽtre quelque vigueur; .... en un mot, ce bain salutaire nous arracha vraisemblablement Ă  la mort : sous sa douce influence, M. Guichenault parut se ranimer."
history: On 19 March 1803, PĂŠron persuaded the gardener Guichenault to accompany him in exploring the eastern coast of the peninsula. Undertaken without prior organisation or adequate provisions, the excursion proved arduous, and Guichenault, weakened by heat, thirst, and exhaustion, collapsed on the return journey to the longboat and was briefly unable to continue. PĂŠron then proposed that they immerse themselves in seawater up to their chests and remain there for a short time. He later recalled that âa gentle coolness seemed to penetrate every poreâŚ we felt our vigour returning; in short, this salutary bath probably saved us from death: under its gentle influence, Mr Guichenault appeared to revive.â

characteristic_fr:
Le Havre Hamelin, le plus vaste bassin de la baie des Chiens-Marins (Shark Bay est cĂŠlĂ¨bre pour ses vastes champs de stromatolites â structures vivantes formĂŠes par des colonies de cyanobactĂŠries microscopiques apparues il y a environ 3,5 milliards dâannĂŠes, parmi les premiĂ¨res formes de vie sur Terre. Les sĂŠdiments charriĂŠs dans la baie se sont liĂŠs Ă  ces micro-organismes et, au fil du temps, ont durci en fines couches superposĂŠes, donnant naissance aux formations visibles aujourdâhui.
Le bassin tient son nom du capitaine du Naturaliste, Hamelin (voir lâentrĂŠe cap Hamelin, en Australie-Occidentale).
characteristic: Hamelin Pool, the largest basin within Shark Bay is renowned for its extensive fields of stromatolitesâliving structures formed by colonies of microscopic cyanobacteria that evolved around 3.5 billion years ago and represent some of the earliest life forms on Earth. Sediments carried into the bay bonded with these organisms and, over time, hardened into thin, layered structures, creating the fossil-like formations visible today. The pool is named after Captain Hamelin of le Naturaliste (see the entry Cape Hamelin, WA).
history_fr:
En aoĂťt 1801, Hamelin envoya une expĂŠdition Ă  bord du canot du Naturaliste, placĂŠe sous le commandement de lâaspirant Charles Moreau, en compagnie du gĂŠographe Pierre Faure.
Faure et Moreau procĂŠdĂ¨rent au levĂŠ cartographique du bassin que Freycinet dĂŠsigna par la suite sous le nom de havre Hamelin (Hamelin Pool), ainsi que des baies de la cĂ´te orientale de la pĂŠninsule de PĂŠron, qui bordent les rives occidentales de ce bassin.
history:
In August 1801, Hamelin dispatched an expedition aboard the Naturalisteâs boat, under the command of midshipman Charles Moreau, accompanied by the geographer Pierre Faure.
Moreau and Faure carried out the hydrographic and cartographic survey of the basin later named Hamelin Harbour (Hamelin Pool) by Freycinet, as well as of the bays along the eastern coast of the PĂŠron Peninsula, which fringe the western shores of this basin.

characteristic_fr:
La baie de LâHaridon est lâune des baies situĂŠes sur le versant oriental de la pĂŠninsule PĂŠron, au sein du site du patrimoine mondial de la baie des Chiens-Marins (Shark Bay), dans la rĂŠgion de la Gascoyne. Les plages de la baie, comme celles des secteurs voisins, sont constituĂŠes de milliards de minuscules coquilles appartenant Ă  une seule espĂ¨ce, le fragum (Fragum cockle). Par endroits, ces dĂŠpĂ´ts atteignent une ĂŠpaisseur de prĂ¨s de dix mĂ¨tres.

FranĂ§ois Ătienne LâHaridon de CrĂŠmĂŠnec (1769â1807), ĂŠgalement orthographiĂŠ Laridon, Lharidon ou Larridon, ĂŠtait le premier chirurgien et le chef de table des savants embarquĂŠs Ă  bord du GĂŠographe. Ă ce titre, il veillait Ă  la qualitĂŠ nutritionnelle de leur alimentation.
characteristic:
L'Haridon Bight is one of the bays on the eastern side of the Peron Peninsula in the Shark Bay World Heritage Site in the Gascoyne region . Trillions of tiny shells from one type of animal, the Fragum cockle, make up the beaches here and around Lâharidon Bight. Deposits are 10 metres deep in places.
Francois Etienne LâHaridon de CrĂŠmĂŠnec (1769-1807), spelt also Laridon/Lharidon/Larridon was the first surgeon and the âchef de tableâ of the men of science on board Le GĂŠographe, taking care of the nutritious quality of their menu.
history_fr: Jean-Baptiste Lharidon fit ses ĂŠtudes de mĂŠdecine Ă  lâĂcole de santĂŠ navale de Brest, lâun des principaux centres de formation des chirurgiens et mĂŠdecins de la Marine royale. De retour en France, il publia en 1804 une thĂ¨se manifestement nourrie par son expĂŠrience Ă  bord des navires de lâexpĂŠdition Baudin, sur les affections dĂŠpressives de lâĂ˘me, dans laquelle il avanĂ§ait que les troubles moraux et psychologiques constituaient lâune des causes majeures du scorbut. ProfondĂŠment affectĂŠ par la dĂŠpression, Lharidon mit fin Ă  ses jours en 1807.
history: Lharidon studied medicine at the naval medical school in Brest, one of the principal training centres for surgeons and physicians in the Royal Navy. After returning to France, he published a medical thesis in 1804 a work clearly informed by his experience aboard the vessels of the Baudin expedition, on the depressive conditions of the soul, in which he argued that moral and psychological distress constituted a primary cause of scurvy. Marked by his own struggles with depression, Lharidon took his own life in 1807s.

characteristic_fr:
La pointe Petit est un cap bas et sableux situĂŠ Ă  lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ septentrionale dâune cellule sĂŠdimentaire dâenviron trente kilomĂ¨tres de longueur, qui prend naissance sur la bordure orientale de la baie de LâHaridon.
Lors de la mission de reconnaissance placĂŠe sous le commandement de lâaspirant Montbazin, lâartiste Nicolas Martin Petit (1777â1804) se joignit au dĂŠtachement avec pour mission de reprĂŠsenter le village autochtone et la forme des habitations. Si cette entreprise se rĂŠvĂŠla plus ĂŠprouvante que prĂŠvu, Petit fut ultĂŠrieurement honorĂŠ dans la toponymie par lâattribution de son nom Ă  ce point du littoral.
characteristic:
Petit Point is a low, sandy headland located at the northern extremity of a sediment cell approximately 30 kilometres in length, which originates along the eastern margin of LâHaridon Bight.
During the reconnaissance conducted under the command of the midshipman Montbazin, the artist Nicolas Martin Petit (1777â1804) joined the party with the specific task of drawing the Aboriginal settlement and the form of the shelters. Although the mission proved more arduous than anticipated, Petit was later honored through the attribution of his name to this coastal point.
history_fr:
PĂŠron raconte que, lors de son excursion sur la cĂ´te orientale de la presquâĂŽle PĂŠron, en compagnie de Guichenault et de Petit (19 mars 1803), alors quâils avaient les pieds dans lâeau pour recueillir de beaux spĂŠcimens de coquillages, un requin sâapprocha soudainement de Petit. PĂŠron ĂŠcrit :
ÂŤ Celui-ci [Petit], dans sa frayeur, fit feu sur lâanimal. Ne doutant pas que les sauvages, dont nous venions dâapercevoir des traces rĂŠcentes sur la grĂ¨ve, ne sâempressassent dây revenir Ă  ce bruit, je proposai Ă  mes compagnons de quitter aussitĂ´t la mer pour aller reprendre nos vĂŞtements et nous cacher dans les broussaillesâŚ Mais lâimprudent M. Petit sâobstina, malgrĂŠ nos priĂ¨res, Ă  rester dans la mer, affectant mĂŞme de se railler de notre prudence. Son indiscrĂ¨te sĂŠcuritĂŠ ne tarda pas Ă  faire place Ă  lâĂŠpouvante. En effetâŚ nous aperĂ§Ăťmes une troupe de sauvages qui, du sommet dâune grosse dune (cap Guichenault), se prĂŠcipitoient sur la grĂ¨ve. Ă cette vue, M. Petit lui-mĂŞme, plein de terreur, saisit Ă  la hĂ˘te quelques-uns de ses vĂŞtements et accourut Ă  moitiĂŠ nu pour venir nous rejoindreâŚ Âť
Petit dĂŠcĂŠda quelques mois aprĂ¨s son retour, des suites dâune grave chute ayant entraĂŽnĂŠ une gangrĂ¨ne.
history:
Peron recounts that during his excursion on the east coast (19 march 1803) of the Peron Peninsula in the company of Guichenault and Petit, while they were wading in the water to collect beautiful specimens of shells, a shark suddenly approached Petit. Peron writes: "Petit, in his fear, fired at the animal. Not doubting that the savages, whose recent tracks we had just seen on the shore, would hurry back at the sound, I suggested to my companions that we leave the sea immediately to retrieve our clothes and hide in the bushes. But the imprudent Mr Petit insisted, despite our pleas, on remaining in the sea, even mocking our caution. His indiscreet confidence soon gave way to terror. Indeed... we saw a group of savages who, from the top of a large dune (Cap Guichenault), were rushing towards the shore. At this sight, even Mr Petit, filled with terror, hastily grabbed some of his clothes and ran half-naked to join us..."
Petit died a few months after his return from a bad fall that resulted in a gangrene.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle est situĂŠe au sein du parc marin de Shark Bay et fait partie du site du patrimoine mondial de Shark Bay. Elle correspond Ă  une portion ĂŠmergĂŠe du Faure Sill, un haut-fond sableux reposant sur un substrat de grĂ¨s, qui traverse le secteur septentrional du Havre Hamelin/Hamelin Pool. Aujourdâhui, lâĂŽle constitue un sanctuaire exempt dâespĂ¨ces introduites, jouant un rĂ´le important dans la conservation des mammifĂ¨res australiens menacĂŠs ainsi que comme site de reproduction pour les oiseaux marins.
LâĂŽle porte le nom du gĂŠographe Pierre Faure, qui fut le premier EuropĂŠen Ă  en effectuer le levĂŠ cartographique (voir lâentrĂŠe cap Faure, en Tasmanie et en Australie-Occidentale).
characteristic:
The island lies within the Shark Bay Marine Park and forms part of the Shark Bay World Heritage Area. It is an emergent section of the Faure Sill, a sandbar resting on a sandstone substrate that spans the northern sector of Hamelin Pool. Today, the island functions as a feral-free sanctuary, playing a significant role in the conservation of threatened Australian mammals and as a breeding site for seabirds.
The island bears the name of the geographer Pierre Faure, who was the first European to chart this feature. (see the entry Cap Faure* in Tasmania and in WA).
history_fr:
PĂŠron rapporte, dans une note de bas de page relative Ă  la baie des Chiens-Marins (Shark Bay) :
ÂŤ Lors du premier sĂŠjour du Naturaliste dans la baie des Chiens-Marins [aout 1801], les tortues ĂŠtaient si abondantes sur lâĂŽle Faure que ceux de nos gens [le dĂŠtachement envoyĂŠ sous le commandement de Moreau, accompagnĂŠ de Faure ] qui la dĂŠcouvrirent purent, en quelques instants, en remplir leur canot ; il nây avait absolument quâĂ  les prendre. AprĂ¨s avoir dĂŠpecĂŠ huit de ces tortues, afin de se dĂŠbarrasser de leurs carapaces et de leurs viscĂ¨res, ils en rapportĂ¨rent dix-huit autres vivantes, qui pesaient de 250 Ă  300 livres chacune. Celles dont ils nâavaient conservĂŠ que la chair ĂŠtaient encore plus fortes. Âť
(Note communiquĂŠe par M. Ransonnet, qui se trouvait, Ă  lâĂŠpoque dont il sâagit, lui-mĂŞme embarquĂŠ sur le Naturaliste.)*
history:
PĂŠron reports in a footnote relating to Shark Bay:
âDuring the Naturalisteâs first stay in Shark Bay [August 1801], turtles were so abundant on Faure Island that those of our men who discovered it [the party sent under the command of Moreau, accompanied by Faure] were able, in a matter of moments, to fill their canoe; there was nothing to do but pick them up. After cutting up eight of these turtles in order to remove their shells and entrails, they brought back eighteen others alive, each weighing between 250 and 300 pounds. Those from which only the flesh was retained were even larger.â
(Note communicated by Mr Ransonnet, who was himself on board the Naturaliste at the time in question.)
On Faureâs earliest sketches, the island was initially named Ăle dâAuteuil.

La carte fait apparaĂŽtre lâĂŽle Faure Ă  lâest, dĂŠsignĂŠe initialement sous le nom dâÂŤ ĂŽle dâAuteuil Âť.

characteristic_fr: Haut-fond sableux traversant le secteur septentrional du havre Hamelin (Hamelin Pool). Le nom dâÂŤ ĂŽle des Tortues Âť fait rĂŠfĂŠrence Ă  lâabondance de tortues observĂŠe Ă  lâĂŠpoque de sa dĂŠcouverte par le canot du Naturaliste.
characteristic: A sandy shoal crossing the northern sector of Hamelin Harbour (Hamelin Pool). The name âTurtle Islandâ refers to the abundance of turtles observed at the time of its discovery by the longboat of the Naturaliste.
history_fr: Dans la baie des Chiens-Marins, le 17 mars 1803, afin de subvenir Ă  lâalimentation de son ĂŠquipage, Baudin envoya deux canots pĂŞcher la tortue autour de lâĂŽle oĂš Faure et Moreau avaient prĂŠcĂŠdemment signalĂŠ leur grande abondance. Il donna Ă  Ransonnet lâordre de se procurer le plus grand nombre possible de tortues pour la consommation gĂŠnĂŠrale Ă  bord. Toutefois, lâopĂŠration se rĂŠvĂŠla moins fructueuse que prĂŠvu, les animaux ĂŠtant ÂŤ trĂ¨s difficiles Ă  capturer Âť : pour en obtenir quelques-unes, les ĂŠquipages durent les saisir directement dans lâeau et sâaventurer sur les hauts-fonds oĂš elles se trouvaient, dans des profondeurs atteignant jusquâĂ  quatre pieds.
history: In Shark Bay, on 17 March 1803, to feed his crew, Baudin sends two canoes to go turtle fishing around the island where Faure and Moreau found the marine turtles in abundance. He gave Ransonnet orders to obtain as many turtles as possible for general consumption aboard, but the mission was not as successful as expected as âthey were very difficult to obtain, and in order to get any, the boats âcrews were obliged to catch them in the water and to go out over the shoals where they were found in depths of up to 4 feet.â

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Cuvier, situĂŠ Ă  pres de cent kilometres au nord de Carnarvon, est un secteur cĂ´tier escarpĂŠ, caractĂŠrisĂŠ par des falaises atteignant soixante mĂ¨tres de hauteur et par la prĂŠsence de souffleurs marins Ă  proximitĂŠ. Freycinet dĂŠfinit ce cap comme ÂŤ la limite septentrionale Âť de la baie des Chiens-Marins.
Il porte le nom de Georges Cuvier, professeur dâanatomie comparĂŠe au MusĂŠum national dâHistoire naturelle.
characteristic:
Cape Cuvier, located about 90 minutes north of Carnarvon, is a rugged coastal area with 60-metre cliffs and nearby blowholes. Freycinet defines the cap as "the northernmost limit" of  of Shark bay. 
It is named in honour of Georges Cuvier, professor of comparative anatomy at the MusĂŠum national dâHistoire naturelle.
history_fr:
Freycinet dĂŠcrit le cap Cuvier comme un relief dominant nettement les terres environnantes, sâĂŠlevant comme "un ĂŠnorme bastion " et se distinguant, Ă  lâinstar de nombreux points de cette cĂ´te, par une teinte dâun rouge particuliĂ¨rement vif, visible de loin, bien quâÂŤ on nây aperĂ§oive aucune trace de vĂŠgĂŠtation Âť. Il prĂŠcise par ailleurs que la position du cap fut dĂŠterminĂŠe Ă  partir des observations et relevĂŠs effectuĂŠs par le gĂŠographe Boullanger Ă  bord du GĂŠographe.
Lâattribution du nom de Cuvier Ă  ce cap fut vraisemblablement proposĂŠe par PĂŠron, dans un contexte oĂš les eaux avoisinantes se rĂŠvĂŠlaient particuliĂ¨rement riches en invertĂŠbrĂŠs marins. Les spĂŠcimens de mollusques quâil y collecta fournirent Ă  Georges Cuvier un matĂŠriau empirique privilĂŠgiĂŠ pour ĂŠtayer sa conception de lâordre du vivant. LâĂŠtude de ces organismes lui permettait en effet de dĂŠmontrer que, malgrĂŠ la diversitĂŠ des formes extĂŠrieures, il existe des structures anatomiques constantes â telles que la coquille, le systĂ¨me nerveux ou lâorganisation des organes â autorisant une classification rigoureuse des espĂ¨ces et une distinction nette entre les grands groupes zoologiques. Pour Cuvier, cette stabilitĂŠ anatomique constituait une preuve de lâimmuabilitĂŠ des espĂ¨ces et de lâexistence dâun ordre naturel fixe, une conception que PĂŠron, pour sa part, ne partageait pas nĂŠcessairement sans rĂŠserve (Voir dans l'entree Ăle Cuvier en en Australie-MĂŠridionale, 'en savoir plus').
history:
Freycinet describes Cape Cuvier as a landform rising prominently above the surrounding terrain, appearing like âan enormous bastionâ and, like many points along this coast, distinguished by a particularly vivid red hue, visible from afar, although âno trace of vegetation can be perceived there.â He further notes that the position of the cape was established on the basis of the observations and surveys carried out by the geographer Boullanger aboard the GĂŠographe.
The attribution of the name Cuvier to this cape was most likely proposed by PĂŠron, in a context in which the surrounding waters proved especially rich in marine invertebrates. The mollusc specimens collected there provided Georges Cuvier with privileged empirical material in support of his conception of the order of the living world. The study of these organisms enabled him to demonstrate that, despite the diversity of external forms, constant anatomical structuresâsuch as shells, nervous systems, and the organisation of organsâmake possible a rigorous classification of species and a clear distinction between major zoological groups. For Cuvier, this anatomical stability constituted evidence of the immutability of species and of the existence of a fixed natural order, a conception that PĂŠron himself did not necessarily share without reservation. (See the entry Ăle Cuvier in SA, details).

characteristic_fr:
Point Murat est situĂŠ Ă  lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ du cap Nord-Ouest (North West Cape), Ă  environ 16 km au nord dâExmouth. Aujourdâhui, ce site marque la limite septentrionale du parc marin de Ningaloo qui abrite le plus grand rĂŠcif frangeant d'Australie   (Ningaloo Marine Park), dĂŠjĂ  reprĂŠsentĂŠ par Freycinet sur les cartes de lâatlas de lâexpĂŠdition afin dâen signaler lâextension et le danger quâil constituait pour la navigation. Le Point Murat Navy Pier designe la jetĂŠe militaire construite en 1964 pour desservir une station de communications navales amĂŠricaine.
Le site porte le nom de Joachim Murat (voir lâentrĂŠe baie Murat, en Australie-MĂŠridionale).
characteristic:
Point Murat is located at the extremity of North West Cape, approximately 16 km north of Exmouth. Today, the site marks the northern limit of the Ningaloo Marine Park, which encompasses the largest fringing reef in Australia and which had already been depicted by Freycinet on the expeditionâs atlas maps in order to indicate both its extent and the danger it posed to navigation.
The Point Murat Navy Pier refers to the military jetty constructed in 1964 to service an American naval communications station. 
The site is named in honour of Joachim Murat (see the entry Baie Murat, South Australia).
history_fr: Freycinet insiste sur la fonction de limite gĂŠographique du cap Murat, quâil dĂŠfinit comme lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ septentrionale de la Terre dâEndracht, Ă  la fois pointe nord-occidentale du continent et point de transition vers la Terre de Witt. Le cap marque ainsi un seuil spatial et nautique, au-delĂ  duquel la cĂ´te change de caractĂ¨re et les conditions de navigation deviennent sensiblement plus pĂŠrilleuses. Il ĂŠcrit: "Le cap Murat est la limite extrĂŞme Septentrionale de cette Terre [Terre d'Endracht]; il est Ă  la fois la pointe N. O. du continent et lâorigine de la Terre de Witt. Cette derniĂ¨re, dâun abord plus dangereux quâaucune des prĂŠcĂŠdentes, est parsemĂŠe dâune multitude dâĂŽles et dâĂŽlots, de bancs et de rĂŠcifs, qui souvent entravent la navigation..."
history: Freycinet emphasises the role of Cape Murat as a geographical boundary, defining it as the northernmost extremity of Endrachtâs Land, at once the north-western point of the continent and a point of transition toward Wittâs Land. The cape thus marks a spatial and nautical threshold, beyond which the coastline changes in character and navigational conditions become markedly more hazardous. Freycinet writes: "Cape Murat is the northernmost point of this land [Terre d'Endracht]; it is both the north-western tip of the continent and the origin of Terre de Witt. The latter, more dangerous to approach than any of the previous ones, is dotted with a multitude of islands and islets, shoals and reefs, which often hinder navigation..."

characteristic_fr:
Les ĂŽles de Rivoli, situĂŠes dans le golfe dâExmouth, en Australie-Occidentale, forment un groupe dâĂŽles au large reconnu aujourd'ui pour sa forte valeur patrimoniale et ĂŠcologique. Elles constituent des milieux insulaires intacts et isolĂŠs, largement prĂŠservĂŠs des animaux introduits et des espĂ¨ces vĂŠgĂŠtales envahissantes, et jouent un rĂ´le essentiel comme sites de reproduction et de nidification pour les oiseaux marins et les tortues.
Freycinet  dĂŠcrivit ces iles comme "sablonneuses, stĂŠriles et basses" et donna Ă  cet ensemble insulaire le nom dâĂles de Rivoli (voir lâentrĂŠe baie de Rivoli, en Australie-MĂŠridionale), ÂŤ en mĂŠmoire de la cĂŠlĂ¨bre victoire de ce nom Âť, en rĂŠfĂŠrence Ă  la bataille de Rivoli, au cours de laquelle lâarmĂŠe de Bonaparte vainquit les forces autrichiennes lors des campagnes dâItalie (voir le glossaire).
characteristic:
The Rivoli Islands, located within Exmouth Gulf in Western Australia, form a group of offshore islands  now recognised for their high conservation value. They comprise intact and isolated habitats, largely free from feral animals and invasive weeds, and provide critical ecological functions as breeding and nesting sites for seabirds and marine turtles.
Freycinet Freycinet described these islands as âsandy, barren, and low-lying.â and bestowed upon this island group the name Ăles de Rivoli (see the entry Baie de Rivoli, South Australia), âin memory of the famous victory of that name,â referring to the Battle of Rivoli, during which Bonaparteâs army defeated Austrian forces in the Italian campaigns (see Glossary).
history_fr: Les diffĂŠrentes ĂŽles de lâarchipel de Rivoli furent nommĂŠes en lâhonneur de gĂŠnĂŠraux ĂŠminents et de futurs marĂŠchaux ayant contribuĂŠ aux victoires italiennes des armĂŠes rĂŠvolutionnaires franĂ§aises. Elles comprennent notamment les ĂŽles Muiron, Victor, BessiĂ¨res, Serrurier et ThĂŠvenard.
history: The individual islands of the Rivoli group were named after prominent generals and future marshals who contributed to the Italian victories of the French Revolutionary armies. They include Muiron, Victor, BessiĂ¨res, Serrurier and ThĂŠvenard Islands.

characteristic_fr:
Les ĂŽles Muiron sont situĂŠes au large du cap Nord-Ouest (North West Cape), en Australie-Occidentale, Ă  environ 1 200 km au nord de Perth. Elles abritent un environnement marin dâune grande diversitĂŠ, comprenant des rĂŠcifs coralliens, des communautĂŠs dâorganismes filtreurs et des herbiers de macroalgues.
Les ĂŽles constituent en outre dâimportantes zones de nidification pour les oiseaux marins et les tortues vertes. Elles font partie dâune aire marine protĂŠgĂŠe intĂŠgrĂŠe au RĂŠseau national reprĂŠsentatif des aires marines protĂŠgĂŠes (National Representative System of Marine Protected Areas, NRSMPA).
Elles portent le nom de Jean-Baptiste Muiron (1774â1796), figure hĂŠroĂŻque des guerres napolĂŠoniennes, qui fut aide de camp de Bonaparte lors de la campagne dâItalie.
characteristic:
The Muiron Islands are located off the North West Cape of Western Australia, approximately 1200 km north of Perth. They contain a very diverse marine environment, with coral reefs, filter-feeding communities and macroalgal beds.
In addition the Islands are important seabird and green turtle nesting areas. They are part of a marine protected area of the National Representative System of Marine Protected Areas (NRSMPA).They are named after Jean-Baptiste Muiron (1774-1796), one of the heroic figures of the Napoleonic wars and was aide-de-camp to Bonaparte in Italy.
history_fr: Lorsque les troupes rĂŠvolutionnaires franĂ§aises furent prises sous le feu de la mitraille autrichienne au $pont dâArcole$Bataille_du_pont_d'Arcole$, le 15 novembre 1796, prĂ¨s de la confluence de lâAdige et de lâAlpone, Ă  une vingtaine de kilomĂ¨tres au sud-est de VĂŠrone, Muiron protĂŠgea son gĂŠnĂŠral â en lâoccurrence Bonaparte â en sâinterposant de son propre corps, lui sauvant la vie au prix de la sienne.
history: When the French revolutionary troops came under fire from Austrian grapeshot at the $Pont d'Arcole$Battle_of_Arcole$ on November 15, 1796, near the confluence of the Adige and Alpone rivers, at about twenty kilometres south-east of Verona, Murion shielded his general with his own body and saved his life at the expense of his own.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Victor est une petite ĂŽle dâenviron 6 435 mÂ˛, situĂŠe au large de la cĂ´te de la Pilbara, en Australie-Occidentale, et classĂŠe en rĂŠserve pour la conservation de la flore et de la faune. Elle se trouve Ă  proximitĂŠ dâOnslow et fait partie du rĂŠseau dâaires protĂŠgĂŠes de lâĂtat, demeurĂŠ Ă  lâĂŠcart de toute exploitation commerciale.

Elle porte le nom du commandant militaire franĂ§ais Claude-Victor Perrin, dit Victor, duc de Bellune (1764â1841), qui servit durant les $Guerres de Coalitions$Guerres_de_Coalitions$.
characteristic:
Victor Island is a small (approx. 6,435 square metre) island located off the Pilbara coast of Western Australia, designated as Reserve for the "Conservation of Flora and Fauna". It is situated near Onslow . The island is part of the state's protected, un-commercialized conservation network. 
It is named after the French military commander who served during the $French Revolutionary Wars and the Napoleonic Wars$French_Revolutionary_and_Napoleonic_Wars$, Claude-Victor Perrin, known as Victor, Duc de Bellune (1764 - 1841),
history_fr:
Victor commanda une brigade en Italie en 1796, au sein de la division placĂŠe sous les ordres dâAugereau. Il combattit Ă  la bataille de Dego, avant de rejoindre la division de MassĂŠna. Victor prit ensuite possession de la ville de Peschiera, puis participa aux combats de Roveredo et de Cerea, avant dâĂŞtre blessĂŠ Ă  Saint-Georges.
En janvier 1797, il participa Ă  la bataille de Rivoli, puis se distingua lors du siĂ¨ge de Mantoue et Ă  la bataille de La Favorita. Promu gĂŠnĂŠral de division, il combattit ensuite les troupes de lâarmĂŠe pontificale. En 1800, il fut envoyĂŠ en renfort auprĂ¨s du gĂŠnĂŠral Lannes Ă  la bataille de Montebello, et son corps contint lâattaque autrichienne Ă  Marengo jusquâĂ  lâarrivĂŠe de Lannes pour lui prĂŞter appui. Il fut ĂŠlevĂŠ Ă  la dignitĂŠ de marĂŠchal en 1807, Ă  la suite de la bataille de Friedland (14 juin 1807).
history: Victor commanded a brigade in Italy in 1796, in the division commanded by $Augereau$Charles-Pierre_Augereau$. He fought in the $battle of Dego$Second_Battle_of_Dego$, before joining MassĂŠna's division. Victor subsequently captured the stronghold of Peschiera, before participating in the battles of Roveredo and Cerea, and being wounded at Saint-Georges. In January 1797, he fought at Rivoli and then distinguished himself at the siege of Mantua and at the battle of La Favorita. Promoted to gĂŠnĂŠral de division, he fought against troops of the Papal army. In 1800, he moved up to reinforce $General Lannes$Jean_Lannes$ at the battle of Montebello, and his corps contained the Austrian attack at Marengo until Lannes arrived to support him. He was made a Marshal in 1807, after the battle of Friedland (14 June 1807).

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle BessiĂ¨res est une petite ĂŽle situĂŠe au large de la cĂ´te de la Pilbara, dans le nord-ouest de lâAustralie. ClassĂŠe en rĂŠserve naturelle, elle abrite nĂŠanmoins un phare.
Elle tire son nom de Jean-Baptiste BessiĂ¨res, duc dâIstrie (1768â1813), chef militaire franĂ§ais qui se distingua durant les $Guerres de Coalitions$Guerres_de_Coalitions$.
characteristic:
Bessieres Island is a small island off the Pilbara coast of north-west Australia. It is a Nature Reserve, but does contain a lighthouse.
It takes its name from Jean-Baptiste BessiĂ¨res, duc d'Istrie, (1768 â 1813), a French military leader of the $French Revolutionary Wars and the Napoleonic Wars$French_Revolutionary_and_Napoleonic_Wars$.
history_fr:
Jean-Baptiste BessiĂ¨res, duc dâIstrie (1768â1813), combattit aux batailles de Roveredo et dâArcole, avant de se distinguer Ă  Rivoli et Ă  La Favorita. BessiĂ¨res reĂ§ut ÂŤ lâhonneur Âť de rapporter Ă  Paris onze des drapeaux capturĂŠs lors des batailles dâArcole et de Rivoli.
Il fut ĂŠlevĂŠ Ă  la dignitĂŠ de marĂŠchal de lâEmpire par lâempereur NapolĂŠon en 1804.
history:
Jean-Baptiste BessiĂ¨res, duc d'Istrie, (1768 â 1813) fought at the battles of Roveredo* and Arcole* before distinguishing himself at $Rivoli$Battle-of-Rivoli$* and $La Favorita$Battle_of_La_Favorita$. BessiĂ¨res was given the âhonourâ of taking 11 of the captured flags in Arcole and Rivoli back to Paris.
He was made a Marshal of the Empire by Emperor Napoleon in 1804

characteristic_fr: Ăle Serrurier, ĂŠgalement appelĂŠe Long Island, est une rĂŠserve naturelle situĂŠe Ă  environ 40 milles nautiques au nord-est d'Exmouth rĂŠputĂŠe pour sa biodiversitĂŠ. Elle porte le nom du gĂŠnĂŠral Jean Mathieu Philibert SĂŠrurier (1742â1819).
characteristic: Serrurier Island, also known as Long Island, is a nature reserve located approximately 40 nautical miles north-east of Exmouth and is renowned for its biodiversity. It is named after General Jean-Mathieu Philibert SĂŠrurier (1742â1819).
history_fr:
Jean Mathieu Philibert SĂŠrurier (1742â1819), orthographiĂŠ ĂŠgalement Serrurier, battit les PiĂŠmontais Ă  Mondovi en 1796. En 1797, il se distingua face au gĂŠnĂŠral autrichien Johann Provera Ă  La Favorita et prit le commandement du siĂ¨ge de Mantoue. Au cours de la campagne, ses soldats lui donnĂ¨rent le surnom singulier de ÂŤ Vierge dâItalie Âť, en raison de sa droiture et de son intĂŠgritĂŠ morale.
Ă lâissue de la campagne dâItalie de NapolĂŠon, en 1797, il fut ĂŠgalement chargĂŠ par le gĂŠnĂŠral Bonaparte, conjointement avec BessiĂ¨res, de remettre au Directoire vingt-deux drapeaux ennemis capturĂŠs. Il devint par la suite gouverneur de Venise, puis, en 1804, gouverneur de lâHĂ´tel des Invalides, fonction dans laquelle il sâemploya Ă  amĂŠliorer les conditions de vie et lâalimentation des soldats blessĂŠs.
SĂŠrurier est ĂŠlevĂŠ Ă  la dignitĂŠ de marĂŠchal d'Empire honoraire le 19 mai 1804.
history:
Jean Mathieu Philibert SĂŠrurier (1742 â 1819), spelt also Serrurier, defeated the Piedmontese at Mondovi* in 1796. In 1797, he distinguished himself against the Austrian general Johann Provera at La Favorita* and took the command of the siege of Mantua. Over the course of the campaign his soldiers had strangely nicknamed him the âVirgin of Italyâ for his upright and honest ways. After the conclusion of NapolĂŠonâs Italian campaign in 1797, he was also sent to BessiĂ¨res, by General Bonaparte to take 22 captured enemy flags to le Directoire. He became governor of Venice, and, in 1804, of the HĂ´tel des Invalides, which enabled him to improve the living conditions and diet of the wounded soldiers. 
SĂŠrurier was elevated to the honorary rank of Marshal of the Empire on 19 May 1804.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle ThĂŠvenard est situĂŠe Ă  environ 22 kilomĂ¨tres au large de la cĂ´te dâOnslow, dans la rĂŠgion de la Pilbara, en Australie-Occidentale. La majeure partie de lâĂŽle, ainsi que les eaux marines qui lâentourent, est intĂŠgrĂŠe Ă  la rĂŠserve naturelle de lâĂŽle ThĂŠvenard. Elle constitue aujourdâhui la plus vaste ĂŽle dâun ensemble de dix ĂŽles et atolls communĂŠment dĂŠsignĂŠs, de maniĂ¨re informelle, sous le nom dâĂŽles Mackerel.
LâĂŽle porte le nom de lâofficier de marine franĂ§ais Antoine-Jean-Marie ThĂŠvenard (1733â1815), nommĂŠ vice-amiral en 1792, puis prĂŠfet maritime de Lorient et, ultĂŠrieurement, de Toulon.
characteristic:
Thevenard Island is located approximately 22 kilometres (14 miles) off the coast of Onslow, in the Pilbara region of Western Australia. Most of the island, together with the surrounding marine waters, is included within the Thevenard Island Nature Reserve. The island is currently the largest of a group of ten islands and atolls collectively known, in informal usage, as the Mackerel Islands.
The island takes its name from the French naval officer Antoine-Jean-Marie ThĂŠvenard.
history_fr: Freycinet fournit les indications gĂŠographiques et nautiques suivantes : ÂŤ Au nord-est des ĂŽles de Rivoli se prĂŠsentent deux ĂŽles sablonneuses et stĂŠriles, auxquelles nous avons donnĂŠ les noms dâĂŽle ThĂŠvenard et dâĂŽle Rosily. Âť
history: Freycinet provides the following geographical and nautical information: âTo the north-east of the Rivoli Islands lie two sandy and barren islands, to which we have given the names ThĂŠvenard Island and Rosily Island.â

characteristic_fr: Les ĂŽles Rosily se situent aujourdâhui au sein de lâune des plus importantes provinces pĂŠtroliĂ¨res terrestres dâAustralie, intĂŠgrĂŠe au vaste bassin de Carnarvon Nord â un bassin sĂŠdimentaire formĂŠ lors de la dislocation du supercontinent Gondwana, au moment oĂš lâAustralie se sĂŠparait des autres masses continentales. Ce bassin comprend plusieurs sous-bassins ouverts sur lâocĂŠan Indien, dont le sous-bassin de Barrow, Ă  lâintĂŠrieur duquel se trouvent les ĂŽles Rosily. Celles-ci portent le nom de lâofficier de marine franĂ§ais FranĂ§ois Ătienne de Rosily-Mesros (1748â1832).
characteristic: The Rosily Islands now lie within one of Australiaâs largest onshore hydrocarbon provinces, located in the extensive Northern Carnarvon Basinâa sedimentary basin formed during the breakup of the Gondwana supercontinent, as Australia separated from other continental masses. The basin comprises several smaller sub-basins opening onto the Indian Ocean, including the Barrow Sub-basin, within which the Rosily Islands are situated. The islands take their name from the French naval officer FranĂ§ois Ătienne de Rosily-Mesros (1748â1832).
history_fr:
Freycinet fournit lâindication nautique suivante :
ÂŤ Au sud de lâĂŽle Rosily se trouve un rĂŠcif assez ĂŠtendu, sur lequel la mer brise fortement, mĂŞme par temps calme ; et, Ă  lâouest de lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ mĂŠridionale de lâĂŽle, un petit rocher isolĂŠ. Âť
PĂŠron, pour sa part, prĂŠcise :
ÂŤ âŚ en remontant vers le nord-est [depuis le groupe des ĂŽles de Rivoli], se trouvent deux autres ĂŽles, de trois Ă  quatre milles chacune, dont la plus septentrionale fut dĂŠsignĂŠe sous le nom dâĂŽle Rosily, en lâhonneur du navigateur cĂŠlĂ¨bre Ă  qui la marine franĂ§aise est redevable de tant de cartes prĂŠcieuses de la mer Rouge, du golfe Persique, de la Cochinchine, des Philippines, &c. Âť
history:
Freycinet provides the following nautical information:
âTo the south of Rosily Island lies a fairly extensive reef, upon which the sea breaks heavily even in calm weather; and to the west of the islandâs southern extremity stands a small detached rock.â
PĂŠron, for his part, explains:
â... proceeding north-eastwards [from the Rivoli Islands group], there are two further islands, each measuring three to four miles, the more northerly of which was designated Rosily Island, in honour of the celebrated navigator to whom the French Navy owes so many valuable charts of the Red Sea, the Persian Gulf, Cochinchina, the Philippines, and other regions.â

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Poivre est un promontoire situĂŠ sur la cĂ´te occidentale de Barrow Island, caractĂŠrisĂŠ par des affleurements de formations marines du MiocĂ¨ne. Ces strates constituent un tĂŠmoignage remarquable dâanciens environnements marins tropicaux, Ă  une ĂŠpoque oĂš le niveau de la mer ĂŠtait sensiblement plus ĂŠlevĂŠ quâaujourdâhui. Leur conservation Ă  lâaffleurement reflĂ¨te la relative stabilitĂŠ tectonique de lâĂŽle et confĂ¨re au site un intĂŠrĂŞt particulier pour la reconstitution des palĂŠo-environnements et de lâhistoire gĂŠologique du plateau nord-ouest australien. 
Il doit son nom Ă  Pierre Poivre (1719â1786), administrateur colonial franĂ§ais renommĂŠ pour son engagement en faveur de la botanique et pour son rĂ´le dans la circulation et lâacclimatation des plantes utiles dans lâespace de lâocĂŠan Indien.
characteristic:
Cape Poivre is a headland located on the western coast of Barrow Island, characterised by outcrops of Miocene marine formations. These strata provide a remarkable record of ancient tropical marine environments, dating from a period when sea levels were significantly higher than at present. Their preservation at the surface reflects the islandâs relative tectonic stability and lends the site particular significance for the reconstruction of palaeoenvironments and the geological history of Australiaâs North West Shelf.
It takes its name from Pierre Poivre (1719â1786), a French colonial administrator renowned for his contributions to botany and the circulation of useful plants within the Indian Ocean world.
history_fr:
Freycinet avance avec prudence que ÂŤ la portion de cĂ´tes comprise entre le cap Poivre et le cap Dupuy appartient probablement aux terres continentales Âť, tout en soulignant le caractĂ¨re encore lacunaire de la reconnaissance de la Terre de Witt. Son propos traduit une rĂŠserve mĂŠthodologique, liĂŠe aux incertitudes persistantes sur la continuitĂŠ du littoral et sur la distinction entre ĂŽles et continent.
PĂŠron, en revanche, adopte un ton plus affirmatif et narratif lorsquâil ĂŠcrit : ÂŤ Enfin nous atteignĂŽmes le continent : il ĂŠtait sur ce point extrĂŞmement bas, sablonneux et stĂŠrile ; une ligne lĂŠgĂ¨rement ondulĂŠe en dessinait le profil ; nous y reconnĂťmes successivement le cap Poivre, le cap Malouet et le cap Dupuy. Âť Dans son rĂŠcit, ce passage marque le moment symbolique de lâaccĂ¨s au continent proprement dit, mais il repose sur une extrapolation gĂŠographique dĂŠpourvue de confirmation empirique â affirmation que Freycinet, pour sa part, se garda dĂŠlibĂŠrĂŠment de formuler. Les explorations ultĂŠrieures ĂŠtablirent que ces caps nâappartiennent pas au continent, mais constituent les extrĂŠmitĂŠs dâune ĂŽle, qui fut ultĂŠrieurement nommĂŠe ĂŽle de Barrow par Phillip Parker King en 1818.
history:
Freycinet cautiously suggests that âthe stretch of coast lying between Cape Poivre and Cape Dupuy probably belongs to the continental landmass,â while at the same time emphasising the still fragmentary nature of the reconnaissance of De Wittâs Land. His wording reflects a clear methodological restraint, rooted in the persistent uncertainties surrounding the continuity of the coastline and the distinction between islands and the mainland.
PĂŠron, by contrast, adopts a more assertive and narrative tone when he writes: âAt last we reached the continent: at this point it was extremely low, sandy, and barren; a gently undulating line traced its profile; we successively identified Cape Poivre, Cape Malouet, and Cape Dupuy.â In his account, this passage marks the symbolic moment of arrival on the continent proper,but it rests on a geographical extrapolation that lacked empirical confirmationâan assertion that Freycinet, by contrast, deliberately refrained from making. Subsequent exploration would establish that these capes do not belong to the mainland, but form part of an island later named Barrow Island by Phillip Parker King in 1818.

characteristic_fr:
La baie Flacourt, sur la cote ouest de l'ile Barrow, est identifiĂŠe comme un site de nidification important pour les tortues vertes. La Baie sâinscrit dans un paysage qui accueille depuis plus dâun demi-siĂ¨cle le plus grand champ pĂŠtrolier terrestre en activitĂŠ dâAustralie, illustrant la tension de longue date â mais aussi la coexistence nĂŠgociĂŠe â entre dĂŠveloppement industriel et conservation de lâenvironnement sur lâĂŽle de Barrow et notamment dans sa baie Flacourt.
La baie tire son nom d'Ătienne de Flacourt (1607-1660), nommĂŠ gouverneur de Madagascar en 1648 sous l'ĂŠgide de la Compagnie franĂ§aise des Indes orientales.
characteristic:
Flacourt Bay, on the west coast of Barrow Island, is identified as an important nesting habitat for green turtles. It lies within a landscape that has hosted Australiaâs largest operating onshore oilfield for more than half a century, exemplifying the long-standing tensionâyet also the negotiated coexistenceâbetween industrial development and environmental conservation on Barrow Island.
The bay is named after Ătienne de Flacourt (1607-1660) who was appointed Governor of Madagascar in 1648, under the auspices of the French East India Company.
history_fr:
Freycinet dĂŠcrit les rivages de la baie de Flacourt comme ÂŤ uniformes, de hauteur mĂŠdiocre, et dâailleurs stĂŠriles et sablonneux, Ă  lâinstar des diverses ĂŽles dĂŠjĂ  dĂŠcrites Âť.
Aujourdâhui, Ă  lâopposĂŠ de cette baie, la cĂ´te orientale de lâĂŽle accueille le plus vaste champ pĂŠtrolier terrestre encore en activitĂŠ en Australie, oĂš la production dâhydrocarbures sâappuie, depuis les annĂŠes 1960, sur des puits forĂŠs directement sur lâĂŽle.
history:
Freycinet describes the shores of Flacourt Bay as âuniform, of moderate elevation, and moreover barren and sandy, like the various islands already described.â
Today, on the opposite side of the island, the eastern coast hosts Australiaâs largest onshore oilfield still in operation, where hydrocarbon production has relied since the 1960s on wells drilled directly on the island.

characteristic_fr: Le cap Malouet se caractĂŠrise par des affleurements de calcaires miocĂ¨nes appartenant Ă  la formation de Poivre (voir cap Poivre), renfermant une faune fossile diversifiĂŠe dâĂŠchinides et de mollusques. Ces ĂŠlĂŠments palĂŠontologiques, aujourdâhui bien documentĂŠs, nâĂŠtaient toutefois pas accessibles aux savants de lâexpĂŠdition : PĂŠron ne put en avoir connaissance, lâexpĂŠdition Baudin nâayant pas abordĂŠ cette portion du littoral  et de ces iles. Il porte le nom de Pierre Victor, baron Malouet (1740â1814), administrateur et commissaire gĂŠnĂŠral Ă  Anvers, chargĂŠ de la direction des chantiers navals.
characteristic: Cape Malouet is characterised by outcrops of Miocene limestones belonging to the Poivre Formation (see Cape Poivre), which contain a diverse assemblage of echinoids and molluscs. These palaeontological features, now well documented, were not accessible to the scientists of the expedition: PĂŠron could not have been aware of them, as the Baudin expedition did not land on this section of the coastline and its islands. It was named after Pierre Victor, baron Malouet (1740 â1814), who was administrator commissaire general in Anvers in charge of the shipyards.
history_fr:
Pierre Victor, baron Malouet, fut un administrateur aux convictions monarchistes affirmĂŠes. Il ĂŠmigra durant la RĂŠvolution franĂ§aise, avant dâĂŞtre rappelĂŠ par Bonaparte en 1803 et nommĂŠ commissaire gĂŠnĂŠral Ă  Anvers, oĂš il fut chargĂŠ de la direction des chantiers navals et du dĂŠveloppement de lâarsenal. Ă ce titre, il mit en Ĺuvre des mesures efficaces pour protĂŠger le port contre les attaques britanniques. Freycinet lây rencontra trĂ¨s probablement entre 1804 et 1805.
En 1812, aprĂ¨s lâĂŠtablissement de la nomenclature officielle, Malouet fut soupĂ§onnĂŠ de sympathies royalistes favorables au retour de la dynastie des Bourbons et fut, pour cette raison, exilĂŠ par lâEmpereur.
Il publia par ailleurs ses poĂ¨mes, ses mĂŠmoires et divers autres ĂŠcrits, parmi lesquels ConsidĂŠrations historiques sur lâempire de la mer chez les anciens et les modernes (Anvers, 1810).
history:
Pierre Victor, Baron Malouet, was an administrator with pronounced monarchist convictions. He went into exile during the French Revolution but was recalled by Bonaparte in 1803 and appointed commissaire gĂŠnĂŠral in Antwerp, where he was responsible for the shipyards and the development of the naval arsenal. In this capacity, he implemented effective measures to protect the harbour against British attacks. Freycinet almost certainly encountered Malouet in Antwerp in 1804â1805.
In 1812, after the official nomenclature had been established, Malouet fell under suspicion for his royalist sympathies and his support for the restoration of the Bourbon dynasty, and was consequently exiled by the Emperor.
He also edited and published his poems, memoirs, and other works, including ConsidĂŠrations historiques sur lâempire de la mer chez les anciens et les modernes (Antwerp, 1810).

characteristic_fr: Le cap Dupuy constitue lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ septentrionale de lâĂŽle de Barrow. Il porte le nom dâAndrĂŠ Julien (1753â1832), anobli comte Dupuy en 1807, qui exerĂ§a les fonctions de magistrat et dâadministrateur.
characteristic: Cape Dupuy is the northernmost headland of Barrow Island. It is named after AndrĂŠ Julien (1753â1832), ennobled as Count Dupuy in 1807, who served as a magistrate and administrator.
history_fr:
En 1790, Dupuy fut envoyĂŠ Ă  lâĂle de France pour y exercer les fonctions dâintendant gĂŠnĂŠral des ĂŠtablissements franĂ§ais Ă  lâest du Cap, chargĂŠ de lâadministration des possessions franĂ§aises situĂŠes Ă  lâest du cap de Bonne-EspĂŠrance. De retour en France, il participa aux nĂŠgociations avec les Britanniques ayant conduit Ă  la signature de la paix dâAmiens (1802â1803), avant dâexercer les fonctions de conseiller dâĂtat au sein du ministĂ¨re de la Marine.
PĂŠron ĂŠtait parfaitement conscient de ll'interet du travail et activitĂŠs de Poivre, de Flacourt et du comte Dupuy, notamment dans la rĂŠgion des Mascareignes et de Madagascar, pour avoir sĂŠjournĂŠ Ă  lâĂle de France lors des escales de lâexpĂŠdition Baudin, tant Ă  lâaller quâau retour du voyage en Australie.
history:
In 1790, Dupuy was sent to the Ăle de France to assume the duties of intendant gĂŠnĂŠral des ĂŠtablissements franĂ§ais Ă  lâest du Cap, overseeing the administration of French possessions east of the Cape of Good Hope. After his return to France, he took part in the negotiations with Britain that led to the Peace of Amiens (1802â1803), before serving as a conseiller dâĂtat within the Ministry of the Navy.
PĂŠron was well aware of the significance of the work et activities of Poivre, Flacourt, and Dupuy, particularly in the Mascarene Islands and Madagascar region, having stayed on the Ăle de France during the Baudin expeditionâs stopovers on both the outward and return voyages to Australia.

characteristic_fr: Ces ĂŽles sont situĂŠes au nord-est de lâĂŽle de Barrow, au large de la cĂ´te du Pilbara, en Australie-Occidentale. Elles portent le nom dâUlrich FrĂŠdĂŠric Woldemar, comte de Lowendal (1700-1755). Descendant des rois de Danemark, Lowendal servit successivement en Saxe, en Pologne, en Autriche et en Russie, avant de choisir dâachever sa carriĂ¨re militaire en France. Il devint lâun des plus brillants gĂŠnĂŠraux de Louis XV durant la guerre de Succession dâAutriche et fut rĂŠcompensĂŠ par le titre de marĂŠchal de France.
characteristic: These islands are located northeast of Barrow Island, off the Pilbara coast in West Australia. They are named after Ulrich Frederix Woldemar, Count of Lowendal (1700-1755).  Lowendal was a descendant of the kings of Denmark. After serving in Saxony, Poland, Austria and Russia, he chose to complete his military service in France. and became one of the most brilliant generals of Louis XV during the war of the Austrian succession. He was rewarded with the title Marshall of France.
history_fr: PĂŠron mentionne briĂ¨vement lâĂŽle Lowendal en association avec les ĂŽles Trimouille et Hermite, et dĂŠsigne cet ensemble sous le nom dâĂŽles Montebello. Le choix de donner Ă  cette ĂŽle le nom dâun haut gradĂŠ militaire renvoie au service dâUlrich FrĂŠdĂŠric Woldemar, comte de Lowendal, dans lâarmĂŠe franĂ§aise durant la guerre de Succession dâAutriche, et sâinscrit dans une logique commĂŠmorative plus large, qui ĂŠtablit des parallĂ¨les entre les campagnes militaires du XVIIIáľ siĂ¨cle et celles, plus rĂŠcentes, de lâĂŠpoque napolĂŠonienne.
history: PĂŠron briefly refers to Lowendal Island together with Trimouille and Hermite Islands, grouping them under the name âMontebello Islands.â The decision to name the island after a high-ranking military figure reflects Ulrich FrĂŠdĂŠric Woldemar, comte de Lowendalâs service in the French army during the War of the Austrian Succession, and echoes the broader commemorative logic of the nomenclature, which frequently drew parallels between earlier eighteenth-century campaigns and the more recent French Revolutionary and Napoleonic campaigns.

Atlas du Voyage de dĂŠcouvertes aux terres australes, Navigation et gĂŠographie, plate 25, Paris, 1812 (sic; published in 1815).

characteristic_fr:
Dans les annĂŠes 1950, trois essais nuclĂŠaires britanniques furent rĂŠalisĂŠs sur les ĂŽles Montebello. Aujourdâhui, celles-ci font partie dâune rĂŠserve de conservation marine, situĂŠe Ă  quelques kilomĂ¨tres seulement de champs gaziers et de corridors de pipelines, illustrant la juxtaposition entre protection de lâenvironnement et activitĂŠs industrielles dans la rĂŠgion.
Les ĂŽles doivent leur nom Ă  Jean Lannes (voir lâentrĂŠe cap Lannes, en Australie-MĂŠridionale), lâun des plus ĂŠminents gĂŠnĂŠraux de NapolĂŠon, qui fut fait duc de Montebello en reconnaissance de son rĂ´le dĂŠcisif dans la victoire remportĂŠe lors de la bataille de Montebello (province de Pavie, en Lombardie) contre les forces autrichiennes.
characteristic:
In the 1950s, three British nuclear tests were conducted on the Montebello Islands. Today, the islands form part of a marine conservation reserve, located only a few kilometres from major gas fields and pipeline corridors, illustrating the juxtaposition of environmental protection and industrial activity in the region.
The islands take their name from Jean Lannes (see the entry Cap Lannes in South Australia), one of Napoleonâs most distinguished generals, who was created Duke of Montebello in recognition of his role in securing victory at the Battle of Montebello (Province of Pavia, Lombardy) against Austrian forces.
history_fr:
Freycinet ĂŠcrit Ă  propos du groupe dâĂŽles quâil nomme Montebello que ÂŤ la grande distance Ă  laquelle nos bĂ˘timents en ont passĂŠ ne nous a permis de les voir que trĂ¨s imparfaitement. Ces ĂŽles sont au nombre de troisâŚ Âť. Les trois ĂŽles ainsi identifiĂŠes et figurant sur la carte de lâatlas du voyage correspondent Ă  lâĂŽle Hermite, Ă  lâĂŽle Trimouille et Ă  lâĂŽle Lowendal.
Les connaissances gĂŠographiques actuelles montrent toutefois que les ĂŽles Montebello (parfois orthographiĂŠes Monte Bello) constituent en rĂŠalitĂŠ un vaste archipel dâenviron 174 ĂŽles et ĂŽlots, dont une trentaine portent un nom, situĂŠ Ă  quelque 130 kilomĂ¨tres au large de la cĂ´te du Pilbara. Par ailleurs, la cartographie moderne distingue dĂŠsormais le groupe des ĂŽles Lowendal comme un ensemble sĂŠparĂŠ, situĂŠ plus au sud, tandis que lâarchipel des Montebello proprement dit se trouve plus au nord.
Cet ĂŠcart entre la reprĂŠsentation initiale et la configuration aujourdâhui admise souligne Ă  la fois les limites des observations effectuĂŠes Ă  distance lors de lâexpĂŠdition Baudin et le caractĂ¨re progressif de lâĂŠlaboration de la connaissance cartographique du littoral nord-ouest australien.
history:
Freycinet writes of the group of islands he named Montebello that âthe great distance at which our vessels passed them allowed us to see them only very imperfectly. These islands are three in numberâŚâ. The three islands thus identified and shown on the atlas map of the voyage correspond to Hermite Island, Trimouille Island, and Lowendal Island.
Modern geographical knowledge shows, however, that the Montebello Islands (sometimes spelled *Monte Bello*) in fact form a vast archipelago of approximately 174 islands and islets, around thirty of which are named, located about 130 kilometres offshore from the Pilbara coast. Contemporary cartography also distinguishes the Lowendal Islands as a separate group, situated farther south, while the Montebello archipelago proper lies to the north.
This discrepancy between the initial representation and the configuration now accepted highlights both the limitations of observations made at a distance during the Baudin expedition and the gradual, cumulative nature of the cartographic understanding of Australiaâs north-west coastline.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Trimouille est connue pour avoir ĂŠtĂŠ le site de lâopĂŠration Hurricane, premier essai nuclĂŠaire britannique, rĂŠalisĂŠ en 1952 dans une baie de lâĂŽle. Deux autres essais nuclĂŠaires eurent lieu en 1956, dont le second, ĂŠgalement menĂŠ sur lâĂŽle Trimouille. Cet essai, baptisĂŠ Mosaic G2, constitua la plus puissante explosion nuclĂŠaire jamais rĂŠalisĂŠe en Australie, avec une puissance estimĂŠe Ă  environ 98 kilotonnes.
LâĂŽle tire son nom de Louis II de La TrĂŠmoille (1460â1525), ĂŠgalement appelĂŠ La Trimouille, gĂŠnĂŠral franĂ§ais issu dâune ancienne famille noble de Bretagne. Il servit successivement sous trois rois de France â Charles VIII, Louis XII et FranĂ§ois Ier â et fut tuĂŠ au combat en Lombardie en protĂŠgeant FranĂ§ois Ier lors de la bataille de Pavie en 1525.
characteristic:
Trimouille Island is known as the site of Operation Hurricane, the first British nuclear test, which took place in a bay on the island in 1952. Two further nuclear tests were conducted in 1956, the second again on Trimouille Island. This test, code-named Mosaic G2, was the largest nuclear explosion ever carried out in Australia, with a yield of approximately 98 kilotonnes.
The island takes its name from Louis II de La TrĂŠmoille (1460â1525), also known as La Trimouille, a French general descended from an ancient noble family of Brittany. He served successively under three French kingsâCharles VIII, Louis XII, and Francis Iâand was killed in combat in Lombardy while protecting Francis I during the Battle of Pavia in 1525.
history_fr: Lâattribution du nom **Trimouille** peut ĂŞtre comprise comme relevant dâun ensemble plus large de rĂŠfĂŠrences commĂŠmoratives propres Ă  lâĂŠpoque napolĂŠonienne, inscrivant cet acte toponymique franĂ§ais dans la mĂŠmoire des engagements militaires antĂŠrieurs en Italie du Nord. Dans cette gĂŠographie symbolique, le souvenir de La TrĂŠmoille, dont la carriĂ¨re sâacheva Ă  Pavie, se trouve ainsi mis en regard de celui du gĂŠnĂŠral **Lannes**, dont les campagnes menĂŠes dans la mĂŞme rĂŠgion furent marquĂŠes par des succĂ¨s dĂŠcisifs.
history: The attribution of the name Trimouille may be understood as part of a broader pattern of Napoleonic-era commemorations, linking this French toponymic act to earlier military engagements in northern Italy. In this symbolic geography, the memory of La TrĂŠmoilleâwhose career ended at Paviaâstands in contrast to that of General Lannes, whose campaigns in the same region were decisively victorious.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Hermite est la plus vaste des ĂŽles du groupe des Montebello, constituĂŠ essentiellement dâĂŽles calcaires de faible altitude.
Elle porte le nom de Jean-Marthe-Adrien LâHermite (1766â1826), officier de marine franĂ§ais qui accĂŠda ultĂŠrieurement au grade de contre-amiral.
characteristic:
Hermite Island is the largest island in the Montebello group, composed predominantly of low-lying limestone formations.
The island is named after Jean-Marthe-Adrien LâHermite (1766â1826), a French naval officer who rose to the rank of rear admiral.
history_fr:
LâHermitte prit part Ă  la quatriĂ¨me bataille dâOuessant, ĂŠgalement connue sous le nom de Glorious First of June, ou, en franĂ§ais, le combat de Prairial (28 maiâ1er juin 1794), qui opposa les marines franĂ§aise et britannique au large de lâĂŽle dâOuessant, dans lâocĂŠan Atlantique. Il ĂŠtait alors affectĂŠ au vaisseau amiral de lâescadre de Brest, La Montagne, placĂŠe sous le commandement de Villaret de Joyeuse.
LâHermitte se distingua ĂŠgalement par ses actions dans lâocĂŠan Indien. Bonaparte le qualifia de ÂŤ brave Âť. En 1805â1806, Ă  la tĂŞte de son escadre, il captura de nombreux navires britanniques dans lâAtlantique, ce qui lui valut dâĂŞtre promu contre-amiral.
history: L'Hermitte was involved in the Fourth Battle of Ushant, also referred to as the Glorious First of June, or in French the Combat de Prairial (28 May -1rst June 1794), which was fought between the French and British navies, close to the island of Ushant in the Atlantic Ocean. LâHermite was attached to the flagship of the Brest squadron, La Montagne, under the command of Villaret de Joyeuse*. He was also renowned for his actions in the Indian Ocean. Bonaparte referred to him as âbraveâ. In 1805-1806, in command of his squadron, he captured many British ships in the Atlantic and was promoted to rear admiral.

characteristic_fr:
La baie Regnard est une grande baie situĂŠe sur le continent, au sud de l'archipel Dampier.
PĂŠron l'a nommĂŠe en l'honneur de Jean-FranĂ§ois RĂŠgnard (1655-1709), ĂŠcrivain et dramaturge franĂ§ais.
characteristic:
Regnard Bay is a large bay on the mainland to the south of Dampier Archipelago.
PĂŠron named it after Jean-FranĂ§ois RĂŠgnard (1655 â 1709), a French writer and playwright.
history_fr:
RĂŠgnard fut considĂŠrĂŠ aux XVIIIáľ et XIXáľ siĂ¨cles comme le meilleur poĂ¨te comique franĂ§ais aprĂ¨s MoliĂ¨re. Voltaire disait de lui : ÂŤ Qui ne se plaĂŽt pas avec RĂŠgnard nâest pas digne dâadmirer MoliĂ¨re. Âť
Ă lâĂŠpoque napolĂŠonienne, ses piĂ¨ces ĂŠtaient encore rĂŠguliĂ¨rement reprĂŠsentĂŠes sur les scĂ¨nes parisiennes et provinciales, notamment ses comĂŠdies dâintrigue telles que *Le LĂŠgataire universel*, qui met en scĂ¨ne des fraudes liĂŠes aux successions.
 Toutefois, sa dĂŠsignation pourrait ĂŠgalement devoir beaucoup Ă  ses voyages Ă  travers lâEurope et aux rĂŠcits quâil fit de ses aventures. Parmi ceux-ci figure un voyage en Laponie, dont il dĂŠcrivit les habitants comme arriĂŠrĂŠs et pratiquant des coutumes jugĂŠes ĂŠtrangĂ¨res ou inhabituelles.
history: RĂŠgnard was considered in the 18th and 19th centuries as the best French comic poet after MoliĂ¨re*. Voltaire* said about him: âQui ne se plaĂŽt pas avec RĂŠgnard n'est pas digne d'admirer MoliĂ¨re.â (Whoever does not like Regnard is not worthy of admiring MoliĂ¨re). During the Napoleonic period, his plays were still performed in Parisian and provincial theatres (including his comedy of intrigue, Le LĂŠgataire universel, about inheritance fraud). However, he may owe his nomination to his travels in Europe and the accounts of his adventures there. These included a journey to Lapland whose people he described as backward and practising unfamiliar customs.

characteristic:
Cape Bruguieres is part of an island from the Dampier Archipelago classified as strict reserve nature with ancient archaeological underwater Aboriginal sites and artifacts.
PĂŠron named it after the zoologist Jean Guillaume BruguiĂ¨re (1749 â1798).
history_fr: BruguiĂ¨re was of particular interest to PĂŠron, as both were mainly interested in molluscs and other invertebrates (jellyfishes). BruguiĂ¨re named more than 140 marine genera or species and worked closely with Olivier (see cap Olivier in Tasmania).

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Legendre est une ĂŽle longue et ĂŠtroite de lâarchipel de Dampier, sâĂŠtendant sur environ 15,5 km de longueur et couvrant une superficie dâenviron 1 344 hectares au-dessus du niveau des plus hautes marĂŠes. Sa rive mĂŠridionale se caractĂŠrise par de vastes hauts-fonds sableux peu profonds, qui la relient partiellement aux ĂŽles voisines, tandis que son flanc septentrional plonge rapidement vers des fonds dĂŠpassant 20 mĂ¨tres de profondeur. LâĂŽle est constituĂŠe principalement de calcaires dunaires du PlĂŠistocĂ¨ne, bordĂŠs de sables holocĂ¨nes, ce qui en fait la plus vaste ĂŽle calcaire de lâarchipel de Dampier.
Elle porte le nom dâAdrien-Marie Legendre (1752â1833, parfois orthographiĂŠ *Le Gendre*), mathĂŠmaticien franĂ§ais de premier plan, dont les travaux ont exercĂŠ une influence durable sur la formation scientifique des ingĂŠnieurs et gĂŠographes du tournant des XVIIIáľ et XIXáľ siĂ¨cles.
characteristic:
Legendre Island is a long, narrow island in the Dampier Archipelago, extending roughly 15.5 km in length and covering about 1,344 hectares above high-water mark. Its southern shores are characterised by extensive shallow sandy flats linking it to neighbouring islands, while its northern side drops steeply into deeper waters exceeding 20 metres. The island is composed primarily of Pleistocene dune limestone, fringed by Holocene sands, making it the largest limestone island in the Dampier Archipelago.
It has been named after Adrien-Marie Legendre, (1752-1833, spelt sometimes Le Gendre) was a mathematician.
history_fr:
Les ĂlĂŠments de gĂŠomĂŠtrie de Legendre (1794) comptaient parmi les ouvrages fondamentaux que devaient ĂŠtudier les officiers, ingĂŠnieurs et gĂŠographes de lâĂŠpoque. Largement diffusĂŠ dans lâenseignement scientifique supĂŠrieur, ce traitĂŠ constituait un socle essentiel pour les pratiques de la mesure, de la navigation et de la cartographie. Legendre fut ĂŠgalement examinateur en mathĂŠmatiques Ă  lâĂcole polytechnique et a pu ĂŠvaluer Boullanger lors des ĂŠpreuves dâadmission de 1795.
Membre de lâInstitut (section des mathĂŠmatiques), Legendre fut ensuite ĂŠlu, en 1813, au Bureau des longitudes, institution centrale pour le dĂŠveloppement de lâastronomie, de la navigation et de la gĂŠodĂŠsie franĂ§aises.
history:
Legendreâs ĂlĂŠments de gĂŠomĂŠtrie (1794) was one of the foundational textbooks studied by officers, engineers, and geographers of the period. Widely used in advanced scientific training, it formed part of the mathematical culture essential to surveying, navigation, and cartography. Legendre also served as a mathematics examiner at the Ăcole polytechnique and may have examined Boullanger when the latter sat the entrance examinations in 1795.
A member of the Institut (mathematical section), Legendre was later elected, in 1813, to the Bureau des longitudes, a key institution in the development of French astronomy, navigation, and geodesy.

LâĂŽle HaĂźy est situĂŠe dans le prolongement sud-est de lâĂŽle Legendre.

characteristic:
HaĂźy Island is located in the south-eastern extension of Legendre Island.
It is named after the mineralogist and crystallographer RenĂŠ Just HaĂźy (1743â1822).
history_fr:
Lorsque PĂŠron dĂŠcrit, au fil de son rĂŠcit, la rĂŠgularitĂŠ gĂŠomĂŠtrique et lâorganisation interne des cristaux observĂŠs dans les ĂŠchantillons minĂŠralogiques recueillis par lâexpĂŠdition, ses analyses reflĂ¨tent lâinfluence de la cristallographie naissante, telle quâelle avait ĂŠtĂŠ formulĂŠe par HaĂźy Ă  la fin du XVIIIáľ siĂ¨cle. Celle-ci reposait sur lâidĂŠe que la forme externe des cristaux rĂŠsulte de lâempilement rĂŠgulier dâunitĂŠs ĂŠlĂŠmentaires soumises Ă  des lois gĂŠomĂŠtriques constantes. PĂŠron ne se limite donc pas Ă  une description empirique des roches, mais mobilise un cadre thĂŠorique issu des sciences minĂŠralogiques contemporaines, tĂŠmoignant de la circulation des savoirs entre le MusĂŠum et les pratiques de terrain des expĂŠditions de dĂŠcouverte.
Pour sa part, le rĂŠcit de Freycinet se rĂŠfĂ¨re au TraitĂŠ de physique de HaĂźy, dans lequel lâauteur souligne que les variations de la dĂŠclinaison magnĂŠtique â câest-Ă -dire lâangle formĂŠ entre le nord magnĂŠtique et le nord gĂŠographique â en un lieu donnĂŠ ne sont pas toujours proportionnelles au temps (Freycinet, VoyageâŚ Navigation et gĂŠographie, p. 53).
history:
As PĂŠron describes, throughout his narrative, the geometric regularity and internal organisation of the crystals observed in the mineralogical samples collected by the expedition, his analyses reflect the influence of early crystallography as formulated by HaĂźy at the end of the eighteenth century. This approach was based on the idea that the external form of crystals results from the regular stacking of elementary units governed by constant geometric laws. PĂŠron therefore goes beyond a purely empirical description of rocks, drawing instead on a theoretical framework derived from contemporary mineralogical sciences, thus bearing witness to the circulation of knowledge between the MusĂŠum and the field practices of exploratory expeditions.
For his part, Freycinetâs account refers to HaĂźyâs Treatise on Physics, in which the author emphasises that variations in magnetic declinationâthat is, the angle formed between magnetic north and geographic northâat a given location are not always proportional to time (Freycinet, VoyageâŚ Navigation and Geography, p. 53).

characteristic_fr:
Lâarchipel de Dampier, situĂŠ au large de la cĂ´te nord-ouest de lâAustralie-Occidentale, se compose dâun ensemble de quarante-deux ĂŽles. Les populations aborigĂ¨nes occupent cette rĂŠgion depuis environ 50 000 ans, et lâarchipel est riche en sites archĂŠologiques, dont des ensembles dâart rupestre dâune ampleur et dâune importance remarquables.
Ă lâĂŠpoque contemporaine, la prĂŠsence de vastes gisements offshore de gaz et de pĂŠtrole, ainsi que de riches dĂŠpĂ´ts de minerai de fer Ă  proximitĂŠ, a favorisĂŠ un dĂŠveloppement industriel de grande ampleur. Cette dynamique a transformĂŠ le port de Dampier en un pĂ´le majeur dâexportation des ressources naturelles australiennes, donnant lieu Ă  un paysage oĂš infrastructures industrielles et patrimoine culturel de longue durĂŠe coexistent dans une ĂŠtroite proximitĂŠ.
Les FranĂ§ais nommĂ¨rent lâarchipel en lâhonneur de William Dampier, flibustier, navigateur et explorateur anglais qui aborda cette cĂ´te en 1699. Cette dĂŠsignation reflĂ¨te lâĂŠtat des connaissances europĂŠennes de lâĂŠpoque, fondĂŠes exclusivement sur les rĂŠcits de navigation antĂŠrieurs, dans lâignorance du patrimoine culturel autochtone plurimillĂŠnaire, dont la rĂŠgion est aujourdâhui reconnue comme lâun des tĂŠmoignages majeurs.
characteristic:
The Dampier Archipelago, located off the north-west coast of Western Australia, consists of a group of forty-two islands. Aboriginal populations have inhabited this region for approximately 50,000 years, and the archipelago is rich in archaeological sites, including rock art assemblages of remarkable scale and significance.
In the contemporary period, the presence of vast offshore gas and oil fields, together with nearby rich deposits of iron ore, has driven large-scale industrial development. This dynamic has transformed the port of Dampier into a major hub for the export of Australiaâs natural resources, resulting in a landscape in which industrial infrastructure and long-standing cultural heritage coexist in close proximity.
The French named the archipelago in honour of William Dampier, an English buccaneer, navigator and explorer who landed on this coast in 1699. This designation reflects the state of European knowledge at the time, based exclusively on earlier maritime accounts and unaware of the millennia-old Indigenous cultural heritage for which the region is now recognised as one of the most significant testimonies.
history_fr:
PĂŠron note, en citant Dampier: "Pendant toute la journĂŠe du 29, nous naviguĂ˘mes Ă  vue d'un nombre prodigieux d'ĂŽles et d'ĂŽlots, que nous jugeĂ˘mes devoir ĂŞtre l'archipel oĂš aborda Dampier en 1699. Du haut de notre grand mĂ˘t, dit ce navigateur cĂŠlĂ¨bre, nous dĂŠcouvrĂŽmes une infinitĂŠ d'ĂŽles Ă  l'Est et Ă  l'Ouest, aussi loin que notre vue pouvoit s'ĂŠtendre. De mĂŞme, vers le Sud, on ne voyoit que des ĂŽles. . . Les grandes ĂŠtoient assez hautes, mais elles paroissoient arides et jaunĂ˘tres. Âť (Dampier, Voy. aux Terres Austr. tom. IV, pag. 109 et 112. ) Ces ĂŽles nombreuses semblorent former une triple chaĂŽne en avant de la terre continentale ..."
Ce ÂŤ nombre prodigieux dâĂŽles et dâĂŽlots Âť sâest constituĂŠ il y a environ 6 000 Ă  8 000 ans, lorsque lâĂŠlĂŠvation du niveau marin, au cours dâune pĂŠriode interglaciaire plus chaude, a submergĂŠ les vallĂŠes cĂ´tiĂ¨res, ne laissant ĂŠmerger que les collines et les crĂŞtes, transformĂŠes en ĂŽles.
history:
PĂŠron notes, quoting Dampier:
âThroughout the day of the 29th, we sailed within sight of a prodigious number of islands and islets, which we judged to be the archipelago where Dampier landed in 1699. From the top of our mainmast,â said the famous navigator, âwe discovered an infinite number of islands to the east and to the west, as far as the eye could reach. Likewise, to the south, nothing was visible but islandsâŚ The larger ones were of considerable height, but they appeared barren and yellowish.â (Dampier, Voyage aux Terres Australes, vol. IV, pp. 109 and 112.)
These numerous islands appeared to form a triple chain extending in front of the mainland.
This âprodigious number of islands and isletsâ was formed between 6,000 and 8,000 years ago, when rising sea levels during a warmer interglacial period inundated coastal valleys, leaving hills and ridges exposed as islands.

characteristic:
Delambre Island is an uninhabited sandy island in Ngarluma Country off the coast of Karratha on the eastern extent of the Dampier Archipelago.
It is named after the French astronomerJean-Baptiste Delambre (1749-1822)
history_fr:
Jean-Baptiste Delambre participa Ă  la mesure de la longueur de lâarc du mĂŠridien terrestre entre Dunkerque et Barcelone, aux cĂ´tĂŠs de Pierre MĂŠchain et de JĂŠrĂ´me Lalande. AprĂ¨s la mort de MĂŠchain, Delambre lui succĂŠda Ă  lâObservatoire de Paris. Sâappuyant sur les rĂŠsultats et les notes de MĂŠchain, ainsi que sur ceux de Lalande et sur ses propres observations, il rĂŠdigea les trois volumes de la Base du systĂ¨me mĂŠtrique dĂŠcimal, publiĂŠs entre 1806 et 1810, ouvrage fondateur qui ĂŠtablit les principes du systĂ¨me mĂŠtrique dĂŠcimal.
Delambre fut ĂŠgalement membre du Bureau des longitudes et de lâInstitut de France.
history:
Delambre took part in the measurement of the length of the Earthâs meridian arc between Dunkirk and Barcelona, alongside Pierre MĂŠchain and JĂŠrĂ´me Lalande. After MĂŠchainâs death, Delambre succeeded him at the Observatoire de Paris. Drawing on MĂŠchainâs results and notes, as well as on those of Lalande and his own observations, he authored the three-volume Base du systĂ¨me mĂŠtrique dĂŠcimal, published between 1806 and 1810, a foundational work that established the principles of the decimal metric system.
Delambre was also a member of the Bureau des longitudes and the Institut de France.

Ăle BĂŠzout se situe au sud est de l'Ăle Delambre, a environ 5 km au large du littoral continental.
