characteristic: BĂŠzout Island is located to the south-east of Delambre Island, approximately 5 km offshore from the mainland coast. It is named after the French mathematician Ătienne BĂŠzout (1730â1783).
history_fr: BĂŠzout fut chargĂŠ par le ministre de la Marine sous le rĂ¨gne de Louis XV de rĂŠorganiser la formation des officiers de la Marine royale. Il conĂ§ut un nouveau programme dâenseignement et mit en place des concours dâadmission. Il rĂŠdigea ĂŠgalement une sĂŠrie de manuels pĂŠdagogiques destinĂŠs Ă  la Marine et aux ĂŠcoles militaires, parmi lesquels le Cours de mathĂŠmatiques Ă  lâusage des gardes du Pavillon et de la Marine, plusieurs fois rĂŠĂŠditĂŠ dans des versions rĂŠvisĂŠes et augmentĂŠes, ainsi que le Cours complet de mathĂŠmatiques Ă  lâusage de la marine et de lâartillerie. Cet ouvrage fut ensuite amendĂŠ et enrichi afin de tenir compte de lâadoption du systĂ¨me mĂŠtrique et des nouveaux dĂŠveloppements scientifiques, et fut utilisĂŠ de maniĂ¨re systĂŠmatique par les ĂŠlĂ¨ves de lâĂcole polytechnique. BĂŠzout publia par ailleurs des versions actualisĂŠes de ses travaux mathĂŠmatiques plus thĂŠoriques, notamment la ThĂŠorie gĂŠnĂŠrale des ĂŠquations algĂŠbriques.
history: BĂŠzout was entrusted by the Navy Minister under Louis XV with the task of reorganising the training of officers in the Royal Navy. He designed a new curriculum and oversaw competitive entrance examinations. He also authored a series of instructional manuals for the Navy and military schools, including Cours de mathĂŠmatiques Ă  lâusage des gardes du Pavillon et de la Marine (âMathematics Course for the Guards of the Pavilion and the Navyâ), which was reprinted several times in revised and expanded editions, and the Cours complet de mathĂŠmatiques Ă  lâusage de la marine et de lâartillerie. The latter was subsequently amended and enlarged to reflect the adoption of the metric system and new scientific developments, and was used systematically by students at the Ăcole Polytechnique in their studies. BĂŠzout also published updated versions of his more theoretical mathematical works, notably the ThĂŠorie gĂŠnĂŠrale des ĂŠquations algĂŠbriques.

characteristic_fr: Le passage Bouguer est un chenal situĂŠ entre le cĂ´tĂŠ sud de lâĂŽle Dixon et le littoral continental. Il doit son nom Ă  Pierre Bouguer (1698â1758), savant dont les travaux portaient tout particuliĂ¨rement sur la gĂŠophysique et les questions nautiques.
characteristic: Bouguer entrance is a passage between the southern side of Dixon Island to the mainland. It is named after Pierre Bouguer (1698-1758), a man of science with a particular interest in geophysics and nautical issues.
history_fr:
Bouguer fut une figure majeure des sciences maritimes au XVIIIáľ siĂ¨cle. EnvoyĂŠ au PĂŠrou en 1735 avec Charles-Marie de La Condamine afin de mesurer un arc de mĂŠridien Ă  lâĂŠquateur, il contribua de maniĂ¨re dĂŠcisive aux dĂŠbats sur la forme de la Terre. Il publia les rĂŠsultats de cette expĂŠdition dans La Figure de la Terre (1749).
Bouguer consacra une large part de sa carriĂ¨re aux questions de navigation et dâarchitecture navale. Ses travaux sur les manĹuvres des navires, la conception des coques et la formulation mathĂŠmatique du mĂŠtacentre comme mesure de la stabilitĂŠ des bĂ˘timents devinrent des rĂŠfĂŠrences fondamentales de la thĂŠorie navale et de la formation des officiers. Ă partir de 1745, il conseilla lâAcadĂŠmie des sciences et la Marine franĂ§aise sur les mĂŠthodes de dĂŠtermination de la longitude en mer et succĂŠda Ă  Maupertuis comme officier pour le perfectionnement de la marine. Sceptique Ă  lâĂŠgard des technologies de mesure du temps alors disponibles, Bouguer sâopposa Ă  lâusage des chronomĂ¨tres marins pour le calcul des longitudes, une position qui fut ultĂŠrieurement considĂŠrĂŠe comme ayant freinĂŠ le dĂŠveloppement de la chronomĂŠtrie marine en France par rapport Ă  la Grande-Bretagne.
history:
Bouguer was a key figure in eighteenth-century maritime science, He was sent to Peru in 1735 with Charles-Marie de La Condamine to measure a meridian arc at the equator, contributing decisively to debates on the shape of the Earth. He published the results in La Figure de la Terre (1749).
Bouguer devoted much of his career to navigation and naval architecture. His studies of ship manoeuvring, hull design, and the mathematical formulation of the metacentre as a measure of vessel stability became foundational to naval theory and officer training. From 1745, he advised the AcadĂŠmie des sciences and the French Navy on methods for determining longitude at sea and succeeded Maupertuis as officier pour le perfectionnement de la marine. Sceptical of contemporary timekeeping technology, Bouguer opposed the use of marine chronometers for longitude determination, a stance that later came to be seen as limiting French advances in marine chronometry compared with Britain.

characteristic_fr: Picard Island est une petite ĂŽle situĂŠe au sud-est du cap Lambert. Elle doit son nom au gĂŠodĂŠsien et astronome franĂ§ais Jean Picard, dit lâabbĂŠ Picard (1620â1682).
characteristic:
Picard Island is a little island south east of Cape Lambert.
 It is named after the French geodesist and astronomer, Jean Picard.
history_fr: Jean Picard (1620â1682), connu sous le nom de lâabbĂŠ Picard, fut lâun des pionniers de la mesure de la dimension de la Terre. Bien avant Delambre et Legendre, il mit en Ĺuvre une mĂŠthode de triangulation systĂŠmatique associĂŠe Ă  lâusage de lunettes astronomiques montĂŠes sur un quadrant afin de mesurer la longueur dâun arc de mĂŠridien entre Amiens et Paris. Ă partir dâune base soigneusement mesurĂŠe, il ĂŠtablit une chaĂŽne de treize triangles successifs, dont il releva les angles depuis des points ĂŠlevĂŠs tels que des tours et des clochers. Connaissant la longueur de cette portion de mĂŠridien, Picard put en dĂŠduire la circonfĂŠrence terrestre avec une prĂŠcision alors inĂŠgalĂŠe.
history: Jean Picard (1620â1682), known as lâabbĂŠ Picard, was a pioneer in the measurement of the Earthâs size. Decades before Delambre and Legendre, he applied systematic triangulation combined with telescopic instruments mounted on a quadrant to determine the length of a meridian arc between Amiens and Paris. Starting from a carefully measured baseline, Picard constructed a chain of thirteen interconnected triangles, measuring their angles from elevated points such as towers and bell towers. From the length of this meridian segment, he was able to calculate the Earthâs circumference with unprecedented accuracy.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Depuch est une ĂŽle rocheuse sĂŠparĂŠe du littoral continental par environ 3 km dâeaux peu profondes et de vasiĂ¨res tidales bordĂŠes de mangroves. Elle est principalement constituĂŠe de blocs anguleux de dolĂŠrite, avec seulement quelques plages sableuses limitĂŠes sur ses rivages mĂŠridionaux et orientaux. Des sols peu ĂŠpais apparaissent ponctuellement entre les affleurements rocheux au sommet de lâĂŽle et dans les vallĂŠes profondĂŠment incisĂŠes. De forme globalement oblongue, lâĂŽle Depuch mesure environ 5 km de long sur 3 km de large et culmine Ă  160 mĂ¨tres dâaltitude (Depuch Beacon).
LâĂŽle revĂŞt une importance culturelle exceptionnelle en tant que site majeur pour les populations aborigĂ¨nes ngarluma, abritant lâune des plus fortes concentrations de gravures rupestres dâAustralie, comparable Ă  celle de la pĂŠninsule de Burrup. LâĂŽle Depuch doit son nom au minĂŠralogiste principal de lâexpĂŠdition, Louis Depuch (1774â1803 ; voir Anse Depuch, Australie-Occidentale).
characteristic:
Depuch Island is a rocky island separated from the mainland coast by approximately 3 km of shallow waters and tidal mudflats bordered by mangroves. The island is largely composed of angular dolerite blocks, with only limited sandy beaches along its southern and eastern shores. Sparse soils occur between rock outcrops on the summit and within deeply incised valleys. Roughly oblong in shape, Depuch Island measures about 5 km in length and 3 km in width, rising to 160 meters (Depuch Beacon).
The island holds exceptional cultural significance as a major Ngarluma Aboriginal site, containing one of the highest concentrations of rock engravings in Australia, comparable to those of the Burrup Peninsula. Depuch Island was named after the expeditionâs senior mineralogist Louis Depuch (1774â1803, see Anse Depuch, in WA).
history_fr:
Depuch confirma que les ĂŠchantillons rocheux recueillis par Ronsard lors de sa visite de lâĂŽle le 27 juillet 1801, effectuĂŠe Ă  bord de la chaloupe du GĂŠographe, ĂŠtaient du basalte et en dĂŠduisit, avec Ronsard, que lâĂŽle ĂŠtait dâorigine volcanique.
PĂŠron prĂŠcise : ÂŤ La couleur de ce basalte, dit mon intĂŠressant ami Depuch, qui en avait plusieurs ĂŠchantillons Ă  sa disposition, est dâun bleu grisĂ˘tre ; sa texture est trĂ¨s compacte, son grain fin et dâapparence siliceuse ; de petites lames brillantes, irrĂŠguliĂ¨rement rĂŠparties, sont dissĂŠminĂŠes dans toute la masse ; il nâeffervescence point avec les acides et nâagit que trĂ¨s faiblement sur la barre magnĂŠtique ; sa partie extĂŠrieure a subi une sorte dâaltĂŠration produite par des molĂŠcules ferrugineusesâŚ Âť (p.131).
De son cĂ´tĂŠ, Freycinet note : ÂŤ DâaprĂ¨s les observations de M. Ronsard, lâĂŽle Depuch ne paraĂŽt pas habitĂŠe, Ă  moins que lâon ne veuille considĂŠrer comme preuve dâun sĂŠjour temporaire de lâhomme sur ces rivages la rencontre de deux troncs dâarbres brĂťlĂŠs et de quelques laves rĂŠcemment fracturĂŠes Âť (p. 231).
Aujourdâhui, les ĂŠtudes gĂŠologiques identifient les roches de lâĂŽle comme de la dolĂŠrite â une roche magmatique de composition chimique identique Ă  celle du basalte, mais Ă  cristaux plus grossiers â et lâĂŽle Depuch est aujourdâhui reconnue comme un tĂŠmoignage majeur dâune prĂŠsence aborigĂ¨ne plurimillĂŠnaire, avec plus de 5 000 gravures rupestres recensĂŠes.
history:
Depuch confirmed that the rock specimens collected by Ronsard during his visit to the island on 27 July 1801, made in the GĂŠographeâs longboat, were basalt, and, together with Ronsard, inferred that the island was of volcanic origin.
PĂŠron explained: âThe colour of this basalt,â says my interesting friend Depuch, who had several samples at his disposal, âis greyish blue; its texture is very dense, its grain fine and siliceous in appearance; small shiny blades, irregularly distributed, are scattered throughout the mass; it does not effervesce with acids and does not significantly affect the magnetic bar; its outer part has undergone a kind of alteration produced by ferruginous molecules âŚâ.
For his part, Freycinet noted: âAccording to the observations of M. Ronsard, Depuch Island does not appear to be inhabited, unless, however, one wishes to consider as proof of manâs temporary stay on these shores the encounter there of two burnt tree trunks and some freshly broken lavaâ (p. 231).
Today, geological studies identify the islandâs rocks as doleriteâan igneous rock with the same chemical composition as basalt but with larger crystal sizesâand Depuch Island is recognised for its long-standing Aboriginal presence, with more than 5,000 individual rock engravings recorded.

CĂ´te du nord-ouest de la Nouvelle-Hollande, carte refaite au DĂŠpĂ´t par Boullanger. LâĂŽle Depuch figure dans la partie droite de la carte ; ses deux extrĂŠmitĂŠs ont servi de repĂ¨res pour les mesures angulaires utilisĂŠes pour lâestimation du dĂŠveloppement longitudinal de lâĂŽle, MAP/6JJ/4/C, piĂ¨ce 140.
Source : Archives nationales de France.

North-west coast of New Holland, map redrawn at the DĂŠpĂ´t by Boullanger. Depuch Island appears in the right-hand part of the map; its two extremities served as reference points for angular measurements used to calculate distances and construct the charts, MAP/6JJ/4/C, item 140.
Source: National Archives of France.

characteristic_fr:
Ce groupe dâĂŽles se compose de deux ĂŽles principales, lâĂŽle Depuch et lâĂŽle Ronsard, ainsi que de quelques ĂŽlots sableux, frĂŠquentĂŠs depuis des millĂŠnaires par les populations aborigĂ¨nes.
Il doit son nom Ă  Jacques Antoine Isidore Forestier (1762â1825), haut fonctionnaire, chef de la premiĂ¨re division du ministĂ¨re de la Marine et des Colonies.
characteristic:
This group  of islands consists of two main ones, Ăle Depuch and Ăle Ronsard, and a few sandy islets, visited for millenium by aboriginal people. 
It is named after Jacques Antoine Isidore Forestier (1762- 1825), a senior public servant, chief of the first subdivision of the Ministry of the Navy and Colonies.
history_fr: PĂŠron, amer, rapporte: "Le 27, nous eĂťmes la vue d'un petit archipel, que nous nommĂ˘mes Archipel Forestier, en l'honneur du Chef de la premiĂ¨re division du MinistĂ¨re de la marine. Ces ĂŽles, que nous avons depuis reconnues d'une maniĂ¨re plus exacte, sont Ă  peu de distance de celles du Romarin et de l'archipel de Dampier. Nous en avons comptĂŠ six principales, dont la plus grande n'a pas plus de 3 ou 4 lieues de longueur : elles paroissent gĂŠnĂŠralement basses et stĂŠriles, comme celles de Rivoli ; mais l'une d'entre elles, qui reĂ§ut le nom d'ĂŽle Depuch, et qui gĂŽt par 200 35' 30" de latitude, et par 11 12' 50" de longitude, se prĂŠsentoit avec des caractĂ¨res si particuliers, que le Commandant crut devoir la faire reconnoĂŽtre d'une maniĂ¨re plus exacte. En consĂŠquence, M. Ronsard partit avec le grand canot Vainement les naturalistes demandĂ¨rent Ă  descendre Ă  terre, aucun d'eux ne put l'obtenir."
history: A bitter PĂŠron noted: âOn the 27th we sighted a group of islands that we named the Forestier Islands, in honour of the head of the first division of the Ministry of Marine. These islands, which we have since reconnoitred more thoroughly, lie in a little way from Rosemary Island and the Dampier Archipelago. We counted six of themâŚbut the main one â which was named Depuch Island â is no more than 3- or 4-miles longs. They appear generally low and barren, like the Rivoli group. Depuch Island, however, âŚ displayed such distinctive features that the Commander thought that it should be examined in greater detail. Consequently, M. Ronsard set off in the big dinghyâŚThe naturalists asked in vain to go ashore â none of them was permitted to âŚâ

characteristic_fr: Sable Island fait partie des ĂŽles Forestier. Elle fut nommĂŠe Ălot de sable par Freycinet, en rĂŠfĂŠrence Ă  sa nature essentiellement sableuse.
characteristic: Sable Island is part of the Forestier Islands. It was named Ălot de sable by Freycinet, in reference to its predominantly sandy character.
history_fr: Freycinet note que " LâĂŽlot de sable, situĂŠ au NE. de lâĂŽle Depuch, dit M. RONSARD, nâest couvert que dâune vĂŠgĂŠtation foible et misĂŠrable; un banc qui assĂ¨che trĂ¨s-probablement dans lesplus grandes marĂŠes, joint cet ĂŽlot avec le continent: Ă  sa pointe Occidentale il y a un brisant dont il est bon de se dĂŠfier."
history: Freycinet reported that âthe sandy islet [Sable island] to the NE of Depuch Island, says M. Ronsard*, is only covered with weak and miserable vegetation; a shoal which, very probably, dries up in the highest tides, joins this islet with the mainland: at its western tip there is a breakwater which is worth being wary of.â

characteristic_fr: LâĂŽle Ronsard prĂŠsente une forme allongĂŠe et constitue lâĂŽle la plus au nord-est de lâarchipel Forestier. Elle doit son nom Ă  lâingĂŠnieur de la Marine FranĂ§ois-Michel Ronsard, embarquĂŠ Ă  bord du GĂŠographe.
characteristic: Ronsard Island has an elongated form and is the north-easternmost of the Forestier Islands. It is named after the naval engineer FranĂ§ois-Michel Ronsard, who served aboard Le GĂŠographe.
history_fr:
Ronsard (voir lâentrĂŠe Cap Ronsard, Australie-Occidentale) fut chargĂŠ par Baudin de relever les ĂŽles les plus occidentales de lâarchipel Forestier. Toutefois, il ne disposa pas du temps nĂŠcessaire pour examiner chacune dâentre elles en dĂŠtail et, comme le prĂŠcise Freycinet,  "LâĂŽle Ronsard nâa point ĂŠtĂŠ visitĂŠe dâune maniĂ¨re ParticuliĂ¨re" . Freycinet observe nĂŠanmoins que "ses bords sont assez ĂŠlevĂŠs, comparĂŠs aux terres environnantes."
Freycinet avertit en outre le lecteur que LâĂŽle Ronsard et les ĂŽlots qui lâavoisinent ont une position douteuse, quâindiquent dâailleurs les lettres P. D. qui les accompagnent.â [sur la carte de lâatlas] Âť, signalant ainsi lâincertitude de leur dĂŠlimitation cartographique.
history:
Ronsard (see the entry Cap Ronsard, WA) was entrusted by Baudin with the surveying of the westernmost islands of the Forestier Archipelago. However, he did not have sufficient time to examine each island in detail, and, as Freycinet notes, âRonsard Island was not visited in any particular way.â Freycinet nevertheless observes that âits shores are quite high compared to the surrounding land.â
Freycinet further cautions readers that âRonsard Island and the islets surrounding it are in a dubious position, as indicated by the letters P. D. accompanying them [on the chart of the atlas],â signalling the uncertain nature of their cartographic delineation.

characteristic_fr: Les Basses du GĂŠographe sont situĂŠes entre les caps Cossigny et Thouin. Cette dĂŠnomination vise Ă  dĂŠsigner et Ă  signaler aux navigateurs lâexistence dâun vaste haut-fond et de sa marge abrupte, que la corvette Le GĂŠographe dut affronter et cartographier.
characteristic: The Basses du GĂŠographe are located between Capes Cossigny and Thouin. The name was given to designate and warn navigators of an extensive shoal and its steep outer margin, which the corvette Le GĂŠographe had to confront and chart.
history_fr:
Peron notes: "Le 28 juillet [1801], sur les cinq heures du soir, nous dĂŠcouvrĂŽmes une batture assez ĂŠtendue, que nous eĂťmes beaucoup de peine Ă  ĂŠviter : la mer y brisoit avec force, et la sonde avoit baissĂŠ si rapidement Ă  son approche, qu'en peu d'instans elle ĂŠtoit tombĂŠe audessous de 8 brasses. Nous nommĂ˘mes ce haut -fond Basses duGĂŠographe."
PrĂ¨s de deux ans plus tard, la corvette Le GĂŠographe, naviguant de conserve avec le Casuarina, eut de nouveau de grandes difficultĂŠs Ă  ĂŠviter le long rĂŠcif bordant les Basses du GĂŠographe, oĂš la profondeur diminuait trĂ¨s rapidement. Freycinet prĂŠcise toutefois quâils purent y mouiller par dix brasses dâeau, sur un bon fond.
history:
PĂŠron notes: âOn 28 July [1801], at about five oâclock in the evening, we sighted a fairly extensive shoal, which we had great difficulty in avoiding. The sea was breaking there with force, and the depth given by the lead had decreased so rapidly on our approach that, within a few moments, it had fallen to less than eight fathoms. We named this shoal the GĂŠographe Shoals.â
Nearly two years later, the corvette Le GĂŠographe, sailing in company with the Casuarina, again experienced great difficulty in avoiding the long reef bordering the Basses du GĂŠographe, where the depth diminished very rapidly. Freycinet nevertheless notes that they were able to anchor there in ten fathoms of water, over a good holding ground.

characteristic_fr: Une grande partie de ce secteur du littoral de la Pilbara correspond Ă  une cĂ´te basse de type deltaĂŻque, sujette aux submersions marines et aux crues fluviales. Ses deux caps ont reĂ§u des noms en hommage Ă  des botanistes renommĂŠs, dont Joseph-FranĂ§ois Charpentier de Cossigny (1736â1809) pour le cap mĂŠridional.
characteristic: A large part of this section of the Pilbara coast consists of low-lying, deltaic shoreline, prone to marine inundation and river flooding. Its two headlands were named in honour of renowned botanists, the southern cape bearing the name of Joseph-FranĂ§ois Charpentier de Cossigny (1736â1809).
history_fr: Joseph-FranĂ§ois Charpentier de Cossigny (1736â1809), ĂŠgalement connu sous le nom de Joseph-FranĂ§ois Charpentier de Cossigny de Palm, fonda sur lâIsle de France (voir le glossaire) une colonie quâil nomma Palma et y crĂŠa plusieurs jardins destinĂŠs Ă  lâacclimatation dâespĂ¨ces fruitiĂ¨res importĂŠes. Il est notamment connu pour avoir introduit le litchi dans les ĂŽles de Bourbon (rebaptisĂŠe Bonaparte au moment du voyage) et de lâIsle de France en 1764, Ă  la suite de plusieurs sĂŠjours en Chine et en Asie du Sud-Est. De retour Ă  Paris, il devint membre de lâInstitut de France en 1803, au sein de la section de botanique, et se consacra alors Ă  ses travaux et publications scientifiques.
history: Joseph-FranĂ§ois Charpentier de Cossigny, also known as Joseph-FranĂ§ois Charpentier de Cossigny de Palm, founded on Isle de France (see the glossary) a colony he named Palma and created several gardens for acclimatising imported fruit species. He is known for introducing the lychee to the islands of Bourbon (renamed Bonaparte at the time of the voyage) and Isle de France in 1764 after making several trips to China and the Southeast Asia. Back in Paris, he became a member of the Institut in 1803 in the botanical section and worked on his scientific publications.

characteristic_fr: Une grande partie de ce secteur du littoral de la Pilbara correspond Ă  une cĂ´te basse de type deltaĂŻque, sujette aux submersions marines et aux crues fluviales. Ses deux caps ont reĂ§u des noms en hommage Ă  des botanistes renommĂŠs, dont AndrĂŠ Thouin (1746â1824)  pour le cap septentrional.
characteristic: A large part of this section of the Pilbara coast consists of low-lying, deltaic shoreline, prone to marine inundation and river flooding. Its two headlands were named in honour of renowned botanists, the northern cape bearing the name of AndrĂŠ Thouin (1746â1824).
history_fr:
AndrĂŠ Thouin (1746â1824) fut un botaniste franĂ§ais influent et disposant de nombreux rĂŠseaux. En 1793, il fut nommĂŠ titulaire de la chaire dâhorticulture au MusĂŠum national dâhistoire naturelle Ă  Paris.
Dans le contexte des guerres rĂŠvolutionnaires, certains hommes de science, parmi lesquels Thouin, adhĂŠrĂ¨rent Ă  lâuniversalitĂŠ des idĂŠaux rĂŠvolutionnaires et estimĂ¨rent lĂŠgitime la saisie de collections dâhistoire naturelle et dâanimaux vivants rares comme prises de guerre. Ainsi, lorsque les armĂŠes rĂŠvolutionnaires franĂ§aises occupĂ¨rent les Pays-Bas en 1794, Thouin, avec BarthĂŠlemy Faujas de Saint-Fond (voir Piton Faujas, Tasmanie), dirigea le transfert Ă  Paris de collections nĂŠerlandaises dâhistoire naturelle, comprenant notamment des ĂŠlĂŠphants vivants et des animaux naturalisĂŠs provenant de territoires dâoutre-mer. En 1796, il fut chargĂŠ dâune mission similaire lors de la campagne dâItalie de Bonaparte.
En juin 1803, lorsque Le Naturaliste revint au Havre avec une grande partie des collections dâhistoire naturelle de lâexpĂŠdition Baudin, le sĂŠnateur Jean-Antoine Chaptal, scientifique renommĂŠ et promoteur de nouvelles mĂŠthodes agricoles â telles que lâajout de sucre au moĂťt non fermentĂŠ afin dâaugmenter le degrĂŠ alcoolique du vin aprĂ¨s fermentation (chaptalisation) â chargea Thouin de recevoir ces collections et dâen assurer la conservation. Si la majoritĂŠ des spĂŠcimens vĂŠgĂŠtaux collectĂŠs en Australie ne survĂŠcurent pas au voyage, certaines graines de plantes alors inconnues en France purent toutefois ĂŞtre mises en culture, notamment des eucalyptus et des acacias. Ă leur arrivĂŠe Ă  Paris, Thouin reĂ§ut lâordre dâenvoyer une large part des collections, incluant des kangourous, des casoars, des tortues et des cygnes noirs, non pas au MusĂŠum et Ă  sa mĂŠnagerie, mais au jardin de lâimpĂŠratrice Ă  la Malmaison.
history:
AndrĂŠ Thouin (1746â1824) was an influential and well-connected French botanist. In 1793, he was appointed to the chair of horticulture at the MusĂŠum national d'histoire naturelle in Paris. 
In the context of the revolutionary wars, some men of science, including Thouin, accepted the universality of the revolutionary ideals and that the seizure of natural history collections and rare live animals as prizes of war was permissible. When, in 1794, the French revolutionary armies occupied the Netherlands, Thouin with BarthĂŠlemy Faujas de Saint-Fond (see Piton Faujas*, Tasmania) directed the move to Paris, of Dutch natural history collections, including life elephants and stuffed animals derived from countries overseas. Later, in 1796, he was charged with a similar mission, during Bonaparte's Italian campaign. 
In June 1803, when le Naturaliste returned to Le Havre with a large part of the Baudin expeditionâs collections of natural history specimens, the senator Jean-Antoine Chaptal, a well-regarded scientist keen to develop in agriculture new methods such as the technique of adding sugar to unfermented grape to increase their alcohol level after fermentation (chaptalisation), sent Thouin to receive and take care of them. Unfortunately, most of the plant specimens collected in Australia did not survive the voyage, but some seeds from plants new to France were germinated, e.g., eucalypts and acacias. On their arrival in Paris, Thouin was ordered to send a large part of the collections, including kangaroos, cassowaries, tortoises and black swans, to the empressâs garden at La Malmaison, rather than to the Museum and its menagerie.

characteristic_fr: Les ĂŽlots des Tortues se composent de deux ĂŽlots, North Turtle Island et Little Turtle Island, situĂŠs au sud et au large de Port Hedland. Cette dĂŠnomination provient de lâabondance de tortues observĂŠes dans les eaux environnantes par lâĂŠquipage du GĂŠographe.
characteristic: The Turtle Islands consist of two islets, North Turtle Island and Little Turtle Island, located south of, and offshore from, Port Hedland. The toponym derives from the abundance of turtles observed in the surrounding waters by the crew of the GĂŠographe.
history_fr: Freycinet rapporte: "Les ĂŽlots des Tortues (carte 25} sont au nombre de deux, et gisent Ă  lâO. N. O. du cap Larrey. Celui du S.0. n'est qu'un plateau de sable de peu dĂŠtendue, auquel tient un brisant : lâautre, plus Septentrional, et non moins stĂŠrile, est entourĂŠ dâun banc de roches et de corail, Ă  fleur dâeau, sur lequel la mer dĂŠferle presque pair-tout. Je me suis approchĂŠ de ce banc, sur le Casuarina, Ă  un demiâmille de distance, et par les 9 brasses dâeau; il m'a paru ĂŠcore, et je crois que de beau temps il seroit facile dây dĂŠbarquer, en se plaĂ§ant sous le vent."
history: Freycinet reports: âThe Turtle Islets (map 25) number two and lie to the W.N.W. of Cape Larrey. The south-western one is nothing more than a small sandy platform, to which a breaker is attached; the other, farther north and no less barren, is surrounded by a bank of rocks and coral, awash, over which the sea breaks almost everywhere. I approached this bank aboard the *Casuarina* to within half a mile, in nine fathoms of water; it appeared to me to be steep-edged, and I believe that in fine weather it would be easy to land there by taking a position to leeward.â

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Larrey est situĂŠ sur la cĂ´te mĂŠridionale de la rĂŠgion du Kimberley, au nord-est de Port Hedland, Ă  lâembouchure de la riviĂ¨re Shaw.
Il doit son nom Ă  Dominique Jean Larrey, considĂŠrĂŠ comme le pĂ¨re de la mĂŠdecine militaire moderne.
characteristic:
Cape Larrey is located on the southern coast of the Kimberley region, north-east of Port Hedland, at the mouth of the Shaw River.
It is named after Dominique Jean Larrey, widely regarded as the father of modern military medicine.
history_fr: Freycinet note que le Geographe et le casuarina ont trop souvent naviguĂŠ Ă  une grande distance des cĂ´tes pour quâil fĂťt toujours possible de bien observer. la forme prĂŠcise des terres, mais que le cap Larrey doit etre regarder comme un point exact sur cette cĂ´te, avec ĂŽlots des Tortues et le cap KĂŠraudren.
history: Freycinet notes that Le GĂŠographe and the Casuarina too often sailed at a considerable distance from the coast for it to have been possible to observe the precise form of the land in all cases, but that Cape Larrey should nevertheless be regarded as a reliable fixed point along this coast, together with the Turtle Islets and Cape KĂŠraudren.

characteristic_fr: Le Banc des Amphinomes relĂ¨ve du domaine de la vie marine. BruguiĂ¨re (voir lâentrĂŠe cap BruguiĂ¨re, Australie-Occidentale) dĂŠcrivait les Amphinomidae (vers de feu) comme un ÂŤ genre de vers marins de la famille des mollusques Âť, conformĂŠment aux classifications en usage Ă  son ĂŠpoque..
characteristic: The Amphinomes Bank falls within the domain of marine life. BruguiĂ¨re (see the entry Cape BruguiĂ¨re, Western Australia) described the Amphinomidae (fire worms) as a âgenus of marine worms of the mollusc family,â in accordance with the classifications in use at the time.
history_fr:
Freycinet note que le banc des Amphinomes est "remarquable par son ĂŠtendue et par les dangers quây courut la corvette le GĂŠagmp/oe, dans la nuit du 2 avril 1803 '. Nous nâavons pu fixer les limites de cette batture dans le Nord; mais au Sud, il paroĂŽt qu'elle va se joindre Ă  la grande terre, et quâuneâ partie assĂ¨che de basse mer. Par-tout le brassiage est fort irrĂŠgulier, et les sauts de sonde souvent considĂŠrables, sur un fond tantĂ´t de roches et corail, et tantĂ´t de beau sable." Il en fait une esquisse qu'il joint a la premiere feuille de la carte  dâune partie de la Terre de Witt de l'atlas.
De son cote, Peron note que, malgre tout, " nous parvĂŽnmes ainsi Ă  dĂŠborder cette vaste batture, Ă  laquelle nous avons donnĂŠ le nom de Banc des Amphinomes.Tous ces bancs, tous ces rĂŠcifs qui nous dĂŠsespĂŠroient en nous
empĂŞchant de nous occuper de la gĂŠographie des cĂ´tes, ĂŠtoient au contraire bien favorables Ă  nos recherches d'histoire naturelle ;" Il decrivait les amphinomes parmi les animaux dont ÂŤ le corps est pourvu de pieds ciliĂŠsâŚ oblong, articulĂŠ et rampant, la bouche et lâanus sĂŠparĂŠs et placĂŠs aux deux extrĂŠmitĂŠs, une crĂŞte charnue au-dessus de lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ antĂŠrieure, et deux rangĂŠes de branchies ramifiĂŠes sur toute la longueur du dos Âť.
history:
Freycinet notes that the Amphinomes Bank is âremarkable both for its extent and for the dangers to which the corvette Le GĂŠographe was exposed there during the night of 2 April 1803. We were unable to determine the northern limits of this shoal; but to the south it appears to join the mainland, and part of it dries at low tide. Everywhere the soundings are highly irregular, with often considerable sudden changes in depth, over a bottom that is sometimes rock and coral, and at other times fine sand.â He produced a sketch of the bank, which he appended to the first sheet of the Map of a Part of Terre de Witt in the atlas.

For his part, PĂŠron observes that, despite everything, âwe thus succeeded in rounding this vast shoal, to which we gave the name Amphinomes Bank. All these banks and reefs, which caused us such distress by preventing us from attending to the geography of the coast, were on the contrary highly favourable to our researches in natural history.â He described amphinomes as animals whose âbody is provided with ciliated feetâŚ oblong, articulated and crawling, with the mouth and anus separated and placed at the two extremities, a fleshy crest above the anterior extremity, and two rows of ramified gills extending along the entire length of the back.â

characteristic_fr:
Contrairement Ă  ce quâindique la carte de Freycinet, lâĂŽlot Poissonnier ne constitue pas un ĂŽlot distinct, mais correspond Ă  la pointe orientale de Breaker Inlet, sur la rive droite de la riviĂ¨re Shaw. Cette imprĂŠcision cartographique sâexplique par le grand nombre de hauts-fonds et de rĂŠcifs qui empĂŞchaient le GĂŠographe et le Casuarina dâapprocher la cĂ´te de suffisamment prĂ¨s pour en relever avec prĂŠcision la configuration.
Ce toponyme rend hommage aux frĂ¨res Pierre Isaac Poissonnier (1720â1797) et Antoine-Marie Poissonnier DesperriĂ¨res (1723â1793 ?), dont les travaux contribuĂ¨rent aux progrĂ¨s de la mĂŠdecine et des politiques de santĂŠ navales au XVIIIáľ siĂ¨cle.
characteristic:
Contrary to what is shown on Freycinetâs map, Poissonnier Islet does not constitute a distinct islet but corresponds to the eastern point of Breaker Inlet, on the right bank of the Shaw River. This cartographic inaccuracy can be explained by the large number of shoals and reefs, which prevented the GĂŠographe and the Casuarina from approaching the coast closely enough to survey its configuration with precision.
This toponym pays tribute to the brothers Pierre Isaac Poissonnier (1720â1797) and Antoine-Marie Poissonnier DesperriĂ¨res (1723â1793?), whose work contributed to advances in medicine and public health during the eighteenth century.

characteristic_fr:
Le cap KĂŠraudren, situĂŠ Ă  lâouest de la rĂŠserve de Jarrkunpungu, est un cap bas et sableux, bordĂŠ de vasiĂ¨res soumises aux marĂŠes.
Il porte le nom du mĂŠdecin de la Marine Pierre-FranĂ§ois KĂŠraudren (1769â1858), auteur dâÂŤ instructions sanitaires de la plus haute importance Âť pour le voyage.
characteristic:
The Cape Kerauden, west of the Jarrkunpungu Reserve, is a low, sandy headland with tidal flats.
It bears the name of the Navy medical officer Pierre-FranĂ§ois KĂŠraudren (1769-1858)  who wrote â health instructions of the greatest interest â for the voyage.
history_fr: Freycinet rapporte que le passage du GĂŠographe au large du cap KĂŠraudren aurait pu sâavĂŠrer fatal. Il ĂŠcrit : ÂŤ Un banc qui, quoique petit, est cependant trĂ¨s dangereux Ă  cause du faible brassiage, gĂŽt Ă  peu prĂ¨s au N.-E. du cap KĂŠraudren, par 19Â° 41â˛ de latitude ; le GĂŠographe, dont le tirant dâeau ĂŠtait de quatorze pieds, sây trouva engagĂŠ sur un fond de quatorze pieds et demi : heureusement la mer ĂŠtait parfaitement calme, et ce fut la cause du salut de ce bĂ˘timent. La sonde passa rapidement de dix Ă  sept, cinq, quatre et trois brasses, dans lâespace de deux minutes, et augmenta ensuite avec la mĂŞme cĂŠlĂŠritĂŠ. Âť
history: Freycinet reports that the passage of the *GĂŠographe* off Cape KĂŠraudren could have proved fatal. He writes: âA bank which, though small, is nevertheless very dangerous because of the shallow depth, lies approximately to the N.E. of Cape KĂŠraudren, at latitude 19Â° 41â˛. The *GĂŠographe*, whose draught was fourteen feet, found herself engaged there over a depth of fourteen and a half feet. Fortunately, the sea was perfectly calm, and this alone ensured the vesselâs safety. The soundings fell rapidly from ten to seven, five, four, and three fathoms within the space of two minutes, and then increased again with the same rapidity.â

characteristic_fr: Les Banc des Planaires comme celui des  Amphinome relĂ¨vent du domaine de la vie marine.
characteristic: The Planaires Banks, such as the Amphinomes Bank, fall within the domain of marine life.
history_fr:
Freycinet rapporte : ÂŤ Les bancs des Planaires, plus avancĂŠs du cĂ´tĂŠ de lâEst, semblent avoir une ĂŠtendue considĂŠrable en longitude ; mais nous nâavons pu dĂŠterminer sâils sâavanĂ§aient jusquâaux terres continentales, dont toutefois ils ne paraissent pas ĂŞtre trĂ¨s ĂŠloignĂŠs. Nos vigies ont cru apercevoir que la sommitĂŠ de ces bancs assĂ¨che Ă  basse mer. Âť
En raison de cette connaissance encore partielle du littoral, Freycinet a reprĂŠsentĂŠ ces terres continentales en pointillĂŠs sur les cartes de lâatlas.
history:
Freycinet reports: âThe Planarians Banks, projecting further to the east, appear to extend over a considerable distance in longitude; however, we were unable to determine whether they reach as far as the mainland, from which they nevertheless do not seem to be very distant. Our lookouts believed they could discern that the crest of these banks dries at low tide.â
Owing to this still incomplete knowledge of the coastline, Freycinet represented these mainland coasts with dotted lines on the atlas maps.
As a consequence of this incomplete knowledge,, on the atlas maps, these mainland coasts are represented by Freycinet with dotted lines.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle BĂŠdout est situĂŠe Ă  environ 50 km au large de lâestuaire de la riviĂ¨re Shaw.
Elle doit son nom Ă  lâofficier de marine franĂ§ais Jacques BĂŠdout.
characteristic:
Bedout Island is located approximately 50 km offshore from the estuary of the Shaw River.
It is named after the French naval officer Jacques BĂŠdout.
history_fr:
Jacques Bedout ĂŠtait un officier de marine nĂŠ au Canada qui a passĂŠ quatorze ans dans la marine marchande au dĂŠbut de sa carriĂ¨re, avant de rejoindre la marine franĂ§aise.
 AprĂ¨s la dĂŠfaite franĂ§aise Ă  Trafalgar en 1805, Bedout a mis fin Ă  sa carriĂ¨re officielle en prĂŠsidant le Conseil de guerre chargĂŠ de juger les officiers dont la conduite avait ĂŠtĂŠ jugĂŠe rĂŠprĂŠhensible lors de l'attaque de la flotte au large de l'ĂŽle d'Aix par les brĂťlots anglais. Sur les quatre officiers impliquĂŠs, deux ont ĂŠtĂŠ condamnĂŠs, dont un Ă  mort.
history:
Jacques Bedout was a Canadian-born naval officer who spent fourteenth years in the merchant navy at the beginning of his career, before joining the French Navy. 
After the French disaster of Trafalgar in 1805, Bedout ended his official career with the painful task of presiding over the Council of War to judge the officers whose conduct had been deemed reprehensible during the attack on the fleet on the Isle of Aix by English fire ships. Of the four officers involved, two were sentenced, one to death.

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Missiessy est un promontoire cĂ´tier situĂŠ dans la rĂŠgion de Pilbara, en Australie-Occidentale, situĂŠ au sein du Jinmarnkur National Park, Ă  proximitĂŠ du Eighty Mile Beach Marine Park. Cette zone se caractĂŠrise par de vastes estrans sableux et vaseux, qui constituent dâimportantes aires dâalimentation pour les oiseaux limicoles migrateurs et des sites majeurs de nidification pour les tortues Ă  dos plat.
Le cap doit son nom Ă  lâofficier de marine franĂ§ais Ădouard Thomas de Burgues, comte de Missiessy (1756â1837).
characteristic:
Cape Missiessy is a coastal headland in the Pilbara region of Western Australia, within Jinmarnkur National Park, adjoining the Eighty Mile Beach Marine Park, an area characterised by extensive intertidal sand and mudflats that support major feeding grounds for migratory shorebirds and nesting sites for flatback turtles.
The cape was named after the French naval officer Ădouard Thomas de Burgues, comte de Missiessy (1756 -1837).
history_fr:
Missiessy fut un officier de marine franĂ§ais promu au grade de contre-amiral en 1793. Comme de nombreux officiers durant la Terreur, il quitta temporairement la France avant de reprendre du service. Ă son retour, il fut chargĂŠ par Jean-Charles de Borda de contribuer Ă  lâintroduction du systĂ¨me mĂŠtrique dans les ports et arsenaux de la Marine, et joua un rĂ´le important dans la normalisation des signaux cĂ´tiers, participant ainsi Ă  lâamĂŠlioration de la sĂŠcuritĂŠ de la navigation et des communications maritimes.
Entre 1804 et 1805, Missiessy commanda lâescadre de Rochefort durant le blocus britannique des Antilles, assurant avec succĂ¨s le ravitaillement des colonies franĂ§aises et perturbant les opĂŠrations ennemies sans perdre un seul bĂ˘timent. Cette mission sâinscrivait dans une stratĂŠgie de diversion plus large liĂŠe au projet de NapolĂŠon dâinvasion de lâAngleterre, mĂŞme si lâabsence de communication complĂ¨te sur les objectifs stratĂŠgiques limita la coordination avec les autres escadres franĂ§aises.
Missiessy est ĂŠgalement reconnu pour son traitĂŠ influent sur lâarmement et lâorganisation interne des bĂ˘timents de guerre, publiĂŠ en 1797. FondĂŠ sur le modĂ¨le dâun vaisseau de 74 canons, cet ouvrage prĂŠconisait une standardisation des amĂŠnagements, prĂŠcisait les fonctions des diffĂŠrents espaces Ă  bord, dĂŠfinissait des rĂ´les spĂŠcifiques pour chaque membre de lâĂŠquipage et proposait des amĂŠliorations techniques destinĂŠes Ă  accroĂŽtre la manĹuvrabilitĂŠ et lâefficacitĂŠ au combat.
Pour lâensemble de ces raisons, Missiessy semble avoir ĂŠtĂŠ tenu en haute estime par Freycinet.
history:
Missiessy was a French naval officer promoted to the rank of rear admiral in 1793. Like many officers during the Terror, he temporarily left France before returning to service. On his return, he was entrusted by Jean-Charles de Borda with assisting in the introduction of the metric system in French ports and naval arsenals, and he played a key role in the standardisation of coastal signals, contributing to safer navigation and improved maritime communication.
Between 1804 and 1805, Missiessy commanded the Rochefort squadron during British blockades in the West Indies, successfully supplying French colonies and disrupting British operations without losing a ship. His mission formed part of a broader diversionary strategy linked to Napoleonâs planned invasion of England, though the lack of full strategic disclosure limited coordination with other French squadrons.
Missiessy was also recognised for his influential treatise on the fitting-out and internal organisation of warships, published in 1797. Drawing on the model of a 74-gun vessel, the work promoted standardised layouts, clarified the functions of shipboard spaces, defined precise roles for crew members, and proposed technical improvements to enhance manoeuvrability and combat readiness. 
For all these reasons, Missiessy appears to have been held in high esteem by Freycinet.

characteristic_fr: La baie Desault se caractĂŠrise par un littoral composĂŠ de vastes ĂŠtendues sableuses et de franges de mangroves.
characteristic:
Desault Bay is characterised by a coastline of extensive sandy flats and mangrove fringes.
Its name is for a reknown surgeon, Pierre-Joseph Desault (1738-1795).
history_fr: Pierre-Joseph Desault sâimposa comme lâune des figures majeures du renouvellement de lâenseignement chirurgical Ă  la fin du XVIIIáľ siĂ¨cle. Il introduisit une mĂŠthode novatrice dâapprentissage de lâanatomie, fondĂŠe sur lâĂŠtude mĂŠthodique et directe des corps humains, rompant avec une tradition encore largement livresque. NommĂŠ chirurgien en chef de lâHĂ´tel-Dieu de Paris, il transforma en profondeur la formation des chirurgiens en articulant ĂŠtroitement thĂŠorie et pratique clinique, dispensant son enseignement au cĹur mĂŞme de lâacte opĂŠratoire. Autour de lui se forma une gĂŠnĂŠration appelĂŠe Ă  marquer durablement la mĂŠdecine franĂ§aise, parmi laquelle Jean-Nicolas Corvisart, Pierre-FranĂ§ois Percy et Dominique Jean Larrey. Les bouleversements politiques de la Terreur, dont la radicalitĂŠ ĂŠbranla jusquâaux institutions hospitaliĂ¨res, affectĂ¨rent gravement sa santĂŠ et contribuĂ¨rent Ă  sa disparition prĂŠmaturĂŠe.
history: Pierre-Joseph Desault was a surgeon who introduced an innovative method of teaching anatomy based on the systematic study of human corpses. As chief surgeon at the HĂ´tel-Dieu in Paris, he transformed surgical training by combining lectures and close supervision with live clinical practice, teaching while performing surgical procedures. Desault trained many of the most renowned surgeons of his time, notably Jean-Nicolas Corvisart, Pierre-FranĂ§ois Percy, and Dominique Jean Larrey. The political violence and radicalism of the Terror took a severe toll on his own health, contributing to his premature death.

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Jaubert constitue un promontoire littoral remarquable appartenant au territoire du Karajarri Jurrar â littĂŠralement le ÂŤ pays cĂ´tier Âť dans la langue karajarri, rattachĂŠe Ă  la famille linguistique pama-nyungan. Il se situe Ă  lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ septentrionale de Eighty Mile Beach, en direction de Barn Hill, au sud de Broome, et sâouvre sur un vaste systĂ¨me de zones humides intertidales et de mangroves dâune importance ĂŠcologique majeure.
Il doit son nom Ă  Pierre AmĂŠdĂŠe Ămilien-Probe Jaubert (1779â1847), orientaliste qui exerĂ§a les fonctions dâinterprĂ¨te dâĂtat.
characteristic:
Cape Jaubert is a prominent headland forming part of the Karajarri Jurrar (meaning "Coastal Country" in Karajarri which is part of the Pama-Nyungan language family). It lies at the northern extremity of Eighty Mile Beach, extending toward Barn Hill, south of Broome, and fronts an extensive intertidal wetland system of significant ecological importance.
It was named after Pierre AmĂŠdĂŠe Ămilien-Probe Jaubert (1779â1847), an Orientalist who served as a state interpreter.
history_fr:
Jaubert ĂŠtudia les langues turque, arabe et persane Ă  lâĂcole des Langues orientales Ă  Paris. Il fut dâabord nommĂŠ interprĂ¨te auprĂ¨s de la lĂŠgation franĂ§aise Ă  Constantinople, avant dâĂŞtre attachĂŠ, en qualitĂŠ dâinterprĂ¨te, Ă  lâexpĂŠdition de NapolĂŠon en Ăgypte (1798-1799). En 1799, il accĂŠda aux fonctions de premier interprĂ¨te au service de NapolĂŠon.
Lorsque PĂŠron entreprit, en 1805, son travail de nomenclature, Jaubert avait reĂ§u mission dâĂŠtablir une alliance avec le chah de Perse, Fatá¸Ľ-Ali Shah QÄjÄr, dirigĂŠe contre lâAngleterre et la Russie. En route vers la Perse, il fut fait prisonnier par les Ottomans et demeura dĂŠtenu pendant huit mois. Il fut finalement reĂ§u en audience par le chah Ă  TĂŠhĂŠran en juin 1806.
Les nĂŠgociations aboutirent Ă  des rĂŠsultats significatifs et conduisirent Ă  la signature du traitĂŠ de Finkenstein, le 4 mai 1807. Ce service rendu Ă  la nation explique probablement lâinclusion du nom de Jaubert dans la nomenclature ĂŠtablie par PĂŠron. Toutefois, la paix conclue par NapolĂŠon avec Alexandre Ier de Russie le 7 juillet 1807 â signĂŠe sur un radeau au milieu du NiĂŠmen â priva le traitĂŠ de Finkenstein dâune grande part de sa portĂŠe politique.
history: Jaubert studied Turkish, Arabic, and Persian languages at the Ăcole des Langues Orientales in Paris, was appointed interpreter to the French legation in Constantinople before being assigned as interpreter to NapolĂŠonâs expedition to Egypt (1798-99) and in 1799, became senior interpreter in the service of NapolĂŠon. When PĂŠron started working at the nomenclature, in 1805, Jaubert was entrusted with the establishment of an alliance with the Persian Shah Fatá¸Ľ-Ali Shah QÄjÄr directed against England and Russia. On his way to Persia, he was made prisoner by the Ottomans and was held for eight months. He was finally received by the Shah at an audience in Tehran in June 1806. The negotiations were quite successful and led to the Treaty of Finkenstein, which was signed on 4 May 1807. This service to the nation probably led PĂŠron to include Jaubert in his nomenclature. However, NapolĂŠonâs peace treaty with Alexander I of Russia on 7 July 1807, signed on a raft in the middle of the Neman River, made the Finkenstein Treaty practically insignificant.

characteristic_fr:
Ă lâinstar du cap Jaubert, bien que dâune saillance plus modeste, le cap Frezier est un promontoire littoral appartenant au territoire du Karajarri Jurrar.
Il doit son nom Ă  AmĂŠdĂŠe-FranĂ§ois FrĂŠzier (1682â1773), savant aux talents multiples, qui sâillustra dans des domaines aussi variĂŠs que le gĂŠnie militaire, lâarchitecture, les mathĂŠmatiques, lâethnologie, la littĂŠrature critique et de voyage, lâexploration, la cartographie, la botanique, et mĂŞme lâespionnage.
characteristic:
Similar to Cape Jaubert, though somewhat less prominent, Cape Frezier is a coastal promontory forming part of the Karajarri Jurrar lands.
It was named after AmĂŠdĂŠe-FranĂ§ois FrĂŠzier (1682â1773), a polymath who distinguished himself in numerous fields, including military engineering, architecture, mathematics, ethnology, travel writing and critical literature, exploration, cartography, botany, and even espionage.
history_fr:
La Relation du voyage dans la mer du Sud de FrĂŠzier (voir lâillustration dans "en savoir plus") constitua une source largement consultĂŠe par les navigateurs, parmi lesquels Bougainville (voir la notice Baie Bougainville en Australie-MĂŠridionale) et La PĂŠrouse ; lâouvrage fut dâailleurs traduit en anglais. Il connut ĂŠgalement un vif succĂ¨s ĂŠditorial et figura dans les bibliothĂ¨ques de philosophes tels que Montesquieu, ainsi que dans celles de nombreux voyageurs.
Les descriptions prĂŠcises, les analyses approfondies et lâabondante illustration qui caractĂŠrisent les publications de FrĂŠzier furent particuliĂ¨rement apprĂŠciĂŠes des lecteurs intĂŠressĂŠs par la botanique et lâhistoire naturelle, Ă  lâimage de PĂŠron. FrĂŠzier y dĂŠcrit notamment la plante indigĂ¨ne Fragaria chiloensis (la fraise de plage), observĂŠe sur lâĂŽle de ChiloĂŠ, au Chili. Il en introduisit des plants en France, oĂš ils furent hybridĂŠs, donnant naissance Ă  la fraise moderne telle que nous la consommons aujourdâhui.
history: FrĂŠzier's  Relation du voyage dans la mer du Sud (see the illustration in Details), was a source widely consulted by navigators, including Bougainville (see the notice Baie Bougainville* in SA) and La PĂŠrouse, and was translated into English. It was also a bestseller bought by philosophers like Montesquieu* and voyageurs. The detailed descriptions, analysis, and illustrations of FrĂŠzierâs publications were particularly appreciated by botanical or natural history readers like PĂŠron. FrĂŠzier describes the native plant Fragaria chiloensis (beach strawberry) from the island of Chiloe in Chili which he introduced to France where it was hybridised and became the strawberry we now eat.

characteristic_fr: BordĂŠ de mangroves, le cap Duhamel doit son nom Ă  Henri-Louis Duhamel du Monceau (1700â1782). Celui-ci fut nommĂŠ inspecteur gĂŠnĂŠral des constructions navales par le ministre de la Marine de Louis XV, Maurepas.
characteristic: Fringed by mangroves, Cape Duhamel was named in honour of Henri-Louis Duhamel du Monceau (1700â1782). Duhamel was appointed Inspector-General of Naval Construction by Louis XVâs Minister of the Navy, Maurepas.
history_fr:
Dans lâexercice de ces fonctions, il ĂŠtudia les divers problĂ¨mes auxquels la marine franĂ§aise fut confrontĂŠe au XVIIIáľ siĂ¨cle : ceux relatifs Ă  la construction navale, Ă  la nĂŠcessitĂŠ dâemployer des bois rĂŠsistants Ă  lâeau et aux tarets, ainsi quâĂ  lâapprovisionnement en matĂŠriaux essentiels tels que le chanvre.
Duhamel possĂŠdait une connaissance approfondie des arbres, de la sylviculture, de la culture des plantes et de la botanique ; il sâintĂŠressa ĂŠgalement Ă  la formation du corps des ingĂŠnieurs et officiers de la construction navale, ainsi quâĂ  la santĂŠ des ĂŠquipages.
Câest dans cette perspective quâil fonda une ĂŠcole destinĂŠe Ă  former les futurs constructeurs de navires (voir en savoir plus).
history: In this position, he studied the various problems that faced the French Navy during the 18th century: those linked to shipbuilding, and the need to use timber resistant to water and worms, and basic materials like hemp. Duhamel had extensive knowledge and expertise about trees, sylviculture, plant cultivation and botany, the training of the corps of shipbuilding engineers and officers, and the health of crews. Accordingly, Duhamel created a school to train future shipbuilders (see Details).

characteristic_fr:
Le cap est bordĂŠ, en arriĂ¨re, par une dune littorale instable, puis par lâestuaire du Cape Bossut Creek, ourlĂŠ de mangroves. Dans son rĂŠcit, Freycinet note: "Au N. du cap Duhamel commence un banc de sable et de roches qui borde la cĂ´te; une partie en est Ă  sec, le reste est Ă  fleur dâeau voyez le plan particulier, carte n.26.  La mer brise sur quelques points de ce banc, mais nulle part avec plus de force quâaux environs du cap Bossut."
Charles Bossut (1730â1814), abbĂŠ et mathĂŠmaticien, occupa une place importante dans la culture scientifique des officiers de marine du XVIIIáľ siĂ¨cle. Son TraitĂŠ thĂŠorique et expĂŠrimental dâhydrodynamique (en deux volumes) constituait une rĂŠfĂŠrence majeure pour lâĂŠtude des mouvements des fluides, question centrale pour lâarchitecture navale, la manĹuvre et la navigation.
characteristic:
The cape is backed by an unstable foredune thenby mangrove-lined Cape Bossut Creek. In his account, Freycinet notes: âTo the north of Cape Duhamel begins a sandbank and rocks that border the coast; part of it is dry, the rest is at water level (see detailed map, map no. 26). The sea breaks on a few points of this bank, but nowhere with more force than in the vicinity of Cape Bossut.â
Charles Bossut (1730-1814) was an abbe and a mathematician who was well-known to Navy officers for his TraitĂŠ thĂŠorique et expĂŠrimental d'hydrodynamique (in 2 volumes) and his mathematical textbooks that they widely used in making calculations.
history_fr: Bossut ĂŠtait considĂŠrĂŠ comme un spĂŠcialiste de lâĂŠtude de la rĂŠsistance de lâeau au mouvement des navires et mena des expĂŠriences sur la rĂŠsistance des fluides aux cĂ´tĂŠs de dâAlembert et de Condorcet. Il enseigna Ă  lâĂcole de MĂŠziĂ¨res â prĂŠcurseur de lâĂcole polytechnique â et compta parmi ses ĂŠlĂ¨ves Borda et Coulomb. Il devint ensuite lâun des examinateurs de lâĂcole polytechnique (voir glossaire).
history: Bossut was seen as an expert in the study of monitoring the resistance of water to the movement of vessel and carried out experiments on fluid resistance with d'Alembert* and Condorcet*. He taught at the Ecole de MĂŠziĂ¨res (the precursor to the Ecole Polytechnique) and had Borda* and Coulomb* among his students. He then, became one of the examiners at the Ăcole Polytechnique (see the glossary). See also: Charles Bossut https://mathshistory.st-andrews.ac.uk/Biographies/Bossut/

Source: National Archives of France.

characteristic_fr:
Freycinet notait: "Les terres de la baie Desault sont basses et sablonneuses ; il en est de mĂŞme de celles de la baie Geoffroy, dont lâĂŠtendue est dâailleurs bien moins considĂŠrable. En gĂŠnĂŠral, toute la cĂ´te jusquâau cap Duhamel offre la mĂŞme constitution sablonneuse et la mĂŞme stĂŠrilitĂŠ; on doit en excepter cependant les caps et les pointes avancĂŠes qui sont formĂŠs de falaises de roches, mais sans aucune apparence de vĂŠgĂŠtation."
Observant le rivage depuis la Casuarina, Freycinet ne pouvait toutefois percevoir quâen arriĂ¨re de ce littoral en apparence dĂŠsolĂŠ sâĂŠtendent dâimportants systĂ¨mes de mangroves â milieux intertidaux dâune grande richesse et dâune forte valeur ĂŠcologique â largement dissimulĂŠs Ă  la vue depuis le large et difficilement perceptibles dans le cadre dâune reconnaissance hydrographique cĂ´tiĂ¨re.
La baie GĂŠoffroy doit son nom soit Ă  Lislet Geoffroy (voir la notice Cap Lislet en Tasmanie), soit, plus probablement, Ă  Ătienne Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire (1772â1844), professeur chargĂŠ des mammifĂ¨res et des oiseaux, que le MusĂŠum de Paris dĂŠpĂŞcha pour prendre en charge lâensemble des collections dâhistoire naturelle et des objets ethnographiques rapportĂŠs par le GĂŠographe en mars 1804.
characteristic:
Freycinet noted that âThe land around Desault Bay is low-lying and sandy; the same is true of Geoffroy Bay, which is much smaller in size. In general, the entire coastline up to Cape Duhamel has the same sandy composition and appears equally barren; only the capes and headlands, formed of rocky cliffs and showing no visible signs of vegetation, constitute an exception.â
Observing the shore from aboard the Casuarina, however, Freycinet could not perceive that behind this seemingly desolate littoral the coast is in fact backed by extensive mangrove systemsâan ecologically rich and highly significant intertidal environment, largely concealed from view at sea level and inaccessible to distant hydrographic observation.
Baie GĂŠoffroy is either for Lislet Geoffroy (see the entry Cap Lislet* in Tasmania), or, more likely, Etienne Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire (1772â 1844), professor of mammals and birds, who was sent by the Paris Museum to take charge of all the natural and ethnographic materials brought back by le GĂŠographe in March 1804.
history_fr:
Geoffroy donna un nom Ă  plusieurs espĂ¨ces nouvelles, telle que le rat dâeau australien, recueilli par PĂŠron sur lâĂŽle Bruny, quâil dĂŠsigna sous le nom dâHydromys chrysogaster. Il rĂŠdigea ĂŠgalement une Note sur les animaux vivants venus Ă  bord du GĂŠographe, consacrĂŠe aux spĂŠcimens transportĂŠs en France.
Il dĂŠveloppa par ailleurs un systĂ¨me de classification anatomique fondĂŠ sur le principe de lâunitĂŠ de plan, postulant une organisation structurelle commune Ă  lâensemble des ĂŞtres vivants.
history: Geoffroy named some of the new species such as the Australian water-rat, collected by PĂŠron on Bruny Island, as Hydromys chrysogaster. He wrote on the live animals transported to France âNote sur les animaux vivans venus Ă  bord du GĂŠographeâ. He developed a system of classification for anatomy based on the unity of plan shared by all human beings.

Source : Archives nationales de France.

characteristic_fr:
Freycinet rapporta: "Dans lâO. [du cap Bossut) se trouve le rĂŠcif du Casuarina, sur lequel la mer dĂŠferle avec fureur. La passe entre la terre et lui est ĂŠtroite et dangereuse, Ă  cause du foible brassiage et de la force des courans ; le Casuarina, qui la traversa en prolongeant la cĂ´te, se vit plusieurs fois couvert des lames formĂŠes par les brisans qui lâenvironnoient."
En consĂŠquence, le nom de RĂŠcif du Casuarina commĂŠmore le relevĂŠ cartographique de ce rĂŠcif dangereux par Freycinet alors quâil commandait le Casuarina (voir la notice EntrĂŠe du Casuarina en Tasmanie).
characteristic:
Freycinet reported: âTo the west [of Cape Bossut] lies the Casuarina Reef, over which the sea breaks with fury. The passage between the land and the reef is narrow and dangerous, owing to the weak current and the strength of the tides; the Casuarina, which attempted to pass through by hugging the coastline, was several times engulfed by the waves generated by the surrounding breakers.â
Consequently, the name RĂŠcif du Casuarina commemorates Freycinetâs charting of this hazardous reef while he was in command of le Casuarina (see the entry EntrĂŠe du Casuarina in Tasmania).

characteristic_fr:
Freycinet ĂŠcrit : ÂŤ Dans lâE. du cap Bossut est un enfoncement dont nous nâavons aperĂ§u que lâouverture ; nous lui donnĂ˘mes le nom de baie Lagrange. Âť
La baie Lagrange est une indentation du littoral situĂŠe dans la rĂŠgion du Kimberley, en Australie-Occidentale, au sud de Broome. Le paysage cĂ´tier y est essentiellement plat et bas, caractĂŠristique des rivages sableux et faiblement ĂŠlevĂŠs de cette portion de la cĂ´te nord-ouest australienne.
Le toponyme rend hommage au mathĂŠmaticien Giuseppe Lodovico Lagrangia, plus connu sous le nom de Joseph-Louis de Lagrange (1736â1813). NĂŠ Ă  Turin, alors capitale du royaume de Sardaigne, Lagrange fut nĂŠanmoins considĂŠrĂŠ comme citoyen franĂ§ais â statut qui correspondait ĂŠgalement Ă  son propre souhait, la branche paternelle de sa famille ĂŠtant dâorigine franĂ§aise. Lagrange fut lâune des grandes figures scientifiques de lâEurope des LumiĂ¨res et ses travaux en mĂŠcanique et en analyse mathĂŠmatique exercĂ¨rent une influence durable sur les sciences exactes.
characteristic:
Freycinet wrote: âTo the east of Cape Bossut there is an indentation of which we only saw the opening; we gave it the name Lagrange Bay.â
Lagrange Bay is a coastal indentation located in the Kimberley region of Western Australia, south of Broome. The surrounding landscape is largely flat and low-lying, typical of the sandy and gently elevated shorelines that characterise this part of the north-western Australian coast.
The name honours the mathematician Giuseppe Lodovico Lagrangia, better known as Joseph-Louis Lagrange (1736â1813). Born in Turin, then the capital of the Kingdom of Sardinia, Lagrange later became associated with France and was regarded as a French citizen â a status that also corresponded to his own wishes, as the paternal branch of his family was of French origin. One of the great scientific figures of the Enlightenment, Lagrange made fundamental contributions to mechanics and mathematical analysis.
history_fr:
Lagrange participa Ă  la fondation de lâĂcole polytechnique, provisoirement appelĂŠe Ăcole centrale des travaux publics, dont il fut le premier professeur dâanalyse ((calcul diffĂŠrentiel et intĂŠgral, thĂŠorie des fonctions, etc.)
Bonaparte apprĂŠciait les travaux de Lagrange, quâil conservait dans sa bibliothĂ¨que, et lui dĂŠcerna des titres honorifiques tels que celui de comte de lâEmpire et de grand officier de la LĂŠgion dâhonneur.
history:
Lagrange played a part in the founding of the Ăcole Polytechnique*, provisionally named Ecole Centrale des Travaux Publics, of which he was the first professor of analysis. 
Bonaparte appreciated Lagrangeâs works, which he kept in his library, and awarded him honorific titles such as Count of the Empire and Grand Officer of the Legion of Honour.

characteristic_fr:
SituĂŠ sur la cĂ´te du Kimberley, ce cap se distingue par des affleurements rocheux intertidaux saillants, qui en marquent lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ et traduisent les dynamiques morphologiques du littoral.
Ce promontoire porte le nom de Louis RenĂŠ Madeleine Le Vassor de La Touche-TrĂŠville, officier de marine franĂ§ais qui commanda lâescadre partie de Rochefort Ă  destination de $lâexpĂŠdition de Saint-Domingue$ExpĂŠdition_de_Saint-Domingue$. De retour en France, il exerĂ§a ensuite le commandement de la flotte mĂŠditerranĂŠenne Ă  Toulon, oĂš il termina sa carriĂ¨re.
characteristic:
Situated along the Kimberley coast, the cape terminates in a series of protruding intertidal rock outcrops, indicative of the active littoral dynamics shaping this shoreline.
It commemorates Louis RenĂŠ Madeleine Le Vassor de La Touche-TrĂŠville (Latouche-TrĂŠville), who led the squadron sailing from Rochefort for the $Saint-Domingue expedition$Saint-Domingue_expedition$, and who later died in France while in command of the Mediterranean fleet at Toulon.
history_fr:
Selon des contemporains tels que Jurien de La GraviĂ¨re, sa mort en aoĂťt 1804 priva NapolĂŠon Bonaparte dâun talent naval exceptionnel, susceptible de lui ĂŠpargner le dĂŠsastre de Trafalgar.
Gicquel des Touches (voir lâentrĂŠe Gicquel en Tasmanie) ĂŠcrit de mĂŞme dans Souvenirs dâun marin de la RĂŠpublique :
ÂŤ Jâavais dĂŠjĂ  vu, au cours de ma carriĂ¨re, un grand nombre dâofficiers ; mais je nâavais pas rencontrĂŠ de vĂŠritable chef donnant, comme celui que nous venions de perdre [TrĂŠville], lâimpression dâune volontĂŠ supĂŠrieure, capable de transformer les hommes et de dominer les ĂŠvĂŠnements. Âť
history:
According to contemporaries such as Jurien de La GraviĂ¨re, his death in August 1804 deprived NapolĂŠon Bonaparte of a rare naval talentâone who might have spared him the disaster of Trafalgar.
Gicquel des Touches (see the notice Gicquel in Tasmania) similarly wrote in Souvenirs dâun marin de la RĂŠpublique:
âI had already seen a great number of officers in the course of my career, but I had not encountered a true leader who, like the one we had just lost [TrĂŠville], conveyed the impression of a higher will, capable of transforming men and mastering events.â

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Gourdon correspond Ă  un petit promontoire cĂ´tier escarpĂŠ, qui sĂŠpare des plages sableuses au sud de Broome.
Cette appellation renvoie Ă  Antoine Louis Gourdon (1765-1833), qui commandait la division navale de Port-de-Paix dans le cadre de lâexpĂŠdition de Saint-Domingue ($Saint-Domingue expedition$Saint-Domingue_expedition$).
characteristic:
Cape Gourdon is a small cliffed headland separating sandy beaches south of Broome.
It is named after Antoine Louis Gourdon (1765â1833), who commanded the naval division at Port-de-Paix while serving during the $Saint-Domingue expedition$Saint-Domingue_expedition$.
history_fr:
De retour en France en avril 1809, Antoine Louis Gourdon prit part Ă  la bataille des Basques, livrĂŠe Ă  lâembouchure de la Charente, sur le littoral du golfe de Gascogne, Ă  bord du Foudroyant.
En 1811, il prit le commandement de lâescadre franĂ§aise stationnĂŠe dans lâEscaut, chargĂŠe de dĂŠfendre le fleuve contre les attaques britanniques.
history:
After returning to France in April 1809, Antoine Louis Gourdon took part in the Battle of the Basque Roads, fought at the mouth of the Charente River on the Bay of Biscay coast, aboard Le Foudroyant.
In 1811, he took command of the French squadron stationed in the Scheldt, tasked with defending the river against British attacks.

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Villaret correspond Ă  un promontoire cĂ´tier sâinscrivant au sein dâun paysage de dunes sableuses et de formations arbustives littorales sur la cĂ´te du Kimberley.

Cette appellation renvoie Ă  Louis Thomas Villaret de Joyeuse (1747-1812), connu Ă  partir de 1792 sous le nom de Villaret-Joyeuse, qui commandait la flotte partie de Brest pour rejoindre lâexpĂŠdition de Saint-Domingue ($Saint-Domingue expedition$Saint-Domingue_expedition$).
characteristic:
Cape Villaret is a coastal headland set within a landscape of sand dunes and coastal scrub along the Kimberley coastline.
The name refers to Louis Thomas Villaret de Joyeuse (1747â1812), known from 1792 as Villaret-Joyeuse, who commanded the fleet that departed from Brest to join the $Saint-Domingue expedition$Saint-Domingue_expedition$.
history_fr:
En 1802, NapolĂŠon Bonaparte le nomma capitaine gĂŠnĂŠral de la Martinique et de Sainte-Lucie, fonctions qui sâinscrivent dans le dispositif colonial franĂ§ais de la CaraĂŻbe et dans le soutien logistique et militaire Ă  lâexpĂŠdition de Saint-Domingue.
LâexpĂŠdition de Saint-Domingue, Ă  laquelle cette dĂŠsignation se rĂŠfĂ¨re, sâinscrit dans la tentative de reprise en main de la colonie par la France en 1802. MalgrĂŠ la prise rapide de plusieurs ports et plusieurs succĂ¨s militaires, les troupes franĂ§aises durent faire face Ă  une rĂŠsistance soutenue, menĂŠe par Toussaint Louverture et ses lieutenants, dans lâintĂŠrieur montagneux de lâĂŽle. Les combats, notamment autour de la CrĂŞte-Ă -Pierrot, se rĂŠvĂŠlĂ¨rent particuliĂ¨rement coĂťteux. Dans un contexte de guerre de harcĂ¨lement, de pĂŠnuries et dâĂŠpuisement des effectifs, lâexpĂŠdition fut durablement affaiblie. Lâarrestation de Toussaint Louverture en juin 1802 ne suffit pas Ă  rĂŠtablir durablement la domination franĂ§aise.
history:
In 1802, NapolĂŠon Bonaparte appointed him Captain-General of Martinique and Saint Lucia, positions that formed part of the French colonial system in the Caribbean and supported the logistical and military framework of the Saint-Domingue expedition.
The Saint-Domingue expedition, to which this designation refers, formed part of Franceâs attempt to reassert control over the colony in 1802. Despite the rapid capture of several ports and a number of military successes, French forces encountered sustained resistance led by Toussaint Louverture and his lieutenants in the islandâs mountainous interior. Engagements, particularly around CrĂŞte-Ă -Pierrot, proved especially costly. In a context of guerrilla warfare, supply shortages, and the exhaustion of troops, the expedition was progressively weakened. The arrest of Toussaint Louverture in June 1802 did not suffice to restore lasting French control.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂle Gantheaume ne correspond pas, Ă  proprement parler, Ă  une ĂŽle ; sa position sur lâatlas de Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet correspond en rĂŠalitĂŠ Ă  une baie situĂŠe au sud de Broome, bordĂŠe par une plateforme rĂŠcifale intertidale de beachrock formant un linĂŠaire cĂ´tier rectiligne.
Cette appellation renvoie Ă  HonorĂŠ Joseph Antoine Ganteaume (voir les notices cap Gantheaume, en Australie-MĂŠridionale, et baie Gantheaume, en Australie-Occidentale, au sud de Shark Bay), qui commandait la flotte partie de Toulon pour rejoindre lâexpĂŠdition de Saint-Domingue ($Saint-Domingue expedition$Saint-Domingue_expedition$).
characteristic:
Ăle Gantheaume does not, strictly speaking, correspond to an island; its position on Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinetâs atlas instead corresponds to a bay located south of Broome, bordered by an intertidal beachrock reef platform forming a rectilinear stretch of coastline.
The name refers to HonorĂŠ Joseph Antoine Ganteaume (see the entries Cape Gantheaume* in South Australia and Gantheaume Bay* in Western Australia, south of Shark Bay), who commanded the fleet that departed from Toulon to join the $Saint-Domingue expedition$Saint-Domingue_expedition$.
history_fr:
Dans le cadre de la prĂŠparation de cette expĂŠdition, NapolĂŠon Bonaparte adresse, le 23 octobre 1801, des instructions au contre-amiral Denis DecrĂ¨s, visant Ă  renforcer les troupes franĂ§aises et Ă  reprendre le contrĂ´le de lâĂŽle face Ă  $Toussaint Louverture$Toussaint_Louverture$ :
ÂŤ Il me paraĂŽt donc nĂŠcessaire [âŚ] de faire partir le contre-amiral Ganteaume avec ses cinq vaisseaux [âŚ] Il fera embarquer sur ces bĂ˘timents 3 000 hommes, qui lĂ¨veront lâancre au plus tard le 25 brumaire. Âť
history:
As part of the preparations for this expedition, NapolĂŠon Bonaparte issued instructions on 23 October 1801 to Vice-Admiral Denis DecrĂ¨s, calling for reinforcements to be sent to Saint-Domingue and for control of the island to be reasserted against $Toussaint Louverture$Toussaint_Louverture$:
âIt therefore appears necessary [âŚ] to dispatch Rear-Admiral Ganteaume with his five ships [âŚ] He will embark 3,000 men on these vessels, which are to sail no later than 25 Brumaire.â

characteristic_fr:
Gantheaume Point, nommĂŠ initialement cap Huygens dans lâatlas de Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet, correspond Ă  un promontoire de grĂ¨s rouge, constituĂŠ de formations de type grĂ¨s dâEmeriau riches en sĂŠdiments ferrugineux, sâavanĂ§ant dans lâocĂŠan Indien. Il comprend Ă  la fois une ĂŠtendue de plages sableuses blanches et un escarpement rocheux rouge dominant le littoral.
Ce premier toponyme rend hommage Ă  Christiaan Huygens (1629-1695), lâun des rares savants non franĂ§ais inclus dans cette nomenclature.
characteristic:
Gantheaume Point, initially named Cape Huygens in the atlas of Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet, is a red sandstone headland composed of iron-rich Emeriau sandstone formations projecting into the Indian Ocean. It encompasses both a stretch of white sandy beach and a red rock cliff face overlooking the coastline.
This original name commemorates Christiaan Huygens (1629â1695), one of the few non-French scholars included in this nomenclature.
history_fr:
Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet note : ÂŤ Les terres, aprĂ¨s le cap Villaret, prennent leur direction vers lâest, et forment un enfoncement de huit lieues dâouverture, oĂš nous ne pĂŠnĂŠtrĂ˘mes point : le cap Huygens en est lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ septentrionale. Âť 
Le tracĂŠ de ce segment du littoral a ainsi ĂŠtĂŠ ĂŠtabli Ă  distance, ce qui explique en partie les imprĂŠcisions observĂŠes dans la cartographie de lâatlas du voyage. Seul Freycinet mentionne ce cap dans son rĂŠcit nautique et gĂŠographique publiĂŠ en 1815 ; le nom nâapparaĂŽt pas dans les ĂŠcrits de FranĂ§ois PĂŠron. Il nâa par ailleurs pas ĂŠtĂŠ enregistrĂŠ dans le Composite Gazetteer of Australia ĂŠtabli par lâIntergovernmental Committee on Surveying and Mapping.
Si Freycinet a choisi de dĂŠdier ce lieu Ă  Christiaan Huygens, câest en raison de lâestime que les officiers de marine portaient Ă  ce savant, notamment pour lâinvention de lâhorloge Ă  pendule, qui amĂŠliora considĂŠrablement la mesure du temps et contribua aux progrĂ¨s de la dĂŠtermination de la longitude en mer.
history:
Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet notes: âAfter Cape Villaret, the land trends eastward, forming an indentation eight leagues wide, which we did not enter; Cape Huygens marks its northern extremity.â
This section of the coastline was therefore charted from a distance, which partly explains the inaccuracies observed in the atlas of the voyage. Cape Huygens is mentioned only by Freycinet in his 1815 nautical and geographical account; the name does not appear in the writings of FranĂ§ois PĂŠron. It has not been recorded in the Composite Gazetteer of Australia compiled by the Intergovernmental Committee on Surveying and Mapping.
Freycinetâs decision to name this feature after Christiaan Huygens reflects the high regard in which naval officers held him, particularly for his development of the pendulum clock, which significantly improved timekeeping and contributed to advances in determining longitude at sea.

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Latreille ne correspond pas Ă  un cap, mais Ă  une plage sableuse situĂŠe Ă  environ 12 kilomĂ¨tres au nord de Broome, Ă  proximitĂŠ de la communautĂŠ aborigĂ¨ne Goolarabooloo Millibinyarri Aboriginal Community, dans la rĂŠgion du Kimberley, en Australie-Occidentale.
Cette appellation renvoie Ă  Pierre-AndrĂŠ Latreille, spĂŠcialiste trĂ¨s reconnu de lâentomologie et des arthropodes â voir lâentrĂŠe ÂŤ Ăle Latreille, Victoria Âť.
characteristic:
Cape Latreille does not correspond to a cape but to a sandy beach located approximately 12 kilometres north of Broome, adjacent to the Goolarabooloo Millibinyarri Aboriginal Community, in the Kimberley region of Western Australia.
This name refers to Pierre-AndrĂŠ Latreille, a highly regarded specialist in entomology and arthropods â see the entry âLatreille Island, Victoria.â
history_fr:
Lâattribution de ce toponyme Ă  Latreille pourrait ĂŞtre liĂŠe Ă  la prĂŠsence, dans la rĂŠgion, de crabes particuliers quâil a dĂŠcrits et classĂŠs dans le genre Portunus â famille des Portunidae â caractĂŠrisĂŠ par une derniĂ¨re paire de pattes aplatie en palettes natatoires.
Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet note que la portion de littoral oĂš se trouve le cap Latreille est ÂŤ plus ĂŠlevĂŠe et dâun aspect plus agrĂŠable : lorsque nous en fĂŽmes lâexploration (en avril), les arbres y ĂŠtaient frais et passablement touffus ; de belles prairies tapissaient quelques points de la cĂ´te, spectacle dâautant plus remarquable que nous y ĂŠtions depuis longtemps peu accoutumĂŠs Âť.
history:
The attribution of this place name to Latreille may be linked to the presence of particular crabs in the area, which he described and classified within the genus Portunus (family Portunidae), characterised by their hindmost pair of legs flattened into swimming paddles.
Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet notes that the section of coastline where Cape Latreille is located is âhigher and of a more pleasing aspect: when we explored it (in April), the trees were fresh and fairly dense; fine meadows covered certain parts of the coast, a sight all the more remarkable as we had long been unaccustomed to such scenery.â

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Boileau correspond Ă  un linĂŠaire cĂ´tier de beachrock dâenviron 2 kilomĂ¨tres de longueur, formĂŠ autour de formations de mangrove lors dâun niveau marin supĂŠrieur de 2 Ă  3 mĂ¨tres par rapport au niveau actuel, probablement au cours du dernier haut niveau du PlĂŠistocĂ¨ne. Les dĂŠpĂ´ts littoraux lithifiĂŠs et les cavitĂŠs laissĂŠes par les souches de mangrove constituent lâensemble localement dĂŠsignĂŠ comme une ÂŤ forĂŞt pĂŠtrifiĂŠe Âť.
Dans ce contexte, lâexpression de ÂŤ forĂŞt pĂŠtrifiĂŠe Âť ne renvoie pas Ă  une fossilisation vĂŠgĂŠtale au sens gĂŠomorphologique et palĂŠontologique strict, mais dĂŠsigne un ensemble de structures liĂŠes Ă  dâanciennes mangroves, conservĂŠes dans des dĂŠpĂ´ts littoraux consolidĂŠs, dont lâorganisation ĂŠvoque un paysage forestier fossilisĂŠ.
Nicolas Boileau (1636â1711) est un poĂ¨te et critique franĂ§ais du XVIIe siĂ¨cle.
characteristic:
Cape Boileau corresponds to a coastal stretch of beachrock approximately 2 kilometres in length, formed around mangrove stands during a sea-level highstand of 2â3 metres above present levels, likely dating to the late Pleistocene. The lithified coastal deposits and the cavities left by mangrove stumps together form what is locally referred to as a âpetrified forest.â
In this context, the term âpetrified forestâ does not correspond to fossilised vegetation in the strict geomorphological or palaeontological sense, but rather refers to a set of mangrove-related structures preserved within consolidated coastal deposits, whose arrangement evokes that of a fossilised forest landscape.
Nicolas Boileau-DesprĂŠaux (1636 â1711) was a 17th century French poet and critic.
history_fr: Les satires sociales en vers de Nicolas Boileau furent perĂ§ues comme dĂŠcrivant, avec ĂŠlĂŠgance, rigueur rythmique et puissance dâexpression, les travers de la sociĂŠtĂŠ et de la nature humaine, tout en affirmant les normes et les principes du classicisme dans la littĂŠrature franĂ§aise. Ce double registre â Ă  la fois critique et fondĂŠ sur une exigence de rigueur formelle â contribua Ă  sa rĂŠception favorable au moment de la montĂŠe au pouvoir de NapolĂŠon Bonaparte, dans un contexte marquĂŠ par la valorisation de lâordre, de la discipline et des rĂŠfĂŠrences classiques.
history: The social verse satires of Nicolas Boileau were regarded as portraying, with elegance, rhythmic precision, and expressive force, the quirks of society and human nature, while upholding the standards and principles of classicism in French literature. This dual dimensionâboth critical and grounded in formal rigourâcontributed to his favourable reception at the time of the rise to power of NapolĂŠon Bonaparte, in a context marked by the valorisation of order, discipline, and classical references.
