characteristic_fr:
La pointe Coulomb correspond Ă  un ensemble de platiers rocheux intertidaux de grĂ¨s, sâĂŠtendant jusquâĂ  environ 1 kilomĂ¨tre au large depuis le rivage et comportant plusieurs vastes mares tidales, tandis quâun chenal sableux, gĂŠnĂŠralement Ă  sec, serpente le long du cĂ´tĂŠ septentrional de la pointe.
Charles-Augustin de Coulomb (1736-1806) appartient Ă  lâĂŠlite scientifique du XVIIIe siĂ¨cle et joue un rĂ´le majeur dans le dĂŠveloppement de la physique et des techniques.
characteristic:
Coulomb Point consists of low intertidal sandstone rock flats extending up to 1 km seaward from the shore and containing several large tidal pools, with a usually dry sandy creek meandering along the northern side of the point.
Charles Augustin Coulomb (1736-1806) belongs to the eighteenth-century scientific elite playing an influential part in physics and technology.
history_fr:
Freycinet note:  "PrĂ¨s de la mer, le sol est formĂŠ de dunes de sable blanc et de falaises de roches dâun  rouge trĂ¨s-vif; un rĂŠcif continu et Ă  fleur dâeau prolonge la plus  grande partie de la cĂ´te, sans sâavancer cependant beaucoup au  large. Câest au point oĂš commence le rĂŠcif dans le Sud, que commencent aussi les falaises rougeĂ˘tres dont nous venons de parler;  elles sâĂŠtendent, sans interruption, jusquâĂ  la pointe Coulomb, aprĂ¨s laquelle les dunes reparoissent. 
Un ravin aperĂ§u sur la cĂ´te, entre le cap Boileau et la pointe  Coulomb, nous a paru ĂŞtre le lit dâun ruisseau; la fraĂŽcheur plus grande de la vĂŠgĂŠtation sur ce point semble lĂŠgitimer cette conjecture.", p.239
Cette description correspond ĂŠtroitement Ă  la morphologie actuelle de la pointe Coulomb, principalement constituĂŠe de grĂ¨s dont la teinte rouge caractĂŠristique rĂŠsulte de la prĂŠsence dâoxydes de fer issus de lâaltĂŠration et de lâoxydation des minĂŠraux ferrifĂ¨res contenus dans la matrice rocheuse.
history:
Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet notes: âNear the sea, the ground consists of white sand dunes and cliffs of vividly coloured red rock; a continuous reef, awash at the surface, extends along most of the coastline, though without projecting far offshore. It is at the point where the reef begins to the south that the reddish cliffs we have just mentioned also commence; they extend uninterrupted as far as Coulomb Point, beyond which the dunes reappear.
A ravine observed along the coast, between Cape Boileau and Coulomb Point, appeared to us to be the bed of a stream; the greater freshness of the vegetation at this location seems to support this conjectureâ (p. 239).

This description corresponds closely to the present-day morphology of Coulomb Point, which is primarily composed of sandstone whose characteristic red colour results from iron oxides formed through the weathering and oxidation of iron-bearing minerals within the rock matrix.

characteristic_fr:
Le cap Bertholet correspond Ă  un promontoire cĂ´tier bas. Ă cet endroit, le littoral change dâorientation et se dirige vers lâest, formant un secteur cĂ´tier dâenviron 14 km de longueur, Ă  trĂ¨s faible ĂŠnergie hydrodynamique, qui se prolonge jusquâaux vastes estrans tidaux de Carnot Bay. Ce tronĂ§on se caractĂŠrise par un profil cĂ´tier faiblement inclinĂŠ et une faible action des vagues.
Entre le cap et la baie sâĂŠtend un systĂ¨me de cordons littoraux bas, bordĂŠ par des mangroves Ă  ses deux extrĂŠmitĂŠs et enfermant en son centre des vasiĂ¨res salĂŠes pouvant atteindre jusquâĂ  3 km de largeur.
Le cap Bertholet â orthographiĂŠ avec un seul ÂŤ t Âť dans lâatlas de Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet â doit son nom au chimiste Claude Louis Berthollet (voir Ăle Berthollet*, en Australie-Occidentale).
characteristic:
Cape Bertholet corresponds to a low coastal headland. At this point, the coastline shifts orientation and trends eastward, forming a 14 km-long stretch of very low-energy shore that extends into the extensive tidal flats of Carnot Bay. This sector is characterised by a gently sloping coastal profile and limited wave action.
Between the cape and the bay extends a low-lying barrier system, bordered by mangrove forests at both ends and enclosing central salt flats reaching up to 3 km in width.
Cape Bertholetâspelled with a single âtâ in the atlas of Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinetâis named after the chemist Claude Louis Berthollet (see Ăle Berthollet*, Western Australia).
history_fr:
La proximitĂŠ de la pointe Coulomb et du cap Bertholet dans la nomenclature nâest probablement pas fortuite. Elle pourrait reflĂŠter la succession institutionnelle par laquelle Claude Louis Berthollet succĂŠda Ă  Charles-Augustin de Coulomb Ă  la prĂŠsidence de la classe des sciences mathĂŠmatiques et physiques de lâInstitut de France en 1801.
Voir aussi Ile Berthollet (Australie-Occidentale)
history:
The proximity of Coulomb Point and Cape Bertholet within the nomenclature is unlikely to be coincidental. Rather, it may reflect the logic of institutional affiliation that underpinned the naming practices of the expedition, whereby Claude Louis Berthollet succeeded Charles-Augustin de Coulomb as president of the Class of Mathematical and Physical Sciences at the Institut National des Sciences et des Arts in 1801. The spatial association of their names along the same stretch of coast thus mirrors their succession within the scientific hierarchy of the Institut.
See also: Ăle Berthollet (Western Australia).

characteristic_fr:
Ce que Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet dĂŠsigne sous le nom dâĂle Carnot ne correspond pas Ă  une ĂŽle, mais Ă  un segment du littoral principal : la baie Carnot constitue un enfoncement cĂ´tier dâenviron 2 500 ha, largement comblĂŠ par des vasiĂ¨res tidales, bordĂŠ de mangroves et entourĂŠ dâĂŠtendues ĂŠtendues de marais salĂŠs.
Lazare Nicolas Marguerite Carnot (1753â1823) est un savant qui appliqua les mathĂŠmatiques Ă  lâĂŠtude de la physique  (voir lâentrĂŠe Cap Carnot*, Australie-MĂŠridionale).
characteristic:
What Freycinet named Ăle Carnot is not an island, but part of the main coast: Carnot Bay corresponds to a 2,500 ha embayment largely infilled with tidal flats, fringed by mangroves and bordered by extensive salt flats.
Lazare Nicolas Marguerite Carnot (1753 - 1823) was a savant who applied mathematics in his study of physics (see the entry Cap Carnot*, SA).
history_fr: Lâattribution de ce toponyme sâinscrit dans un systĂ¨me de dĂŠnomination ĂŠtroitement liĂŠ aux rĂŠseaux savants de lâInstitut National des Sciences et des Arts (actuel Institut de France), dont Lazare Nicolas Marguerite Carnot constitue lâune des figures majeures. Ă lâinstar de Charles-Augustin de Coulomb, Lazare Carnot appliqua les mathĂŠmatiques Ă  lâĂŠtude de la physique. Ses travaux en gĂŠomĂŠtrie furent particuliĂ¨rement utiles aux ingĂŠnieurs gĂŠographes â notamment ceux de lâexpĂŠdition Baudin â ainsi quâaux officiers de marine comme Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet, en ce quâils dĂŠpassaient le cadre de la gĂŠomĂŠtrie euclidienne â centrĂŠe sur lâĂŠtude des figures et des formes â pour proposer des approches plus gĂŠnĂŠrales et des dĂŠmonstrations susceptibles dâĂŞtre mobilisĂŠes dans les opĂŠrations de levĂŠ et de mesure sur le terrain.
history:
The attribution of this toponym forms part of a system of naming closely connected to the scholarly networks of the Institut National des Sciences et des Arts (now the Institut de France), of which Lazare Nicolas Marguerite Carnot was one of the leading figures.
Like Charles-Augustin de Coulomb, Carnot applied mathematics to the study of physics. His work in geometry proved particularly valuable to engineer-geographersâespecially those of the Baudin expeditionâas well as to naval officers such as Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet, insofar as it went beyond Euclidean geometryâcentred on the study of shapes and figuresâto develop more general approaches and demonstrations that could be applied in surveying and field measurement operations.

characteristic_fr:
Les ĂŽles Lacepede constituent un groupe de petits atolls bas, formĂŠs de sables grossiers et de calcaires, reposant sur une vaste plateforme rĂŠcifale frangeante. Les rĂŠcifs, qui forment quatre ĂŽles principales, ont ĂŠtĂŠ nommĂŠs en lâhonneur du naturaliste Bernard Germain de LacĂŠpĂ¨de (voir lâentrĂŠe baie LacĂŠpĂ¨de*, Australie-MĂŠridionale).
Ils prĂŠsentent un intĂŠrĂŞt ĂŠcologique majeur, abritant lâune des plus importantes colonies de fous bruns ainsi que la plus grande population reproductrice de frĂŠgates ariel de lâocĂŠan Indien, et constituant ĂŠgalement un site de ponte important pour les tortues vertes.
Aujourdâhui inhabitĂŠes, ces ĂŽles furent exploitĂŠes pour le guano Ă  la fin du XIXe siĂ¨cle et sont dĂŠsormais protĂŠgĂŠes et gĂŠrĂŠes en raison de leur valeur environnementale.
characteristic: The Lacepede Islands are a group of small, low-lying atolls composed of coarse sand and limestone, resting on a broad fringing reef platform. The reefs, forming four principal islands, were named after the naturalist Bernard Germain de LacĂŠpĂ¨de (see Baie LacĂŠpĂ¨de* in South Australia). They are of major ecological significance, supporting one of the largest colonies of brown boobies and the largest breeding population of lesser frigatebirds in the Indian Ocean, as well as serving as an important nesting site for green turtles. Uninhabited today, the islands were exploited for guano in the late nineteenth century and are now protected and managed for their environmental value.
history_fr:
Les rĂŠcifs se rĂŠvĂŠlĂ¨rent dangereux pour le Casuarina, commandĂŠ par Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet, qui, le 10 avril 1803, fut entraĂŽnĂŠ par les courants dans un fort courant de marĂŠe Ă  proximitĂŠ de ces hauts-fonds. 
Freycinet signala ĂŠgalement la prĂŠsence de ÂŤ troupes considĂŠrables dâoiseaux de mer Âť au large de ces ĂŽles. Il notes dans son rĂŠcit: "Lorsque nous passĂ˘mes (en avril 1803) prĂ¨s des ĂŽles LacepĂ¨de, nous aperĂ§Ăťmes, au lever du soleil, des bandes considĂŠrables dâoiseaux de mer qui partoient de terre et dirigeoient leur vol dans lâO. et l'O. N. 0.; le nombre en ĂŠtoit si grand, quâon eĂťt pu croire qu'il se faisoit une migration complĂ¨te des oiseaux du pays. Depuis trois heures aprĂ¨s midi jusquâau coucher du soleil, ils vinrent rejoindre la terre; ce qui nous fit penser quâil existoit dâautres battures au large des ĂŽles LacepĂ¨de , et que ces battures fournissoient une nourriture abondante aux oiseaux qui les frĂŠquentoient.", pp.239-240
history:
The reefs proved hazardous to the Casuarina, commanded by Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet, when, on 10 April 1803, it was carried by the currents into a tide race near these shoals. 
Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet also noted the presence of âconsiderable flocks of seabirdsâ off the coasts of these islands. He writes in his account: âWhen we passed (in April 1803) near the Lacepede Islands, at sunrise we observed large flocks of seabirds leaving the land and flying towards the west and west-northwest. Their numbers were so great that one might have thought a complete migration of the birds of the region was taking place. From about three in the afternoon until sunset, they returned to the land, which led us to suppose that there were additional shoals offshore from the Lacepede Islands, and that these shoals provided abundant food for the birds that frequented them.â (pp. 239â240)

characteristic_fr: Au nord-ouest du rĂŠcif des ĂŽles Lacepede, un banc fut dĂŠsignĂŠ en 1801 sous le nom de ÂŤ banc des Baleines Âť. Sâil est figurĂŠ sur les cartes de lâatlas officiel, il nây est pas nommĂŠ, alors quâil est explicitement mentionnĂŠ dans les rĂŠcits du voyage.
characteristic: To the north-west of the reef of the Lacepede Islands, a bank was designated in 1801 as the âWhale Bankâ. Although it is depicted on the charts of the official atlas, it is not named there, whereas it is explicitly mentioned in the accounts of the voyage.
history_fr:
Nicolas Baudin note dans son journal de mer, le 26 juillet 1801 : ÂŤ Nous avions vu, sur le soir et peu de temps avant le coucher du soleil, une grande quantitĂŠ de baleines qui nous parurent beaucoup plus grosses que celles que nous avions vu sur les terres de la Concorde. CâĂŠtait un avertissement que nous ĂŠtions sur une profondeur dâeau mĂŠdiocre car il ne nous a pas paru ordinaire dâen rencontrer par 50 et 60 brasses et suivant nos observations, elles se tiennent plus gĂŠnĂŠralement par vingt brasses et au dessous quâau dessus. Âť
Les rĂŠcits du voyage ne mentionnent plus une telle abondance lors du second passage de lâexpĂŠdition en 1803, et signalent mĂŞme leur ÂŤ absence Âť, soulignant la variabilitĂŠ des observations naturalistes en fonction des conditions locales et saisonniĂ¨res, ainsi que dâautres facteurs plus difficiles Ă  identifier.
history:
Nicolas Baudin notes in his sea journal on 26 July 1801: âIn the evening, shortly before sunset, we observed a large number of whales, which appeared to us much larger than those seen off Terre dâEndracht. This indicated that we were in relatively shallow waters, as it does not seem usual to encounter them in depths of 50 to 60 fathoms; according to our observations, they are more commonly found in depths of less than twenty fathoms rather than greater.â
The accounts of the voyage no longer report such abundance during the expeditionâs second passage in 1803, and even note their âabsenceâ, highlighting the variability of naturalist observations depending on local and seasonal conditions, as well as other, less easily identifiable factors.

Ăles Emeriau (en rĂŠalitĂŠ un promontoire) se situent au nord de Broome. Le nom dâEmeriau a ĂŠgalement ĂŠtĂŠ appliquĂŠ Ă  une formation de grĂ¨s relativement rĂŠcente (environ 75 millions dâannĂŠes), affleurant Ă  la pointe Emeriau, caractĂŠrisĂŠe par des sĂŠdiments riches en fer, de teinte allant du jaune au noir, et frĂŠquemment surmontĂŠe dâune cuirasse ferrugineuse.
Ce toponyme rend hommage Ă  Maurice Julien Ămeriau de Beauverger (1762â1845), officier de la Marine franĂ§aise et amiral.
characteristic:
Ăles Emeriau (in fact a headland) are located north of Broome. The name Emeriau has also been applied to a relatively recent sandstone formation (c. 75 million years old) that outcrops at Point Emeriau, characterised by iron-rich sediments ranging in colour from yellow to black and frequently capped by ironstone.
The feature is named after Maurice Julien Ămeriau de Beauverger (1762â1845), a French Navy officer and admiral.
history_fr:
Maurice Julien Ămeriau de Beauverger se distingua au service de la Marine franĂ§aise lors de la guerre dâIndĂŠpendance amĂŠricaine et des guerres rĂŠvolutionnaires. Il participa Ă  la bataille du Nil, lors de lâexpĂŠdition dâĂgypte de NapolĂŠon, oĂš sa conduite fut jugĂŠe particuliĂ¨rement courageuse.
Au moment oĂš FranĂ§ois PĂŠron et Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet ĂŠlaborent la nomenclature, Ămeriau est prĂŠfet maritime de Toulon, oĂš il supervise la construction de nombreux bĂ˘timents, dont quinze vaisseaux de ligne et dix frĂŠgates.
Faisant ĂŠcho aux conditions environnementales rencontrĂŠes le long de ces cĂ´tes, Freycinet â ĂŠprouvĂŠ par des rivages dĂŠpourvus de vĂŠgĂŠtation â note que ÂŤ lâaspect de toutes ces ĂŽles est trĂ¨s triste et annonce lâextrĂŞme stĂŠrilitĂŠ du sol Âť, p.239.
history:
Maurice Julien Ămeriau de Beauverger gained distinction while serving in the French Navy during the American War of Independence and the Revolutionary Wars. He took part in the Battle of the Nile during NapolĂŠonâs Egyptian expedition, where his conduct was regarded as particularly courageous.
At the time when FranĂ§ois PĂŠron and Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet were establishing the nomenclature, Ămeriau was serving as maritime prefect of Toulon, where he oversaw the construction of numerous naval vessels, including fifteen ships of the line and ten frigates.
Echoing the environmental conditions encountered along this coast, Freycinetâwearied by vegetation-free shorelinesâobserved that âthe appearance of all these islands is very sad and indicates the extreme sterility of the soil.â

characteristic_fr:
Cap Borda est une avancĂŠe littorale basse situĂŠe au nord de Broome, sur la cĂ´te occidentale de la pĂŠninsule de Dampier, qui constitue une zone de transition environnementale marquĂŠe entre le dĂŠsert de Great Sandy, au sud, et les rĂŠgions tropicales de mousson, au nord.
Ă lâinstar dâEmeriau, le nom de Borda a ĂŠgalement ĂŠtĂŠ appliquĂŠ Ă  une formation gĂŠologique, le grĂ¨s de Borda, lĂŠgĂ¨rement plus rĂŠcent que celui dâEmeriau (environ 65 millions dâannĂŠes). Cette formation, reconnue uniquement au cap Borda, est composĂŠe de sables et de graviers fluviatiles latĂŠritisĂŠs.
Jean-Charles de Borda (1733â1799), connu sous le nom de chevalier de Borda, fut un mathĂŠmaticien, physicien et officier de marine franĂ§ais.
characteristic:
Cape Borda is a headland located north of Broome, on the western coast of the Dampier Peninsula, which forms a significant environmental transition zone between the Great Sandy Desert to the south and the monsoonal tropics to the north.
Similarly to Emeriau, the name Borda has also been applied to a geological formation, the Borda Sandstone, which is slightly younger than the Emeriau formation (c. 65 million years old). This unit, recognised only at Cape Borda, is composed of lateritised fluvial sands and gravels.
Jean-Charles de Borda (1733â1799), known as the chevalier de Borda, was a French mathematician, physicist, and naval officer.
history_fr:
FranĂ§ois PĂŠron note :
ÂŤ Au nord des ĂŽles LacĂŠpĂ¨de paroissoit un grand cap blanchĂ˘tre, que nous dĂŠsignĂ˘mes sous le nom de cap Borda, du savant gĂŠomĂ¨tre qui, par le perfectionnement du cercle Ă  rĂŠflexion, sâest acquis des droits si rĂŠels Ă  la reconnaissance des navigateurs de tous les pays. Le cap Borda gĂŽt par 16Â° 36' de latitude australe et par 120Â° 08' de longitude Ă  lâest du mĂŠridien de Paris Âť (t. I, p. 135).
La teinte blanchĂ˘tre Ă  laquelle PĂŠron fait rĂŠfĂŠrence correspond vraisemblablement aux bancs de sable clair qui bordent le cap. Dans son propre rĂŠcit, Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet se contente de citer William Dampier, qui dĂŠcrit cette portion du littoral comme ÂŤ basse et unie Âť, avec ÂŤ des bancs de sable prĂ¨s de la mer, les pointes seulement ĂŠtant pierreuses Âť (p. 240).
A la mi-avril 1803, les conditions de navigation â courants, orages, vents contraires, bancs de sable â ne permirent pas au Geographe et au Casuarina dâaborder la cĂ´te. Les FranĂ§ais nâeurent ainsi guĂ¨re lâoccasion dâobserver la richesse biologique de la pĂŠninsule et de ses caps, leurs descriptions demeurant largement tributaires dâobservations effectuĂŠes depuis le large.
history:
FranĂ§ois PĂŠron notes:
âNorth of the Lacepede Islands there appeared a large whitish cape, which we designated under the name Cape Borda, after the learned geometer who, through the improvement of the reflecting circle, has earned such genuine recognition from navigators of all nations. Cape Borda lies at 16Â°36â˛ south latitude and 120Â°08â˛ east of the Paris meridianâ (vol. I, p. 135).
The whitish colour to which PĂŠron refers likely corresponds to the pale sandbanks surrounding the headland. In his own account, Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet merely quotes William Dampier, who described this section of the coast as âlow and evenâ, with âsandbanks near the shore, only the points being rockyâ (p. 240).
Navigation conditionsâadverse winds and squallsâprevented the corvettes from reaching the shore. As a result, the French had little opportunity to observe the rich biodiversity of the peninsula and its headlands, their descriptions remaining largely based on observations made from offshore.

characteristic_fr:
Ă lâinstar du cap Borda, le cap LĂŠvĂŞque est un cap situĂŠ sur la cĂ´te occidentale de la pĂŠninsule de Dampier, au sein du bassin de Canning, dont il marque lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ septentrionale. Cet espace triangulaire est couvert dâune vĂŠgĂŠtation semi-aride de type pindan scrub, dĂŠveloppĂŠe sur des sols rouges sableux et limoneux dâorigine ĂŠolienne, ĂŠgalement dĂŠsignĂŠs sous le nom de pindan. Le cap se caractĂŠrise par des falaises entaillĂŠes dans des alternances subhorizontales de grĂ¨s ferrugineux et de schistes crĂŠtacĂŠs.
Le plateau continental au large du cap LĂŠvĂŞque porte ĂŠgalement le nom de LĂŠvĂŞque, de mĂŞme que lâun de ses puits, oĂš des ressources en gaz ont ĂŠtĂŠ identifiĂŠes.
Pierre LĂŠvĂŞque (1746â1814) fut un physicien, mathĂŠmaticien et hydrographe franĂ§ais.
characteristic:
Like Cape Borda, Cape Leveque is a headland on the western coast of the Dampier Peninsula, within the Canning Basin, marking the peninsulaâs northernmost point. This triangular tract is covered by semi-arid pindan scrub developed on red sandy and silty soils of aeolian origin, also known as pindan. The cape is characterised by cliffs incised into subhorizontal alternations of ferruginous sandstone and Cretaceous shales.
The continental shelf off Cape Leveque also bears the name Leveque as does one of its wells where gas resources have been identified.
Pierre LĂŠvĂŞque (1746- 1814) was a French physicist, mathematician and hydrographer.
history_fr:
Ă dĂŠfaut de lâavoir visitĂŠ, Freycinet, comme PĂŠron, se rĂŠfĂ¨re au journal de William Dampier pour dĂŠcrire le cap LĂŠvĂŞque, notant que ce dernier avait abordĂŠ prĂ¨s dâune pointe â correspondant au cap LĂŠvĂŞque â dâoĂš le pays ÂŤ sâĂŠtend de lâOrient au Midi durant dix ou douze lieues ; ce quâil fait au-delĂ , câest ce que je ne puis dire. Ă environ trois lieues Ă  lâOrient de cette pointe, il y a une assez longue baie [King Sound] avec quantitĂŠ dâĂŽles et un fort bon endroit Ă  mouiller, ou Ă  haler les vaisseaux Ă  terre Âť.
Freycinet prĂŠcise toutefois : ÂŤ La route que nos corvettes ont suivie ne nous a pas permis dâavoir connoissance de la baie ni des ĂŽles dont il sâagit Âť (p. 240).
history:
Failing to visit it directly, Freycinet, like PĂŠron, relied on the journal of William Dampier to describe Cape Leveque, noting that Dampier had landed near a pointâcorresponding to Cape Levequeâfrom which the land âextends from the east to the south for ten or twelve leagues; what it does beyond that, I cannot say. About three leagues to the east of this point, there is a fairly long bay [King Sound], with many islands and a very good place to anchor, or to haul vessels ashore.â
Freycinet nevertheless added: âThe course followed by our corvettes did not allow us to gain knowledge of the bay, nor of the islands in questionâ (p. 240).

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Caffarelli fait partie de lâarchipel des Buccaneer, au sein du Kimberley Marine Park, et en occupe le secteur le plus occidental. Il sâagit dâune ĂŽle rocheuse situĂŠe dans un environnement cĂ´tier caractĂŠrisĂŠ par de trĂ¨s fortes amplitudes de marĂŠe.
Elle doit peut-ĂŞtre son nom soit Ă  Louis Marie Maximilien de Caffarelli du Falga (1756â1799), connu sous le nom de Maximilien Caffarelli, gĂŠnĂŠral franĂ§ais admirĂŠ pour sa bravoure et cĂŠlĂŠbrĂŠ en son temps comme un hĂŠros national (voir: cap Caffarelli, Australie-MĂŠridionale), soit Ă  son frĂ¨re cadet, Louis Marie Joseph Maximilien FranĂ§ois Auguste Caffarelli du Falga (1766â1849), lui aussi gĂŠnĂŠral rĂŠputĂŠ, comme Maximilien, chevalier de la LĂŠgion dâhonneur et dotĂŠ dâimportantes responsabilitĂŠs gouvernementales.
characteristic:
Caffarelli Island forms part of the Buccaneer Archipelago, within the Kimberley Marine Park, and occupies its westernmost sector. It is a rocky island situated in a coastal environment characterised by very large tidal ranges.
It has been named either after Louis Marie Maximilien de Caffarelli du Falga (1756â1799), known as Maximilien Caffarelli, a French general admired for his bravery and celebrated in his time as a national hero (see: Cap Caffarelli, South Australia), or after his younger brother, Louis Marie Joseph Maximilien FranĂ§ois Auguste Caffarelli du Falga (1766â1849), who, like Maximilien, was also a distinguished general, a Knight of the Legion of Honour, and held important ministerial responsibilities.
history_fr:
Dans le tome II du rĂŠcit du voyage, Freycinet revient sur une erreur de dĂŠnomination commise par PĂŠron dans le premier volume de la partie historique, oĂš lâĂŽle Caffarelli avait ĂŠtĂŠ dĂŠsignĂŠe Ă  tort sous le nom de baie Berthoud. La construction des cartes nâĂŠtant pas achevĂŠe au moment de la publication de ce premier rĂŠcit, cette erreur nâavait pas encore pu ĂŞtre ĂŠtablie ; elle est rectifiĂŠe par Freycinet dans ce second volume, oĂš il explique que ÂŤ le 15 [avril 1803], peu de temps aprĂ¨s que nous fĂťmes sous voiles, on aperĂ§ut la terre ; nous la reconnĂťmes pour une ĂŽle de deux lieues de longueur, et qui fut nommĂŠe ĂŽle Caffarelli Âť.
Le nom de Caffarelli apparaĂŽt Ă  deux reprises en Australie-MĂŠridionale et une fois en Australie-Occidentale, signe de lâimportance accordĂŠe Ă  cette figure hĂŠroĂŻque, perĂ§ue comme celle dâun homme mort pour la nation.
history:
In volume II of the voyage account, Freycinet corrected a naming error made by PĂŠron in the first volume of the historical narrative, where Caffarelli Island had mistakenly been designated as Berthoud Bay. As the charts had not yet been completed when that first account was published, the mistake had not yet been demonstrated; it was rectified by Freycinet in this second volume, where he explains that âon the 15th [April 1803], shortly after we were under sail, land was sighted; we recognised it as an island two leagues in length, which was named Caffarelli Island.â
The name Caffarelli appears twice in South Australia and once in Western Australia, indicating the importance attached to this heroic figure, who was regarded as a model of patriotic sacrifice.

characteristic_fr:
Le rĂŠcif BruĂŠ borde au loin la partie la plus occidentale de lâarchipel des Buccaneer, sur le plateau continental de Rowley.
Le choix du nom rĂŠcif BruĂŠ renvoie Ă  Adrien-Hubert BruĂŠ (1786â1832), membre de lâexpĂŠdition, quâil ne faut pas confondre avec un autre membre de lâĂŠquipage au nom proche, lâaspirant Joseph BruĂŠ. Ce dernier ĂŠtait dĂŠjĂ  en route vers la France Ă  bord du Naturaliste lorsque cette portion du littoral fut levĂŠe, en avril 1803, par Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet sur le Casuarina et par Nicolas Baudin sur le GĂŠographe.
characteristic:
Brue Reef fringes, in the distance, the westernmost part of the Buccaneer Archipelago, on the Rowley Shelf.
The name RĂŠcif BruĂŠ refers to Adrien-Hubert BruĂŠ (1786â1832), who was a member of the expedition, and should not be confused with another crew member bearing a similar name, the midshipman Joseph BruĂŠ. The latter was already on his way back to France aboard the Naturaliste when this section of the coast was surveyed in April 1803 by Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet on the Casuarina and by Nicolas Baudin on the GĂŠographe.
history_fr:
Adrien-Hubert BruĂŠ, trĂ¨s jeune marin associĂŠ Ă  lâexpĂŠdition, fut dĂŠcouvert Ă  bord du Naturaliste le 19 octobre 1800, alors que le navire avait dĂŠjĂ  pris la mer, puis inscrit sur les rĂ´les comme novice de premiĂ¨re classe. Il fut ensuite transfĂŠrĂŠ par Baudin sur le Casuarina. PĂŠron explique que, le 15 avril 1803, ÂŤ un banc de sable submergĂŠ se rattache Ă  ce rĂŠcif et sâĂŠtend Ă  une grande distance ; nous ne croyons pas toutefois quâil se joigne Ă  lâĂŽle Caffarelli, bien quâil coure dans cette direction. Nous avons donnĂŠ Ă  ce danger le nom de rĂŠcif BruĂŠ, dâaprĂ¨s le pilote en chef du Casuarina, jeune homme plein dâaptitude et de zĂ¨le pour la gĂŠographie Âť.
Une difficultĂŠ demeure toutefois. Dans son journal, Freycinet identifie le pilote du Casuarina sous le nom de Le Thiers â ĂŠgalement transcrit Lethiers, Dethieu ou Le Miers â et note que celui-ci fut remplacĂŠ quelques semaines plus tard par LeliĂ¨vre aprĂ¨s ĂŞtre tombĂŠ malade. Adrien-Hubert BruĂŠ ĂŠtait nĂŠanmoins reconnu pour ses capacitĂŠs, et il est possible que Freycinet lui ait confiĂŠ, selon les besoins, des fonctions de barre ou de pilotage Ă  bord de la goĂŠlette, et que BruĂŠ ait ĂŠtĂŠ le premier Ă  signaler le rĂŠcif.
history:
Adrien-Hubert BruĂŠ, a very young sailor associated with the expedition, was found aboard the Naturaliste on 19 October 1800, after it had already sailed, and was then entered on the rolls as a first-class novice. He was later transferred by Baudin to the Casuarina. PĂŠron explains that, on 15 April 1803, âA submerged sandbank is attached to this reef and extends for a great distance; we do not believe, however, that it joins Caffarelli Island, although it runs in that direction. We have named this danger BruĂŠ Reef, after the chief pilot of the Casuarina, a young man full of aptitude and zeal for geography.â
A difficulty nevertheless remains. In his journal, Freycinet identifies the pilot of the Casuarina as Le Thiers â also transcribed as Lethiers, Dethieu, or Le Miers â and notes that he was replaced a few weeks later by Lelievre after falling ill. Adrien-Hubert BruĂŠ was, however, recognised for his abilities, and it is possible that Freycinet entrusted him, when necessary, with duties at the helm or in pilotage aboard the schooner, and that BruĂŠ may have been the first to notice the reef.

characteristic:

The name was derived from the GĂŠographe, the lead vessel of the Baudin expedition.

characteristic_fr: Aujourdâhui, le cap que les FranĂ§ais nommĂ¨rent Mollien correspond Ă  lâextrĂŠmitĂŠ septentrionale de lâĂŽle AdĂ¨le. Mollien ĂŠtait le nom marital dâAdĂ¨le : elle ĂŠtait lâĂŠpouse de Nicolas FranĂ§ois Mollien, nommĂŠ ministre du TrĂŠsor public en 1806.
characteristic: Today, the cape named Mollien by the French corresponds to the northernmost headland of AdĂ¨le Island. Mollien was AdĂ¨leâs married name: she was the wife of Nicolas FranĂ§ois Mollien, who was appointed Minister of the Public Treasury in 1806.
history_fr: Freycinet note: "Dans notre prĂŠcĂŠdente campagne, nous avions cru remarquer que le cap Mallien appartenoit au continent. Une bande de brume et la grande distance oĂš nous ĂŠtions de terre furent causĂŠ de cette erreur. Nous reconnĂťmes, le 5 avril: que ce cap se rattache Ă  une ĂŽle de trois lieues de longueur, que nous avons nommĂŠe Ăle AdĂ¨le."
history: Freycinet notes: âIn our previous campaign [in 1801], we had believed to notice that the Cape Mollien belonged to the continent. A band of fog and the great distance we were from the land were the cause of this error. We recognized, on April 5, that this cape is attached to an island of three leagues in length, which we named Ăle AdĂ¨le.â

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle AdĂ¨le est une ĂŽle trĂ¨s basse, entourĂŠe de vastes estrans sableux qui sâĂŠtendent jusquâĂ  25 km de ses rivages Ă  marĂŠe basse. Elle constitue lâĂŽle la plus ĂŠloignĂŠe au large de lâarchipel des Buccaneer, lui-mĂŞme composĂŠ dâenviron 1 000 ĂŽles.
Aujourdâhui, lâĂŽle AdĂ¨le se trouve au sein du Kimberley Marine Park et offre un habitat de reproduction important Ă  de nombreux oiseaux marins et limicoles, parmi lesquels le courlis dâExtrĂŞme-Orient et le bĂŠcasseau cocorli, tous deux en danger critique dâextinction.
AdĂ¨le-Rosalie Collart-Dutilleul fut la seconde ĂŠpouse de Nicolas-FranĂ§ois Mollien (voir baie Mollien et cap Mollien, en Australie-MĂŠridionale) et dame dâhonneur de la reine AmĂŠlie (voir cap AmĂŠlie, en Australie-MĂŠridionale).
characteristic:
AdĂ¨le Island is a very low-lying island surrounded by extensive sand flats that extend up to 25 km from the shore at low tide. It is the outermost island of the Buccaneer Archipelago, itself comprising some 1,000 islands.
Today, AdĂ¨le Island lies within the Kimberley Marine Park and provides an important breeding habitat for numerous seabirds and shorebirds, including the critically endangered eastern curlew and curlew sandpiper.
AdĂ¨le-Rosalie Collart-Dutilleul was the second wife of Nicolas-FranĂ§ois Mollien (see Baie Mollien and Cap Mollien in South Australia) and lady-in-waiting to Queen AmĂŠlie (see Cap AmĂŠlie in South Australia).
history_fr: AdĂ¨le Mollien apprĂŠciait le travail de PĂŠron et intercĂŠda auprĂ¨s de son mari afin de lui faire accorder une pension de lâĂtat, qui demeura toutefois dâun montant trĂ¨s modeste. Ăvoquant lâattention portĂŠe par Mme Mollien Ă  la faiblesse des ressources de PĂŠron aprĂ¨s sa mort Ă  CĂŠrilly (Allier), le 14 dĂŠcembre 1810, Mme de Chastenay ĂŠcrivit dans ses mĂŠmoires que les sĹurs de PĂŠron ÂŤ ĂŠtaient dans une situation presque voisine de lâindigence ; on ne put faire reverser sur elles la plus faible portion de la pension modique dont PĂŠron avait Ă  peine joui, et tout lâeffort de Mme Mollien, qui avait apprĂŠciĂŠ ce gĂŠnie si promptement ĂŠteint, nâaboutit quâĂ  leur procurer un modeste dĂŠbit de tabac. Âť t2, p.145
history: AdĂ¨le Mollien held PĂŠronâs work in high regard and intervened with her husband to secure him a state pension, although the sum granted proved to be very modest. Recalling Mme Mollienâs concern over PĂŠronâs meagre means after his death at CĂŠrilly (Allier) on 14 December 1810, Mme de Chastenay wrote in her memoirs that PĂŠronâs sisters âwere in a situation little removed from indigence; it proved impossible to have even the smallest part of the modest pension, of which PĂŠron had scarcely had time to benefit, transferred to them, and all the efforts of Mme Mollien, who had so quickly recognised this genius extinguished too soon, succeeded only in obtaining for them a modest tobacco shop.ât2, p.145

characteristic_fr:
Lâarchipel Bonaparte se situe dans la rĂŠgion du Kimberley, en Australie-Occidentale, sur les territoires des peuples Balanggarra, Wunambal Gaambera, Ngarinyin et Miriuwung Gajerrong.
Il comprend plusieurs centaines dâĂŽles et dâĂŽlots rĂŠpartis au large du littoral compris entre la baie Collier, au sud-ouest, et le golfe dâAdmiralty, au nord-est, y compris Ă  lâintĂŠrieur mĂŞme de ce dernier. La plupart sont de petites ĂŽles, des ĂŽlots ou de simples affleurements rocheux, auxquels sâajoutent plusieurs hauts-fonds et bancs submergĂŠs.
Les habitats cĂ´tiers et marins y sont caractĂŠrisĂŠs par des rĂŠcifs, des mangroves, des herbiers marins, ainsi que par des vasiĂ¨res et estrans sableux intertidaux. On y a recensĂŠ 225 espĂ¨ces de coraux durs dans la zone intertidale. La rĂŠgion est soumise Ă  de fortes amplitudes de marĂŠe, de lâordre de 8 mĂ¨tres, ainsi quâĂ  une forte turbiditĂŠ. Elle est ĂŠgalement influencĂŠe par des courants de plus grande ĂŠchelle, notamment lâIndonesian Throughflow via la partie interne du plateau nord-ouest. Lâensemble est reconnu comme une zone importante pour les requins et les raies, abritant des espĂ¨ces menacĂŠes ainsi que des habitats reproductifs majeurs.
PĂŠron donna Ă  cet archipel le nom de Bonaparte ÂŤ en lâhonneur du premier magistrat de notre nation et auguste protecteur de notre expĂŠdition Âť, en rĂŠfĂŠrence Ă  NapolĂŠon Bonaparte, alors Premier Consul du gouvernement franĂ§ais. Ce toponyme reflĂ¨te ainsi le contexte politique dans lequel Bonaparte patronna le voyage de dĂŠcouverte aux Terres australes (voir aussi Golfe Bonaparte en Australie-MĂŠridionale).
characteristic:
The Bonaparte Archipelago is located in the Kimberley region of Western Australia, within the Countries of the Balanggarra, Wunambal Gaambera, Ngarinyin, and Miriuwung Gajerrong peoples.
The archipelago comprises several hundred islands and islets lying offshore along the stretch of coast between Collier Bay to the southwest and Admiralty Gulf to the northeast, including islands within Admiralty Gulf itself. Most are small islands, islets, or emergent rocks, and the area also includes several submerged banks and shoals.
Its coastal and marine habitats are characterised by reefs, mangroves, seagrass meadows, and intertidal mud and sand flats. A total of 225 species of hard corals have been recorded in the intertidal zone. The region is subject to large tidal fluctuations, with tidal ranges of around 8 metres, and to high turbidity. It is also influenced by broader-scale currents, including the Indonesian Throughflow via the inner North-West Shelf. The area is recognised as an Important Shark and Ray Area, supporting threatened species as well as key reproductive habitats.
PĂŠron named this archipelago âin honour of the first magistrate of our nation and august protector of our expeditionâ, NapolĂŠon Bonaparte, in reference to the period when he was First Consul of the French government. The name thus reflects the political context in which Bonaparte sponsored the voyage of discovery to the Southern Lands (see also Golfe Bonaparte* in South Australia).
history_fr:
Freycinet prĂŠvient : ÂŤ La quantitĂŠ de rĂŠcifs qui rĂ¨gnent dans lâarchipel Bonaparte, le nombre considĂŠrable dâĂŽles et dâĂŽlots qui sây rencontrent, rendent la navigation dans ces parages difficile et mĂŞme pĂŠrilleuse. Les rĂŠcifs ĂŠtant tous ĂŠcores, la sonde ne peut en indiquer lâapproche ; inconvĂŠnient qui seroit fort grave pendant la nuit, si la facilitĂŠ quâon a de jeter lâancre par-tout ne permettoit au navigateur de se mettre tous les soirs Ă  lâabri du danger. Sur quelques points les rĂŠcifs sâavancent beaucoup au large, tandis quâailleurs ils sont situĂŠs prĂ¨s de la cĂ´te. Notre carte en indique un grand nombre, et cependant il ne faut pas croire quâils y aient tous ĂŠtĂŠ marquĂŠs ; entre les ĂŽles sur-tout, nous avons lieu de penser quâil se rencontre beaucoup de bancs que nous nâavons pas examinĂŠs. Ainsi, jusquâĂ  ce quâon ait fait une exploration complĂ¨te de ces parages, il convient de sây tenir exactement sur ses gardes. Âť (Navigation et GĂŠographie, p. 248).
De son cĂ´tĂŠ, PĂŠron prĂŠcise : ÂŤ Nous avons compris sous la dĂŠnomination gĂŠnĂŠrale dâArchipel Bonaparte (carte n. 27) la longue suite dâĂŽles et dâĂŽlots qui sâĂŠtendent depuis les ĂŽles Champagny Ă  lâO. jusquâaux ĂŽles de lâInstitut, sur un espace dâenviron 60 lieues marines. DiffĂŠrents groupes composent ce vaste ensemble, et ont ĂŠtĂŠ dĂŠsignĂŠs sous les noms dâĂŽles Champagny, ĂŽles dâArcole, ĂŽles Maret, ĂŽles Montalivet et ĂŽles de lâInstitut Âť (PĂŠron, t. I, p. 140). Voir les notices suivantes.
history:
Freycinet warns: âThe number of reefs that extend throughout the Bonaparte Archipelago, together with the considerable number of islands and islets found there, makes navigation in these waters difficult and even dangerous. As all the reefs are awash, the sounding line cannot indicate their proximity; this would be a very serious inconvenience at night, were it not for the ease with which one can anchor almost everywhere, allowing the navigator each evening to place himself out of danger. In some places the reefs extend far offshore, whereas elsewhere they lie close to the coast. Our chart indicates a great many of them; however, one must not suppose that they have all been marked. Especially between the islands, we have reason to believe that there are many banks that we did not examine. Thus, until a complete survey of these waters has been carried out, it is advisable to proceed with the utmost caution.â (*Navigation et GĂŠographie*, p. 248).
For his part, PĂŠron states: âUnder the general designation of Bonaparte Archipelago (chart no. 27), we have included the long succession of islands and islets extending from the Champagny Islands in the west to the Institut Islands, over a distance of about 60 nautical leagues. This vast ensemble is made up of different groups, which have been designated by the names of Champagny Islands, Arcole Islands, Maret Islands, Montalivet Islands, and Institut Islandsâ (PĂŠron, vol. I, p. 140). See the next entries.

characteristic_fr:
Les ĂŽles Champagny se situent au large de la rĂŠgion du Kimberley, dans la partie mĂŠridionale de lâarchipel Bonaparte, et font partie du Camden Sound Marine Park, connu des propriĂŠtaires traditionnels sous le nom de Nimenba. Elles sont aujourdâhui reconnues pour la richesse de leur avifaune. Ces ĂŽles constituent ĂŠgalement des sites de ponte pour les tortues, notamment la tortue verte (Chelonia mydas) et la tortue imbriquĂŠe (Eretmochelys imbricata), et sont bordĂŠes, du cĂ´tĂŠ du large comme du cĂ´tĂŠ intĂŠrieur, par dâimportants rĂŠcifs intertidaux sâĂŠtendant sur des profondeurs variables.
Elles doivent leur nom Ă  Jean-Baptiste de NompĂ¨re de Champagny, duc de Cadore (1756â1834), qui fut ministre de lâIntĂŠrieur de 1804 Ă  1807, puis ministre des Relations extĂŠrieures de 1807 Ă  1811. Voir aussi Port Champagny en Australie-MĂŠridionale
characteristic:
The Champagny Islands are located off the coast of the Kimberley region, in the southern part of the Bonaparte Archipelago, and form part of the Camden Sound Marine Park, known to the Traditional Owners as Nimenba. Today, they are recognised for their rich bird biodiversity. The islands are also known turtle nesting sites (Green Turtles and Hawksbill Turtles, which are both listed as threatened or endangered) and are fringed by extensive intertidal reefs on both their seaward and landward sides, across a range of depths.
They are named after Jean-Baptiste de NompĂ¨re de Champagny, duc de Cadore (1756â1834), who served as Minister of the Interior from 1804 to 1807, and then as Minister of Foreign Affairs from 1807 to 1811 (see the entry Port Champagny in South Australia).
history_fr:
LâexpĂŠdition Baudin ne fut pas en mesure dâobserver la biodiversitĂŠ de ces ĂŽles, le GĂŠographe et le Casuarina sâĂŠtant seulement approchĂŠs des cĂ´tes de ces ĂŽles, sans y dĂŠbarquer.
Freycinet note simplement que les ĂŽles Champagny ÂŤ au nombre de cinq, ont toutes un aspect stĂŠrile et blanchĂ˘tre Âť.
history:
The Baudin expedition was not able to observe the biodiversity of these islands, as le GĂŠographe and le Casuarina only approached these islandsâ coasts, without landing. 
Freycinet simply notes that the Ăles Champagny âare five, have all a sterile and whitish look

characteristic_fr:
La plus mĂŠridionale des ĂŽles Champagny reĂ§ut un nom particulier : lâĂŽle DegĂŠrando.
Joseph Marie DegĂŠrando (1772â1842), ĂŠgalement connu sous le nom de de GĂŠrando, fut un linguiste, pĂŠdagogue et philanthrope franĂ§ais. Il compte aussi parmi les prĂŠcurseurs de lâanthropologie et parmi les premiers penseurs dâune mĂŠthode fondĂŠe sur lâobservation participante.
characteristic:
The southernmost of the Champagny Islands was given a specific name: Ăle DegĂŠrando.
Joseph Marie DegĂŠrando (1772â1842), also known as de GĂŠrando, was a French linguist, educator, and philanthropist. He is also regarded as a precursor of anthropology and as one of the earliest thinkers to formulate a method based on participant observation.
history_fr: Lâassociation des noms de DegĂŠrando et de Champagny dans la nomenclature â Ă  la fois dans lâarchipel Bonaparte, en Australie-Occidentale, et dans le golfe Bonaparte, en Australie-MĂŠridionale (voir Port Champagny et canal DegĂŠrando) â paraĂŽt reflĂŠter un lien administratif et politique prĂŠcis, plutĂ´t quâune simple juxtaposition fortuite de noms. Au moment oĂš PĂŠron et Freycinet ĂŠlaborent la nomenclature, Joseph Marie DegĂŠrando est secrĂŠtaire de Jean-Baptiste de NompĂ¨re de Champagny, et tous deux collaborent Ă  lâorganisation administrative des nouveaux territoires de lâEmpire franĂ§ais.
history: The association of the names DegĂŠrando and Champagny in the nomenclatureâboth in the Bonaparte Archipelago in Western Australia and in the Golfe Bonaparte in South Australia (see Port Champagny and Canal DegĂŠrando)âappears to reflect a specific administrative and political connection, rather than a merely fortuitous juxtaposition of names. At the time when PĂŠron and Freycinet were working on the nomenclature, Joseph Marie DegĂŠrando was serving as secretary to Jean-Baptiste de NompĂ¨re de Champagny, and the two men were collaborating in the administrative organisation of the new territories of the French Empire.

characteristic_fr:
Les ĂŽles dâArcole se situent au large de la rĂŠgion du Kimberley, au nord de Derby, dans lâarchipel Bonaparte, au nord-ouest de lâAustralie-Occidentale. Elles font partie dâune zone protĂŠgĂŠe du Kimberley Marine Park et se caractĂŠrisent par des rivages rocheux et des rĂŠcifs frangeants soumis Ă  de fortes amplitudes de marĂŠe.
Le groupe fut nommĂŠ en rĂŠfĂŠrence Ă  la bataille dâArcole, livrĂŠe entre les forces franĂ§aises et autrichiennes en 1796 (voir lâentrĂŠe pic dâArcole* en Tasmanie), lâun des ĂŠpisodes les plus cĂŠlĂ¨bres de la lĂŠgende napolĂŠonienne. Cet usage commĂŠmoratif tĂŠmoigne de la forte portĂŠe propagandiste attachĂŠe Ă  cet affrontement, bien que sa rĂŠalitĂŠ historique ait ĂŠtĂŠ plus complexe que ne le laissa ensuite croire le mythe hĂŠroĂŻque.
characteristic:
The DâArcole Islands lie off the coast of the Kimberley region, north of Derby, within the Bonaparte Archipelago in the north-west of Western Australia. They form a protected area within the Kimberley Marine Park and are characterised by rocky shorelines and fringing reefs exposed to strong tidal movements.
The group was named after the Battle of Arcole, fought between French and Austrian forces in 1796 (see the entry Pic dâArcole* in Tasmania), one of the most celebrated episodes of the Napoleonic legend. Its commemorative use reflects the powerful propaganda value attached to this battle, even though its historical reality was more complex than the heroic myth later suggested.
history_fr:
Baudin demanda Ă  ses artistes de reprĂŠsenter en couleurs les formes ÂŤ bizarres et pittoresques Âť de ces ĂŽles, qui furent ensuite intĂŠgrĂŠes Ă  lâatlas historique de lâexpĂŠdition. 
PĂŠron ĂŠcrit mĂŞme que plusieurs dâentre elles ressemblent Ă  ÂŤ dâimmenses tombeaux antiques Âť. Son rĂŠcit montre ĂŠgalement que lâexpĂŠdition observa et documenta les trĂ¨s fortes amplitudes de marĂŠe propres aux eaux du Kimberley, qui comptent parmi les plus importantes au monde. Il note ainsi : ÂŤ Pendant le temps que nous ĂŠtions restĂŠs au mouillage des ĂŽles dâArcole, la diffĂŠrence des sondes nous fit connoĂŽtre que la marĂŠe monte ici de 20 Ă  25 pieds ; observation qui semble confirmer celle de M. Ronsard sur lâĂŽle Depuch, et qui se trouve conforme elle-mĂŞme Ă  celle de Dampier. Ce navigateur cĂŠlĂ¨bre faillit ĂŞtre, comme on sait, la victime de ces marĂŠes extraordinaires, son navire sâĂŠtant trouvĂŠ Ă  sec dans le lieu mĂŞme oĂš, la veille, il avoit mouillĂŠ par 5 brasses. Cette circonstance ajoute beaucoup au danger de la navigation dans ces mers, et paroĂŽt ĂŞtre la cause principale des courans violens qui sây font sentir. Âťt1, p.137
history:
Baudin asked his artists to sketch in colour the âbizarre and picturesqueâ forms of these islands, which were later included in the historical atlas of the expedition. 
PĂŠron even wrote that several of them resembled âimmense ancient tombs.â His narrative also shows that the expedition observed and documented the very large tidal ranges characteristic of the Kimberley waters, which are among the greatest in the world. He notes: âDuring the time we remained at anchor among the DâArcole Islands, the difference in soundings showed us that the tide rises here by 20 to 25 feet; an observation that seems to confirm that of M. Ronsard on Depuch Island, and which itself agrees with that of Dampier. As is well known, that celebrated navigator nearly fell victim to these extraordinary tides, his vessel having been left dry in the very place where, on the previous day, he had anchored in 5 fathoms. This circumstance greatly increases the danger of navigation in these seas, and appears to be the principal cause of the violent currents felt there.â

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Freycinet constitue un ĂŽlot isolĂŠ pour lequel sont ĂŠtablis des horaires de marĂŠe et des prĂŠvisions mĂŠtĂŠorologiques spĂŠcifiques, et qui sert frĂŠquemment de repĂ¨re Ă  la navigation sur les cartes marines. Sa partie centrale forme un relief bas et plat, couvert dâune vĂŠgĂŠtation tropicale verdoyante, qui contraste nettement avec les rivages plus accidentĂŠs de lâĂŽlot.
Elle reĂ§ut le nom dâĂŽle Freycinet en lâhonneur des deux frĂ¨res, Henri et Louis de Freycinet.
characteristic:
De Freyciney Islet is a remote islet for which specific tide tables and weather forecasts are issued, and it frequently serves as a navigational marker on charts. Its central part forms a low, flat relief covered with green tropical vegetation, in marked contrast to the more rugged shores of the islet.
It was named Freycinet Island in honour of the two brothers, Henri and Louis de Freycinet.De Freyciney Islet
history_fr: PĂŠron rapporte que la plupart des Ăles "affectoient une conformation bizarre et pittoresque ; l'une d'elles se faisoit sur-tout remarquer par sa forme parfaitement semblable Ă  celle d'un bolle renversĂŠ. On lui donna le nom d'Ile Freycinet,  de ces deux estimables frĂ¨res auxquels notre expĂŠdition est redevable de tant de travaux utiles. Elle est facile Ă  distinguer de toutes les autres, non-seulement par sa forme singuliĂ¨re, mais encore par son ĂŠlĂŠvation beaucoup plus grande.", t1, p.136.
history: PĂŠron recorded: âMost of them [Champagny Islands] had a strange and picturesque conformation; one of them was especially remarkable for its shape, which was perfectly similar to that of an upturned bowl. It was given the name of Freycinet Island, for those two esteemed brothers to whom our expedition owns so much useful work. It is easy to distinguish from all the others, not only by its singular shape, but also by its much greater elevationâ (See the entries PresquâĂŽle Freycinet* and Havre Henri Freycinet*).

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Lucas prĂŠsente plusieurs des traits caractĂŠristiques du littoral du Kimberley : de hautes falaises rouges, des rivages bordĂŠs de mangroves et des environnements fortement soumis aux marĂŠes, qui rendent la navigation difficile. Elle constitue ĂŠgalement un habitat pour les crocodiles marins, ce qui impose une vigilance particuliĂ¨re.
Elle doit son nom Ă  Jean Jacques Ătienne Lucas (1764â1819), surtout connu pour le rĂ´le quâil joua lors de la bataille de Trafalgar, le 21 octobre 1805, en tant que commandant du vaisseau de ligne franĂ§ais Le Redoutable.
characteristic:
Lucas Island displays several of the characteristic features of the Kimberley coastline: high red cliffs, mangrove-fringed shores, and strongly tidal environments that make navigation difficult. It also provides habitat for saltwater crocodiles, which calls for particular caution.
The island is named after Jean Jacques Ătienne Lucas (1764â1819), who is chiefly remembered for his role in the Battle of Trafalgar on 21 October 1805, when he commanded the French ship of the line Le Redoutable.
history_fr: PĂŠron explique : ÂŤ Non loin de lâĂŽle Freycinet, il en est une autre qui se prĂŠsente sous la forme du comble dâun ĂŠdifice immense ; nous lâavons nommĂŠe lâĂŽle Lucas, en lâhonneur du capitaine de vaisseau qui, dans le combat du Redoutable contre le Victory, sâest fait naguĂ¨res tant dâhonneur Âť (t. I, p. 136).
history: PĂŠron writes: âNot far from Freycinet Island, there is another which presents itself in the form of the roof of an immense building; we named it Lucas Island, in honour of the captain who, in the battle of the Redoutable against the Victory, had so recently distinguished himselfâ (vol. I, p. 136).

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Duguesclin** est une ĂŽle isolĂŠe et inhabitĂŠe, situĂŠe le long dâun littoral accidentĂŠ, marquĂŠ par de fortes amplitudes de marĂŠe et par la prĂŠsence dâanciens sites dâart rupestre des peuples autochtones.
Elle doit son nom Ă  Bertrand Du Guesclin (v. 1320â1380), ĂŠgalement orthographiĂŠ Duguesclin, chevalier et chef de guerre associĂŠ Ă  ce que lâhistoriographie du XIXe siĂ¨cle a dĂŠsignĂŠ sous le nom de **guerre de Cent Ans**. Ce long cycle de conflits, entrecoupĂŠ de trĂŞves, opposa de 1337 Ă  1453 le royaume dâAngleterre des PlantagenĂŞts au royaume de France des Valois. Lâinscription du nom de Duguesclin dans la nomenclature du voyage rattache ainsi ce littoral australien Ă  une mĂŠmoire militaire franĂ§aise de longue durĂŠe, antĂŠrieure de plusieurs siĂ¨cles Ă  lâĂŠpoque napolĂŠonienne mais toujours mobilisable dans la construction symbolique des Terres Australes par les francais.
characteristic:
Duguesclin Island is a remote and uninhabited island, situated along a rugged stretch of coast marked by strong tidal movements and by the presence of ancient Indigenous rock-art sites.
It is named after Bertrand Du Guesclin (c. 1320â1380), also spelled Duguesclin, a knight and military leader associated with what nineteenth-century historiography came to designate as the **Hundred Yearsâ War**. This long cycle of conflicts, interrupted by truces, opposed the Plantagenet kingdom of England and the Valois kingdom of France from 1337 to 1453. The inclusion of Duguesclinâs name in the voyageâs nomenclature thus linked this Australian coastline to a long French military memory, predating the Napoleonic era by several centuries yet still available for the symbolic construction of the Southern Lands by the French.
history_fr: Bertrand du Guesclin est souvent associĂŠ Ă  la formule suivante : ÂŤ Le courage donne ce que refuse la beautĂŠ. Âť Cette maxime rĂŠsume lâimage transmise par la tradition dâun homme rĂŠputĂŠ peu favorisĂŠ par son apparence, mais dont la bravoure, lâendurance et les qualitĂŠs militaires firent lâun des grands hĂŠros franĂ§ais de la guerre de Cent Ans. La lĂŠgende rapporte ĂŠgalement quâaprĂ¨s avoir ĂŠtĂŠ fait prisonnier en Espagne par le Prince de Galles, Ă  la suite de la bataille de NĂĄjera le 3 avril 1367, et alors quâĂŠtait discutĂŠe sa ranĂ§on, il aurait dĂŠclarĂŠ : ÂŤ Monseigneur, le roi de Castille en paiera moitiĂŠ, et le roi de France le reste ; et si ce nâĂŠtait assez, il nây a fileuse en France qui sĂťt filer, qui ne gagnĂ˘t Ă  filer ma finance. Âť Cette parole souligne symboliquement lâattachement que lui portait le royaume de France tout entier, jusque dans ses couches les plus modestes. Charles V (1338-1380), roi de France, le nomma connĂŠtable de France en 1370, faisant de lui le chef des armĂŠes royales. NapolĂŠon admira en lui Ă  la fois la tĂŠnacitĂŠ guerriĂ¨re, la reconquĂŞte mĂŠthodique des territoires occupĂŠs par les Anglais et lâusage dâune stratĂŠgie dâusure voisine de la politique de la terre brĂťlĂŠe. Lâironie de lâhistoire veut toutefois que lâEmpereur nâait pas su prĂŠvoir que les Russes recourraient contre lui, lors de la campagne de 1812, Ă  des procĂŠdĂŠs comparables.
history: Bertrand du Guesclin is most commonly associated with the following saying: âCourage grants what beauty denies.â The phrase encapsulates the traditional image of a man reputed to be physically ungainly, yet whose bravery, endurance, and military abilities made him one of the great French heroes of the Hundred Yearsâ War. Legend also relates that, after being taken prisoner in Spain by the Prince of Wales following the Battle of NĂĄjera on 3 April 1367, and while the terms of his ransom were being discussed, he declared: âMy lord, the King of Castile will pay one half, and the King of France the rest; and if that should not suffice, there is not a spinner in France who can spin who would not earn my ransom by spinning.â The remark symbolically suggests the depth of support he enjoyed throughout the kingdom of France, even among its humblest people. King Charles V of France (1338-1380) appointed him Constable of France in 1370, thus making him commander of the royal armies. Napoleon admired in him both his martial tenacity, his methodical reconquest of territories held by the English, and his use of an attritional strategy akin to scorched-earth warfare. Yet history carries a certain irony, for the Emperor failed to foresee that the Russians would employ comparable methods against him during the campaign of 1812.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle dâAguesseau est une autre des nombreuses ĂŽles de lâarchipel Bonaparte, que PĂŠron rattacha au groupe des ĂŽles Champagny.
Henri-Cardin-Jean-Baptiste dâAguesseau (1752â1826) fut nommĂŠ par Bonaparte prĂŠsident de la cour dâappel, puis ministre plĂŠnipotentiaire de France au Danemark.
characteristic:
D'Aguesseau Island is another of the numerous of islands of the Bonaparte archipelago, that Peron associated with the  Champagny Islands.
Henri-Cardin-Jean-Baptiste d'Aguesseau (1752 â1826) was appointed by Bonaparte president of the court of appeal and later as French minister to Denmark.
history_fr: DâAguesseau ĂŠtait le petit-fils du chancelier Henri FranĂ§ois dâAguesseau, qui sâĂŠtait opposĂŠ au systĂ¨me de John Law, lâĂŠconomiste ĂŠcossais qui tenta dâintroduire des rĂŠformes du systĂ¨me bancaire en France. Il fut fait commandeur de la LĂŠgion dâhonneur en 1804.
history: D'Aguesseau was the grandson of the chancellor Henri FranĂ§ois d'Aguesseau who was opposed to the system of John Law, the Scottish economist, who tried to introduce changes to the banking system in France. He was made a commander of the LĂŠgion dâHonneur en 1804.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Commerson fait partie du vaste archipel Bonaparte, en grande partie inhabitĂŠ, marquĂŠ par dâanciens reliefs du Kimberley, des rivages rocheux et de hautes cĂ´tes rougeĂ˘tres. LâĂŽle se situe dans une zone de conservation prioritaire. Les eaux qui lâentourent constituent un habitat important pour plusieurs espĂ¨ces marines menacĂŠes, notamment les dauphins Ă  bosse australiens, les dugongs et les baleines Ă  bosse en migration.
Elle doit son nom Ă  Philibert Commerson (1727â1773), naturaliste franĂ§ais de renom.
characteristic:
Commerson Island forms part of the vast, largely uninhabited Bonaparte Archipelago, a landscape marked by ancient Kimberley landforms, rocky shores, and steep rust-red coasts. The island lies within a high-priority conservation zone. Its surrounding waters provide important habitat for threatened marine species, including snubfin dolphins, dugongs, and migrating humpback whales.
It is named in honour of Philibert Commerson (1727â1773), a renowned French naturalist.
history_fr:
Les travaux de Philibert Commerson sâinscrivent, directement ou indirectement, dans le prolongement de ceux de plusieurs autres naturalistes ĂŠminents dont les noms figurent ĂŠgalement dans la nomenclature de lâarchipel Bonaparte, parmi lesquels Buffon, Desfontaines, Tournefort et Lamarck.
Il est ĂŠgalement possible que cette ĂŽle, situĂŠe dans des eaux frĂŠquentĂŠes par les cĂŠtacĂŠs, ait reĂ§u le nom de Commerson en rĂŠfĂŠrence aux observations quâil consacra Ă  ces animaux au cours du voyage de Bougainville.
history:
Philibert Commersonâs work was connected, directly or indirectly, with that of several other eminent naturalists whose names also appear in the nomenclature of the Bonaparte Archipelago, including Buffon, Desfontaines, Tournefort, and Lamarck.
It is also possible that this particular island, located in waters frequented by cetaceans, was named after Commerson in reference to his observations on cetaceans made during Bougainvilleâs voyage.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Forbin est lâune des trĂ¨s nombreuses petites ĂŽles rocheuses de lâarchipel Bonaparte, ensemble insulaire fragmentĂŠ qui borde le littoral dĂŠcoupĂŠ du Kimberley.
Elle doit son nom Ă  Claude de Forbin (1656â1733), officier de la Marine royale, connu sous le nom de chevalier de Forbin, qui se distingua dans de nombreux combats navals.
characteristic:
Forbin Island is one of the many small rocky islands of the Bonaparte Archipelago, a fragmented insular ensemble fringing the deeply indented Kimberley coast.
It is named after Claude de Forbin (1656â1733), an officer of the Royal Navy, known as the chevalier de Forbin, who distinguished himself in numerous naval engagements.
history_fr: PĂŠron ĂŠcrit, avec un certain accent de lassitude : ÂŤ Le 13 aoĂťt, nous continuĂ˘mes Ă  prolonger lâarchipel du N.-O., dĂŠpassant successivement quinze ou vingt ĂŽles plus ou moins grandes, absolument semblables Ă  celles des jours prĂŠcĂŠdens, et que nous nommĂ˘mes ĂŽle Forbin, ĂŽle du Guillaume-Tell, ĂŽle Suffren, ĂŽle Berthier, ĂŽle Tournefort, ĂŽle Corvisart, ĂŽle Jussieu, etc., etc. On peut voir dans la carte gĂŠnĂŠrale de la Nouvelle-Hollande tout ce qui concerne la position absolue ou relative de ces ĂŽles nombreuses ; et M. L. Freycinet, dans sa relation nautique, en prĂŠsentera tous les dĂŠtails Âť (t. I, p. 138).
history: PĂŠron writes, with a faint note of weariness: âOn 13 August, we continued to follow the archipelago to the north-west, successively passing fifteen or twenty islands of greater or lesser size, entirely similar to those of the preceding days, and which we named Forbin Island, Guillaume-Tell Island, Suffren Island, Berthier Island, Tournefort Island, Corvisart Island, Jussieu Island, etc., etc. The general chart of New Holland shows everything concerning the absolute and relative position of these numerous islands; and M. L. Freycinet, in his nautical account, will provide all the detailsâ (vol. I, p. 138).

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Colbert fait partie du groupe des ĂŽles dâArcole, dans lâarchipel Bonaparte.
Elle doit son nom Ă  Auguste FranĂ§ois-Marie de Colbert-Chabanais (1777â1809), descendant de Colbert, qui servit comme officier lors des campagnes dâItalie et dâĂgypte.
characteristic:
Colbert Island is part of the d'Arcole group in the Bonaparte Archipelago.
It is named after Auguste FranĂ§ois-Marie de Colbert-Chabanais (1777â1809), a descendant of Colbert, served as an officer in the Italian and Egyptian campaigns.
history_fr:
Avec une certaine irritation Ă  lâĂŠgard de lâofficier Ronsard, Freycinet relĂ¨ve ÂŤ une observation curieuse, mais isolĂŠe Âť, recueillie le 11 aoĂťt 1801 prĂ¨s de lâĂŽle Colbert : ÂŤ cette observation nous a donnĂŠ 4 brasses pour la plus grande ascension de lâeau le lendemain de la nouvelle lune ; il est malheureux quâon nâait pas pu dĂŠterminer avec exactitude lâinstant de la pleine mer. M. Ronsard, Ă  qui ces rĂŠsultats sont dus, se borne Ă  dire que le renversement du courant eut lieu Ă  7 h 20 du matin, mais quâĂ  cette ĂŠpoque la mer ĂŠtoit dĂŠjĂ  montĂŠe dâun pied ou deux. Âť
Cette remarque souligne lâimportance que Freycinet accordait Ă  la mesure prĂŠcise des marĂŠes dans ces parages, oĂš lâampleur des marnages commandait aussi celle des courants de marĂŠe et, par consĂŠquent, les conditions de navigation. Lâinstant de la pleine mer, entendu comme le moment exact oĂš lâeau atteint sa hauteur maximale avant le jusant, constituait Ă  ses yeux une donnĂŠe essentielle.
Ronsard notait seulement dans son journal de bord : ÂŤ Le 23 thermidor [an IX, le 11 aoĂťt 1801] au matin, on fit une tentative pour mettre Ă  terre sur des isles situĂŠes par 14Â° 48' et 122Â° 12' ; mais le canot revint sans avoir pu trouver un dĂŠbarcadĂ¨re. Âť Il relevait ĂŠgalement dans son journal nautique : ÂŤ Ă 7 h la marĂŠe ĂŠtait basse, Ă  20 brasses dâeau ; le renversement a eu lieu Ă  7 h 20 ; le brassiage avait dĂŠjĂ  augmentĂŠ dâenviron un pied. Âť
history:
With a certain irritation toward the officer Ronsard, Freycinet draws attention to âa curious, though isolated, observationâ made on 11 August 1801 near Colbert Island: âthis observation gave us 4 fathoms for the greatest rise of the water on the day after the new moon; it is unfortunate that the exact moment of high tide could not be determined. M. Ronsard, to whom these results are due, merely states that the reversal of the current took place at 7.20 in the morning, but that by that time the sea had already risen by one or two feet.â
This remark underscores the importance Freycinet attached to the precise measurement of tides in these waters, where the magnitude of the tidal range determined that of the tidal currents and, consequently, the conditions of navigation. The moment of high tide, understood as the exact instant when the water reaches its maximum height before the ebb begins, was for him an essential datum.
Ronsard noted only in his journal: âOn 23 Thermidor [Year IX, 11 August 1801] in the morning, an attempt was made to land on islands situated at 14Â°48' and 122Â°12'; but the boat returned without having been able to find a landing place.â He also recorded in his nautical journal: âAt 7 oâclock the tide was low, in 20 fathoms of water; the reversal took place at 7.20; the depth had already increased by about one foot.â

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Buffon fait partie du groupe des ĂŽles dâArcole, dans lâarchipel Bonaparte. Ses cĂ´tes accidentĂŠes, dĂŠfendues par des rĂŠcifs et exposĂŠes Ă  une forte houle, rendaient tout dĂŠbarquement pĂŠrilleux pour lâexpĂŠdition.
Elle doit son nom Ă  grand naturaliste et savant Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon (1707-1788). Voir aussi Cap Buffon en Australie mĂŠridionale.
characteristic:
Buffon Island forms part of the DâArcole group in the Bonaparte Archipelago. Its rugged coasts, defended by reefs and exposed to heavy surf, made any landing attempt hazardous for the expedition.
It is named after the great naturalist and scholar Georges-Louis Leclerc de Buffon (1707â1788). See also Cap Buffon in South Australia.
history_fr:
PĂŠron note : ÂŤ Un officier partit aussitĂ´t pour aller les reconnoĂŽtre de plus prĂ¨s, et chercher un lieu propre au dĂŠbarquement ; mais vainement M. de Montbazin prolongea pendant plusieurs heures ces ĂŽles redoutables, il les trouva dĂŠfendues sur tous les points par de longues chaĂŽnes dâĂŠcueils contre lesquels la mer brisoit avec fureur, et qui ne laissoient entre eux aucune espĂ¨ce de passage ou de coupure. Ces ĂŽles sont au nombre de dix ou douze, et font partie du grand archipel du N. O. [lâArchipel Bonaparte] ; elles furent appelĂŠes ĂŽles dâArcole, et les plus remarquables dâentre elles reĂ§urent les noms dâĂŽle Colbert, ĂŽle Buffon, ĂŽle Desaix, ĂŽle KĂŠraudren, ĂŽle BernouilliâŚ Âť
Dans cet ensemble toponymique, le nom de Buffon prend place aux cĂ´tĂŠs de grands officiers de lâEmpire et de savants de premier rang : il apparaĂŽt ainsi comme lâune des figures appelĂŠes Ă  incarner, dans la gĂŠographie de lâexpĂŠdition, le prestige intellectuel et politique de la France.
history:
PĂŠron writes: âAn officer immediately set out to examine them more closely and to look for a suitable landing place; but in vain did M. de Montbazin follow these formidable islands for several hours, for he found them defended on every side by long chains of reefs against which the sea broke with fury, leaving no kind of passage or opening between them. These islands number ten or twelve, and form part of the great north-western archipelago [the Bonaparte Archipelago]; they were named the DâArcole Islands, and the most remarkable among them received the names of Colbert Island, Buffon Island, Desaix Island, KĂŠraudren Island, Bernouilli IslandâŚâ
Within this toponymic ensemble, the name of Buffon appears alongside those of senior imperial officers and leading savants, and thus emerges as one of the figures chosen to embody, within the geography of the expedition, the intellectual and political prestige of France.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Desaix est aujourdâhui comprise dans un petit groupe dâĂŽles portant le mĂŞme nom, situĂŠ immĂŠdiatement au nord de lâensemble appelĂŠ Coronation Islands, les deux appartenant Ă  lâarchipel Bonaparte.
Elle doit son nom au gĂŠnĂŠral Louis Charles Antoine Desaix (1768â1800), ĂŠlevĂŠ au rang de hĂŠros national dans la mĂŠmoire napolĂŠonienne.
characteristic:
Desaix Island is now included within a small island group of the same name, situated immediately to the north of the Coronation Islands, both belonging to the Bonaparte Archipelago.
It is named after General Louis Charles Antoine Desaix (1768â1800), who was elevated to the status of national hero in Napoleonic memory.
history_fr: En attribuant les noms dâĂŽle Colbert et dâĂŽle Desaix, PĂŠron associe deux figures militaires unies par une mĂŞme fidĂŠlitĂŠ Ă  Bonaparte et par une semblable ardeur au combat, quâelles portĂ¨rent lâune et lâautre jusquâĂ  la mort.
history: By assigning the names Colbert Island and Desaix Island, PĂŠron associated two military figures united by the same loyalty to Bonaparte and by a similar ardour in battle, both carried through to death in combat.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Desfontaines est aujourdâhui comprise dans un petit groupe dâĂŽles portant le mĂŞme nom, situĂŠ parmi les Coronation Islands, dans lâarchipel Bonaparte.
Elle doit son nom Ă  RenĂŠ Louiche Desfontaines (1750â1833), naturaliste influent qui, Ă  lâinstar de son contemporain Jussieu, joua un rĂ´le important dans le dĂŠveloppement de lâhistoire naturelle franĂ§aise.
characteristic:
Desfontaines Island is now included within a small island group of the same name, situated among the Coronation Islands in the Bonaparte Archipelago.
It is named after RenĂŠ Louiche Desfontaines (1750â1833), an influential naturalist who, like his contemporary Jussieu*, played an important role in the development of French natural history.
history_fr: En attribuant le nom de Desfontaines, PĂŠron rendait hommage Ă  un naturaliste associĂŠ Ă  la fois aux fondements institutionnels du MusĂŠum et aux rĂŠflexions contemporaines sur lâacclimatation des plantes exotiques en France. Ce choix fait ĂŠgalement ĂŠcho aux horizons ĂŠconomiques et littoraux plus larges du voyage. Dans son rapport sur les Avantages obtenus par les Anglais de la pĂŞche des phoques dans les mers australes, PĂŠron sâappuya sur le mĂŠmoire de Desfontaines consacrĂŠ au thĂŠ pour souligner lâimportance croissante du commerce avec la Chine, et suggĂŠrer que les produits tirĂŠs des cĂ´tes mĂŠridionales parcourues par lâexpĂŠdition â notamment les peaux et lâhuile de phoque australiennes â pourraient ĂŞtre avantageusement ĂŠchangĂŠs contre des marchandises chinoises, en particulier le thĂŠ.
history: By assigning the name Desfontaines, PĂŠron honoured a naturalist associated both with the institutional foundations of the MusĂŠum and with contemporary reflections on the acclimatisation of overseas plants in France. This choice also resonates with the broader economic and coastal horizons of the voyage. In his report, Advantages Gained by the English from the Seals of the Southern Seas, PĂŠron drew on Desfontainesâs memoir on tea to emphasise the growing importance of trade with China, and to suggest that products derived from the southern coasts visited by the expeditionâespecially Australian sealskins and seal oilâmight be exchanged to advantage for Chinese commodities, notably tea.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Bernouilli fait partie du groupe des ĂŽles dâArcole, dans lâarchipel Bonaparte.
Elle doit son nom Ă  la famille Bernoulli, illustre dynastie de mathĂŠmaticiens qui contribua aux fondements des mathĂŠmatiques appliquĂŠes et de la physique (voir cap Bernouilli).
characteristic:
Bernouilli Island forms part of the DâArcole group in the Bonaparte Archipelago.
It is named after the Bernoulli family, a gifted dynasty of mathematicians who contributed to the foundations of applied mathematics and physics (see Cape Bernouilli).
history_fr:
Freycinet prĂŠcise quâil sâappuie sur les tables dâobservation ĂŠtablies par les astronomes-mathĂŠmaticiens Bouguer et La Caille pour dĂŠterminer lâheure Ă  laquelle la pleine mer doit se produire aux moments de la nouvelle et de la pleine lune. Il mentionne cependant aussi la mĂŠthode proposĂŠe par Bernoulli, tout en la jugeant moins aisĂŠe Ă  manier : ÂŤ Bernouilli en a donnĂŠ une dont la forme est diffĂŠrente et lâemploi moins commode ; peut-ĂŞtre est-elle plus exacte dans la thĂŠorie ; mais, dans la pratique, cette extrĂŞme exactitude est encore bien loin de se trouver dans les observations. Âť
Cette remarque est rĂŠvĂŠlatrice de lâattention que Freycinet portait Ă  lâĂŠcart entre lâĂŠlĂŠgance des constructions mathĂŠmatiques et les contraintes concrĂ¨tes de la navigation, oĂš la prĂŠcision des calculs devait sans cesse composer avec lâincertitude des mesures prises en mer.
history:
Freycinet states that he relied on the observational tables established by the astronomer-mathematicians Bouguer and La Caille in order to determine the time at which high tide should occur at the moment of the new and full moon. He also mentions the method proposed by Bernoulli, though he considered it less convenient to use: âBernouilli has given one whose form is different and whose use is less convenient; perhaps it is more exact in theory, but in practice such extreme exactness is still far from being found in observations.â
This remark is revealing of the importance Freycinet attached to the gap between the elegance of mathematical constructions and the concrete constraints of navigation, where the precision of calculation constantly had to contend with the uncertainty of measurements taken at sea.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle KĂŠraudren fait partie du groupe des ĂŽles dâArcole, dans lâarchipel Bonaparte.
Elle doit son nom au docteur KĂŠraudren (voir Cap KĂŠraudren, en Australie occidentale)
characteristic:
KĂŠraudren Island forms part of the DâArcole group in the Bonaparte Archipelago.
It is named after Doctor KĂŠraudren (see Cape KĂŠraudren, Western Australia).
history_fr:
Le fait que KĂŠraudren ait ĂŠtĂŠ honorĂŠ dâune ĂŽle dans lâarchipel Bonaparte, au sein du groupe des ĂŽles dâArcole, aux cĂ´tĂŠs de grands savants et de hĂŠros militaires de lâĂŠpoque, montre la place ĂŠminente que PĂŠron lui accordait dans lâunivers scientifique de la France napolĂŠonienne. MĂŠdecin ouvert aux mĂŠthodes nouvelles fondĂŠes sur lâobservation mesurĂŠe et lâobjectivitĂŠ des chiffres, KĂŠraudren soigna la tuberculose de PĂŠron et suivit de prĂ¨s ses travaux. Comme le note Joseph-Philippe-FranĂ§ois Deleuze : ÂŤ MM. Corvisart et KĂŠraudren lui ayant conseillĂŠ d'aller passer un hiver Ă  Nice, il crut devoir cĂŠder Ă  leur conseil ; le voyage lui fit du bien et la douceur du climat parut le rĂŠtablir. Âť
AprĂ¨s la mort de PĂŠron, KĂŠraudren procura Ă  Freycinet le dynamomĂ¨tre de lâĂcole de mĂŠdecine pour rectifier les erreurs affectant les observations dynamomĂŠtriques du naturaliste, par lesquelles celui-ci entendait comparer la force musculaire des membres de lâĂŠquipage Ă  celle des populations autochtones rencontrĂŠes pendant lâexpĂŠdition, et pour comprendre ce qui, dans lâinstrument utilisĂŠ Ă  bord, nâavait pas fonctionnĂŠ correctement.
history:
The fact that KĂŠraudren was honoured with an island in the Bonaparte Archipelago, within the DâArcole group, alongside leading savants and military heroes of the period, shows the eminent place that PĂŠron accorded him within the scientific world of Napoleonic France. A physician receptive to new methods grounded in measured observation and the objectivity of numbers, KĂŠraudren treated PĂŠronâs tuberculosis and followed his work closely. As Joseph-Philippe-FranĂ§ois Deleuze notes: âMM. Corvisart and KĂŠraudren having advised him to spend a winter in Nice, he thought it right to follow their advice; the journey did him good, and the mildness of the climate seemed to restore him.â
After PĂŠronâs death, KĂŠraudren provided Freycinet with the dynamometer of the Ăcole de mĂŠdecine in order to correct the errors affecting the naturalistâs dynamometric observations, through which he had sought to compare the muscular strength of the crew members with that of the Indigenous peoples encountered during the expedition, and to understand what, in the instrument used on board, had failed to function properly.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Mably fait aujourdâhui partie du groupe des Coronation Islands.
Elle doit son nom Ă  Gabriel Bonnot de Mably (1709â1785), ĂŠcrivain et philosophe des LumiĂ¨res de grande renommĂŠe. Voir aussi Cap Mably.
characteristic:
Mably Island is now part of the Coronation Islands group. 
It is named after Gabriel Bonnot de Mably (1709â1785), an important writer and philosopher of the Enlightenment. See also Cap Mably
history_fr: Pour comprendre pourquoi PĂŠron choisit dâinclure lâabbĂŠ Mably dans la nomenclature officielle du voyage, il faut sans doute chercher moins lâinfluence directe dâun auteur explicitement revendiquĂŠ que la prĂŠsence dâun horizon intellectuel commun, profondĂŠment inscrit dans la culture politique de la gĂŠnĂŠration rĂŠvolutionnaire. Comme beaucoup de jeunes rĂŠpublicains formĂŠs dans les annĂŠes 1790, PĂŠron paraĂŽt avoir ĂŠtĂŠ sensible Ă  des thĂ¨mes que Mably avait contribuĂŠ Ă  diffuser : exaltation de la vertu civique, critique des inĂŠgalitĂŠs et des corruptions sociales, primat du bien public sur les intĂŠrĂŞts particuliers. Il sâagirait moins dâune filiation explicite que dâune communautĂŠ de langage moral et politique, qui ĂŠclaire la tension, chez PĂŠron, entre admiration pour certaines formes de simplicitĂŠ naturelle et volontĂŠ de refonder, par les sciences et lâobservation, un savoir utile Ă  la nation. Bonaparte lui-mĂŞme fut un temps sensible Ă  cet horizon intellectuel, avant de devenir Premier Consul puis empereur en rĂŠinstallant une forme de pouvoir autoritaire.
history: To understand why PĂŠron chose to include the abbĂŠ Mably in the official nomenclature of the voyage, one must probably look less for the direct influence of a specifically acknowledged author than for the presence of a shared intellectual horizon, deeply rooted in the political culture of the revolutionary generation. Like many young republicans formed in the 1790s, PĂŠron seems to have been receptive to themes that Mably had helped to disseminate: the exaltation of civic virtue, the critique of inequalities and social corruption, and the primacy of the common good over private interests. What is at stake here is less an explicit filiation than a shared moral and political language, which helps to explain the tension in PĂŠronâs thought between admiration for certain forms of natural simplicity and the ambition to refound, through science and observation, a form of knowledge useful to the nation. Bonaparte himself was also sensitive to this language for a time, before, as First Consul and then Emperor, he re-established an authoritarian form of power.
