characteristic_fr:
L'ĂŽle Fontanes fait dĂŠsormais partie des ĂŽles Coronation.
Elle doit son nom Ă  Louis Jean-Pierre, marquis de Fontanes (1757-1821), poĂ¨te, orateur et homme d'Ătat.
characteristic:
Fontanes Island is now part of  the Coronation Islands.
It is named after Louis Jean-Pierre, marquis de Fontanes (1757â1821), poet, orator, and statesman.
history_fr: Louis de Fontanes  fut lâune des figures intellectuelles majeures du Consulat et de lâEmpire. RalliĂŠ Ă  Bonaparte aprĂ¨s les annĂŠes rĂŠvolutionnaires, il devint prĂŠsident du Corps lĂŠgislatif puis grand-maĂŽtre de lâUniversitĂŠ impĂŠriale, incarnant une alliance de culture classique, de modĂŠration politique et de service de lâĂtat. Son nom a vraisemblablement ĂŠtĂŠ retenu par PĂŠron et Freycinet parce quâil reprĂŠsentait, dans la France napolĂŠonienne, une autoritĂŠ littĂŠraire et morale de premier plan, ĂŠtroitement liĂŠe Ă  lâencadrement des savoirs et Ă  la formation de la jeunesse.
history: Louis de Fontanes was one of the major intellectual figures of the Consulate and the Empire. Having rallied to Bonaparte after the Revolutionary years, he became President of the Corps lĂŠgislatif and later Grand Master of the Imperial University, embodying an alliance of classical culture, political moderation, and service to the state. His name was probably retained by PĂŠron and Freycinet because, in Napoleonic France, he represented a leading literary and moral authority, closely associated with the supervision of knowledge and the education of youth.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Lamarck se situe Ă  lâentrĂŠe du York Sound, dans lâarchipel Bonaparte. MarquĂŠe par un relief accidentĂŠ et des poches fragmentĂŠes de forĂŞt pluviale, elle fait partie dâune rĂŠgion cĂ´tiĂ¨re et marine particuliĂ¨rement riche en biodiversitĂŠ, exempte de mammifĂ¨res introduits et abritant une faune indigĂ¨ne, notamment des tortues Ă  dos plat venant y pondre. LâĂŽle possĂ¨de ĂŠgalement une importance culturelle pour le peuple Wunambal Gaambera, qui lâutilise traditionnellement pour la collecte de ressources marines, en particulier les tortues Ă  dos plat, et elle est aujourdâhui gĂŠrĂŠe dans le cadre de leur zone cĂ´tiĂ¨re dâUunguu.
Elle doit son nom Ă  Jean-Baptiste de Lamarck (1744â1829), professeur au MusĂŠum dâHistoire naturelle de Paris et cĂŠlĂ¨bre pour sa thĂŠorie de lâĂŠvolution.
characteristic:
Lamarck Island lies at the entrance to York Sound in the Bonaparte Archipelago. Marked by rugged relief and fragmented pockets of rainforest, it forms part of a biodiverse coastal and marine region free of introduced mammals and supporting native fauna, including nesting flatback turtles. The island also holds cultural significance for the Wunambal Gaambera people, for whom it is a traditional site for harvesting marine resources, notably flatback turtles, and is managed within their Uunguu Coast zone.
It is named after Jean-Baptiste de Lamarck (1744â1829), professor at the MusĂŠum dâHistoire naturelle in Paris and renowned for his theory of evolution.
history_fr:
Lamarck identifia une partie des ĂŠchantillons de mollusques et de balanes rapportĂŠs par PĂŠron. Plus largement, il ĂŠlabora une mĂŠthode analytique fondĂŠe sur la description et la comparaison des caractĂ¨res morphologiques, permettant de distinguer les espĂ¨ces par des diffĂŠrenciations de plus en plus prĂŠcises. Il en vint Ă  penser que les organismes pouvaient se transformer au cours du temps, Ă  lâopposĂŠ de Cuvier, qui dĂŠfendait la fixitĂŠ des espĂ¨ces.
PĂŠron paraĂŽt avoir ĂŠtĂŠ sensible aux idĂŠes transformistes de Lamarck et, dans plusieurs passages, adopta des positions qui le tenaient implicitement Ă  distance du fixisme de Cuvier, mĂŞme si cela demeura discret, celui-ci ĂŠtant lâun de ses principaux soutiens. Lâabsence du nom de Cuvier parmi les naturalistes mobilisĂŠs pour dĂŠsigner les ĂŽles du groupe dâArcole peut se lire Ă  la lumiĂ¨re de cette divergence intellectuelle. Dans le mĂŞme temps, le dĂŠsĂŠquilibre de la nomenclature â trois toponymes pour Cuvier, contre un seul pour Lamarck â reflĂ¨te aussi les rĂŠalitĂŠs institutionnelles, notamment le rĂ´le dĂŠcisif jouĂŠ par Cuvier dans lâappui apportĂŠ Ă  PĂŠron et au voyage.
Pour toutes ces raisons, le choix des noms doit ĂŞtre compris non comme une simple juxtaposition de rĂŠfĂŠrences savantes, mais dans le contexte des rĂŠseaux intellectuels, institutionnels et personnels qui reliaient les figures ainsi commĂŠmorĂŠes.
history:
Lamarck identified some of PĂŠronâs samples of molluscs and barnacles. More broadly, he developed an analytical method based on the description and comparison of morphological characters, enabling species to be distinguished through increasingly precise differentiations. He became convinced that organisms could change over time, in opposition to Cuvier, who defended the fixity of species.
PĂŠron appears to have been receptive to Lamarckâs transformist ideas and, in several passages, adopted positions that implicitly distanced him from Cuvierâs fixism, even if this remained discreet, since Cuvier was one of his principal patrons. The absence of Cuvierâs name among the naturalists used to designate the islands of the DâArcole group may be read in light of this intellectual divergence. At the same time, the imbalance in the nomenclatureâthree place names for Cuvier, but only one for Lamarckâalso reflects institutional realities, notably the decisive support Cuvier gave to PĂŠron and to the voyage.
For these reasons, the choice of names should be understood not as a simple list of learned references, but in the context of the intellectual, institutional, and personal networks linking the figures they commemorate.

characteristic: Tournefort Island, south of Lamarck Island at the entrance to York Sound, is named after Joseph Pitton de Tournefort (1656â1708), one of the foremost botanists of his time at the Jardin du Roi in Paris, now the Jardin des Plantes of the MusĂŠum national dâHistoire naturelle.
history_fr: LâĂŽle ne fut observĂŠe que dans le lointain. PĂŠron, non sans une lĂŠgĂ¨re frustration, rapporte que ÂŤ nous dĂŠcouvrĂŽmes encore plusieurs autres ĂŽles qui nâavoient point ĂŠtĂŠ aperĂ§ues [en aoĂťt 1801] : elles furent nommĂŠes ĂŽle Fontanes, ĂŽle Tournefort, ĂŽle Augereau, ĂŽle Championnet, &c. Le commandant, ne voulant pas sâengager au milieu de cet archipel, et ne jugeant pas Ă  propos dây envoyer le Casuarina, se contenta dâen prolonger une seconde fois [le 20 avril 1803] extĂŠrieurement le contour. Âť
history: The island was observed only from a distance. PĂŠron, not without a slight note of frustration, reports that âwe discovered several other islands which had not been sighted [in August 1801]: they were named Fontanes Island, Tournefort Island, Augereau Island, Championnet Island, etc. The commander, not wishing to venture into the midst of this archipelago, and not judging it advisable to send in the Casuarina, contented himself with following its outer contour a second time [on 20 April 1803].â

LâĂŽle Augereau fait aujourdâhui partie de lâaire protĂŠgĂŠe autochtone dâUunguu â *uunguu* signifiant ÂŤ living home Âť â, vaste espace protĂŠgĂŠ dâune grande importance ĂŠcologique et culturelle situĂŠ dans lâextrĂŞme nord du Kimberley, en Australie-Occidentale, et gĂŠrĂŠ par le peuple Wunambal Gaambera.
Elle doit son nom Ă  Charles Pierre FranĂ§ois Augereau (1757â1816), officier militaire ĂŠlevĂŠ Ă  la dignitĂŠ de marĂŠchal dâEmpire en 1804.
characteristic:
Augereau Island is today part of the Uunguu Indigenous Protected Area â *uunguu* meaning âliving homeâ â a vast protected area of major ecological and cultural significance in the far northern Kimberley region of Western Australia, managed by the Wunambal Gaambera people.
It is named after Charles Pierre FranĂ§ois Augereau (1757â1816), a military officer who was elevated to the rank of Marshal of the Empire in 1804.
history_fr: Le nom dâAugereau figure dans lâatlas de Freycinet parmi les ĂŽles dâArcole, parce quâil sâĂŠtait illustrĂŠ durant la campagne dâItalie, notamment aux batailles de Castiglione et dâArcole. En fĂŠvrier 1797, il fut chargĂŠ de rapporter Ă  Paris les drapeaux pris Ă  Mantoue par lâarmĂŠe dâItalie. Son prestige militaire se trouvait ainsi ĂŠtroitement liĂŠ aux victoires qui contribuĂ¨rent Ă  forger la premiĂ¨re lĂŠgende napolĂŠonienne, mĂŞme si son crĂŠdit et sa rĂŠputation dĂŠclinĂ¨rent vers la fin de lâEmpire.
history: Augereauâs name appears in Freycinetâs atlas among the DâArcole Islands because he had distinguished himself during the Italian campaign, notably at the battles of Castiglione and Arcole. In February 1797, he was entrusted with carrying back to Paris the flags captured at Mantua by the Army of Italy. His military prestige was therefore closely associated with the victories that helped shape the early Napoleonic legend, even if his standing and reputation declined toward the end of the Empire.

LâĂŽle Championnet est situĂŠe Ă  lâest des ĂŽles Maret.
Jean-Ătienne Championnet (1762-1800) fut un patriote convaincu et un rĂŠpublicain engagĂŠ, qui servit dans les armĂŠes rĂŠvolutionnaires en France, en Allemagne et en Italie.
characteristic:
Championnet Island is located east of the Maret Islands.
Jean-Ătienne Championnet (1762-1800) was a convinced patriot and a committed Republican who served in the Revolutionary armies in France, Germany, and Italy.
history_fr: En 1798, alors quâil commandait en chef lâarmĂŠe de Rome, ses troupes sâemparĂ¨rent en Italie de Capoue, puis de Naples, oĂš il proclama la RĂŠpublique parthĂŠnopĂŠenne, ce qui le mit en conflit avec le Directoire (voir le glossaire), hostile Ă  lâautonomie de cette rĂŠpublique italienne. AprĂ¨s sa mort du typhus, son nom passa Ă  la postĂŠritĂŠ comme celui dâun modĂ¨le de patriotisme pour la nation franĂ§aise et pour ses soldats.
history: In 1798, when commander-in-chief of the Army of Rome, his unit seized in Italy Capua, then Naples where he proclaimed the Parthenopean Republic, that put him in conflict with le Directoire (see the glossary) that was opposed to the autonomy of this Italian republic. After his death from typhus, his name passed to posterity as a patriotic model for the French nation and its soldiers.

characteristic_fr:
Ce groupe dâĂŽles se situe au large de la cĂ´te du Kimberley, dans la partie septentrionale de lâarchipel Bonaparte, et relĂ¨ve aujourdâhui du North Kimberley Marine Park. Il se distingue par une biodiversitĂŠ marine remarquable : plus de 210 espĂ¨ces de coraux durs y ont ĂŠtĂŠ recensĂŠes dans les seules ĂŽles Maret (Richards et al. 2015). Lâensemble insulaire comprend ĂŠgalement un systĂ¨me de mangroves particuliĂ¨rement riche en espĂ¨ces, ainsi que dâimportantes zones de concentration de dugongs sâĂŠtendant des ĂŽles Maret aux ĂŽles Montalivet.
Les ĂŽles Maret doivent leur nom Ă  Hugues-Bernard Maret, secrĂŠtaire dâĂtat (voir la baie Maret, en Australie-MĂŠridionale). LâĂŽle principale du groupe porte elle aussi le nom dâĂŽle Maret.
characteristic:
This group of islands lies off the Kimberley coast, in the northern sector of the Bonaparte Archipelago, and now forms part of the North Kimberley Marine Park. It is notable for its rich marine environments: more than 210 species of hard coral have been recorded on the Maret Islands alone (Richards et al. 2015). The archipelago also includes a highly diverse mangrove system, together with important dugong hotspots extending from the Maret Islands to the Montalivet Islands.
The Maret Islands take their name from Hugues-Bernard Maret, Secretary of State (see Maret Bay in South Australia). The principal island of the group likewise bears the name Maret Island.
history:
Maret was honoured by having a group of islands named after him because, in his capacity as Minister-Secretary of State, he stood at the very centre of the imperial regime. He served as the indispensable intermediary through whom passed discussions, correspondence, projects, as well as the work and petitions of the other ministers. Maret also appears to have exercised a significant influence over appointments. In the draft reports and memoranda he submitted to the Emperor, lists of candidates for particular offices were attached, and NapolĂŠon often left the choice of nomination to him.
Freycinet notes that the Maret Islands are marked by steep, high red cliffs, with the exception of Jussieu Island, which he describes as sandy and wooded.

LâĂŽle Jussieu est lâune des ĂŽles Maret.

characteristic:
Jussieu Island is one of the Maret Islands. 
It is named after the naturalist Antoine-Laurent de Jussieu (1748-1836).
history_fr:
PĂŠron ĂŠtait particuliĂ¨rement redevable Ă  Jussieu, dont le soutien joua un rĂ´le dĂŠterminant dans son embarquement pour lâexpĂŠdition des Terres australes, comme lâexplique Deleuze dans son Ăloge historique de FranĂ§ois PĂŠron :
ÂŤ Le Gouvernement franĂ§ois avoit ordonnĂŠ une expĂŠdition pour les Terres Australes [...] PĂŠron demande Ă  y ĂŞtre employĂŠ ; mais le nombre des savans ĂŠtant complet, il ne peut d'abord se faire accueillir. Il s'adresse Ă  M. de Jussieu, l'un des commissaires chargĂŠs du choix des naturalistes, qui lui conseille de faire un mĂŠmoire dans lequel il exposera ses motifs. Il va ensuite rendre compte Ă  ses collĂ¨gues de la conversation qu'il avoit eue avec PĂŠron ; et, de concert avec M. de LacepĂ¨de, il les dĂŠtermine Ă  ne pas repousser un jeune homme qui joignoit une ardeur extraordinaire Ă  une ĂŠtendue de connoissances bien rare Ă  son Ă˘ge. Quelques jours aprĂ¨s, PĂŠron lit Ă  l'Institut un mĂŠmoire sur l'utilitĂŠ de joindre aux autres savans de l'expĂŠdition un mĂŠdecin-naturaliste, spĂŠcialement chargĂŠ de faire des recherches sur l'anthropologie ou histoire de l'homme ; il rĂŠunit tous les suffrages [...] Âť
history:
PĂŠron was particularly indebted to Jussieu, whose support played a decisive role in securing his embarkation on the expedition to the Southern Lands, as Deleuze explains in his Historical Eulogy of FranĂ§ois PĂŠron:
âThe French Government had ordered an expedition to the Southern Lands [...] PĂŠron asked to be employed on it; but as the number of savants was already complete, he was at first unable to gain acceptance. He turned to M. de Jussieu, one of the commissioners responsible for selecting the naturalists, who advised him to write a memorandum setting out his reasons. Jussieu then reported to his colleagues on the conversation he had had with PĂŠron and, together with M. de LacepĂ¨de, persuaded them not to reject a young man who combined extraordinary ardour with a breadth of knowledge quite rare at his age. A few days later, PĂŠron read before the Institut a memoir on the usefulness of adding to the other savants of the expedition a physician-naturalist specifically charged with conducting research in anthropology, or the history of man; he won all the votes [...]â

ausEName: possibly one of todayâs southern Albert Islands?
characteristic_fr: SituĂŠe dans lâatlas de Freycinet entre lâĂŽle Jussieu et lâĂŽle Corvisart, lâĂŽle sâinscrivait dans les itinĂŠraires maritimes traditionnels parcourus par les Wunambal Gaambera au moyen de radeaux doubles et de canots. Les FranĂ§ais lui donnĂ¨rent le nom de Guillaume Tell, hĂŠros lĂŠgendaire de la Suisse, demeurĂŠ cĂŠlĂ¨bre pour avoir, selon la tradition, abattu dâun trait la pomme posĂŠe sur la tĂŞte de son fils.
characteristic: Located in Freycinetâs atlas between Ăle Jussieu and Ăle Corvisart, the island lay along the traditional maritime routes travelled by the Wunambal Gaambera people by double raft and canoe. The French named it after William Tell, the legendary Swiss hero celebrated for shooting an apple from his sonâs head.
history_fr: Le nom Guillaume-Tell dans la nomenclature ĂŠvoque la figure du hĂŠros patriotique qui, selon la tradition, combattit pour lâindĂŠpendance de sa Suisse natale contre lâautoritĂŠ des Habsbourg au XIVe siĂ¨cle. La lĂŠgende de Guillaume Tell, devenue lâun des ĂŠlĂŠments marquants de la mĂŠmoire nationale suisse, fut remise en valeur pendant la RĂŠvolution franĂ§aise et rĂŠinterprĂŠtĂŠe comme un emblĂ¨me de la rĂŠsistance Ă  la tyrannie, en particulier Ă  celle que lâon associait aux monarchies dynastiques de lâEurope. Cette appropriation politique et morale trouva aussi une expression musicale lorsque AndrĂŠ GrĂŠtry composa Ă  Paris, en 1791, un opĂŠra-comique sur ce sujet, Ĺuvre que NapolĂŠon admirait beaucoup. Durant la pĂŠriode rĂŠvolutionnaire, un vaisseau de quatre-vingts canons de la marine franĂ§aise porta ĂŠgalement le nom de Guillaume Tell. CapturĂŠ par les Britanniques en 1800 aprĂ¨s la bataille du Nil, au cours de la campagne dâĂgypte (voir glossaire), ce bĂ˘timent illustre Ă  son tour la circulation de ce nom hĂŠroĂŻque dans la culture militaire et politique de lâĂŠpoque.
history: The name Guillaume-Tell [William Tell] in the nomenclature invokes the figure of the patriotic hero who, in tradition, fought for the independence of his native Switzerland against Habsburg authority in the fourteenth century. The legend of William Tell, which became an important element of Swiss national memory, was revived during the French Revolution and reinterpreted as an emblem of resistance to tyranny, particularly that associated with the dynastic monarchies of Europe. This political and moral appropriation also found expression in music, when AndrĂŠ GrĂŠtry composed a comic opera on the subject in Paris in 1791, a work much admired by NapolĂŠon. During the revolutionary period, an eighty-gun ship of the French Navy likewise bore the name Guillaume Tell. Captured by the British in 1800 after the Battle of the Nile, during the Egyptian Campaign (see glossary), the vessel further illustrates the circulation of this heroic name within the military and political culture of the age.

characteristic_fr: SituĂŠe au sud des ĂŽles Maret, lâĂŽle Corvisart fait aujourdâhui partie de la cĂ´te dâUunguu, placĂŠe sous la garde des Wunambal Gaambera, et sâinscrit dans une rĂŠgion remarquable Ă  la fois par son importance culturelle et par sa diversitĂŠ environnementale. Les FranĂ§ais donnĂ¨rent Ă  lâĂŽle le nom de Jean-Nicolas Corvisart-Desmarets (1755-1821), premier mĂŠdecin de la famille impĂŠriale (voir la baie Corvisart).
characteristic: Situated to the south of the Maret Islands, Corvisart Island now forms part of the Uunguu coast, under the custodianship of the Wunambal Gaambera people, and belongs to a region distinguished by both its cultural importance and its environmental diversity. The French named the island after Jean-Nicolas Corvisart-Desmarets (1755â1821), physician-in-chief to the imperial family (see Corvisart Bay).
history_fr:
Corvisart ĂŠtait professeur Ă  lâĂcole de mĂŠdecine de Paris. Il sâĂŠtait spĂŠcialisĂŠ dans lâanatomie et dans la clinique interne, câest-Ă -dire dans le traitement des maladies fondĂŠ sur lâinterrogation directe du malade et sur son observation. Corvisart enseigna ĂŠgalement au prestigieux CollĂ¨ge de France, avant de dĂŠmissionner et dâĂŞtre remplacĂŠ Ă  ce poste par HallĂŠ en 1803, lorsque NapolĂŠon le nomma son premier mĂŠdecin et praticien.

Câest Corvisart qui, avec Pierre-FranĂ§ois Keraudren (1769-1858)*, soigna PĂŠron aprĂ¨s que celui-ci eut reĂ§u le diagnostic de tuberculose et lui conseilla de passer quelques mois Ă  Nice afin de recouvrer une partie de sa santĂŠ dans le climat plus doux de la MĂŠditerranĂŠe.
history:
Corvisart was a professor at the Parisâ School of Medicine. He specialised in anatomy and internal clinics (treatment of diseases through the direct questioning and observation of the patient). Corvisar also taught at the prestigious CollĂ¨ge de France, before his resignation and replacement in the position by HallĂŠ in 1803, when NapolĂŠon appointed Corvisart as his chief surgeon and medical practitioner.
It was Corvisart, together with Pierre FranĂ§ois Keraudren (1769-1858)* who treated PĂŠron after he was diagnosed with tuberculosis, and advised him to spend some months in Nice to recover some of his health in the warmer Mediterranean climate.

characteristic_fr: FranĂ§ois-Louis Rousselet, dâabord comte puis marquis de ChĂ˘teaurenault (ou ChĂ˘teaurenaut, ou ChĂ˘teauregnaud), fut un officier de marine franĂ§ais qui prit part aux principaux conflits europĂŠens des XVIIe et XVIIIe siĂ¨cles.
characteristic: FranĂ§ois-Louis Rousselet, count then marquis of ChĂ˘teaurenault (or ChĂ˘teaurenaut, or ChĂ˘teauregnaud), was a French naval officer who took part in the main European wars of the 17th and 18th centuries.
history_fr: Son nom fut retenu parce quâil acheva sa carriĂ¨re militaire couvert de gloire et dâhonneurs, avec les titres de vice-amiral du Levant et de marĂŠchal de France. AprĂ¨s ĂŞtre entrĂŠ dans la Marine royale, il participa Ă  la guerre de Hollande (1672-1678), Ă  la guerre de la Ligue dâAugsbourg et Ă  la guerre de Succession dâAutriche (voir le glossaire), oĂš il se distingua Ă  de nombreuses reprises, par exemple dans la baie de Bantry, au large de lâIrlande, en 1689, et dans la baie de Lagos, au Portugal, en 1693, sous le commandement de Tourville.
history: His name was selected because he ended his military career covered with glory and honor, with the rank of vice-admiral of the Levant and marshal of France. After joining the Royal Navy, he took part in the Franco-Dutch War (1672-1678), the Nine Years' War, and War of the Austrian Succession (see the glossary), where he distinguished himself on numerous occasions (for example in Bantry Bay off the coast of Ireland in 1689 and in Lagos Bay in Portugal in 1693, under Tourville*âs command).

ausEName: East Montalivet Island and West Montalivet Island
characteristic_fr:
Aujourdâhui, du groupe dâĂŽles que les FranĂ§ais nommĂ¨rent Montalivet, seules les deux plus grandes ont conservĂŠ ce nom. West Montalivet est une ĂŽle volcanique coiffĂŠe de latĂŠrite. Accessible en canot ou en radeau, elle servait de lieu dâoccupation durant la saison humide et de terrain de chasse au mangguru (tortue). Pendant la Seconde Guerre mondiale, lâĂŽle abrita les hommes de la 344 Radar Station. Son estran prĂŠsente une remarquable diversitĂŠ dâespĂ¨ces coralliennes, mĂŞme si Freycinet ne put observer le rĂŠcif quâindirectement, Ă  travers les lames qui venaient sây briser.
LâĂŽle doit son nom Ă  Jean-Pierre Bachasson, comte de Montalivet (1766-1823), haut administrateur qui dirigea dâimportants travaux dâinfrastructure de transport ayant contribuĂŠ Ă  la modernisation de la France, et dont le nom fut attribuĂŠ Ă  deux lieux dans cette nomenclature ; voir aussi cap Montalivet.
characteristic:
Today, of the group of islands named Montalivet by the French, only the two largest still retain the name Montalivet. West Montalivet is a volcanic island capped with laterite. Reached by canoe or raft, it served as a wet-season occupation site and as a hunting ground for mangguru (turtle). During the Second World War, the island was occupied by the men of the 344 Radar Station. Its intertidal zone displays a remarkable diversity of coral species, although Freycinet was only able to observe the reef indirectly, through the surf breaking over it.
The island takes its name from Jean-Pierre Bachasson, comte de Montalivet (1766â1823), a senior administrator who oversaw major transport infrastructure works that contributed to the modernisation of France and whose name was given to two places in this nomenclature; see also Cape Montalivet.
history_fr:
Freycinet note : ÂŤ Les ĂŽles Montalivet nous ont offert partout un coup dâĹil agrĂŠable ; quoique petites, elles sont ĂŠlevĂŠes et passablement fertiles : un fort brisant gĂŽt Ă  peu de distance de ces ĂŽles dans le N.-E. ... Diverses fumĂŠes aperĂ§ues sur le continent, prĂ¨s des ĂŽles dâArcole et des ĂŽles Montalivet, sont une preuve ĂŠvidente de la prĂŠsence de lâespĂ¨ce humaine sur ces bords... Âť
En 1806, au moment oĂš PĂŠron travaillait activement Ă  la nomenclature, Montalivet fut nommĂŠ Ă  la tĂŞte du Corps des Ponts et ChaussĂŠes, administration chargĂŠe des routes, des ponts et des grands travaux publics. Ă ce titre, il eut de frĂŠquents entretiens avec NapolĂŠon pour organiser des projets dâinfrastructure. En 1809, NapolĂŠon le nomma ministre de lâIntĂŠrieur, fonction dans laquelle il Ĺuvra au dĂŠveloppement de lâindustrie nationale, notamment en encourageant de nouveaux produits comme le sucre tirĂŠ de la betterave, conĂ§u comme un substitut au sucre de canne. Le ministĂ¨re de lâIntĂŠrieur ĂŠtait ĂŠgalement lâautoritĂŠ gouvernementale qui finanĂ§a la publication du rĂŠcit officiel du Voyage de dĂŠcouvertes aux Terres australes, ĂŠditĂŠ par PĂŠron et Freycinet.
history:
Freycinet noted: âThe Montalivet Islands everywhere offered us a pleasing prospect; though small, they are elevated and fairly fertile. A heavy breaker lies a short distance from these islands to the north-east ... Various smokes observed on the mainland, near the Arcole Islands and the Montalivet Islands, are clear evidence of the presence of humankind on these shores ...â
In 1806, when PĂŠron was actively engaged in shaping the nomenclature, Montalivet was appointed head of the Corps des Ponts et ChaussĂŠes, the body responsible for roads, bridges, and major public works. In that capacity, he met NapolĂŠon frequently to organise infrastructure projects. In 1809, NapolĂŠon appointed him Minister of the Interior, where he worked to promote national industry, including the development of new products such as sugar derived from beet, intended as a substitute for cane sugar. The Ministry of the Interior was also the government authority that financed the publication of the official narrative of the Voyage de dĂŠcouvertes aux Terres australes, edited by PĂŠron and Freycinet.

characteristic_fr:
Sur les cartes actuelles, lâĂŽle Suffren est rattachĂŠe au groupe des ĂŽles Albert. Dans lâatlas de Freycinet, elle apparaĂŽt toutefois plus au nord, parmi les ĂŽles Maret, au nord-est de lâĂŽle Corvisart. 
Elle fut nommĂŠe en lâhonneur de Pierre AndrĂŠ de Suffren de Saint-Tropez, bailli de Suffren (1729-1788), lâamiral franĂ§ais que les Britanniques surnommĂ¨rent ÂŤ lâamiral Satan Âť.
characteristic:
On present-day maps, Suffren Island is associated with the Albert Islands group. In Freycinetâs atlas, however, it appears farther north, among the Maret Islands, to the north-east of Corvisart Island. 
It was named in honour of Pierre AndrĂŠ de Suffren de Saint-Tropez, bailli de Suffren (1729â1788), the French admiral whom the British nicknamed âAdmiral Satan.â
history_fr: Pour nommer cette ĂŽle, que PĂŠron range parmi les terres ÂŤ absolument semblables Âť et ÂŤ inhospitaliĂ¨res Âť de lâarchipel Bonaparte, il retint le nom dâun ancien amiral franĂ§ais qui sâĂŠtait illustrĂŠ par des victoires dans lâocĂŠan Indien et que NapolĂŠon aurait aimĂŠ avoir Ă  son service. Son nom fut ĂŠgalement attribuĂŠ Ă  un cap et Ă  une anse en Australie-MĂŠridionale.
history: To designate this island, which he included among the âentirely similarâ and âinhospitableâ islands of the Bonaparte Archipelago, PĂŠron chose the name of a former French admiral who had led French forces to victory in the Indian Ocean and whom NapolĂŠon would no doubt have wished to count among his own commanders. He also gave his name to a cape and a cove in South Australia.

Son nom prolonge ainsi, dans lâarchipel Bonaparte, la mĂŠmoire de lâun des principaux artisans de la guerre napolĂŠonienne.
characteristic: Berthier Island, situated just south of the Maret Islands, was named after one of NapolĂŠonâs closest collaborators, Louis-Alexandre Berthier, who played a central role in the Italian and Egyptian campaigns as well as in the military history of the Empire. Its name thus extends into the Bonaparte Archipelago the commemoration of one of the principal architects of Napoleonic warfare.

characteristic_fr:
Aujourdâhui, les ĂŽles de lâInstitut, situĂŠes dans le district du North Kimberley en Australie-Occidentale, relĂ¨vent dâune Special Purpose Zone destinĂŠe Ă  la rĂŠcrĂŠation et Ă  la conservation, afin de protĂŠger les habitats et les espĂ¨ces intertidaux et subtidaux contre lâexploitation commerciale. Aujourdâhui, les ĂŽles de lâInstitut, situĂŠes dans le district du North Kimberley en Australie-Occidentale, relĂ¨vent dâune Special Purpose Zone destinĂŠe Ă  la rĂŠcrĂŠation et Ă  la conservation, afin de protĂŠger les habitats et les espĂ¨ces intertidaux et subtidaux contre lâexploitation commerciale. 
Le groupe fut nommĂŠ par les FranĂ§ais en lâhonneur de lâInstitut national des sciences et des arts, couramment dĂŠsignĂŠ sous le nom dâInstitut, aujourdâhui lâInstitut de France, conĂ§u comme une assemblĂŠe dâĂŠlite rĂŠunissant les principaux ĂŠcrivains, savants et artistes de la nation.
characteristic:
Today, the Institut Islands are a named island cluster within the far northern Bonaparte/Admiralty Gulf coastal mosaic, centered off Cape Voltaire. They fall within a Special Purpose Zone established for recreation and conservation, intended to protect intertidal and subtidal habitats and species from commercial exploitation. 
The group was named by the French in honour of the National Institute of Sciences and Arts, commonly referred to simply as the Institut, now the Institut de France, conceived as an elite body bringing together the foremost writers, scholars, and artists of the nation.
history_fr: Rassemblant les noms de grandes figures culturelles et savantes de la France, PĂŠron explique que ce groupe dâĂŽles situĂŠ dans la partie septentrionale de lâarchipel Bonaparte â une vingtaine selon lui, bien que Freycinet prĂŠcise que leur nombre demeurait indĂŠterminĂŠ â fut nommĂŠ ÂŤ en l'honneur de cette sociĂŠtĂŠ cĂŠlĂ¨bre dont s'enorgueillit notre patrieÂť.
history: Bringing together the names of major French cultural figures and men of learning, PĂŠron explains that this group of islands in the northern part of the Bonaparte Archipelagoâabout twenty islands according to him, although Freycinet notes that their number remained undeterminedâwas named âin honour of the famous society [the Institut] of which our homeland is so proud.â

characteristic_fr: LâĂŽle Lavoisier est bordĂŠe par un rĂŠcif frangeant interinsulaire se dĂŠveloppant dans la partie infĂŠrieure de lâestran, au sein dâun environnement cĂ´tier caractĂŠrisĂŠ par de forts courants de marĂŠe et par des remous violents. Les FranĂ§ais donnĂ¨rent Ă  lâĂŽle le nom de Lavoisier, en lâhonneur dâAntoine-Laurent Lavoisier (1743-1794), chimiste pionnier qui joua un rĂ´le dĂŠcisif dans la refonte de la nomenclature chimique
characteristic: Lavoisier Island is bordered by a fringing inter-island reef developing across the low intertidal zone, in a coastal setting marked by strong tidal currents and turbulent overfalls. The French gave the island the name Lavoisier, in honour of Antoine-Laurent Lavoisier (1743â1794), the pioneering chemist who played a decisive role in the reform of chemical nomenclature.
history_fr: Le nom de Lavoisier appartient au groupe des grandes figures intellectuelles franĂ§aises des lettres, des sciences et des arts rĂŠunies dans la nomenclature des ĂŽles de lâInstitut. Le chimiste y figure comme la plus ĂŠminente victime scientifique de la violence aveugle de la Terreur et, sâil nâavait pĂŠri dans de telles circonstances, il aurait sans doute pris place parmi les membres de lâInstitut. Comme lâaurait dit Lagrange : ÂŤ Il ne leur a fallu quâun moment pour faire tomber cette tĂŞte, et cent annĂŠes, peut-ĂŞtre, ne suffiront pas pour en reproduire une semblable. Âť Voir les notices principales correspondantes en Victoria, en Australie-MĂŠridionale et en Tasmanie.
history: The name Lavoisier belongs to the group of major French intellectual figures in literature, science, and the arts brought together in the nomenclature of the Institut Islands. The chemist appears there as the most eminent scientific victim of the blind violence of the Terror and, had he not perished in such circumstances, would no doubt have taken his place among the members of the Institut. As Lagrange is said to have remarked: âIt took them only an instant to cut off this head, and a hundred years, perhaps, will not suffice to produce another like it.â See the principal entries for these names in Victoria, South Australia, and Tasmania.

characteristic_fr: LâĂŽle Laplace fait partie du groupe des ĂŽles de lâInstitut. Pierre-Simon de Laplace (1749-1827) y figure comme lâune des plus hautes autoritĂŠs savantes de son temps, membre influent de lâInstitut dans la section des mathĂŠmatiques, tout comme Bonaparte lui-mĂŞme.
characteristic: Laplace Island forms part of the group of the Institut Islands. Pierre-Simon de Laplace (1749â1827) appears there as one of the foremost savants of his age, an influential member of the Institut in the section of mathematics, like Bonaparte himself.
history_fr: AprĂ¨s avoir lu les volumes dĂŠjĂ  parus du TraitĂŠ de mĂŠcanique cĂŠleste, oĂš Laplace reformulait lâastronomie newtonienne en expliquant les mouvements des planĂ¨tes, des satellites et des comĂ¨tes, tout en montrant la stabilitĂŠ dâensemble du systĂ¨me solaire, Bonaparte lui aurait fait remarquer que Dieu nây ĂŠtait jamais mentionnĂŠ. Laplace lui aurait rĂŠpondu : ÂŤ Sire, je nâavais pas besoin de cette hypothĂ¨se. Âť
history: After reading the published volumes of Laplaceâs TraitĂŠ de mĂŠcanique cĂŠleste, in which the savant reformulated Newtonian astronomy by explaining the motions of planets, satellites, and comets, while demonstrating the overall stability of the solar system, Bonaparte is said to have remarked that God was nowhere mentioned in it. Laplace is said to have replied: âSire, I had no need of that hypothesis.â

characteristic_fr: LâĂŽle Condillac est lâĂŽle la plus septentrionale du groupe des ĂŽles de lâInstitut. Elle fut nommĂŠe en lâhonneur dâĂtienne Bonnot de Condillac (1715-1780), ĂŠcrivain et philosophe majeur des LumiĂ¨res (voir aussi cap Condillac).
characteristic: Condillac Island is the northernmost island of the Institut Islands group. It was named in honour of Ătienne Bonnot de Condillac (1715â1780), one of the major writers and philosophers of the Enlightenment (see also Cape Condillac).
history_fr:
Par sa prĂŠsence dans cette nomenclature, Condillac figure parmi les grandes autoritĂŠs intellectuelles dont les travaux continuaient dâirriguer la culture savante et politique de la pĂŠriode napolĂŠonienne.
La gĂŠnĂŠration des officiers, des savants et des administrateurs Ă  laquelle appartenait lâexpĂŠdition Baudin demeurait familiĂ¨re de son Ĺuvre. Sa philosophie sensualiste, fondĂŠe sur lâidĂŠe que les opĂŠrations de lâesprit procĂ¨dent des sensations transformĂŠes et ordonnĂŠes par le langage, exerĂ§a une influence durable sur les milieux intellectuels de la fin du XVIIIe siĂ¨cle et du dĂŠbut du XIXe siĂ¨cle. Elle nourrissait encore les rĂŠflexions des IdĂŠologues, dont plusieurs entretinrent des liens ĂŠtroits avec les institutions savantes du temps.
Le jeune Bonaparte lui-mĂŞme avait ĂŠtĂŠ marquĂŠ par ces lectures. Son Discours sur le bonheur, prĂŠsentĂŠ en 1791 au concours de lâAcadĂŠmie de Lyon sur ÂŤ lâĂŠducation Ă  donner aux hommes pour les mettre sur le chemin du bonheur Âť, porte la trace de lâinfluence de Condillac, tout autant que de celle de Raynal. Si NapolĂŠon sâĂŠloigna par la suite de certains de ces hĂŠritages intellectuels, la prĂŠsence du nom de Condillac dans le groupe des ĂŽles de lâInstitut rappelle la place ĂŠminente quâoccupait encore la tradition philosophique issue des LumiĂ¨res dans lâunivers savant auquel PĂŠron entendait rendre hommage.
history:
By its inclusion in this nomenclature, Condillac appears among those leading intellectual authorities whose works continued to shape the learned and political culture of the Napoleonic period.
The generation of officers, savants, and administrators to which the Baudin expedition belonged remained deeply familiar with his writings. His sensualist philosophy, grounded in the idea that the operations of the mind arise from sensations transformed and ordered through language, exercised a lasting influence on the intellectual world of the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries. It continued to nourish the reflections of the IdĂŠologues, several of whom maintained close ties with the principal learned institutions of the age.
The young Bonaparte himself had been shaped by such reading. His Discours sur le bonheur, submitted in 1791 to the competition organised by the AcadĂŠmie de Lyon on âthe education to be given to men in order to place them on the path to happiness,â clearly bears the mark of Condillacâs influence, as well as that of Raynal. Although NapolĂŠon later distanced himself from some of these early intellectual inheritances, the presence of Condillacâs name within the Institut Islands recalls the eminent place still occupied by the philosophical tradition of the Enlightenment in the learned world to which PĂŠron and Freycinet wished to pay tribute.

characteristic_fr:
LâĂŽle Pascal est lâĂŽle la plus septentrionale du groupe de lâInstitut et se trouve relativement isolĂŠe des autres ĂŽles de lâarchipel. 
Blaise Pascal (1623-1662) fut lâune des grandes figures savantes et littĂŠraires du XVIIe siĂ¨cle, universellement tenu pour un gĂŠnie.
characteristic:
Pascal Island is the northernmost island of the Institut group and lies relatively isolated from the other islands of the archipelago. Pascal Island is the northernmost island of the Institut group and lies relatively isolated from the other islands of the archipelago. 
Blaise Pascal (1623â1662) was one of the great scientific and literary figures of the seventeenth century and was widely regarded as a genius.
history_fr:
Pascal appears among the Institut Islands because he is regarded as one of the fathers of modern science, as well as one of the great philosophical figures of the seventeenth century. His presence in this nomenclature recalls the enduring importance of his work within the learned culture that the expedition sought to honour.
Pascal observed that, in his own age â and the reflection remained pertinent in the Revolutionary and Imperial periods, as it still does today â falsehood, illusion, private interests, social habits, and dominant forms of discourse can so fill the public sphere that truth becomes difficult to discern. It is not absent, in his view, so much as obscured, covered over by noise, manipulation, pride, or fear. He thus suggested that truth can only truly be recognised by those prepared to prefer it to their comfort, their prejudices, and sometimes even their advantage. Hence his often-quoted warning: âTruth is so obscured in these times, and falsehood so established, that unless one loves truth, one cannot know it.â
history:
Pascal figure dans le groupe des ĂŽles de lâInstitut parce quâil est tenu pour lâun des pĂ¨res de la science moderne, tout autant que pour lâune des grandes figures philosophiques du XVIIe siĂ¨cle. Sa prĂŠsence dans cette nomenclature rappelle lâimportance durable de son Ĺuvre dans la culture savante que lâexpĂŠdition entendait honorer.
Pascal observait que, dans son temps â et cette rĂŠflexion demeurait pertinente Ă  lâĂŠpoque rĂŠvolutionnaire et impĂŠriale, comme elle lâest encore aujourdâhui â le mensonge, les illusions, les intĂŠrĂŞts particuliers, les habitudes sociales et les discours dominants peuvent Ă  ce point envahir lâespace public que la vĂŠritĂŠ en devient difficile Ă  discerner. Elle est, selon lui, moins absente quâobscurcie, recouverte par le bruit, la manipulation, lâorgueil ou la peur. Il suggĂŠrait ainsi quâon ne reconnaĂŽt vĂŠritablement la vĂŠritĂŠ quâĂ  la condition dâĂŞtre prĂŞt Ă  la prĂŠfĂŠrer Ă  son confort, Ă  ses prĂŠjugĂŠs et parfois mĂŞme Ă  son avantage. DâoĂš cet avertissement, souvent citĂŠ : ÂŤ La vĂŠritĂŠ est si obscurcie en ce temps, et le mensonge si ĂŠtabli, quâĂ  moins dâaimer la vĂŠritĂŠ, on ne saurait la connaĂŽtre. Âť

characteristic_fr: Aujourdâhui, lâĂŽle Corneille fait partie du groupe des Kingsmill Islands. Dans lâatlas de Freycinet, elle appartenait toutefois au groupe des ĂŽles de lâInstitut. Elle fut nommĂŠe en lâhonneur de Pierre Corneille, dramaturge franĂ§ais du XVIIe siĂ¨cle, qui renouvela la comĂŠdie et transforma profondĂŠment lâart de la tragĂŠdie, qui devient le lieu dâun combat moral, comme le montre le cĂŠlĂ¨bre vers du Cid: ÂŤ Ă rage ! Ă´ dĂŠsespoir ! Ă´ vieillesse ennemie ! Âť qui nâest pas seulement une plainte, mais aussi le dĂŠbut dâune crise de lâhonneur qui pousse ensuite Ă  lâaction.
characteristic: Today, Corneille Island forms part of the Kingsmill Islands. In Freycinetâs atlas, however, it belonged to the group of the Institut Islands. It was named after Pierre Corneille, the seventeenth-century French playwright who renewed comedy and profoundly transformed the art of tragedy.
history_fr: PĂŠron donna le nom de Corneille Ă  lâune des ĂŽles de lâInstitut, celui-ci apparaissant comme une sorte dâencyclopĂŠdie vivante de la culture franĂ§aise. Pierre Corneille y avait naturellement sa place comme lâun des plus grands auteurs du classicisme franĂ§ais. Il renouvela profondĂŠment la tragĂŠdie en en faisant un thĂŠĂ˘tre de lâaction, de la dĂŠcision et de la grandeur morale, lĂ  oĂš elle demeurait encore souvent marquĂŠe par lâimitation de lâAntiquitĂŠ, lâampleur des discours et une relative fixitĂŠ de lâaction. Pour Corneille, le tragique de la vie vient du choix qui oblige lâhomme Ă  se dĂŠpasser. En plaĂ§ant au centre de la scĂ¨ne la volontĂŠ hĂŠroĂŻque, il donnait Ă  la tragĂŠdie une ĂŠnergie et une dignitĂŠ propres Ă  sĂŠduire Bonaparte, sensible Ă  cette dramaturgie de lâhonneur, du commandement et de la maĂŽtrise de soi.
history: PĂŠron gave the name of Corneille to one of the Institut Islands, the Institut itself appearing as a kind of living encyclopaedia of French culture. Pierre Corneille naturally had his place there as one of the greatest writers of French classicism. He profoundly renewed tragedy by making it a theatre of action, decision, and moral greatness, at a time when French tragedy still often remained marked by imitation of antiquity, long speeches, and relatively static action. By placing heroic will at the centre of the stage, he gave tragedy an energy and dignity that were well suited to appeal to Bonaparte, who was particularly responsive to this dramaturgy of honour, command, and self-mastery.

characteristic_fr: Aujourdâhui, lâĂŽle Racine se trouve Ă  lâentrĂŠe du golfe de lâAmirautĂŠ. Ă lâĂŠpoque du voyage de dĂŠcouverte, elle faisait partie des ĂŽles de lâInstitut, puisque Jean-Baptiste Racine (1639-1699) ĂŠtait considĂŠrĂŠ comme lâun des plus grands reprĂŠsentants de la littĂŠrature franĂ§aise, dont lâInstitut de France incarnait les valeurs.
characteristic: Today, Racine Island lies at the entrance to Admiralty Gulf. At the time of the voyage of discovery, it formed part of the Institut Islands, since Jean-Baptiste Racine (1639â1699) was regarded as one of the greatest representatives of French literature, whose values the Institut de France embodied.
history_fr:
Dans certaines tragĂŠdies de Racine, comme PhĂ¨dre, ce qui domine est moins lâaction politique que le drame intĂŠrieur : la passion interdite, la honte, lâaveu et la fatalitĂŠ morale. Une telle tragĂŠdie pouvait ĂŞtre admirĂŠe par NapolĂŠon pour sa perfection littĂŠraire, la puretĂŠ de sa langue et lâintensitĂŠ de ses vers, mais elle lui offrait moins directement quâune piĂ¨ce de Corneille un modĂ¨le de gouvernement ou de grandeur politique.
Britannicus, en revanche, proposait un sujet beaucoup plus politique. Lorsque Agrippine, mĂ¨re de NĂŠron, sâadresse Ă  son fils en lui disant : ÂŤ Tu nâas pas fait ce pas pour reculer Âť (Britannicus, acte V, scĂ¨ne 6), elle formule la logique de lâirrĂŠversibilitĂŠ du pouvoir : une fois engagĂŠ dans la conquĂŞte de lâautoritĂŠ absolue, le souverain ne peut plus revenir en arriĂ¨re. Une telle phrase pouvait rĂŠsonner fortement pour Bonaparte, dont lâascension politique reposait elle aussi sur une succession de seuils franchis. Mais, parce quâelle sâapplique Ă  NĂŠron, elle portait ĂŠgalement un avertissement : la marche en avant du pouvoir peut conduire Ă  la tyrannie. Racine offrait donc Ă  NapolĂŠon un miroir plus ambigu que Corneille : non pas seulement lâimage hĂŠroĂŻque du chef dâĂtat, mais aussi celle de ses possibles dĂŠrives.
Il nâen demeure pas moins que Racine ĂŠtait abondamment jouĂŠ et admirĂŠ Ă  lâĂŠpoque napolĂŠonienne. Son nom appartenait pleinement au patrimoine littĂŠraire que lâexpĂŠdition franĂ§aise pouvait projeter sur les cĂ´tes australes. En donnant Ă  cette ĂŽle le nom de Racine, PĂŠron et ses compagnons inscrivaient donc dans la gĂŠographie du voyage lâun des grands symboles de la littĂŠrature classique franĂ§aise.
history:
In some of Racineâs tragedies, such as PhĂ¨dre, the dominant themes are not political action but inner conflict: forbidden passion, shame, confession and moral fatality. Such a tragedy could be admired by Napoleon for its literary perfection, the purity of its language and the intensity of its verse, but it offered him a less direct model of government or political grandeur than the plays of Corneille.
Britannicus, however, presented a much more political subject. When Agrippina, Neroâs mother, addresses her son with the words, âYou have not taken this step in order to retreatâ (Britannicus, Act V, Scene 6), she expresses the logic of political irreversibility: once a ruler has embarked upon the conquest of absolute power, he can no longer turn back. Such a line could have resonated strongly with Bonaparte, whose own rise to power was marked by a succession of decisive thresholds. Yet, because the words are addressed to Nero, they also carry a warning: the forward march of power may lead to tyranny. Racine therefore offered Napoleon a more ambiguous mirror than Corneille: not only the heroic image of the statesman, but also the image of powerâs possible excesses.
Nevertheless, Racine was widely performed and admired during the Napoleonic period. His name fully belonged to the literary heritage that the French expedition could project onto the southern coasts. By giving this island the name Racine, PĂŠron and his companions thus inscribed in the geography of the voyage one of the great symbols of French classical literature.

characteristic_fr:
ĂŽle Malus est situĂŠe dans lâangle nord-est de Whalers Bay, au sein de lâarchipel de Dampier, sur la cĂ´te nord-ouest de lâAustralie-Occidentale. Elle se caractĂŠrise par une anse naturellement abritĂŠe, au fond sableux, offrant un mouillage sĂťr. Cette configuration gĂŠographique a favorisĂŠ lâinstallation, au XIXáľ siĂ¨cle, dâune station baleiniĂ¨re cĂ´tiĂ¨re, dont les vestiges sont implantĂŠs sur les hauteurs dominant la crique.
LâĂŽle fut nommĂŠe par Louis-Claude de Freycinet en l'honneur de l'ingĂŠnieur, physicien et mathĂŠmaticien franĂ§ais Ătienne Louis Malus (1775 - 1812).
characteristic:
Malus Island is located in the north-eastern corner of Whalers Bay, within the Dampier Archipelago, off the north-west coast of Western Australia. It is characterised by a naturally sheltered cove with a sandy bottom, providing a safe anchorage. This geographical configuration favoured the establishment, in the nineteenth century, of a shore-based whaling station, the remains of which are situated on the elevated ground overlooking the cove. 
The island was named by Louis-Claude de Freycinet in honour of the French engineer, physicist and mathematician Ătienne Louis Malus (1775â1812).
history_fr:
Freycinet indique que lâĂŽle Malus est de dimensions infĂŠrieures Ă  celles de lâĂŽle du Romarin. Il propose en outre un plan particulier de ces deux ĂŽles dans un encart accompagnant la Carte dâune partie de la Terre de Witt. Dans ce document cartographique, les traits pleins correspondent aux contours ĂŠtablis Ă  partir dâobservations directes, tandis que les tracĂŠs en pointillĂŠs signalent les parties conjecturales des ĂŽles qui nâont pas fait lâobjet de relevĂŠs prĂŠcis.
Il est probable que Freycinet ait rencontrĂŠ Ătienne-Louis Malus Ă  Anvers en 1804, Ă  lâĂŠpoque oĂš ce dernier ĂŠtait chargĂŠ des travaux dâextension du port, tandis que Freycinet commandait le brick Le Voltigeur. Malus est principalement connu comme lâauteur de la loi qui porte son nom, principe fondamental de lâoptique dĂŠcrivant la variation de lâintensitĂŠ lumineuse transmise par un polariseur parfait.
history:
Freycinet notes that Malus Island is smaller than Rosemary Island. He also presents a specific plan of these two islands in an inset accompanying the Map of a Part of Terre de Witt. In this cartographic document, he uses solid lines to indicate the outlines recorded through direct observation, while dotted lines mark the conjectural traces of the remaining portions of the islands that were not directly surveyed.
It is likely that Freycinet met Ătienne-Louis Malus in Antwerp in 1804, when Malus was overseeing port expansion works and Freycinet was serving as commander of the brig Le Voltigeur. Malus is best known as the author of Malusâs law, a fundamental principle in optics describing the variation in light intensity transmitted through a perfect polariser.
